title: Adaptive SLO Shepherding & Capacity Fencing Playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook codifies an adaptive approach we call "SLO shepherding" for day-to-day SRE operations: proactively protecting critical SLOs by applying capacity fencing, tenant reservations, and temporary degradation policies while preserving error-budget fairness across tenants. It combines measurement, automated tooling, and human guardrails to avoid surprise outages and prevent long reconciliations after incidents.

Scope & applicability

- Applies to multi-tenant inference services: inference-gateway, model-host-pool, and routing-proxy.
- Intended for services with latency or availability SLOs and where capacity is a constrained resource (GPU pools, KV cache memory, or token throughput).
- Not intended for non-production dev environments or single-tenant dedicated customers (those use dedicated capacity contracts).

Goals

1. Keep critical customer-facing SLOs within target (example target: 99.95% availability monthly; p99 latency < 350ms for small text-gen).
2. Avoid global emergency scaling that undermines fairness: use fenced capacity to protect tiered SLAs.
3. Provide clear, repeatable runbooks for Capacity Fencing events and Post-Incident Review evidence.

Key service SLOs (examples)

| Service | SLO (primary) | Window | Alert threshold |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| inference-gateway | p99 latency < 350ms | 30d rolling | p99 > 350ms for 30m |
| model-host-pool | request success rate >= 99.95% | 30d rolling | 1h error rate > 0.5% |
| routing-proxy | availability >= 99.9% | 30d rolling | 15m availability < 99% |

Error budget policy summary

- Error budget = 1 - SLO. Consumption is measured at the service-level per 30-day window.
- Progressive response tiers based on burn rate (30m burn-rate windows):
  1. Mild burn (burn rate < 2x): notify owner, enable additional tracing.
  2. Elevated burn (2x–5x): restrict non-critical background workloads; throttle batch ingestion by 30%.
  3. Severe burn (>5x or >50% of remaining budget): apply Capacity Fencing (see below) and escalate to SRE lead.

Capacity fencing model (high level)

Capacity fencing = temporarily reserving a percentage of pool resources for critical tenants or SLO classes, enforcing soft quotas for lower tiers, and enabling rapid rollback steps.

Rules of thumb for fence sizing:
- Critical SLO pool reservation = max(10% of pool, 2x typical peak tokens/sec of top-3 tenants).
- Hedge for warmup = +15% additional reservation for model-host-pool to cover KV cache pressure.
- Minimum fence time = 5 minutes; maximum auto-fence time = 2 hours before manual review.

Reservation tiers (example configuration)

| Tier | Guarantee | Typical enforcement |
|---|---|---|
| Tier-1 (platinum) | Hard reservation (capacity locked) | Dedicated slots on model-host-pool; preempt lower tiers if needed |
| Tier-2 (gold) | Soft reservation (priority) | Priority queuing + token-priority shaping |
| Tier-3 (standard) | Best-effort | Subject to throttles and batch shedding |

Runbook: Emergency Capacity Fence (quick steps)

1. Trigger conditions (one or more):
   - SLO alert enters "Severe burn" state.
   - Sustained autoscaler failure causing accelerated eviction.
   - Cross-region saturation (>90% regional GPU pool for >10m).
2. Initial triage (first 10 minutes):
   - Confirm telemetry: run `curl -s https://telemetry-intake/api/v1/query -d 'service=inference-gateway&metric=p99_latency'`.
   - Notify on-call SRE in #oncall-sre and update incident channel.
   - Snapshot current capacity: `kubectl get pods -l app=model-host-pool -o wide` and `redis-cli INFO memory` for KV cache standing.
3. Apply a temporary fence:
   - Invoke capacity controller API: `POST /ops/reservations` with payload (example below).
   - Start with a conservative fence (reserve 10% of pool) and monitor p99 for 5m.
   - If no improvement, escalate to reserve 25% and optionally reduce batch ingestion.
4. Throttles and graceful degradation:
   - Enable token-priority shaping for background tenants.
   - Reduce max_context_length to 1024 for standard tier: toggle via feature-flag `feature.max_context_reduction`.
5. Communication and timeline:
   - Update incident channel every 10 minutes with CPU/GPU util, p99 latency, and error budget remaining.
   - If fence stands >30 minutes, convene SRE lead + product owner decision.

Example reservation payload (YAML)

```
# POST /ops/reservations
service: model-host-pool
scope: region-us-west-2
reservation_id: fence-20280305-aria
duration_minutes: 60
reservations:
  - tenant_group: tier-1
    reserved_gpu_shares: 12
  - tenant_group: tier-2
    reserved_gpu_shares: 6
policy: preempt-lower-tiers
auto_expire: true
```

Automated tooling hooks

- Capacity Controller: `ops-capacity-controller` (internal service) exposes `/ops/reservations` and `/ops/preview-reservation` for dry runs.
- SLO Watcher: `slo-watcher` computes burn rates and notifies `oncall-sre` via PagerDuty when thresholds met.
- Telemetry dashboards: `slo-ops/inference-gateway` (Grafana); ensure p99, p50, error-rate, GPU-util, and KV-cache-hitrate are visible on one pane.

Incident review & evidence standards

Every event where Capacity Fencing was applied must include the following artifacts in the post-incident package:
- Timeline: minute-level timeline of actions (who, when, what).
- Telemetry exports: p99 series, error-rate, GPU util, queue depth for the incident window and 24h prior.
- Reservation decision rationale: why fence size and duration chosen.
- Rollback artifacts: commands used to revert fences and toggles.
- RCA summary (5–10 sentences) and remediation owner with expected completion date.

Incident review checklist (required fields)

1. Was the fence auto-triggered or manual?
2. Source of overload (traffic surge, model regression, autoscaler bug, external region issue).
3. Evidence that fence improved SLOs (graphs or quant stats).
4. Any customer-facing degradation or API-level contract changes.
5. Follow-up actions (who and ETA).

SRE operational playbook: day-to-day shepherding tasks

- Daily: Review SLO Watcher digest between 09:00–10:00 PT. Note tenants with persistent high burn.
- Weekly: Run fairness audit script to verify reserved vs actual consumption: `python tools/fairness_audit.py --window 7d`.
- Monthly: Recompute reservation baselines based on 95th percentile token/sec per tenant and schedule capacity rebalancing if trend >15% growth.

On-call procedures and escalations

- First responder: apply temporary soft throttles and open incident.
- Second responder (SRE lead): evaluate fence sizing, approve hard reservations.
- Product/security escalations: required if degradation affects compliance-sensitive tenants or if data-plane failures expose PII concerns.

Rollout & verification for changes to fencing logic

1. Feature-flag behind `ops.fencing.v2`.
2. Canary in staging with synthetic traffic profile that simulates: spike, slow-burning sustained increase, and cache-heavy workload.
3. Metrics to validate: p99 latency reduction, fairness index (see appendix), and false-positive fence rate.

Ownership and governance

- Primary owner: eng-sre (team: runtime-capacity), oncall rota: eng-sre/runtime-capacity.
- Product alignment: product-inference owns SLA tiers definitions.
- Quarterly review: SRE lead to present fencing efficacy in reliability-initiatives retro.

Appendix: fairness index and sample queries

- Fairness index (simple): FI = reserved_share / observed_consumption. Target FI ∈ [0.8, 1.2] over a 7d window.

Sample PromQL (pseudocode)
```
sum(rate(tokens_served[5m])) by (tenant_group) / sum(rate(gpu_seconds[5m])) by (tenant_group)
```

SQL-like query to compute error budget spend per tenant (example):
```
SELECT tenant_id, SUM(1 - success_rate) AS budget_used
FROM slo_events
WHERE service = 'inference-gateway' AND ts >= '2028-02-01'
GROUP BY tenant_id;
```

Related pages & references

- See /eng-sre/runbooks/slo-provisioning-and-backfill-orchestration-runbook-2026 for reservation lifecycle.
- See /eng-sre/operational-tooling/slo-observability-pipeline-design-and-validation-2025 for telemetry instrumentation standards.

Change log

- 2028-01-10 (Aria Chen) — Drafted initial playbook.
- 2028-02-20 (reviewers) — Incorporated fairness audit steps and sample payloads.
- 2028-03-05 (published) — Clarified alert tiers and fence auto-expiry behavior.

Contact

Primary owner: eng-sre/runtime-capacity (alias: #eng-sre-runtime-capacity). For immediate help, ping oncall-sre or escalate via PagerDuty rule `slo-shepherding-pager`.

title: SRE Prioritized Risk Remediation Framework
preview:
Overview
========
This document defines a prioritized risk remediation framework that ties SLOs, error budgets, capacity planning, incident reviews, and operational tooling into a single, repeatable remediation lifecycle for Redwood Inference services. It is intended for SREs, service owners, and platform engineers who are responsible for reducing systemic risk across model-serving and control-plane services.

Goals
-----
- Make remediation decisions deterministic and measurable.
- Prioritize work that yields the largest reduction in customer-visible risk per engineering-hour.
- Ensure remediation actions are linked to SLO outcomes and error-budget burn signals.
- Embed remediation into regular reliability initiatives (quarterly guardrails, sprint work, and on-call action items).

Scope
-----
Includes: model-serving frontends, batch embedding pipelines, autoscaling controllers, cache layers (prefix/KV), routing and fallback policies, and observability/telemetry pipelines.
Excludes: internal admin portals (unless they impact public SLOs), billing pipelines (handled by Finance Ops).

Principles
----------
1. SLO-first: Every remediation ticket must reference at least one impacted SLO or error-budget metric.
2. Quantify impact: Estimate expected SLO improvement (absolute/relative) and customer impact minutes saved.
3. Cost-aware: Rank remediation by SLO benefit per engineer-day and estimated infra cost change.
4. Time-boxed proof-of-ideas: Quick mitigations (<=2 days) prioritized for canary validation before larger refactors.
5. Observability-driven: No remediation without pre/post verification metrics and synthetic checks.

Risk Scoring and Prioritization
-------------------------------
We use a composite score (0-100) that combines severity, frequency, blast radius, and remediation effort. Tickets with score >= 60 are eligible for high-priority remediation sprints.

Scoring components (numeric weights):
- Severity (35%): mapped to customer-visible SLO degradation (5 pts = catastrophic outage → 0 pts = negligible).
- Frequency (25%): how often the condition is observed (daily, weekly, monthly).
- Blast radius (20%): % of tenant traffic or critical routes affected.
- Effort multiplier (20%): inverse of estimated engineer-days (lower effort increases score).

Priority bands:
- Critical (score >= 80): immediate on-call escalation; action within 48 hours.
- High (60–79): schedule for next reliability sprint; assigned owner within 5 business days.
- Medium (40–59): backlog candidate for quarterly remediation wave.
- Low (<40): monitor, archive as a candidate for architectural debt planning.

Triage Procedure (step-by-step)
-------------------------------
1. Detection: Automation or human detection (alerts, postmortem, stakeholder report).
2. Evidence collection: attach traces, p99/p50 latency deltas, telemetry queries, relevant runbook links.
3. SLO mapping: list impacted SLO(s) and current error-budget burn rates.
4. Preliminary scoring: apply composite score matrix (see appendix).
5. Mitigation plan: short-term (<=48h), medium-term (<=2 weeks), long-term (>2 weeks) actions.
6. Assign owner and timeline: SRE or service team ownership required; document in ticket.
7. Verification: pre/post telemetry and synthetic canary asserts.
8. Close or escalate to reliability-initiative if recurring or systemic.

SLO and Error Budget Policy (examples)
-------------------------------------
- Request latency SLOs (service examples):
  - inference-api-prod: 95th percentile latency <= 250ms, 99th <= 1.2s, monthly availability target 99.9%.
  - embeddings-bulk: 99th percentile <= 3.0s, availability 99.5%.
- Error budget burn actions:
  - Burn rate > 3x for 60m → create high-priority remediation ticket and schedule immediate mitigation window.
  - Burn rate > 1.5x sustained for 24h → trigger dedicated debugging rotation + daily status updates.
  - Full budget consumed → freeze non-critical releases for affected service(s) until budget recovery.
- Automatic mitigations: When a model-serving tier consumes >70% error budget in one week, traffic is gradually routed to a fallback variant per routing policy (see Operational Tooling integrations).

Capacity Planning Integration
----------------------------
- Capacity signals used in risk scoring: node preemption rates, queue length percentiles (p95), GPU utilization sustained >85% for 30m, cold-start rates.
- Capacity threshold actions:
  - Sustained GPU util >85% (30m) && request latency p95 degradation >20% → urgent scale-up action (autoscale or emergency burst capacity).
  - Queue depth > threshold for 10m → enable backpressure and announce tenant rate-limits to affected teams.
- Monthly capacity reviews: service owners must present forecast vs actual at monthly capacity ops review and propose remediation tickets for sustained overrun.

Operational Tooling and Automation
---------------------------------
The framework leverages Redwood operational tooling. Primary integrations:
- Telemetry: Datadog (metrics/traces), OpenTelemetry traces, and internal SLO pipeline.
- Alerting: PagerDuty incident rules tied to composite SLO-alerts and automated burn-rate detectors.
- Runbook/Playbook: Confluence runbook templates + runbook execution recorder.
- Orchestration: Autoscaler service (autoscaler.redwood.internal), rollout controller (rollout.redwood.internal), and traffic-router service.
- Remediation board: A dedicated Jira project (SRE-RMD) with custom fields (risk_score, slo_refs, burn_rate_snapshot).

Runbook Template (must be attached to every remediation ticket)
--------------------------------------------------------------
1. Title: clear description and primary SLO reference.
2. Detection summary: alert IDs, query snippets, relevant synthetic names.
3. Immediate mitigations: commands, toggles, and runbook steps (time-boxed).
4. Owner and escalation path: primary, secondary, on-call contact.
5. Post-mitigation verification: required metrics, dashboards, and synthetic runs.
6. Long-term work: PRs, architecture changes, and rollout plan.

Sample short-term mitigation checklist (commands)
------------------------------------------------
- Route traffic to fallback variant: curl -XPOST https://router.internal/v1/route -d '{"service":"inference-api-prod","variant":"fallback-v2","weight":0.25}'
- Enable read-only cache mode: kubectl -n inference set env deployment/cache-service MODE=read-only
- Pause scheduled retrains: airflow pause backfill_embeddings_pipeline
- Add synthetic canary: curl -XPOST https://synthetic.redwood/internal/create -d '{"name":"inference-p95-canary","interval":60}'

Verification and Acceptance Criteria
-----------------------------------
- Pre/post comparison: p95 latency, p99 latency, request success ratio, and error-budget burn rate snapshot.
- Canary stability: no elevated errors for 2x canary run (default 10m).
- KPI gating: remediation marked complete when estimated SLO improvement observed for 72h.

Incident Review and RCA Standards
--------------------------------
Every remediation that originates from an incident must follow post-incident standards:
- Evidence bundle: alert history, traces, heap/profile dumps, autoscaler logs, and rollout IDs.
- RCA template: timeline, contributing factors, root cause, detection gap, mitigation gap, and action items.
- Action item tagging: label remediation tickets as \"short-term\" or \"architectural\" and include target completion dates.
- Retro cadence: owners present remediation status at the next SRE retro; recurring issues promoted to reliability initiatives.

Reliability Initiatives and Backlog Management
--------------------------------------------
- Quarterly reliability initiatives: prioritized list of systemic remediation work (capacity architecture changes, autoscaler improvements, KV cache redesign).
- Initiative intake: any owner can nominate items; SRE leadership scores and selects initiatives for the quarter based on composite risk reduction estimate.
- Metrics for success: initiative-level delta in aggregate SLO-weighted uptime and reduction in incident frequency for targeted services.

Communication and Change Controls
--------------------------------
- Change freeze policy: if a service has consumed >50% of monthly error budget it enters a change-freeze for non-emergency releases; emergency exceptions require SRE PM approval.
- Stakeholder updates: daily during high-burn incidents, weekly during active remediations, and retro after completion.
- Public status page updates: customer-impact incidents must be reflected within 30 minutes after impact is detected per status-page guidelines.

Ownership and Roles
-------------------
- Service Owner (product or platform): prioritizes long-term remediation and approves architecture-level changes.
- SRE Remediation Owner: executes short/medium-term mitigations, owns verification and ticket closure.
- Reliability Program Manager: coordinates quarterly initiatives and prioritization reviews.
- Observability Owner: provides telemetry queries, dashboard updates, and synthetic definitions.

Appendix: Scoring matrix (compact)
---------------------------------
| Component     | Example mapping         | Max points |
| Severity      | Outage=5, Degraded=3    | 35         |
| Frequency     | Daily=5, Weekly=3       | 25         |
| Blast radius  | Global=5, Regional=3    | 20         |
| Effort factor | <1 day=5, >10 days=1    | 20         |

(Score normalized to 0–100; thresholds defined in Priority bands)

Appendix: Example Jira fields and sample JSON payload for SRE-RMD ticket creation
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
{
  \"project\": \"SRE-RMD\",
  \"summary\": \"High p99 latency on inference-api-prod (mapped to SLO)\",
  \"labels\": [\"risk-remediation\", \"slo\"],
  \"customfields\": {\"risk_score\": 78, \"slo_refs\": [\"inference-api-prod-p95-250ms\"]}
}

Appendix: Example queries (Datadog/OTel)
-----------------------------------------
- p95 latency by service: query: avg:trace.duration{service:inference-api-prod}.rollup(\"p95\")
- error budget burn rate: derived metric: (errors / allowed_errors) / time_window

How to adopt this framework
---------------------------
1. Pilot with two services (one model-serving, one infra-control-plane) for one quarter.
2. Validate scoring results vs net reduction in incidents and SLO delta.
3. Roll out to all service teams with training sessions and runbook templates.
4. Automate ticket creation for high-burn signals via the SRE-RMD webhook.

Frequently Asked Questions
--------------------------
Q: Who decides whether an item is \"architectural\" vs \"short-term\"?
A: The remediation owner proposes classification; SRE leadership makes final call during review.

Q: How do we estimate SLO improvement?
A: Use historical incident impact, synthetic load tests, and small canary reroutes to generate conservative estimates.

Q: Can product teams schedule work into reliability initiatives?
A: Yes — items are accepted via normal intake; selection is risk-based and scored.

Contact
-------
For questions or to nominate services for the pilot, contact the Reliability Program Manager: reliability-pgm@redwood.ai or Priya Rao (author).

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-11-15: Created by Priya Rao.
- 2027-01-12: Added automated mitigation thresholds and autoscaler integration (review: Ethan Park).
- 2027-02-10: Finalized scoring weights and sample Jira payload (review: Aisha Khatri, Marco Silva).

Scoring components (numeric weights):
- Severity (35%): mapped to customer-visible SLO degradation (5 pts = catastrophic outage → 0 pts = negligible).
- Frequency (25%): how often the condition is observed (daily, weekly, monthly).
- Blast radius (20%): % of tenant traffic or critical routes affected.
- Effort multiplier (20%): inverse of estimated engineer-days (lower effort increases score).

Priority bands:
- Critical (score >= 80): immediate on-call escalation; action within 48 hours.
- High (60–79): schedule for next reliability sprint; assigned owner within 5 business days.
- Medium (40–59): backlog candidate for quarterly remediation wave.
- Low (<40): monitor, archive as a candidate for architectural debt planning.

Triage Procedure (step-by-step)
-------------------------------
1. Detection: Automation or human detection (alerts, postmortem, stakeholder report).
2. Evidence collection: attach traces, p99/p50 latency deltas, telemetry queries, relevant runbook links.
3. SLO mapping: list impacted SLO(s) and current error-budget burn rates.
4. Preliminary scoring: apply composite score matrix (see appendix).
5. Mitigation plan: short-term (<=48h), medium-term (<=2 weeks), long-term (>2 weeks) actions.
6. Assign owner and timeline: SRE or service team ownership required; document in ticket.
7. Verification: pre/post telemetry and synthetic canary asserts.
8. Close or escalate to reliability-initiative if recurring or systemic.

SLO and Error Budget Policy (examples)
-------------------------------------
- Request latency SLOs (service examples):
  - inference-api-prod: 95th percentile latency <= 250ms, 99th <= 1.2s, monthly availability target 99.9%.
  - embeddings-bulk: 99th percentile <= 3.0s, availability 99.5%.
- Error budget burn actions:
  - Burn rate > 3x for 60m → create high-priority remediation ticket and schedule immediate mitigation window.
  - Burn rate > 1.5x sustained for 24h → trigger dedicated debugging rotation + daily status updates.
  - Full budget consumed → freeze non-critical releases for affected service(s) until budget recovery.
- Automatic mitigations: When a model-serving tier consumes >70% error budget in one week, traffic is gradually routed to a fallback variant per routing policy (see Operational Tooling integrations).

Capacity Planning Integration
----------------------------
- Capacity signals used in risk scoring: node preemption rates, queue length percentiles (p95), GPU utilization sustained >85% for 30m, cold-start rates.
- Capacity threshold actions:
  - Sustained GPU util >85% (30m) && request latency p95 degradation >20% → urgent scale-up action (autoscale or emergency burst capacity).
  - Queue depth > threshold for 10m → enable backpressure and announce tenant rate-limits to affected teams.
- Monthly capacity reviews: service owners must present forecast vs actual at monthly capacity ops review and propose remediation tickets for sustained overrun.

Operational Tooling and Automation
---------------------------------
The framework leverages Redwood operational tooling. Primary integrations:
- Telemetry: Datadog (metrics/traces), OpenTelemetry traces, and internal SLO pipeline.
- Alerting: PagerDuty incident rules tied to composite SLO-alerts and automated burn-rate detectors.
- Runbook/Playbook: Confluence runbook templates + runbook execution recorder.
- Orchestration: Autoscaler service (autoscaler.redwood.internal), rollout controller (rollout.redwood.internal), and traffic-router service.
- Remediation board: A dedicated Jira project (SRE-RMD) with custom fields (risk_score, slo_refs, burn_rate_snapshot).

Runbook Template (must be attached to every remediation ticket)
--------------------------------------------------------------
1. Title: clear description and primary SLO reference.
2. Detection summary: alert IDs, query snippets, relevant synthetic names.
3. Immediate mitigations: commands, toggles, and runbook steps (time-boxed).
4. Owner and escalation path: primary, secondary, on-call contact.
5. Post-mitigation verification: required metrics, dashboards, and synthetic runs.
6. Long-term work: PRs, architecture changes, and rollout plan.

Sample short-term mitigation checklist (commands)
------------------------------------------------
- Route traffic to fallback variant: curl -XPOST https://router.internal/v1/route -d '{"service":"inference-api-prod","variant":"fallback-v2","weight":0.25}'
- Enable read-only cache mode: kubectl -n inference set env deployment/cache-service MODE=read-only
- Pause scheduled retrains: airflow pause backfill_embeddings_pipeline
- Add synthetic canary: curl -XPOST https://synthetic.redwood/internal/create -d '{"name":"inference-p95-canary","interval":60}'

Verification and Acceptance Criteria
-----------------------------------
- Pre/post comparison: p95 latency, p99 latency, request success ratio, and error-budget burn rate snapshot.
- Canary stability: no elevated errors for 2x canary run (default 10m).
- KPI gating: remediation marked complete when estimated SLO improvement observed for 72h.

Incident Review and RCA Standards
--------------------------------
Every remediation that originates from an incident must follow post-incident standards:
- Evidence bundle: alert history, traces, heap/profile dumps, autoscaler logs, and rollout IDs.
- RCA template: timeline, contributing factors, root cause, detection gap, mitigation gap, and action items.
- Action item tagging: label remediation tickets as \"short-term\" or \"architectural\" and include target completion dates.
- Retro cadence: owners present remediation status at the next SRE retro; recurring issues promoted to reliability initiatives.

Reliability Initiatives and Backlog Management
--------------------------------------------
- Quarterly reliability initiatives: prioritized list of systemic remediation work (capacity architecture changes, autoscaler improvements, KV cache redesign).
- Initiative intake: any owner can nominate items; SRE leadership scores and selects initiatives for the quarter based on composite risk reduction estimate.
- Metrics for success: initiative-level delta in aggregate SLO-weighted uptime and reduction in incident frequency for targeted services.

Communication and Change Controls
--------------------------------
- Change freeze policy: if a service has consumed >50% of monthly error budget it enters a change-freeze for non-emergency releases; emergency exceptions require SRE PM approval.
- Stakeholder updates: daily during high-burn incidents, weekly during active remediations, and retro after completion.
- Public status page updates: customer-impact incidents must be reflected within 30 minutes after impact is detected per status-page guidelines.

Ownership and Roles
-------------------
- Service Owner (product or platform): prioritizes long-term remediation and approves architecture-level changes.
- SRE Remediation Owner: executes short/medium-term mitigations, owns verification and ticket closure.
- Reliability Program Manager: coordinates quarterly initiatives and prioritization reviews.
- Observability Owner: provides telemetry queries, dashboard updates, and synthetic definitions.

Appendix: Scoring matrix (compact)
---------------------------------
| Component     | Example mapping         | Max points |
| Severity      | Outage=5, Degraded=3    | 35         |
| Frequency     | Daily=5, Weekly=3       | 25         |
| Blast radius  | Global=5, Regional=3    | 20         |
| Effort factor | <1 day=5, >10 days=1    | 20         |

(Score normalized to 0–100; thresholds defined in Priority bands)

Appendix: Example Jira fields and sample JSON payload for SRE-RMD ticket creation
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
{
  \"project\": \"SRE-RMD\",
  \"summary\": \"High p99 latency on inference-api-prod (mapped to SLO)\",
  \"labels\": [\"risk-remediation\", \"slo\"],
  \"customfields\": {\"risk_score\": 78, \"slo_refs\": [\"inference-api-prod-p95-250ms\"]}
}

Appendix: Example queries (Datadog/OTel)
-----------------------------------------
- p95 latency by service: query: avg:trace.duration{service:inference-api-prod}.rollup(\"p95\")
- error budget burn rate: derived metric: (errors / allowed_errors) / time_window

How to adopt this framework
---------------------------
1. Pilot with two services (one model-serving, one infra-control-plane) for one quarter.
2. Validate scoring results vs net reduction in incidents and SLO delta.
3. Roll out to all service teams with training sessions and runbook templates.
4. Automate ticket creation for high-burn signals via the SRE-RMD webhook.

Frequently Asked Questions
--------------------------
Q: Who decides whether an item is \"architectural\" vs \"short-term\"?
A: The remediation owner proposes classification; SRE leadership makes final call during review.

Q: How do we estimate SLO improvement?
A: Use historical incident impact, synthetic load tests, and small canary reroutes to generate conservative estimates.

Q: Can product teams schedule work into reliability initiatives?
A: Yes — items are accepted via normal intake; selection is risk-based and scored.

Contact
-------
For questions or to nominate services for the pilot, contact the Reliability Program Manager: reliability-pgm@redwood.ai or Priya Rao (author).

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-11-15: Created by Priya Rao.
- 2027-01-12: Added automated mitigation thresholds and autoscaler integration (review: Ethan Park).
- 2027-02-10: Finalized scoring weights and sample Jira payload (review: Aisha Khatri, Marco Silva).

title: Real-time SLO Reallocation and Hot-Path Capacity Procedure
preview:
Summary
-------
This runbook describes an operational pattern and step-by-step procedure for re-allocating SLO budget and rapidly provisioning or shifting capacity to hot execution paths ("hot-path" services) during acute traffic events that threaten customer-facing SLOs. It combines manual escalation, deterministic automation gates, and verification checks so that SREs and paged responders can act quickly with low risk.

Scope and motivation
--------------------
- Applies to Redwood services that touch real-time inference and request-routing: inference-gateway, request-router, kv-cache, continuous-batcher, model-worker, and capacity-allocator.
- Use when an SLO is projecting to miss within the current burn window due to sudden load, model regression, or regional capacity loss.
- Goal: restore SLO headroom to a safe state within 10–30 minutes for high-severity incidents while preserving user isolation and avoiding cascading failures.

Key concepts and definitions
----------------------------
- Hot-path: service components in the per-request low-latency stack (inference-gateway, kv-cache, continuous-batcher).
- SLOs considered: latency_p95, latency_p99, availability (percent success rate), and embedding-throughput.
- Error budget: rolling 30-day budget unless otherwise stated.
- Burn rate thresholds: Normal (<3x), Elevated (3–8x), Emergency (>8x). Automation gates and actions differ by band.

Primary owners and on-call responsibilities
-------------------------------------------
- SLO steward: service owner (example: inference-gateway -> @inference-platform).
- On-call: eng-sre pagers for the service and platform-sre for capacity/cluster operations.
- Automation owner: runtime-ops (capacity-allocator, scheduler).
- Escalation: follow the team’s on-call escalation matrix; primary SRE must notify product-resp only for customer-facing SLA risk.

Observability and signals to monitor
-----------------------------------
- Prometheus alerts (examples):
  - slo_burn_rate_30d > 3 for service="inference-gateway" (Severity: P2)
  - slo_projection_reaches_zero_minutes < 60 (Severity: P1)
  - avg_p95_latency{service="inference-gateway",region="*"} > 200ms (Severity: P1)
  - available_gpu_units < 10% (Severity: P1)

- Helpful PromQL (examples):
  - SLO burn rate (30d):
    sum(increase(slo_errors_total[30d])) / sum(increase(slo_requests_total[30d])) / (target_error_fraction)
  - p95 latency: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, service))

Decision matrix (summary)
-------------------------
| Signal | Burn rate band | Immediate action | Automatable? | Owner | Time objective |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---:|
| SLO projection < 60m | Emergency (>8x) | Trigger emergency capacity workflow: force-burst reservations, cross-region shift, traffic shed lower-priority tenants | Semi-automated (requires human confirm) | on-call + platform-sre | 10–30m |
| p95 latency increase > 2x | Elevated (3–8x) | Increase batch concurrency for low-impact models; enable KV aggressive cache promotion | Automatable with canary | service owner | 5–15m |
| GPU scarcity (<10%) | Any | Initiate rapid GPU reclamation & evict non-critical workloads | Automated with guardrails | capacity-allocator | 5–20m |

(See full table in "Actions and thresholds" section below.)

Safe automation gates
---------------------
All automated actions must satisfy the following preconditions before execution:
1) Observability confirm: two independent signals (metrics + tracing slowdown) show the same impact.
2) Capacity sanity: min_headroom >= 5% post-action for primary region or a validated cross-region fallback exists.
3) Rollback path: automation must be reversible within 2 minutes (e.g., scale-down, revoke reservation).
4) Approval bit: for Emergency band, automation must pause and require a single on-call confirmation via PagerDuty UI or CLI token.

Actions and thresholds (detailed)
--------------------------------
1) Emergency (Burn > 8x or projection < 60m)
   - Owner: on-call (primary) + platform-sre (secondary)
   - Steps:
     a) Post incident marker: create incident in incident tracker (CLI example below).
     b) Force reserve 20% extra capacity from capacity-allocator: CLI -> capacity-allocator reserve --service inference-gateway --units 0.2 --duration 60m
     c) Redirect 30% of new traffic to read-only model variant or smaller model via request-router dynamic-route: request-router set-weight --route inference-prod --variant small-model --weight 0.3
     d) If regional GPU shortage: trigger cross-region redistribution: scheduler relocate --from eu-west --to us-west --max 50pods
     e) If headroom cannot be recovered within 30m: execute tenant-priority shedding (see table below)
   - Verification: p95 latency reduces to <1.5x baseline and slo_projection_reaches_zero_minutes > 240

2) Elevated (Burn 3–8x)
   - Owner: service owner + on-call
   - Steps:
     a) Enable aggressive KV caching: kv-cache promote --policy aggressive --target inference-gateway
     b) Increase batcher concurrency for long-running models: continuous-batcher set-concurrency --service model-worker --delta +2
     c) Start gradual model-quality fallback for non-critical tenants: model-worker set-fallback --tenants tenant-list.yaml
   - Verification: sustained p95 improvement within 10 minutes and error budget burn-rate reduces below 3x.

3) Normal (Burn <3x but trending upward)
   - Owner: service owner
   - Steps:
     a) Tune dynamic batching windows: continuous-batcher set-window --target 5ms
     b) Warm key prefixes into KV cache for anticipated request patterns.
     c) Open a short-lived ticket to follow up on root cause.

Tenant priority shedding table
------------------------------
(Used only as last-resort in Emergency band. Always apply least-impact-first.)
| Priority | Action | Max allowed reduction |
| High (SLA customers) | No shedding unless explicit legal-approved escalation | 0% |
| Medium | Disable model variants >2x cost per token; limit tokens per request to 75% | up to 30% |
| Low | Enforce strict rate-limits, reject low-value batched jobs | up to 70% |

Operational commands and examples
----------------------------------
- Create incident: redwood-ops incident create --title "Hot-path latency surge" --services inference-gateway --priority P1 --assignee @oncall
- Reserve capacity: capacity-allocator reserve --service inference-gateway --units 0.2 --duration 60m --requester @oncall
- Evict best-effort jobs: capacity-allocator evict --queue noncritical-batch --target 30%
- Re-route traffic: request-router set-weight --route inference-prod --variant small-model --weight 0.3
- Revoke reservation (rollback): capacity-allocator release --reservation-id <id>

Verification checklist
----------------------
1) Metrics: p95 and p99 latency trending back toward baseline for 20 minutes.
2) SLO ledger: slo_projection_reaches_zero_minutes increased > 240 minutes.
3) Capacity: available_gpu_units >= 10% or cross-region fallback active.
4) Logs/Tracing: no new increases in tail latency traced to the same code path.
5) Customer impact: check customer-facing dashboards for SLA alerts; notify account teams if High customers were impacted.

Post-incident actions and analysis
---------------------------------
1) Postmortem owner: on-call or SLO steward must open an incident review within 48 hours.
2) Evidence to collect: SLO ledger snapshot, graph of burn rate, capacity-allocator decisions, automation logs, and runbook actions timeline.
3) Remediation: create actionable tickets with owners and deadlines for at least the following: root cause fix, automation improvement, and SLO tuning.
4) run a chaos rehearsal that simulates the recovered scenario within 2 weeks if the event required tenant shedding.

Safety notes and constraints
---------------------------
- Never perform tenant shedding for High-priority SLAs without legal and customer success approval.
- All automation that affects model routing must be tested in canary (10% traffic) before full rollout, except during Emergency where safety gates may be overridden with human confirmation.
- Record every manual override in the incident timeline with rationale.

Related pages
-------------
- /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/service-onboarding-runway-and-slo-admission-playbook-2026
- /confluence/eng-sre/operational-tooling/capacity-observability-and-recovery-playbook-2026
- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/oom-cascade-and-memory-throttling-runbook-2026

Appendices
----------
A. Example JSON for an automated reservation request (safe-mode):
{
  "service": "inference-gateway",
  "units": "0.15",
  "duration_minutes": "60",
  "requester": "@oncall",
  "expiration_action": "auto-release"
}

B. Sample PromQL snippets (copyable):
1) p95 latency:
   histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, service))
2) available GPUs:
   sum(gpu_total_units) - sum(gpu_used_units)

C. Contact list:
- on-call pager: #sre-pager
- platform-sre slack: @platform-sre
- runtime-ops pager: #runtime-ops

End of document

title: SLO Signal Correlation and Notification Synthesis Guide
preview: Defines patterns and procedures to synthesize correlated SLO signals into compact notifications that reduce alert fatigue and accelerate mitigation for model-serving fleets.
text: Defines patterns and procedures to synthesize correlated SLO signals into compact notifications that reduce alert fatigue and accelerate mitigation for model-serving fleets.

title: Predictive Throttle and Canary Rollback Procedure
preview:
Overview:

This runbook describes Redwood's standardized procedure for applying predictive capacity throttling and performing a controlled canary rollback when early signals indicate SLO risk. It codifies the decision thresholds, required telemetry, automation hooks, and human escalation steps so teams can reduce blast radius while preserving customer-facing availability.

Purpose and scope:
- Purpose: minimize customer impact by automatically and safely mitigating traffic surges or degraded model performance before error budgets are exhausted.
- Scope: applies to public inference endpoints, Dedicated pools, and Private VPC deployments where automated routing and throttle policies are enabled. Does NOT cover manual DBA/infra maintenance windows.

Key definitions:
- Predictive throttle: automated traffic shaping that reduces request concurrency or drops non-critical request classes based on forecasted capacity shortfalls.
- Canary rollback: automated or manual rollback of a recently deployed model or runtime variant affecting a small percentage of traffic.
- Primary SLOs: p95 latency < 120ms for text-gen, availability >= 99.9%; token-level quality SLOs as defined per model.

Signals used for decisioning (ordered):
1) Telemetry predictions: 15–60m forecast from the capacity-forecaster service indicating >20% probability of exceeding provisioned tokens/sec.
2) SLO burn rate: instantaneous burn rate > 4x and projected to exhaust error budget in < 12h.
3) Latency signal: sustained p95 above threshold for 5m and increasing.
4) Error spike: client error rate (5xx) increased by >200% relative to 1h baseline.
5) Model quality regressions: eval-set quality delta > configured threshold for last 1k requests.

Preconditions and ownership:
- Owning team: service owner listed in the service manifest (ownership on-service page).
- Oncall: primary oncall for the owning team executes human-confirm steps if automation is not enabled.
- Automation owner: infra-runtime team maintains the throttle controller and canary deploy automation.

Decision matrix (quick reference):
| Trigger type | Immediate automated action | Human required? | Escalation window |
|--------------|----------------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| Telemetry forecast (>=20% oversub) | Apply soft throttle: reduce non-critical classes by 25% | no | 5 minutes |
| Burn rate >4x & projection <12h | Apply strict throttle (50%) and notify oncall | yes | 2 minutes |
| Latency p95 > threshold (5m) | Mark for canary rollback candidate; keep throttles | yes | 5 minutes |
| 5xx spike >200% | Fail traffic to fallback model variant + throttle | yes | immediate |
| Eval quality delta exceeded | Pause new deployments; start canary rollback | yes | immediate |

Operational steps (automated path):
1. Detection: capacity-forecaster raises alert -> alert rules in PagerDuty (tag: predictive-throttle).
2. Soft throttle: throttle-controller reduces concurrency for non-critical routes by 25% and reroutes lower-tier customers to best-effort pool. CLI: rdwctl throttle apply --service {svc} --class noncritical --percent 25 --reason "forecasted capacity shortfall"
3. Telemetry re-evaluation: after 3m, controller re-evaluates predicted capacity. If projection still >20% oversub, escalate to strict throttle.
4. Strict throttle: throttle-controller enforces 50% reduction and reduces batch sizes to limit token throughput. CLI: rdwctl throttle apply --service {svc} --percent 50 --batchesize 8 --reason "strict policy"
5. Canary selection: router picks 1% of traffic from the affected pool and switches it to the previous stable model variant (canary rollback). Automation: rollout-controller can perform rollback automatically when configured; otherwise oncall triggers rollback.
6. Recovery monitoring: observe SLOs for 15m. If SLOs recover, gradually remove throttles over 30m (linear unwind: 25% -> 0).
7. Post-action: create incident ticket automatically with root cause artifacts attached (traces, flamegraphs, capacity forecasts, sample inputs).

Operational steps (human-assisted path):
1. PagerDuty alert -> acknowledge and join incident channel. Post initial assessment within 2 minutes.
2. Confirm trigger signals. If safe and automation disabled, execute rdwctl throttle apply (soft) and follow automated re-eval steps.
3. If rollback required: perform canary rollback via rollout-controller UI or CLI: rdwctl rollout rollback --service {svc} --target-version {previous} --canary-percent 1 --wait 300s
4. Monitor and escalate to SRE lead after 10 minutes if no improvement.
5. If rollback fails or regression persists, execute full rollback: rdwctl rollout rollback --service {svc} --target-version {previous} --canary-percent 100 --wait 600s

Runbook playbook for capacity planners (when predictive throttles are frequent):
- Frequency > 3 predictive throttle events per 7 days => require capacity retrospective with infra-runtime and owning team.
- Retrospective agenda: 1) Review forecasts vs reality; 2) Examine batching/quantization opportunities; 3) Re-evaluate routing policies; 4) Consider reserved capacity increase or model quantization changes.

SLO and error budget integration:
- When predictive throttle is triggered by forecast only, increment a special "throttle" counter that counts against a monthly operational allowance (not the customer-visible error budget) to track how often service-level mitigation is applied.
- If strict throttle or rollback is required, record event as an error-budget-impacting incident and annotate incident with burn-rate and estimated customer token loss.

Telemetry & dashboards required (minimum):
- SLO dashboard: p50/p95/p99 latency, error rates, burn-rate, error budget remaining.
- Capacity-forecast view: 15m/30m/60m probability distribution, detected anomalies.
- Throttle-controller audit: active throttles, last-applied reason, owner, and unwind schedule.
- Canary rollback dashboard: canary percent, model versions, quality delta, sample responses.

Essential CLI snippets and automation examples:
- Soft throttle: rdwctl throttle apply --service inference-api --class background --percent 25 --reason "forecasted_high"
- Strict throttle: rdwctl throttle apply --service inference-api --percent 50 --batchesize 8 --reason "strict"
- Canary rollback (automated template):
  {
    "service": "inference-api",
    "from_version": "v1.24",
    "to_version": "v1.23",
    "canary_percent": "1",
    "health_check": {
      "slo_check": "p95_latency < 120ms",
      "quality_check": "eval_delta < 0.5%"
    }
  }

Sample throttle policy (YAML):
- name: predictive-throttle-policy
  trigger:
    - forecast_probability: 0.2
      forecast_window: 60m
  actions:
    - type: soft-throttle
      percent: 25
      scope: noncritical
    - type: strict-throttle
      percent: 50
      scope: all
  canary_rollback:
    enabled: true
    canary_percent: 1
    monitor_window: 15m

Incident review and post-incident requirements:
1. If strict throttle or any rollback occurred, open an incident review within 72 hours. Document: timeline, signals, who ran commands (CLI audit), dashboards, and decision rationale.
2. Include the capacity-forecaster prediction artifacts and the throttle-controller audit log.
3. Action items must be recorded with owners and expected completion dates. Track in the SRE reliability board.

Playbook templates for incident reviews (minimum sections):
- Summary and impact
- Timeline of automated/human actions (with timestamps)
- Evidence (telemetry snapshots, sample bad requests, model eval diffs)
- Root cause hypothesis and validation steps
- Preventative changes (code/config/system)
- Postmortem owner and due date for action items

Safety checks and abort conditions:
- If canary rollback causes quality delta > 1% worse for canary traffic, abort rollback and escalate to oncall lead.
- If throttles are applied but SLOs continue to degrade for 30m, trigger emergency capacity reallocation (move traffic to warm standby region).

Appendices:
A. Ownership contacts:
- Throttle-controller SRE oncall: #infra-throttle-pd
- Rollout-controller owner: infra-runtime team (Slack: @infra-runtime)

B. Playbook change log:
- 2026-05-10: Initial published runbook (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-07-01: Added evaluation-based rollback trigger (Lin Zhao).
- 2026-11-02: Tightened strict throttle thresholds after experiment (Marcus Hale).

C. Useful links:
- rollout-controller runbook: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/rollout-controller-operations
- capacity-forecaster docs: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/capacity-forecaster

End of document.
text:
Overview:

This runbook describes Redwood's standardized procedure for applying predictive capacity throttling and performing a controlled canary rollback when early signals indicate SLO risk. It codifies the decision thresholds, required telemetry, automation hooks, and human escalation steps so teams can reduce blast radius while preserving customer-facing availability.

Purpose and scope:
- Purpose: minimize customer impact by automatically and safely mitigating traffic surges or degraded model performance before error budgets are exhausted.
- Scope: applies to public inference endpoints, Dedicated pools, and Private VPC deployments where automated routing and throttle policies are enabled. Does NOT cover manual DBA/infra maintenance windows.

Key definitions:
- Predictive throttle: automated traffic shaping that reduces request concurrency or drops non-critical request classes based on forecasted capacity shortfalls.
- Canary rollback: automated or manual rollback of a recently deployed model or runtime variant affecting a small percentage of traffic.
- Primary SLOs: p95 latency < 120ms for text-gen, availability >= 99.9%; token-level quality SLOs as defined per model.

Signals used for decisioning (ordered):
1) Telemetry predictions: 15–60m forecast from the capacity-forecaster service indicating >20% probability of exceeding provisioned tokens/sec.
2) SLO burn rate: instantaneous burn rate > 4x and projected to exhaust error budget in < 12h.
3) Latency signal: sustained p95 above threshold for 5m and increasing.
4) Error spike: client error rate (5xx) increased by >200% relative to 1h baseline.
5) Model quality regressions: eval-set quality delta > configured threshold for last 1k requests.

Preconditions and ownership:
- Owning team: service owner listed in the service manifest (ownership on-service page).
- Oncall: primary oncall for the owning team executes human-confirm steps if automation is not enabled.
- Automation owner: infra-runtime team maintains the throttle controller and canary deploy automation.

Decision matrix (quick reference):
| Trigger type | Immediate automated action | Human required? | Escalation window |
|--------------|----------------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| Telemetry forecast (>=20% oversub) | Apply soft throttle: reduce non-critical classes by 25% | no | 5 minutes |
| Burn rate >4x & projection <12h | Apply strict throttle (50%) and notify oncall | yes | 2 minutes |
| Latency p95 > threshold (5m) | Mark for canary rollback candidate; keep throttles | yes | 5 minutes |
| 5xx spike >200% | Fail traffic to fallback model variant + throttle | yes | immediate |
| Eval quality delta exceeded | Pause new deployments; start canary rollback | yes | immediate |

3. Telemetry re-evaluation: after 3m, controller re-evaluates predicted capacity. If projection still >20% oversub, escalate to strict throttle.
4. Strict throttle: throttle-controller enforces 50% reduction and reduces batch sizes to limit token throughput. CLI: rdwctl throttle apply --service {svc} --percent 50 --batchesize 8 --reason "strict policy"
5. Canary selection: router picks 1% of traffic from the affected pool and switches it to the previous stable model variant (canary rollback). Automation: rollout-controller can perform rollback automatically when configured; otherwise oncall triggers rollback.
6. Recovery monitoring: observe SLOs for 15m. If SLOs recover, gradually remove throttles over 30m (linear unwind: 25% -> 0).
7. Post-action: create incident ticket automatically with root cause artifacts attached (traces, flamegraphs, capacity forecasts, sample inputs).

Operational steps (human-assisted path):
1. PagerDuty alert -> acknowledge and join incident channel. Post initial assessment within 2 minutes.
2. Confirm trigger signals. If safe and automation disabled, execute rdwctl throttle apply (soft) and follow automated re-eval steps.
3. If rollback required: perform canary rollback via rollout-controller UI or CLI: rdwctl rollout rollback --service {svc} --target-version {previous} --canary-percent 1 --wait 300s
4. Monitor and escalate to SRE lead after 10 minutes if no improvement.
5. If rollback fails or regression persists, execute full rollback: rdwctl rollout rollback --service {svc} --target-version {previous} --canary-percent 100 --wait 600s

Runbook playbook for capacity planners (when predictive throttles are frequent):
- Frequency > 3 predictive throttle events per 7 days => require capacity retrospective with infra-runtime and owning team.
- Retrospective agenda: 1) Review forecasts vs reality; 2) Examine batching/quantization opportunities; 3) Re-evaluate routing policies; 4) Consider reserved capacity increase or model quantization changes.

SLO and error budget integration:
- When predictive throttle is triggered by forecast only, increment a special "throttle" counter that counts against a monthly operational allowance (not the customer-visible error budget) to track how often service-level mitigation is applied.
- If strict throttle or rollback is required, record event as an error-budget-impacting incident and annotate incident with burn-rate and estimated customer token loss.

Telemetry & dashboards required (minimum):
- SLO dashboard: p50/p95/p99 latency, error rates, burn-rate, error budget remaining.
- Capacity-forecast view: 15m/30m/60m probability distribution, detected anomalies.
- Throttle-controller audit: active throttles, last-applied reason, owner, and unwind schedule.
- Canary rollback dashboard: canary percent, model versions, quality delta, sample responses.

- Strict throttle: rdwctl throttle apply --service inference-api --percent 50 --batchesize 8 --reason "strict"
- Canary rollback (automated template):

    "service": "inference-api",
    "from_version": "v1.24",
    "to_version": "v1.23",
    "canary_percent": "1",
    "health_check": {
      "slo_check": "p95_latency < 120ms",
      "quality_check": "eval_delta < 0.5%"
    }
  }

Sample throttle policy (YAML):
- name: predictive-throttle-policy
  trigger:
    - forecast_probability: 0.2
      forecast_window: 60m

    - type: soft-throttle

2. Include the capacity-forecaster prediction artifacts and the throttle-controller audit log.

- Postmortem owner and due date for action items

Safety checks and abort conditions:

Appendices:

title: Automated Customer Incident Reporting and SLO Impact Generator
preview:
Summary:

This document defines the design, operational runbook, verification checks, and customer-facing templates for Redwood's Automated Customer Incident Reporting and SLO Impact Generator (ACIR-SIG). ACIR-SIG produces standardized, auditable customer incident summaries within a defined SLA window after an incident is classified as customer-impacting. The system computes SLO impact automatically from telemetry and timeline evidence, assembles a concise narrative, and supports automated delivery to customers (via Console, email, or support ticket) with human-in-the-loop gating.

Goals and scope:
- Goal: Reduce time-to-customer-summary while preserving accuracy, traceability, and blameless language.
- Scope: infra and inference incidents that affect production API responses (latency, availability, errors). Excludes: billing, account-level policy, or non-production incidents.
- Output: structured incident summary (human-readable + machine JSON payload) and an SLO impact statement.

Ownership and roles:
1) SRE oncall: responsible for verification and approval when gating is required.
2) Incident commander (IC): final sign-off for any customer report that contains mitigation details or security-sensitive statements.
3) Customer Success (CS): decides routing and cadence for customer notifications and escalations.
4) Reporting service (service-owner: ops-tooling): implements ACIR-SIG automation and maintains templates.

Key constraints and principles:
- Always calculate SLO impact from the canonical SLO aggregator (slo-aggregate-service) and telemetry (tracing, api-metrics, regional-health). Do not use local approximations.
- Maintain a human-in-the-loop for incidents where the automated confidence < 92% (see confidence rules).
- Use standardized, blameless language and give measurable impact (requests affected, time windows, and computed %SLO burn).
- Store evidence pointers (trace IDs, query snippets, dashboards) in the report payload for audits and follow-ups.

SLO impact computation (algorithm):
- Inputs:
  - timeseries of successful requests S(t) and total requests T(t) at 1s aggregation or the highest fidelity available.
  - policy: SLO target (e.g., availability 99.9% over 30d) and SLO window used for customer-facing burn calculation (typically rolling-30d).
  - incident window [t_start, t_end] defined by timeline service.
- Steps:
  1) Compute baseline SLO compliance before the incident: baseline_compliance = sum_{t < t_start}(S(t))/sum_{t < t_start}(T(t)) over a baseline period (default 24h).
  2) Compute incident compliance: incident_compliance = sum_{t in [t_start,t_end]}(S(t))/sum_{t in [t_start,t_end]}(T(t)).
  3) Convert to SLO burn for the rolling window:
     - delta_unavailability = max(0, target - incident_compliance) * (duration_of_window_weight)
     - approximate SLO%_burn = (sum_{window} expected_bad_seconds_from_incident) / (total_window_seconds * (1 - target)) * 100
- Simplified formula (used by ACIR-SIG for short reports):
  SLO_burn_percent = round(100 * ((target - incident_compliance) * incident_duration_seconds) / (rolling_window_seconds * (1 - target)), 2)
- Example:
  - target = 99.9% (0.999), rolling_window_seconds = 30*24*3600 = 2,592,000s, incident duration = 900s, incident_compliance = 0.95.
  - SLO_burn_percent = 100 * ((0.999 - 0.95) * 900) / (2,592,000 * (1 - 0.999)) = 100 * (0.049 * 900) / (2,592,000 * 0.001) = 100 * 44.1 / 2592 = 1.70% (rounded).

Confidence scoring and gating rules:
- Confidence inputs (scored 0–100):
  - telemetry_coverage (percentage of requests covered by high-fidelity metrics): weight 40
  - timeline_agreement (percent overlap between automated detection and timeline boundaries): weight 20
  - anomaly_correlation (correlation of errors/latency with topology/event): weight 20
  - evidence_linkage (presence of traces/logs matching top error signatures): weight 20
- Final confidence = weighted_sum(components).
- Gating thresholds:
  - confidence >= 92: auto-generate and auto-send short customer summary (no mitigation detail) after 2h post-incident verification window.
  - 75 <= confidence < 92: auto-generate draft; require SRE oncall verification and IC approval.
  - confidence < 75: do not generate automated customer content; produce internal report only.

Automated customer summary template (short):
- Title: (Service) Incident Summary — (YYYY-MM-DD) — (region)
- One-line impact: During (time window), customers experienced increased (errors/latency/availability) affecting approximately N requests (~X% of requests) in (region).
- What happened: brief description of root cause or leading hypothesis (automated, with confidence).
- What we did: mitigation steps taken and timeframe.
- SLO impact: SLO_burn_percent for rolling window and absolute requests affected.
- Links & evidence: dashboard links, trace IDs, support ticket link for follow-up.
- Next steps: planned remediation and expected ETA when available.

Full machine payload (JSON) — example snippet for automation (ACIR-SIG sends this to CS or support):
{
  \"incident_id\": \"INC-2026-1234\",
  \"service\": \"inference-api\",
  \"region\": \"us-west-2\",
  \"window\": \"2026-08-14T15:12:00Z/2026-08-14T15:27:00Z\",
  \"requests_affected\": \"18234\",
  \"approx_pct_requests\": \"3.6\",
  \"slo_burn_pct\": \"1.7\",
  \"confidence\": \"94\",
  \"evidence\": [\"dash://api-availability/overview\", \"trace://abc123\"],
  \"narrative\": \"A partial control-plane configuration regression caused router miss-paths leading to elevated 5xxs for 15 minutes. Mitigation: rollback of config at T+6.\"
}

Automation architecture (high level):
- Inputs:
  - slo-aggregate-service (canonical SLO metrics)
  - telemetry-mesh (metrics/traces/logs)
  - timeline-service (incident boundaries and annotations)
  - incident-bus (events from PagerDuty/ops)
- Components:
  - generator-worker: computes SLO burn and assembles payloads.
  - confidence-evaluator: calculates confidence and determines gating.
  - draft-store: stores auto-drafts for human review (if needed).
  - delivery-orchestrator: posts to Console, creates CS ticket, or sends email.
- Safety layers:
  - rate limiter: ensures only one auto-customer report per incident, and no more than one per hour for the same customer group.
  - redaction module: strips PII or security-sensitive fields per policy.
  - audit logger: immutable storage of generated payloads and final send event (retention policy defined below).

Runbook: step-by-step operator flow when a draft is generated and human gate required:
1) Alert: oncall receives automated draft notification (Slack + Console).
2) Quick checks (5–10 min): verify top evidence links, ensure traces contain indicative errors, confirm region and affected customers.
3) Approve or reject: Approve via Console button to publish; reject sends back to draft with reason.
4) If approved, CS will receive the published summary and follow-up engagement steps are initiated automatically.
5) If rejected, SRE tags draft with notes and sets auto-resend window to 2h.

Manual override and safety escalation:
- IC can hold customer notifications for up to 24h for incidents involving legal, compliance, or high-sensitivity customers.
- If an automated summary is already sent and later found inaccurate, publish a correction using the same payload schema and include a clear correction header.

Verification checklist (pre-send):
- [ ] Evidence pointers present (>=2 of {dashboard, trace, logs})
- [ ] SLO computation cross-checked by slo-aggregate-service snapshot
- [ ] PII redaction module passed (redaction=false means pass)
- [ ] Confidence >= configured threshold for auto-send or IC approval obtained
- [ ] Delivery channel contact list validated for affected customer segments

Operational observability and metrics for ACIR-SIG itself:
- Metric: acir.reports.generated (count) — label by outcome: auto_sent/draft/rejected/corrected
- Metric: acir.processing.latency (ms) — time from incident-closed to draft ready
- Metric: acir.confidence.distribution — histogram of confidence scores
- Alert: if acir.processing.latency P95 > 45min for 3 hours => paging to ops-tooling

Retention and audit policy:
- Generated payloads and evidence pointers retained for 365 days in immutable audit store.
- Access to full payloads limited by RBAC: engineering/incident-review and CS lead roles, security/legal by approval.

Security and privacy notes:
- Redaction rules are centrally controlled: do not include customer payloads, secrets, or raw logs in customer-facing narrative. Evidence pointers are allowed but must not embed credentials.
- For incidents involving regulated customers, default confidentiality escalates to 'restricted' and a manual IC approval is required before any outgoing message.

Testing and validation cadence:
- Quarterly TT (tabletop) exercises: validate automated outputs against mock incidents.
- Monthly synthetic incidents: run ACIR-SIG in shadow mode to measure drift in confidence and SLO calculation correctness.

Rollout and migration plan for first production release:
1) Phase 1 (shadow): 2 months, run in draft-only mode for a subset (20%) of incidence types.
2) Phase 2 (co-pilot): human-in-the-loop gating required for all drafts; telemetry and metrics validated.
3) Phase 3 (autorelease): enable auto-send for confidence >= 92; monitor metrics and enable roll-back flag for first 30 days.

Appendix A — Example customer-facing short summary (final):
Service: Redwood Inference — Hosted API
Date: 2026-08-14
Impact window: 15:12–15:27 UTC
Summary: Between 15:12–15:27 UTC customers in us-west-2 experienced elevated 5xx errors affecting approximately 18k requests (~3.6% of normal request volume). We rolled back a recent control-plane configuration change at 15:18 which restored normal routing by 15:18:30 and fully recovered by 15:27.
SLO impact: Estimated to consume 1.7% of the 30-day availability budget for Hosted API (99.9% target).
Links: Dashboard: https://console.redwood.ai/dashboards/incident-1234 Trace: trace://abc123 Ticket: https://support.redwood.ai/t/INC-2026-1234
Next steps: Root cause analysis in progress; RCA will be published within 72 hours.

Appendix B — Quick references:
- SLO aggregation playground: /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/slo-aggregate-playground
- Incident timeline canonicalization: /confluence/eng-sre/incident-review/incident-hypothesis-repro-environment-spec-2024
- PII redaction policy: /confluence/security-and-compliance/data-management/redaction-policy

Questions or feedback: reach out to the ops-tooling owners (ops-tooling@redwood.ai) or file improvements via the Console suggestions flow.

Change log:
- 2026-01-10: Initial draft (Priya Natarajan)
- 2026-04-02: Added confidence scoring schema and gating thresholds (Jonah Meyer)
- 2026-08-15: Added rollout plan, retention table, and expanded examples (Sofia Alvarez)

- SLO impact: SLO_burn_percent for rolling window and absolute requests affected.
- Links & evidence: dashboard links, trace IDs, support ticket link for follow-up.
- Next steps: planned remediation and expected ETA when available.

Full machine payload (JSON) — example snippet for automation (ACIR-SIG sends this to CS or support):
{
  \"incident_id\": \"INC-2026-1234\",
  \"service\": \"inference-api\",
  \"region\": \"us-west-2\",
  \"window\": \"2026-08-14T15:12:00Z/2026-08-14T15:27:00Z\",
  \"requests_affected\": \"18234\",
  \"approx_pct_requests\": \"3.6\",
  \"slo_burn_pct\": \"1.7\",
  \"confidence\": \"94\",
  \"evidence\": [\"dash://api-availability/overview\", \"trace://abc123\"],
  \"narrative\": \"A partial control-plane configuration regression caused router miss-paths leading to elevated 5xxs for 15 minutes. Mitigation: rollback of config at T+6.\"
}

Automation architecture (high level):
- Inputs:
  - slo-aggregate-service (canonical SLO metrics)
  - telemetry-mesh (metrics/traces/logs)
  - timeline-service (incident boundaries and annotations)
  - incident-bus (events from PagerDuty/ops)
- Components:
  - generator-worker: computes SLO burn and assembles payloads.
  - confidence-evaluator: calculates confidence and determines gating.
  - draft-store: stores auto-drafts for human review (if needed).
  - delivery-orchestrator: posts to Console, creates CS ticket, or sends email.
- Safety layers:
  - rate limiter: ensures only one auto-customer report per incident, and no more than one per hour for the same customer group.
  - redaction module: strips PII or security-sensitive fields per policy.
  - audit logger: immutable storage of generated payloads and final send event (retention policy defined below).

Runbook: step-by-step operator flow when a draft is generated and human gate required:
1) Alert: oncall receives automated draft notification (Slack + Console).
2) Quick checks (5–10 min): verify top evidence links, ensure traces contain indicative errors, confirm region and affected customers.
3) Approve or reject: Approve via Console button to publish; reject sends back to draft with reason.
4) If approved, CS will receive the published summary and follow-up engagement steps are initiated automatically.
5) If rejected, SRE tags draft with notes and sets auto-resend window to 2h.

Manual override and safety escalation:
- IC can hold customer notifications for up to 24h for incidents involving legal, compliance, or high-sensitivity customers.
- If an automated summary is already sent and later found inaccurate, publish a correction using the same payload schema and include a clear correction header.

Verification checklist (pre-send):
- [ ] Evidence pointers present (>=2 of {dashboard, trace, logs})
- [ ] SLO computation cross-checked by slo-aggregate-service snapshot
- [ ] PII redaction module passed (redaction=false means pass)
- [ ] Confidence >= configured threshold for auto-send or IC approval obtained
- [ ] Delivery channel contact list validated for affected customer segments

Operational observability and metrics for ACIR-SIG itself:
- Metric: acir.reports.generated (count) — label by outcome: auto_sent/draft/rejected/corrected
- Metric: acir.processing.latency (ms) — time from incident-closed to draft ready
- Metric: acir.confidence.distribution — histogram of confidence scores
- Alert: if acir.processing.latency P95 > 45min for 3 hours => paging to ops-tooling

Retention and audit policy:
- Generated payloads and evidence pointers retained for 365 days in immutable audit store.
- Access to full payloads limited by RBAC: engineering/incident-review and CS lead roles, security/legal by approval.

Security and privacy notes:
- Redaction rules are centrally controlled: do not include customer payloads, secrets, or raw logs in customer-facing narrative. Evidence pointers are allowed but must not embed credentials.
- For incidents involving regulated customers, default confidentiality escalates to 'restricted' and a manual IC approval is required before any outgoing message.

Testing and validation cadence:
- Quarterly TT (tabletop) exercises: validate automated outputs against mock incidents.
- Monthly synthetic incidents: run ACIR-SIG in shadow mode to measure drift in confidence and SLO calculation correctness.

Rollout and migration plan for first production release:
1) Phase 1 (shadow): 2 months, run in draft-only mode for a subset (20%) of incidence types.
2) Phase 2 (co-pilot): human-in-the-loop gating required for all drafts; telemetry and metrics validated.
3) Phase 3 (autorelease): enable auto-send for confidence >= 92; monitor metrics and enable roll-back flag for first 30 days.

Appendix A — Example customer-facing short summary (final):
Service: Redwood Inference — Hosted API
Date: 2026-08-14
Impact window: 15:12–15:27 UTC
Summary: Between 15:12–15:27 UTC customers in us-west-2 experienced elevated 5xx errors affecting approximately 18k requests (~3.6% of normal request volume). We rolled back a recent control-plane configuration change at 15:18 which restored normal routing by 15:18:30 and fully recovered by 15:27.
SLO impact: Estimated to consume 1.7% of the 30-day availability budget for Hosted API (99.9% target).
Links: Dashboard: https://console.redwood.ai/dashboards/incident-1234 Trace: trace://abc123 Ticket: https://support.redwood.ai/t/INC-2026-1234
Next steps: Root cause analysis in progress; RCA will be published within 72 hours.

Appendix B — Quick references:
- SLO aggregation playground: /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/slo-aggregate-playground
- Incident timeline canonicalization: /confluence/eng-sre/incident-review/incident-hypothesis-repro-environment-spec-2024
- PII redaction policy: /confluence/security-and-compliance/data-management/redaction-policy

Questions or feedback: reach out to the ops-tooling owners (ops-tooling@redwood.ai) or file improvements via the Console suggestions flow.

Change log:
- 2026-01-10: Initial draft (Priya Natarajan)
- 2026-04-02: Added confidence scoring schema and gating thresholds (Jonah Meyer)
- 2026-08-15: Added rollout plan, retention table, and expanded examples (Sofia Alvarez)
