title: Incident Rehearsal and Tabletop Facilitation Standards
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This page describes the standard approach for designing, running, and closing SRE incident rehearsals (tabletops and simulated drills) that validate SLO-driven response, capacity continuity, error‑budget governance, and operational tooling readiness. It codifies roles, cadence, success criteria, metrics to collect, and post‑exercise artifacts so rehearsals are repeatable and measurable.

Why this matters
- Regular, well-facilitated rehearsals shorten time-to-recovery in real incidents, improve cross-team coordination, and surface capacity or telemetry gaps before customer impact.
- Tying each rehearsal to specific SLOs and error‑budget states lets us prioritize exercises that protect customer-facing guarantees.

Scope and audience
- Scope: multi-region production service incidents, capacity surges, model-serving regressions, and control-plane outages that affect inference routing. Excludes purely non-production experiments.
- Audience: oncall SREs, service owners, platform engineers, product reliability leads, and incident commanders-in-training.

Key definitions
- Rehearsal (tabletop): a facilitated, scenario-based discussion exercise simulating an incident with clear injects and decision points.
- Drill (simulated): a time-boxed, live simulation with real telemetry and runbook execution on a subset of infrastructure or canary traffic.
- Playbook: the runbook or decision matrix exercised.

Cadence and prioritization
1) Annual program: each critical service should have at least one full-scale rehearsal per year and one focused drill (capacity or SLO regression) per half year.
2) Triggered rehearsals: schedule an ad hoc exercise when any of these occur:
   - An SLO burn rate exceeds 2x for 30 minutes for a customer-facing SLO (example: 99.9% availability with >0.2% error spike persist).
   - A postmortem uncovers a missing decision point or ambiguous ownership.
   - Major infra change (e.g., new autoscaling policy, quantization rollout) affecting serving runtime.
3) Prioritization rubric (used to pick rehearsal scenarios):
   - Customer impact (P1/P2) potential: high/medium/low
   - Estimated mitigation complexity (high/med/low)
   - Cross-team coupling (high/med/low)
   - SLO exposure (% of user queries affected)

Roles and responsibilities
- Facilitator (SRE): prepares injects, runs the timeline, ensures decisions are captured.
- Incident Commander (rotating): practices declaring incident, triage, and escalation.
- Service Owner: clarifies service semantics and approves mitigation tradeoffs.
- Platform Owner: answers capacity, routing and rollback feasibility.
- Observability SME: validates telemetry availability and runbook health.
- Note-taker/Artifacts owner: records decisions, action items, and evidence.

Pre-rehearsal checklist (to be completed 5 business days before)
- Scenario document with objectives, scope, and success criteria.
- Associated playbooks/runbooks identified by path and owner (e.g., /runbooks/service-x/capacity-hedge).
- Telemetry plan: list of dashboards, alerts, and queries to validate during the exercise (see Sample telemetry below).
- Participant invites and roles assigned.
- Impact sandbox (for drills): canary route or isolated tenant chosen and approved.

Sample telemetry to validate during a rehearsal
- Request success rate (p50, p95, p99) and error-rate delta compared to baseline.
- Latency histograms: p50/p95/p99 for token generation and end-to-end response.
- Autoscaler signals: target and actual replica/GPU allocation, queue length, scheduling latency.
- Model-serving KV cache hit rate and prefix cache effectiveness.
- Resource utilization: GPU SM utilization, GPU memory pressure, host CPU and network.
- Routing fallback count and origin (policy vs. manual).

Scenario examples (pick one per rehearsal)
- Regional capacity loss: simulated AZ failure in primary region; automated fallback to secondary region; verify warm standby routing and data residency controls.
- Quantization regression: new quantized artifact increases p99 latency beyond SLO; validate rollback, canary soak, and observability queries.
- Burst from enterprise tenant: single tenant causes head-of-line blocking; exercise multi-tenant fair-share knobs, per-tenant circuit breakers, and eviction procedures.
- Control plane outage: private deployments cannot reach the hosted control plane; verify Private fallback policies and on-prem runbook execution.

Facilitation script (abridged)
1) Kickoff (10 min): introduce objectives, acceptance criteria, and confirm roles.
2) Baseline data review (10 min): show pre-inject telemetry and SLO posture.
3) Inject phase (30–40 min): facilitator introduces time-stamped injects; participants call out runbook steps and decisions.
4) Deep-dive checkpoints (20 min): pause for short demos of telemetry queries, rollback commands, or capacity-scaling commands.
5) Closure (10–15 min): collect decisions, assign action items, and capture evidence.

Success criteria and scoring rubric
- Operational readiness score (0–10): composite of detection (30%), mitigation (40%), communication (15%), and evidence capture (15%).
- Minimum acceptable score for a service rehearsal: 7/10. Failing services must schedule remediation within 30 days.

Post-rehearsal artifacts (must be published within 48 hours)
- Rehearsal report: scenario, participants, timeline, decisions, and action items (owner + due date).
- Evidence bundle: screenshots of dashboards, alert timelines, CLI history, and any telemetry queries used.
- Gap tracker entry: 3 prioritized remediation items (telemetry, runbook, capacity) with owners.
- Update to service SLO/capacity docs if decisions change thresholds or runbook steps.

Integration with error-budget and capacity governance
- If a rehearsal simulates an SLO breach, the error-budget council is notified and the rehearsal is considered a controlled burn; remediation and postmortem cadence follow the normal error-budget remediation timeline.
- Capacity-related drills must publish a capacity-impact summary showing expected vs. observed queue length, time to scale, and sustained burn. This feeds into quarterly capacity planning.

Runbook and tooling guidance (concrete examples)
- Use the standard runbook header template: service, owner, primary contact, SLOs impacted, last tested date.
- Provide one-liners for critical commands. Example:
  - Re-route canary: rdc route set --service service-x --target region-b --weight 100 --reason "rehearsal"
  - Rollback model bundle: rtm deploy rollback --service service-x --since 2026-02-20 --force
- Observability queries (example PromQL-ish pseudocode):
  - error_rate = sum(rate(http_requests_failed[5m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m]))
  - p95_latency = histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_bucket[5m])) by (le))

Privacy and compliance guardrails
- Drills that touch customer data must operate on synthetic datasets or explicitly approved canaries.
- Private deployments require a pre-approved runbook that avoids control-plane calls that would leak configuration.

Training and certification
- New SREs must observe two rehearsals and facilitate at least one tabletop within their first 6 months.
- Certification checklist: facilitator observed, IC observed, evidence artifacts produced, and post-exercise remediation closed or remediated plan created.

Templates and attachments
- Template: Rehearsal scenario doc (use: /templates/incident-rehearsal-template)
- Template: Post-rehearsal report skeleton (use: /templates/rehearsal-report-skeleton)

Governance and enforcement
- The SRE council audits adherence twice a year and publishes a consolidated gap report. Services scoring <7 will be flagged in the quarterly reliability review and may be moved to prioritized remediation tracks.

Appendix: quick checklist summary
- Pre: scenario, telemetry, roles confirmed, playbooks linked.
- During: run facilitator script, capture timestamps, validate telemetry queries.
- Post: publish report + evidence within 48 hours, create gap entries, assign remediation owners, notify error-budget council if relevant.

Contact and feedback
- For facilitation help or to book a rehearsal space, contact the SRE Programs team: sre-programs@redwood.ai.
- Feedback on this standard: add comments on this page or ping the author.

title: Tenant Impact Scorer & Priority Gating Runbook
preview:
Overview:

This runbook defines the Tenant Impact Scorer (TIS), a deterministic scoring algorithm and automation flow that:
- Quantifies incident impact at the tenant/customer level.
- Converts impact into a prioritized remediation level (P0..P3).
- Drives automated gating of non-critical deployments and throttles via the gatekeeper-service when error budgets or capacity signals cross thresholds.

Goal:
- Reduce time-to-prioritize for multi-tenant incidents.
- Ensure capacity/economics aware mitigation (avoid global throttles for a small set of high-error tenants).
- Provide a reproducible signal for oncall and product owners to take action.

Scope:
- Applies to all customer-facing inference endpoints and long-running background jobs that surface tenant errors (service prefixes: inference-api/*, embeddings/*, reranking/*).
- Not applicable to internal platform-only pipelines (use eng-sre/internal-playbook).

Owners and Contacts:
- Primary owner: eng-sre (oncall alias: eng-sre-oncall@redwood.com)
- Gatekeeper service owner: infra-platform (owner: Aaron Patel)
- Product escalation: product-support@redwood.com
- Incident CLI & automation maintainer: Liam Chen

Definitions and Signals:
- tenant_error_rate: percentage of failing requests over last 5m for a tenant (Prometheus metric: inference_request_errors_total / inference_request_total by (tenant_id)).
- tenant_traffic_share: tenant requests / total requests over 1h.
- error_budget_burn_rate: rate at which a tenant is consuming its Service SLO error budget (30m exponential window).
- active_complaints: number of customer support tickets opened in last 30m for the tenant (from Zendesk API).
- tier_score: numeric mapping for plan tier: enterprise=1.0, standard=0.6, free=0.2 (enterprise gets higher weight because impact is higher).

Scoring formula (deterministic):
- Normalize each signal into 0..1 range using soft-floor/ceiling per metric (see params below).
- TenantImpactScore = 0.35*norm_error_rate + 0.25*norm_traffic_share + 0.20*norm_burn_rate + 0.10*norm_active_complaints + 0.10*tier_score

Parameter normalization thresholds (default):
- norm_error_rate: 0% -> 0, 1% -> 0.25, 5% -> 0.75, 20% -> 1.0
- norm_traffic_share: 0% -> 0, 0.5% -> 0.2, 5% -> 0.7, 30% -> 1.0
- norm_burn_rate: 1x -> 0, 2x -> 0.25, 5x -> 0.75, 20x -> 1.0
- norm_active_complaints: 0 -> 0, 1 -> 0.4, 3 -> 0.8, 10 -> 1.0

Priority mapping (TenantImpactScore -> Action):
- P0 (>= 0.85): Immediate incident response; page SRE; auto-gate deploys for tenant; apply temporary traffic diversion or aggressive throttles.
- P1 (0.6 - 0.85): High priority; assign incident commander within 10 minutes; auto-throttle to soft limit; notify account owner and CS.
- P2 (0.35 - 0.6): Monitor and mitigate; schedule regression test, consider targeted retry/backoff policies for affected tenant.
- P3 (<0.35): Low priority; record and window into next reliability retrospective.

Automated gating and policy enforcement:
- gatekeeper-service: internal HTTP API that accepts decisions to quarantine tenants or set dynamic throttles.
- Default gate actions: {
    "quarantine": {"traffic_cap_pct": X},
    "throttle": {"qps_limit": N},
    "pause_deploys": true/false
  }

Sample gatekeeper request (curl):

curl -X POST https://internal-api.redwood.com/gatekeeper/v1/tenants/action 
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer $GATEKEEPER_TOKEN' 
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' 
  -d '{"tenant_id":"acct_1234", "action":"quarantine", "params": {"traffic_cap_pct": 20}, "reason":"TIS P0 auto-respond"}'

PromQL examples used by scorer (replace $TENANT with tenant label):
- tenant_error_rate: sum(increase(inference_request_errors_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[5m])) / sum(increase(inference_request_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[5m]))
- tenant_traffic_share: sum(increase(inference_request_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[1h])) / sum(increase(inference_request_total[1h]))
- tenant_burn_rate: rate(error_budget_consumed_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[5m]) / (error_budget_total{tenant="$TENANT"} / 3600)

Zendesk quick lookup (example curl):
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $ZENDESK_TOKEN" 
  "https://redwood.zendesk.com/api/v2/search.json?query=type:ticket organization:acct_1234 created>now-30m" 
  | jq '.results | length'  # returns active_complaints

Operational runbook: step-by-step
1) Detect: TIS runs every 60s and emits tenant_impact_score{tenant_id}. Alert fires when any tenant score crosses 0.6 (P1) or 0.85 (P0).
2) Page: For P0 -> page eng-sre-oncall and product-owner of tenant. For P1 -> page eng-sre-oncall. Include a generated summary (top signals, top endpoints, recent deploys).
3) Observe: Run automated diagnostic queries (PromQL + slowlog extraction) via the incident CLI:
   - incident-cli query --tenant acct_1234 --duration 15m --metrics errors,latency,traffic
4) Decide: Use the decision matrix below to choose gate action. The first responder should confirm suggested gate in Slack #oncall-decisions with the JSON payload.
5) Enforce: POST to gatekeeper-service (see sample) and record action in incident tracker (Jira/Confluence incident page).
6) Mitigate: Depending on action, apply fixes: rollback last deploy for tenant routing, increase capacity if capacity-related, or roll out a targeted config patch.
7) Validate: Verify tenant metrics return to baseline for 30m before removing quarantine.
8) Post-incident: Create an incident review using templates in /confluence/eng-sre/incident-review. Include TIS telemetry and gate decisions as artifacts.

Decision matrix (quick reference):
- If TenantImpactScore >= 0.85 -> gate: quarantine to 10-25% traffic and pause deploys; escalate to P0.
- If 0.7 <= score < 0.85 and error_budget_burn_rate > 5x -> gate: throttle to safe qps and notify billing/CS.
- If score driven primarily by traffic spike (traffic_share > 10% but low error rate) -> consider autoscale/allocate burst pool instead of quarantining.
- If score driven by active_complaints > 3 -> immediately notify account owner and enable crash-only logging for tenant requests for 1h.

Safety knobs and overrides:
- human_override: any SRE with role "incident_responder" can annotate the gate action with reason: human_override=true and set manual expire timestamp.
- auto_ttl: automated gate actions default to 30 minutes unless extended by explicit operator or a follow-up automation that keeps sampling.
- dry_run_mode: during holiday weekends or platform upgrades, TIS runs in "dry_run" and will not call gatekeeper-service but will still page and log decisions. Toggle via feature flag: feature.tis.enable_gating=false

Telemetry and dashboards:
- Dashboards: Tenant Impact Overview (Grafana board id: SRE-TIS-012) showing top 25 tenants by score, trend sparkline, and active gates.
- Alerts: TIS-P1 (score>=0.6) and TIS-P0 (score>=0.85) in PagerDuty. Alert payload includes: tenant_id, computed_score, top contributing signal, last_deploy_id.

Rollout plan for the scorer and gating automation:
1) Developer preview (week 1-2): run TIS in dry_run against production data; gather feedback from top 50 enterprise tenants.
2) Gradual enable (week 3-6): enable auto-gating for internal/test tenants only; schema and webhook contract validated.
3) Canary (week 7-10): enable gating for low-risk customer buckets; monitor false positive rate < 2% over 2 weeks.
4) Full launch (week 11+): enable for all tenants with monitoring guardrails; SRE oncall trained on playbook.

Testing guidance and QA:
- Unit test scoring logic with synthetic tenants: include edge cases (zero traffic, heavy error burn for free tier, sudden bursty traffic).
- Load test gatekeeper-service at 10k ops/s and ensure <200ms median response.
- Chaos tests: simulate API gateway failures and ensure TIS degrades to read-only (no gating) and still pages SRE.

Runbook snippets & templates (Confluence copy candidates):
- Incident summary template: include computed TIS score, rationale, gate action, and validation checklist.
- Postmortem artifact checklist: log of gate actions, PromQL queries, deploy ids, and Zendesk ticket ids.

Example incident timeline (illustrative):
- 09:02: TIS detects acct_1234 score 0.88 (P0) due to 12% error rate and 8% traffic share.
- 09:03: Eng-sre-oncall paged and auto-suggested action: quarantine 20% traffic + pause deploys.
- 09:04: SRE lead approves in #oncall-decisions and gatekeeper-service POSTs quarantine action.
- 09:20: Errors fall to 1.2% for tenant; score drops to 0.45.
- 09:55: Gate auto-ttl expires; first responder validates 30m baseline and removes quarantine.

Audit and compliance notes:
- All gatekeeper actions are logged to audit-log-service (retention 13 months).
- For regulated customers, additional manual approval is required to apply quarantines (see security-and-compliance/gated-actions-policy).

Appendix: Example scoring pseudo-code

# python-style pseudo-code

def normalize(value, thresholds):
    # thresholds = [(x1, s1), (x2, s2), ...] linear interp clamp
    # returns 0..1
    ...

def compute_tis(tenant_id):
    err = query_promql(tenant_error_rate)
    traffic = query_promql(tenant_traffic_share)
    burn = query_promql(tenant_burn_rate)
    complaints = query_zendesk_count(tenant_id)
    tier_score = lookup_tier(tenant_id)
    return 0.35*norm(err) + 0.25*norm(traffic) + 0.20*norm(burn) + 0.10*norm(complaints) + 0.10*tier_score

# CI/CD gating example (GitHub Actions snippet)
name: Deploy
on: [push]
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Query gatekeeper before deploy
        run: |
          curl -s -X POST https://internal-api.redwood.com/gatekeeper/v1/ci/check \
            -H "Authorization: Bearer $GATEKEEPER_TOKEN" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d '{"target":"svc/inference-api", "tenant_id":"${{ matrix.tenant }}"}' \
          | jq '.status'

Notes and future work:
- Integrate tenant sentiment from support conversations (NLP score) to increase sensitivity for enterprise customers.
- Add machine-learning calibration to dynamically tune normalization thresholds per-service.
- Expand the gate actions to include fine-grained model-variant fallback for inference endpoints.

References:
- Grafana board: SRE-TIS-012
- Gatekeeper API docs: https://internal-api.redwood.com/docs/gatekeeper
- Incident CLI: https://internal-tools.redwood.com/incident-cli

End of document
text:
Overview:

This runbook defines the Tenant Impact Scorer (TIS), a deterministic scoring algorithm and automation flow that:
- Quantifies incident impact at the tenant/customer level.
- Converts impact into a prioritized remediation level (P0..P3).
- Drives automated gating of non-critical deployments and throttles via the gatekeeper-service when error budgets or capacity signals cross thresholds.

Goal:
- Reduce time-to-prioritize for multi-tenant incidents.
- Ensure capacity/economics aware mitigation (avoid global throttles for a small set of high-error tenants).
- Provide a reproducible signal for oncall and product owners to take action.

Scope:
- Applies to all customer-facing inference endpoints and long-running background jobs that surface tenant errors (service prefixes: inference-api/*, embeddings/*, reranking/*).
- Not applicable to internal platform-only pipelines (use eng-sre/internal-playbook).

Owners and Contacts:
- Primary owner: eng-sre (oncall alias: eng-sre-oncall@redwood.com)
- Gatekeeper service owner: infra-platform (owner: Aaron Patel)
- Product escalation: product-support@redwood.com
- Incident CLI & automation maintainer: Liam Chen

Definitions and Signals:
- tenant_error_rate: percentage of failing requests over last 5m for a tenant (Prometheus metric: inference_request_errors_total / inference_request_total by (tenant_id)).
- tenant_traffic_share: tenant requests / total requests over 1h.
- error_budget_burn_rate: rate at which a tenant is consuming its Service SLO error budget (30m exponential window).
- active_complaints: number of customer support tickets opened in last 30m for the tenant (from Zendesk API).
- tier_score: numeric mapping for plan tier: enterprise=1.0, standard=0.6, free=0.2 (enterprise gets higher weight because impact is higher).

Scoring formula (deterministic):
- Normalize each signal into 0..1 range using soft-floor/ceiling per metric (see params below).
- TenantImpactScore = 0.35*norm_error_rate + 0.25*norm_traffic_share + 0.20*norm_burn_rate + 0.10*norm_active_complaints + 0.10*tier_score

Parameter normalization thresholds (default):
- norm_error_rate: 0% -> 0, 1% -> 0.25, 5% -> 0.75, 20% -> 1.0
- norm_traffic_share: 0% -> 0, 0.5% -> 0.2, 5% -> 0.7, 30% -> 1.0
- norm_burn_rate: 1x -> 0, 2x -> 0.25, 5x -> 0.75, 20x -> 1.0
- norm_active_complaints: 0 -> 0, 1 -> 0.4, 3 -> 0.8, 10 -> 1.0

Priority mapping (TenantImpactScore -> Action):
- P0 (>= 0.85): Immediate incident response; page SRE; auto-gate deploys for tenant; apply temporary traffic diversion or aggressive throttles.
- P1 (0.6 - 0.85): High priority; assign incident commander within 10 minutes; auto-throttle to soft limit; notify account owner and CS.
- P2 (0.35 - 0.6): Monitor and mitigate; schedule regression test, consider targeted retry/backoff policies for affected tenant.
- P3 (<0.35): Low priority; record and window into next reliability retrospective.

Automated gating and policy enforcement:
- gatekeeper-service: internal HTTP API that accepts decisions to quarantine tenants or set dynamic throttles.
- Default gate actions: {
    "quarantine": {"traffic_cap_pct": X},
    "throttle": {"qps_limit": N},
    "pause_deploys": true/false
  }

Sample gatekeeper request (curl):

curl -X POST https://internal-api.redwood.com/gatekeeper/v1/tenants/action 
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer $GATEKEEPER_TOKEN' 
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' 
  -d '{"tenant_id":"acct_1234", "action":"quarantine", "params": {"traffic_cap_pct": 20}, "reason":"TIS P0 auto-respond"}'

PromQL examples used by scorer (replace $TENANT with tenant label):
- tenant_error_rate: sum(increase(inference_request_errors_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[5m])) / sum(increase(inference_request_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[5m]))
- tenant_traffic_share: sum(increase(inference_request_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[1h])) / sum(increase(inference_request_total[1h]))
- tenant_burn_rate: rate(error_budget_consumed_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[5m]) / (error_budget_total{tenant="$TENANT"} / 3600)

Zendesk quick lookup (example curl):
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $ZENDESK_TOKEN" 
  "https://redwood.zendesk.com/api/v2/search.json?query=type:ticket organization:acct_1234 created>now-30m" 
  | jq '.results | length'  # returns active_complaints

Operational runbook: step-by-step
1) Detect: TIS runs every 60s and emits tenant_impact_score{tenant_id}. Alert fires when any tenant score crosses 0.6 (P1) or 0.85 (P0).
2) Page: For P0 -> page eng-sre-oncall and product-owner of tenant. For P1 -> page eng-sre-oncall. Include a generated summary (top signals, top endpoints, recent deploys).
3) Observe: Run automated diagnostic queries (PromQL + slowlog extraction) via the incident CLI:
   - incident-cli query --tenant acct_1234 --duration 15m --metrics errors,latency,traffic
4) Decide: Use the decision matrix below to choose gate action. The first responder should confirm suggested gate in Slack #oncall-decisions with the JSON payload.
5) Enforce: POST to gatekeeper-service (see sample) and record action in incident tracker (Jira/Confluence incident page).
6) Mitigate: Depending on action, apply fixes: rollback last deploy for tenant routing, increase capacity if capacity-related, or roll out a targeted config patch.
7) Validate: Verify tenant metrics return to baseline for 30m before removing quarantine.
8) Post-incident: Create an incident review using templates in /confluence/eng-sre/incident-review. Include TIS telemetry and gate decisions as artifacts.

Decision matrix (quick reference):
- If TenantImpactScore >= 0.85 -> gate: quarantine to 10-25% traffic and pause deploys; escalate to P0.
- If 0.7 <= score < 0.85 and error_budget_burn_rate > 5x -> gate: throttle to safe qps and notify billing/CS.
- If score driven primarily by traffic spike (traffic_share > 10% but low error rate) -> consider autoscale/allocate burst pool instead of quarantining.
- If score driven by active_complaints > 3 -> immediately notify account owner and enable crash-only logging for tenant requests for 1h.

Safety knobs and overrides:
- human_override: any SRE with role "incident_responder" can annotate the gate action with reason: human_override=true and set manual expire timestamp.
- auto_ttl: automated gate actions default to 30 minutes unless extended by explicit operator or a follow-up automation that keeps sampling.
- dry_run_mode: during holiday weekends or platform upgrades, TIS runs in "dry_run" and will not call gatekeeper-service but will still page and log decisions. Toggle via feature flag: feature.tis.enable_gating=false

Telemetry and dashboards:
- Dashboards: Tenant Impact Overview (Grafana board id: SRE-TIS-012) showing top 25 tenants by score, trend sparkline, and active gates.
- Alerts: TIS-P1 (score>=0.6) and TIS-P0 (score>=0.85) in PagerDuty. Alert payload includes: tenant_id, computed_score, top contributing signal, last_deploy_id.

Rollout plan for the scorer and gating automation:
1) Developer preview (week 1-2): run TIS in dry_run against production data; gather feedback from top 50 enterprise tenants.
2) Gradual enable (week 3-6): enable auto-gating for internal/test tenants only; schema and webhook contract validated.
3) Canary (week 7-10): enable gating for low-risk customer buckets; monitor false positive rate < 2% over 2 weeks.
4) Full launch (week 11+): enable for all tenants with monitoring guardrails; SRE oncall trained on playbook.

Testing guidance and QA:
- Unit test scoring logic with synthetic tenants: include edge cases (zero traffic, heavy error burn for free tier, sudden bursty traffic).
- Load test gatekeeper-service at 10k ops/s and ensure <200ms median response.
- Chaos tests: simulate API gateway failures and ensure TIS degrades to read-only (no gating) and still pages SRE.

Runbook snippets & templates (Confluence copy candidates):
- Incident summary template: include computed TIS score, rationale, gate action, and validation checklist.
- Postmortem artifact checklist: log of gate actions, PromQL queries, deploy ids, and Zendesk ticket ids.

Example incident timeline (illustrative):
- 09:02: TIS detects acct_1234 score 0.88 (P0) due to 12% error rate and 8% traffic share.
- 09:03: Eng-sre-oncall paged and auto-suggested action: quarantine 20% traffic + pause deploys.
- 09:04: SRE lead approves in #oncall-decisions and gatekeeper-service POSTs quarantine action.
- 09:20: Errors fall to 1.2% for tenant; score drops to 0.45.
- 09:55: Gate auto-ttl expires; first responder validates 30m baseline and removes quarantine.

Audit and compliance notes:
- All gatekeeper actions are logged to audit-log-service (retention 13 months).
- For regulated customers, additional manual approval is required to apply quarantines (see security-and-compliance/gated-actions-policy).

Appendix: Example scoring pseudo-code

# python-style pseudo-code

def normalize(value, thresholds):
    # thresholds = [(x1, s1), (x2, s2), ...] linear interp clamp
    # returns 0..1
    ...

def compute_tis(tenant_id):
    err = query_promql(tenant_error_rate)
    traffic = query_promql(tenant_traffic_share)
    burn = query_promql(tenant_burn_rate)
    complaints = query_zendesk_count(tenant_id)
    tier_score = lookup_tier(tenant_id)
    return 0.35*norm(err) + 0.25*norm(traffic) + 0.20*norm(burn) + 0.10*norm(complaints) + 0.10*tier_score

# CI/CD gating example (GitHub Actions snippet)

on: [push]
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Query gatekeeper before deploy
        run: |
          curl -s -X POST https://internal-api.redwood.com/gatekeeper/v1/ci/check \
            -H "Authorization: Bearer $GATEKEEPER_TOKEN" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d '{"target":"svc/inference-api", "tenant_id":"${{ matrix.tenant }}"}' \
          | jq '.status'

Notes and future work:
- Integrate tenant sentiment from support conversations (NLP score) to increase sensitivity for enterprise customers.

title: Service Stewardship and Budget Guardrails
preview:
Summary:
This document defines the service stewardship lifecycle for Redwood Inference services and prescribes concrete guardrails for error budgets, capacity planning, operational tooling, and incident-review expectations. It ties SLOs and error budgets to capacity runway, post-incident action, and automated tooling so teams can make consistent, rapid decisions when reliability signals change.

Goals:
- Ensure every service has an explicit steward responsible for SLOs, error-budget policies, and capacity runway.
- Standardize guardrails so actions (throttle, admission, provisioning) are predictable and auditable.
- Integrate incident review evidence with capacity remediation tickets and follow-up actions.
- Provide runbooks and tooling references to reduce triage time and avoid ad-hoc mitigations.

Scope:
Applies to all production-facing services in the Redwood inference cluster (hosted, dedicated routing plane, and private connectors). This document covers: SLO definition lifecycle, error-budget policy templates, capacity runway calculation, incident review minimum evidence, operational dashboards, and two tactical runbooks.

Key definitions:
- Service steward: primary owner (engineer) accountable for SLOs, runbooks, and capacity runway.
- Error budget: 1 - SLO (e.g., 99.9% availability -> 0.1% budget). Measured on a rolling 30d window unless otherwise specified.
- Capacity runway: estimated days of safe operation before required provisioning or throttling given current burn rate.
- Critical degradation: any user-impacting event that causes >5% of traffic to fail latency or correctness SLOs for >10 minutes.

Service stewardship lifecycle (process overview):
1) Define SLOs and objectives during service onboarding (see SLO template below).
2) Publish error-budget policy: what to do at 50%, 25%, 10%, 0% remaining.
3) Maintain capacity runway >= 14 days under expected growth OR implement automated admission controls.
4) Run monthly stewardship review: verify dashboards, runbook accuracy, and on-call readiness.
5) After any Critical degradation: open a post-incident review, create capacity remediation tickets, and follow the slice-and-fix cadence in this doc.

Error budget guardrail policy (standard template):
- Measurement window: 30-day rolling by default. Some critical services (payment, auth) use 7-day rolling.
- Threshold actions:
  - >= 50% budget remaining: normal operations; steward monitors burn rate weekly.
  - >= 25% and < 50%: steward must notify product and SRE; schedule capacity forecast within 48 hours.
  - >= 10% and < 25%: steward triggers a targeted mitigation plan (rate limit non-essential traffic, enable caching layers, temporarily reduce batch sizes to reduce CPU).
  - < 10%: automatic admission-controller shifts to conservative mode; throttle new low-priority tokens by 30% and open urgent capacity ticket.
  - 0%: emergency degrade plan: non-critical features disabled, reserve capacity pool engaged, and SRE leads incident exec until recovery.

Automated enforcement actions (examples):
- Admission controller policy: when error budget < 10%, apply annotation on service to route new requests through conservative path (config: admission-policy=conservative).
- Cache warming and prefix KV restore: run warming job to prime frequently used KV segments on recovery.
- Canary throttles: route 5% of traffic to fallback model until KPIs stabilize.

Capacity runway calculation (practical method):
1) Collect baseline metrics: 95th percentile per-token latency, current qps, average tokens per request, GPU throughput (tokens/s), and current spare GPU capacity (GPUs * tokens/s - current load).
2) Estimate growth rate: use 14-day trend (g).
3) Compute burn-adjusted runway days = spare_capacity / (current_load * g).

Example table:
| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---:|---|
| Current QPS | 3,600 | 60 req/s * 60 |
| Avg tokens/req | 80 | measured from telemetry |
| GPU tokens/s capacity | 4,000,000 | per-cluster across pool |
| Spare capacity | 1,200,000 tokens/s | after reservations |
| 14d growth (g) | 0.04/day | 4% daily |
| Runway (days) | 7.5 | spare / (load * g) |

Minimum runway policy:
- Tier 0 (customer-impacting, SLA-backed): runway >= 30 days or dedicated capacity contract.
- Tier 1 (business-critical): runway >= 14 days or automated admission controls + 7d escalation SLA.
- Tier 2 (experimental / internal): runway >= 7 days or throttling + rapid rollback policy.

Incident review and evidence standards (minimum required artifacts):
1) Timeline: 1-minute granularity for first hour, 5-minute thereafter.
2) Telemetry snapshot: SLO timeseries, error rates, 95/99 latencies, token throughput, GPU utilization per pool.
3) Correlated traces: top 20 failed requests with spans annotated.
4) Deployment state: commit hashes, model versions, config flags.
5) Runbook actions taken with timestamps (who executed, exact commands).
6) Postmortem: causal chain, corrective actions, and capacity remediation ticket(s).

Post-incident capacity remediation workflow:
1) Owner opens capacity-remediation ticket, links postmortem, and assigns priority.
2) If issue caused runway < 14 days for Tier 1 services, implement short-term uplift (hot standby or burst capacity) within 24 hours.
3) Schedule durable changes (provision GPUs, change routing, long-term caching) with timelines and verification tests.
4) Close remediation only after runbook-driven verification (synthetic canaries + 7d observation).

Operational tooling (approved reference list):
- Dashboards: Grafana -> service/SERVICE_NAME/slo-summary, capacity-runway, token-breakdown.
- Tracing: Jaeger -> service traces aggregated by route and model-version.
- Alerting: PagerDuty rules tied to SLO burn rate alerts (burn-rate > 10x baseline triggers pg).
- Capacity planner: internal CLI (redwood capacity-est) and scheduled job (capacity-forecast).
- Admission controller: k8s CRD admission-policy with annotations applied by SRE automation.

Runbook: Emergency capacity uplift (step-by-step)
1) Verify telemetry: confirm degraded SLO and runway math.
2) Escalate to SRE duty and service steward.
3) Execute short-term uplift:
   a) If Dedicated pool available: allocate +2 GPUs from reserve (redwood capacity-allocate --pool=reserve --gpus=2).
   b) If no reserve: enable conservative admission-controller with policy=throttle-30.
   c) Reduce model concurrency: update route config and reload (rwdctl route update --concurrency=2 --service=SERVICE_NAME).
4) Run synthetic verification: smoke synthetic-calls -s SERVICE_NAME -n 1000 -> verify P95 < SLO and error rate < target.
5) Open capacity-remediation ticket linked to incident and assign follow-up actions.

SLO configuration template (example JSON for the SLO store):
{
  "service": "SERVICE_NAME",
  "slo": "availability",
  "objective": "99.9",
  "window_days": "30",
  "measurement": "success_per_minute",
  "owner": "team/service-steward",
  "error_budget_policy": {
    "50": "monitor",
    "25": "forecast-48h",
    "10": "admission-throttle-30",
    "0": "emergency-degrade"
  }
}

Note: the SLO store accepts strings only; include numeric fields as strings to ensure compatibility with older ingestion pipelines.

Rollout checklist for changing SLOs or error-budget policies:
1) Draft policy and review with SRE on-call and product manager.
2) Run simulated burn tests in capacity-sandbox with 3 load profiles.
3) Deploy policy to canary namespace for 72 hours and monitor burn-rate.
4) Full rollout via config management with staged percent (10%, 30%, 100%).
5) Post-deployment review after 7 days, update stewardship doc.

Owners, escalation, and contact matrix:
- Service steward: primary engineer (email in team roster).
- SRE on-call: for emergencies and admission-controller overrides.
- Capacity team: provisioning and reserved-pool approvals.
- Product PM: informed at >=25% budget remaining.

Appendix: common commands and verification snippets
- Check SLOs: rwdctl slo get --service SERVICE_NAME --window 30d
- Force conservative admission: rwdctl admission set --service SERVICE_NAME --policy conservative
- Allocate reserve GPUs (emergency): redwood capacity-allocate --pool=reserve --gpus=2 --reason \"incident uplift\"
- Run synthetic canary: synthetic-calls -s SERVICE_NAME -n 1000 -w 60

Revision history:
- 2026-01-12: Created by Priya Kapoor.
- 2026-02-05: Added automated enforcement examples and capacity runway calc. (Reviewer: Ethan Park)
- 2026-03-02: Clarified incident evidence minimum and appended SLO JSON compatibility note. (Reviewer: Sofia Alvarez)

Related artifacts and links:
- SLO store dashboard: /grafana/d/service-slo-store
- Capacity forecasting job: /jobs/capacity-forecast
- Incident postmortem template: /confluence/eng-sre/incident-review/postmortem-template

Questions or requests for updates: ping #sre-stewardship on Slack or open an issue in the eng-sre repo with label \"stewardship-update\".

1) Collect baseline metrics: 95th percentile per-token latency, current qps, average tokens per request, GPU throughput (tokens/s), and current spare GPU capacity (GPUs * tokens/s - current load).
2) Estimate growth rate: use 14-day trend (g).
3) Compute burn-adjusted runway days = spare_capacity / (current_load * g).

Example table:
| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---:|---|
| Current QPS | 3,600 | 60 req/s * 60 |
| Avg tokens/req | 80 | measured from telemetry |
| GPU tokens/s capacity | 4,000,000 | per-cluster across pool |
| Spare capacity | 1,200,000 tokens/s | after reservations |
| 14d growth (g) | 0.04/day | 4% daily |
| Runway (days) | 7.5 | spare / (load * g) |

Minimum runway policy:
- Tier 0 (customer-impacting, SLA-backed): runway >= 30 days or dedicated capacity contract.
- Tier 1 (business-critical): runway >= 14 days or automated admission controls + 7d escalation SLA.
- Tier 2 (experimental / internal): runway >= 7 days or throttling + rapid rollback policy.

Incident review and evidence standards (minimum required artifacts):
1) Timeline: 1-minute granularity for first hour, 5-minute thereafter.
2) Telemetry snapshot: SLO timeseries, error rates, 95/99 latencies, token throughput, GPU utilization per pool.
3) Correlated traces: top 20 failed requests with spans annotated.
4) Deployment state: commit hashes, model versions, config flags.
5) Runbook actions taken with timestamps (who executed, exact commands).
6) Postmortem: causal chain, corrective actions, and capacity remediation ticket(s).

Post-incident capacity remediation workflow:
1) Owner opens capacity-remediation ticket, links postmortem, and assigns priority.
2) If issue caused runway < 14 days for Tier 1 services, implement short-term uplift (hot standby or burst capacity) within 24 hours.
3) Schedule durable changes (provision GPUs, change routing, long-term caching) with timelines and verification tests.
4) Close remediation only after runbook-driven verification (synthetic canaries + 7d observation).

Operational tooling (approved reference list):
- Dashboards: Grafana -> service/SERVICE_NAME/slo-summary, capacity-runway, token-breakdown.
- Tracing: Jaeger -> service traces aggregated by route and model-version.
- Alerting: PagerDuty rules tied to SLO burn rate alerts (burn-rate > 10x baseline triggers pg).
- Capacity planner: internal CLI (redwood capacity-est) and scheduled job (capacity-forecast).
- Admission controller: k8s CRD admission-policy with annotations applied by SRE automation.

Runbook: Emergency capacity uplift (step-by-step)
1) Verify telemetry: confirm degraded SLO and runway math.
2) Escalate to SRE duty and service steward.
3) Execute short-term uplift:
   a) If Dedicated pool available: allocate +2 GPUs from reserve (redwood capacity-allocate --pool=reserve --gpus=2).
   b) If no reserve: enable conservative admission-controller with policy=throttle-30.
   c) Reduce model concurrency: update route config and reload (rwdctl route update --concurrency=2 --service=SERVICE_NAME).
4) Run synthetic verification: smoke synthetic-calls -s SERVICE_NAME -n 1000 -> verify P95 < SLO and error rate < target.
5) Open capacity-remediation ticket linked to incident and assign follow-up actions.

SLO configuration template (example JSON for the SLO store):
{
  "service": "SERVICE_NAME",
  "slo": "availability",
  "objective": "99.9",
  "window_days": "30",
  "measurement": "success_per_minute",
  "owner": "team/service-steward",
  "error_budget_policy": {
    "50": "monitor",
    "25": "forecast-48h",
    "10": "admission-throttle-30",
    "0": "emergency-degrade"
  }
}

Note: the SLO store accepts strings only; include numeric fields as strings to ensure compatibility with older ingestion pipelines.

Rollout checklist for changing SLOs or error-budget policies:
1) Draft policy and review with SRE on-call and product manager.
2) Run simulated burn tests in capacity-sandbox with 3 load profiles.
3) Deploy policy to canary namespace for 72 hours and monitor burn-rate.
4) Full rollout via config management with staged percent (10%, 30%, 100%).
5) Post-deployment review after 7 days, update stewardship doc.

Owners, escalation, and contact matrix:
- Service steward: primary engineer (email in team roster).
- SRE on-call: for emergencies and admission-controller overrides.
- Capacity team: provisioning and reserved-pool approvals.
- Product PM: informed at >=25% budget remaining.

Appendix: common commands and verification snippets
- Check SLOs: rwdctl slo get --service SERVICE_NAME --window 30d
- Force conservative admission: rwdctl admission set --service SERVICE_NAME --policy conservative
- Allocate reserve GPUs (emergency): redwood capacity-allocate --pool=reserve --gpus=2 --reason \"incident uplift\"
- Run synthetic canary: synthetic-calls -s SERVICE_NAME -n 1000 -w 60

Revision history:
- 2026-01-12: Created by Priya Kapoor.
- 2026-02-05: Added automated enforcement examples and capacity runway calc. (Reviewer: Ethan Park)
- 2026-03-02: Clarified incident evidence minimum and appended SLO JSON compatibility note. (Reviewer: Sofia Alvarez)

Related artifacts and links:
- SLO store dashboard: /grafana/d/service-slo-store
- Capacity forecasting job: /jobs/capacity-forecast
- Incident postmortem template: /confluence/eng-sre/incident-review/postmortem-template

Questions or requests for updates: ping #sre-stewardship on Slack or open an issue in the eng-sre repo with label \"stewardship-update\".

title: SLO Safeguard & Canary Governance Playbook
preview:
Overview:
This playbook defines governance, operational controls, and tooling patterns for running SLO-focused experiments and model canaries on Redwood's inference fleet. It establishes a safety-first approach so teams can iterate model rollouts and runtime tuning while protecting customer-facing SLOs and error budgets.

Scope:
- Applies to any production-facing model rollout, quantization change, kernel-switch, or runtime configuration that can alter latency, tail latency, or correctness metrics.
- Intended audience: SREs, Service Stewards, Model Owners, Capacity Engineers, Release Engineers.

Definitions:
- SLO (Service Level Objective): the target level of reliability (example: p99 latency & availability).
- Error Budget: allowable SLO violation over a window (30d default).
- Canary: a staged rollout (traffic percentage + time) used to validate changes.
- Safety Net: automated and manual mitigations triggered when canary threatens SLOs or budget.

Principles:
1) Safety before speed — experiments must not consume more than 10% of a service's remaining short-term error budget without SRE sign-off.
2) Observable experiments — all canaries must ship with an experiment dashboard, trace sampling, and dedicated alert rules.
3) Capacity-aware rollouts — canaries must include headroom checks and a reversible capacity reservation.
4) Evidence-driven rollback — rollback decisions must be based on pre-defined metrics and documented hypotheses.

Roles & Responsibilities:
- Service Steward (Model Owner): defines acceptance criteria, test prompts, and production evaluation prompts; owns the rollout plan.
- Release Engineer: coordinates deployment artifacts, feature flags, and traffic routing.
- Oncall SRE: monitors canary, authorizes emergency rollbacks, and triggers escalation.
- Capacity Team: approves temporary capacity reservations and verifies headroom.
- SRE Review Board: reviews experiments that exceed thresholds in this playbook.

Pre-flight Checklist (required before canary):
1) SLO & acceptance criteria documented in the rollout PR. Example: "latency_p99 <= 240ms, availability >= 99.95%, semantic-quality-regression <= 0.5%".
2) Baseline capture: 72h baseline of key metrics (latency quantiles, error rate, token cost, GPU utilization, KV hit-rate).
3) Dedicated canary dashboard created with at least: p50/p90/p99, error rate, burn rate, GPU mem, input size distribution.
4) Alert rules defined and validated in staging (see alert snippets below).
5) Capacity pre-approval if expected additional peak usage > 5% of cluster capacity.
6) Traffic routing plan: percentages and scheduled ramp steps.

Governance thresholds (auto vs manual):
- Auto-abort (immediate): breach of critical safety thresholds for 3 consecutive 1m windows:
  - Error rate spike > 5x baseline absolute and > 0.1%
  - p99 latency increase > 2x baseline and > 500ms absolute
  - GPU OOM, host OOM, or model load failures > 1
  If any auto-abort triggers, the rollout system must revert traffic within 2 minutes and create an incident ticket.
- Soft-fail (notify + pause ramp): sustained metric degradation for 10m:
  - error budget burn rate > 5 (5x expected burn) over 10m
  - p95 latency increase > 30% for 10m
  For soft-fail, ramp is paused and SRE oncall is paged with the experiment dashboard link.
- SRE Review (board approval): experiments expected to consume > 15% of 30d error budget or increase cluster utilization > 10% for >1h.

Canary Execution Template (recommended):
1) Start: 1% traffic for 15 minutes (validation window).
2) Ramp: 1% -> 5% (30 min) -> 25% (1h) -> full or target share after 24h if no issues.
3) Metrics sampling: increase trace sampling for canary traffic to 10x baseline to collect quality signals.
4) Preserve prefix caches: ensure canary instances share cache layer to avoid cold-start skew unless validating cache behavior.
5) Shadow traffic: mirror 5% of production to non-critical replicas to compare outputs without affecting users.

Capacity & Headroom Negotiation:
- Every canary must submit a "headroom manifest" with expected memory/GPU cycles and tail latency profile. The Capacity Team uses the manifest to run a quick simulated pressure test in a shadow pool.
- Minimum required headroom: 25% free CPU/GPUs and 30% free memory on impacted nodes for the duration of the experiment.
- If headroom cannot be met, the rollout must use lower traffic or extended ramp with SRE-approved acceptance criteria.

Alert rule examples (Prometheus-style pseudo-yaml):
- name: canary-critical-error-rate
  expr: |
    increase(inference_errors_total{job="inference",canary="true"}[1m]) / increase(inference_requests_total{job="inference",canary="true"}[1m]) > 0.001 && increase(inference_errors_total{job="inference",canary="true"}[1m]) > 10
  for: 1m
  labels:
    severity: critical
  annotations:
    summary: "Canary error rate spike on {{ $labels.model }}"

- name: canary-p99-latency-regression
  expr: |
    (histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(inference_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="inference",instance=~"canary.*"}[5m])) by (le)) ) > (baseline_p99_seconds * 2)
  for: 3m
  labels: { severity: critical }

Runbook: immediate actions on auto-abort
1) System reverts traffic to previous stable rollout within 2 minutes (automated).
2) Oncall SRE pages: summary, canary id, revert timestamp, immediate evidence (errors, last 20 traces).
3) Release Engineer opens rollback PR that documents suspected cause and next steps.
4) Capacity Team runs node health checks (oom, gpu-reset, driver errors).
5) If issue persists after automatic revert, follow incident escalation (see Incident Response Playbook).

Rollback decision matrix (condensed):
- If auto-abort triggered: mandatory rollback + postmortem.
- If soft-fail and root cause is configuration (e.g., kernel selection): pause + SRE triage (may patch and resume).
- If soft-fail and root cause is data/semantic drift: stop rollout and run offline eval; SRE Review Board required.

Post-experiment review (required artifacts):
- Canary run summary: duration, traffic curve, metric deltas vs baseline, traces sampled, top 5 failure modes.
- Evidence pack: links to dashboards, raw traces (safely redacted), capacity logs, pod events.
- Decision log: resume/rollback decision with rationale and owner sign-off.
- Action items and owner list with due dates.

Quality & Semantic Checks:
- For model changes, include a prompt set (min 200 examples) representing high-risk customer flows. Define acceptance as: semantic-quality-regression <= 0.5% (measured by automatic eval harness) or a narrower tolerance for high-SLA tenants.
- Monitor non-functional regressions: hallucination rate proxies, truncation rate, and function-call failures.

Tooling integrations and automation suggestions:
- Canary controller (release-engineering) should integrate with:
  - Telemetry: Prometheus, Tempo/Jaeger, Loki
  - Feature flags: LaunchDarkly or internal toggle service
  - Capacity orchestrator: capacity-orchestrator service for temporary reservations
  - Incident system: PagerDuty with experiment runbook links
- Automation: implement a canary-run summary webhook that posts to the experiment ticket when run completes or aborts.

Example quick commands (for runbook operators):
- Fetch canary pods: kubectl get pods -l canary=true,model=mymodel -n inference\-Describe failing pod events: kubectl describe pod <pod> -n inference\-Fetch recent traces (tempo): curl "https://telemetry.prod.internal/api/traces?canary_id=<id>&limit=50"

Audit & Evidence retention:
- Keep canary artifacts for 180 days (dashboards, traces, raw metrics) tied to the experiment ticket id.
- For any canary that triggers an auto-abort, extend retention to 2 years for forensic needs.

Postmortem & Continuous Improvement:
- All non-trivial rollbacks (>5% traffic or >1h run) require a lightweight postmortem within 3 business days.
- Maintain an experiment registry indexed by model, canary id, owner, and verdict to avoid re-running failing experiments without addressing root cause.

Appendix A — Example acceptance table (copy into PR):
| Metric | Baseline | Acceptance | Monitoring window | Reaction |
|-------|---------:|-----------:|------------------:|--------|
| availability | 99.98% | >= 99.95% | 30d | SRE review if breach |
| p99 latency | 220ms | <= 250ms | 1h rolling | auto-abort if >500ms |
| error rate | 0.02% | <= 0.1% | 10m | auto-abort if >0.5% |
| error budget burn rate | 1 | <= 5 (for ramp) | 10m | pause ramp if >5 |

Appendix B — Triage contact tree:
- Primary oncall SRE: oncall-sre@redwood.ai (page via PagerDuty)
- Capacity lead: capacity-oncall@redwood.ai
- Release engineer: releases@redwood.ai
- Model owner: listed on rollout PR

Notes & rationale:
This playbook intentionally separates automated abort conditions (fast, conservative) from reviewable soft failures to balance developer velocity with platform stability. By combining capacity pre-approvals, conservative ramping, and trace-forward evidence collection we can reduce false positives while keeping rollouts reversible and auditable.

Related policies and references:
- /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/multi-model-rollout-hedge-and-escrow-runbook-2026
- /confluence/eng-sre/operational-tooling/capacity-orchestrator-and-slo-reconciliation-cookbook-2026
- Incident Response Playbook: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process/runbook

Document history:
- 2024-09-12: created by Ava Chen
- 2025-06-03: added capacity manifest requirements (Rafael Ortega review)
- 2026-03-01: finalized alert thresholds and auto-abort behavior
