title: Capacity Conservator Handbook: SLO Allocation Patterns and Stewardship
preview:
Summary:
This handbook defines the Capacity Conservator role, standard SLO allocation patterns, error budget stewardship procedures, and capacity reconciliation practices for model-serving and API services. It consolidates guidance for on-call, SRE owners, and product teams to ensure predictable availability while preserving runway for feature velocity and cost optimization.

Purpose and scope:
- Purpose: Reduce ambiguity about how SLOs and capacity commitments are negotiated, provisioned, and reconciled across multi-tenant inference workloads.
- Scope: Redwood-hosted inference services, Dedicated clusters, and Private deployments where Redwood provides operational guidance. Does not change formal SLO contracts for customers (see pricing-and-packaging).

Key definitions:
- SLO (Service Level Objective): Target percent for an observable (e.g., p99 latency < 300ms, availability 99.95%).
- Error budget: 1 - SLO; tracked as burn rate over sliding windows (1h, 24h, 7d).
- Capacity Conservator: Primary SRE responsible for capacity stewardship for a set of services or model families.
- Stewardship window: Standard review cadence for allocations (weekly small, monthly major).

Roles and responsibilities:
1) Capacity Conservator (SRE)
   - Owns SLO allocation ledger and capacity forecasts for assigned tenants.
   - Chairs weekly stewardship sync with product/eng stakeholders.
   - Initiates pre-emptive throttles or tenant rebalancing when forecasted burn indicates >50% risk to SLOs in next 24h.
2) Service Owner (product/eng)
   - Proposes SLO targets, priority classes, and canary/rollback plans.
   - Provides workload profiles (token distribution, seq_len histogram, concurrency patterns).
3) Platform Capacity Planner
   - Maintains cluster sizing models, cost estimates, and provisioning pipelines.

SLO Allocation Patterns (templates):
- Conservative (production-critical):
  - Availability: 99.99%
  - Error budget: 52 minutes/month
  - Default priority: P0
  - Allocation rule: Dedicated pool or strict fairshare with guaranteed min throughput.
- Balanced (high-volume product features):
  - Availability: 99.95%
  - Error budget: ~4.38 hours/month
  - Default priority: P1
  - Allocation rule: Elastic fairshare with burst credit up to 150% for short windows.
- Economical (background/async batch jobs / low-latency-tolerant):
  - Availability: 99.5%
  - Error budget: ~3.65 days/month
  - Default priority: P2
  - Allocation rule: Spot-backed capacity or preemptible nodes with lower admission priority.

Capacity allocation decision flow (summary):
1) Service owner submits Allocation Request (JIRA template) with workload telemetry and desired SLO tier.
2) Capacity Conservator runs the Allocation Simulator (see tooling) and returns recommended reserve (vGPU units) and throttle policies.
3) If recommended reserve increases monthly commit > 20% vs baseline, escalate to Platform Capacity Planner for procurement lead time.
4) Approve -> schedule provisioning window -> update SLO ledger and dashboards.

Allocation decision checklist (for conservators):
- Confirm recent 4-week traffic histogram and 95th / 99th percentiles for sequence length and tokens per request.
- Validate model variant and quantization (FP16, int8) assumptions that affect per-token CPU/GPU cost.
- Ensure KV cache hit-rate assumptions are specified.
- Evaluate cross-tenant correlated surge risk (region outages, marketing events).
- Record acceptance criteria for success (latency p95/p99, throughput targets).

Error budget stewardship (operational rules):
- Burn rate tiers and automated signals:
  - Normal: burn_rate <= 1.0 over 24h -> no action.
  - Elevated: burn_rate 1.0-3.0 over 1h -> Capacity Conservator notified; begin mitigation planning.
  - Critical: burn_rate > 3.0 over 1h or projected > 2.0 over 24h -> auto-invoke throttles per priority policy and schedule incident review.
- Automatic mitigations (high-level):
  1) Soft admission controls: reduce lower-priority tenant concurrency by 20%.
  2) Prefetch/caching rehydration for common prefixes.
  3) Route new traffic to compatible cheaper model variants (fast-fallback policy).
  4) Expand autoscale group (if headroom available) with emergency capacity tag.
- Manual mitigations:
  - Negotiated temporary SLO relaxation clauses: only by Platform + Product sign-off during major incidents.

Operational tooling and telemetry contract:
- Required dashboards:
  - SLO ledger: per-service SLO, current burn (1h/24h/7d), priority class.
  - Capacity posture: cluster headroom %, upcoming scale events, spot eviction risk.
  - Tenant heatmap: per-tenant token/sec and tail-latency exposure.
- Alerting thresholds (Prometheus-style examples):
  - SRE Alert: slo_burn_rate{window=\"1h\"} > 3 -> SEV2 -> paged to oncall.
  - Capacity Alert: cluster_free_gpu_percent < 10 and provisionable == false -> SEV1 -> mandatory page.
  - Throttle Alert: tenant_throttle_rate > 5% -> SEV3 -> notification to owner.

Example Prometheus rule (text):
- record: slo:burrn_rate_1h
  expr: increase(slo_burn[1h]) / 3600

Example alert (text):
- alert: SLOBurnCritical
  expr: slo:burrn_rate_1h > 3
  for: 5m
  labels:
    severity: page
  annotations:
    summary: \"Critical SLO burn rate >3x for {{ $labels.service }}\"

Incident review standards (SLO/capacity lens):
- Evidence collection checklist for SLO incidents:
  1) Timeline of burn rate and traffic spikes (1h/24h windows).
  2) Top 10 tenants by token volume during window.
  3) Model variants and num_gpus in-use; scheduler logs for preemptions.
  4) Autoscaler decision logs and provisioning latency.
  5) Any rollout or config changes in the last 72h.
- Post-incident remediation categories:
  - Capacity: add guardrail (min-reserve, autoscale tuning) or reclassify priority.
  - Operational: improve alerts, fix telemetry gaps.
  - Product: negotiate tenant behavior change or request feature-level smoothing.
- SLO incident timeline artifact: include a 1-page summary with top 3 root causes, required next steps, owners, and target remediation date.

Runbook excerpts (actionable steps for on-call):
1) If SLOBurnCritical fires:
   a) Acknowledge and attach initial notes in incident tracker.
   b) Run `capacity-sim assess --service ${SERVICE} --window 1h` to get projected runway.
   c) If projection < 6h and cluster_free_gpu_percent < 15%: execute failover plan (see step d).
   d) Failover plan: `reroute --from ${SERVICE} --to ${FALLBACK_SERVICE} --ratio 0.25` then iterate until burn stabilizes.
2) If cluster_free_gpu_percent < 5%:
   a) Check pending node provisioning: `kubectl get nodes -l redwood/emergency-capacity=true`
   b) If none, open procurement trigger (automated ticket) and apply temporary throttles to P2 tenants per priority table.

Priority table (short):
- P0: platform-critical, 30% headroom guaranteed, preempt not allowed.
- P1: customer-facing, 10% headroom guaranteed, preempt allowed only for P0.
- P2: best-effort, subject to preemption and throttles during elevated burn.

Capacity reconciliation and monthly stewardship ritual:
- Monthly ritual steps (owner: Capacity Conservator):
  1) Pull actual usage and compare to forecast for each service and tenant.
  2) Identify variance > 15% and flag for optimization or re-provisioning.
  3) Run cost/perf simulation for recommended changes and present in monthly stewardship board (template attached).
  4) Approve or schedule changes with Platform Capacity Planner.

Controls for Dedicated and Private deployments:
- Dedicated: recommend explicit reserve lanes and an isolation test before onboard. Record reserved slot sizes and emergency add procedures.
- Private: provide a runbook template to customers describing how Redwood stewards error budget signals and what actions Redwood can take under support contract (e.g., advisory only vs active throttle).

Templates and CLI snippets (appendix):
- Allocation Request JIRA fields (text list): service, owner, desired_slo_tier, historical_token_profile_link, kv_cache_hit_rate, model_variant, quantization_profile, proposed_commit_change, acceptance_criteria.
- Example CLI: `redwood-capacity request --service search-infer --slo-tier Balanced --expected-qps 1200 --duration 30d`

Related pages and artifacts:
- Link: /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/service-onboarding-runway-and-slo-admission-playbook-2026
- Link: /confluence/eng-sre/operational-tooling/slo-observability-pipeline-design-and-validation-2025

Revision history:
- 2026-01-10: Created by Samira Chen (draft).
- 2026-02-05: Added automated mitigation rules and Prometheus examples (reviewers: Diego Morales).
- 2026-03-01: Published after stewardship board feedback and added Dedicated/Private controls.

Contact and ownership:
- Primary contact: samira.chen@redwood.ai
- Oncall alias for capacity conservators: oncall-capacity@redwood.ai

Appendix: Quick decision flow for emergency throttle (one-liner):
- If projected error budget runway < 6h -> reduce P2 concurrency by 30% -> re-evaluate after 5 minutes -> escalate to SEV1 if no improvement.
text:
Summary:
This handbook defines the Capacity Conservator role, standard SLO allocation patterns, error budget stewardship procedures, and capacity reconciliation practices for model-serving and API services. It consolidates guidance for on-call, SRE owners, and product teams to ensure predictable availability while preserving runway for feature velocity and cost optimization.

Purpose and scope:
- Purpose: Reduce ambiguity about how SLOs and capacity commitments are negotiated, provisioned, and reconciled across multi-tenant inference workloads.
- Scope: Redwood-hosted inference services, Dedicated clusters, and Private deployments where Redwood provides operational guidance. Does not change formal SLO contracts for customers (see pricing-and-packaging).

Key definitions:
- SLO (Service Level Objective): Target percent for an observable (e.g., p99 latency < 300ms, availability 99.95%).
- Error budget: 1 - SLO; tracked as burn rate over sliding windows (1h, 24h, 7d).
- Capacity Conservator: Primary SRE responsible for capacity stewardship for a set of services or model families.
- Stewardship window: Standard review cadence for allocations (weekly small, monthly major).

Roles and responsibilities:
1) Capacity Conservator (SRE)
   - Owns SLO allocation ledger and capacity forecasts for assigned tenants.
   - Chairs weekly stewardship sync with product/eng stakeholders.
   - Initiates pre-emptive throttles or tenant rebalancing when forecasted burn indicates >50% risk to SLOs in next 24h.
2) Service Owner (product/eng)
   - Proposes SLO targets, priority classes, and canary/rollback plans.
   - Provides workload profiles (token distribution, seq_len histogram, concurrency patterns).
3) Platform Capacity Planner
   - Maintains cluster sizing models, cost estimates, and provisioning pipelines.

SLO Allocation Patterns (templates):
- Conservative (production-critical):
  - Availability: 99.99%
  - Error budget: 52 minutes/month
  - Default priority: P0
  - Allocation rule: Dedicated pool or strict fairshare with guaranteed min throughput.
- Balanced (high-volume product features):
  - Availability: 99.95%
  - Error budget: ~4.38 hours/month
  - Default priority: P1
  - Allocation rule: Elastic fairshare with burst credit up to 150% for short windows.
- Economical (background/async batch jobs / low-latency-tolerant):
  - Availability: 99.5%
  - Error budget: ~3.65 days/month
  - Default priority: P2
  - Allocation rule: Spot-backed capacity or preemptible nodes with lower admission priority.

Capacity allocation decision flow (summary):
1) Service owner submits Allocation Request (JIRA template) with workload telemetry and desired SLO tier.
2) Capacity Conservator runs the Allocation Simulator (see tooling) and returns recommended reserve (vGPU units) and throttle policies.
3) If recommended reserve increases monthly commit > 20% vs baseline, escalate to Platform Capacity Planner for procurement lead time.
4) Approve -> schedule provisioning window -> update SLO ledger and dashboards.

Allocation decision checklist (for conservators):
- Confirm recent 4-week traffic histogram and 95th / 99th percentiles for sequence length and tokens per request.
- Validate model variant and quantization (FP16, int8) assumptions that affect per-token CPU/GPU cost.
- Ensure KV cache hit-rate assumptions are specified.
- Evaluate cross-tenant correlated surge risk (region outages, marketing events).
- Record acceptance criteria for success (latency p95/p99, throughput targets).

Error budget stewardship (operational rules):
- Burn rate tiers and automated signals:
  - Normal: burn_rate <= 1.0 over 24h -> no action.
  - Elevated: burn_rate 1.0-3.0 over 1h -> Capacity Conservator notified; begin mitigation planning.
  - Critical: burn_rate > 3.0 over 1h or projected > 2.0 over 24h -> auto-invoke throttles per priority policy and schedule incident review.
- Automatic mitigations (high-level):
  1) Soft admission controls: reduce lower-priority tenant concurrency by 20%.
  2) Prefetch/caching rehydration for common prefixes.
  3) Route new traffic to compatible cheaper model variants (fast-fallback policy).
  4) Expand autoscale group (if headroom available) with emergency capacity tag.
- Manual mitigations:
  - Negotiated temporary SLO relaxation clauses: only by Platform + Product sign-off during major incidents.

Operational tooling and telemetry contract:
- Required dashboards:
  - SLO ledger: per-service SLO, current burn (1h/24h/7d), priority class.
  - Capacity posture: cluster headroom %, upcoming scale events, spot eviction risk.
  - Tenant heatmap: per-tenant token/sec and tail-latency exposure.
- Alerting thresholds (Prometheus-style examples):
  - SRE Alert: slo_burn_rate{window=\"1h\"} > 3 -> SEV2 -> paged to oncall.
  - Capacity Alert: cluster_free_gpu_percent < 10 and provisionable == false -> SEV1 -> mandatory page.
  - Throttle Alert: tenant_throttle_rate > 5% -> SEV3 -> notification to owner.

Example Prometheus rule (text):
- record: slo:burrn_rate_1h
  expr: increase(slo_burn[1h]) / 3600

Example alert (text):
- alert: SLOBurnCritical
  expr: slo:burrn_rate_1h > 3
  for: 5m
  labels:
    severity: page
  annotations:
    summary: \"Critical SLO burn rate >3x for {{ $labels.service }}\"

Incident review standards (SLO/capacity lens):
- Evidence collection checklist for SLO incidents:
  1) Timeline of burn rate and traffic spikes (1h/24h windows).
  2) Top 10 tenants by token volume during window.
  3) Model variants and num_gpus in-use; scheduler logs for preemptions.
  4) Autoscaler decision logs and provisioning latency.
  5) Any rollout or config changes in the last 72h.
- Post-incident remediation categories:
  - Capacity: add guardrail (min-reserve, autoscale tuning) or reclassify priority.
  - Operational: improve alerts, fix telemetry gaps.
  - Product: negotiate tenant behavior change or request feature-level smoothing.
- SLO incident timeline artifact: include a 1-page summary with top 3 root causes, required next steps, owners, and target remediation date.

Runbook excerpts (actionable steps for on-call):
1) If SLOBurnCritical fires:
   a) Acknowledge and attach initial notes in incident tracker.
   b) Run `capacity-sim assess --service ${SERVICE} --window 1h` to get projected runway.
   c) If projection < 6h and cluster_free_gpu_percent < 15%: execute failover plan (see step d).
   d) Failover plan: `reroute --from ${SERVICE} --to ${FALLBACK_SERVICE} --ratio 0.25` then iterate until burn stabilizes.
2) If cluster_free_gpu_percent < 5%:
   a) Check pending node provisioning: `kubectl get nodes -l redwood/emergency-capacity=true`
   b) If none, open procurement trigger (automated ticket) and apply temporary throttles to P2 tenants per priority table.

Priority table (short):
- P0: platform-critical, 30% headroom guaranteed, preempt not allowed.
- P1: customer-facing, 10% headroom guaranteed, preempt allowed only for P0.
- P2: best-effort, subject to preemption and throttles during elevated burn.

Capacity reconciliation and monthly stewardship ritual:
- Monthly ritual steps (owner: Capacity Conservator):
  1) Pull actual usage and compare to forecast for each service and tenant.
  2) Identify variance > 15% and flag for optimization or re-provisioning.
  3) Run cost/perf simulation for recommended changes and present in monthly stewardship board (template attached).
  4) Approve or schedule changes with Platform Capacity Planner.

Controls for Dedicated and Private deployments:
- Dedicated: recommend explicit reserve lanes and an isolation test before onboard. Record reserved slot sizes and emergency add procedures.
- Private: provide a runbook template to customers describing how Redwood stewards error budget signals and what actions Redwood can take under support contract (e.g., advisory only vs active throttle).

Templates and CLI snippets (appendix):
- Allocation Request JIRA fields (text list): service, owner, desired_slo_tier, historical_token_profile_link, kv_cache_hit_rate, model_variant, quantization_profile, proposed_commit_change, acceptance_criteria.
- Example CLI: `redwood-capacity request --service search-infer --slo-tier Balanced --expected-qps 1200 --duration 30d`

Related pages and artifacts:
- Link: /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/service-onboarding-runway-and-slo-admission-playbook-2026
- Link: /confluence/eng-sre/operational-tooling/slo-observability-pipeline-design-and-validation-2025

Revision history:
- 2026-01-10: Created by Samira Chen (draft).
- 2026-02-05: Added automated mitigation rules and Prometheus examples (reviewers: Diego Morales).
- 2026-03-01: Published after stewardship board feedback and added Dedicated/Private controls.

Contact and ownership:
- Primary contact: samira.chen@redwood.ai
- Oncall alias for capacity conservators: oncall-capacity@redwood.ai

Appendix: Quick decision flow for emergency throttle (one-liner):
- If projected error budget runway < 6h -> reduce P2 concurrency by 30% -> re-evaluate after 5 minutes -> escalate to SEV1 if no improvement.

title: SLO to Customer Impact Classifier and Remediation Workflow
preview:
Overview

This page defines a reproducible classifier that maps SLO signals to a customer-impact tier and a prescriptive remediation workflow. It is intended to: align SRE, Product, and Customer Success during SLO degradations; minimize time-to-customer-remediation; and create consistent inputs for post-incident reviews and error-budget decisions.

Audience
- On-call SREs
- SRE leads and managers
- Incident commanders (ICs)
- Customer Success (CS) and Support for high-impact incidents

Principles
1) Tie every SLO alert to an explicit customer-impact tier (1–4).
2) Automate low-friction routing and remediation for common degradations.
3) Escalate proactively for any Tier 1/2 customer impact.
4) Preserve evidence for postmortems and error-budget reconciliations.

Definitions
- SLO: Service Level Objective (e.g., request_latency_p95 <= 200ms).
- Error budget: 1 - SLO target over the defined rolling window.
- Customer-impact tier: a 1–4 classification used to route and prioritize incident response.

Customer-impact tiers (quick guide)
| Tier | User-visible symptoms | Business effect | Escalation urgency | Owner to notify |
|------|----------------------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Widespread request failures or complete service outage across regions | Severe revenue/customer retention impact | Immediate (page) | SRE lead + Product PM + CS Director |
| 2 | Large subset of customers impacted (major tenants) or major feature down | Significant business disruption for key customers | High (page + slack) | SRE oncall + Product owner + CS manager |
| 3 | Localized errors, degraded latency for non-critical endpoints | Minor customer annoyance, limited support tickets | Medium (slack alert) | On-call SRE + Feature owner |
| 4 | Minor latency increase or transient errors with no customer tickets | No immediate business impact | Low (ticket) | On-call SRE |

SLO catalog excerpt (example mapping)
| SLO name | Metric | Target | Error-budget window | Default customer-impact tier |
|----------|--------|--------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| request_latency_p95_public | request_latency_p95{route=~"/v1/.*"} | <= 200ms | 30d | 2 |
| api_error_rate_5m | sum(rate(api_errors[5m])) / sum(rate(api_requests[5m])) | <= 0.5% | 7d | 1 |
| embedding_throughput_tail | embedding_infer_latency_p99 | < 1.2s | 14d | 3 |
| console_ui_availability | synthetic_check(console_login) | 99.5% monthly | 30d | 3 |

Classification algorithm (operational)
1) Alert receives telemetry: detector alerts on SLI breach or error-budget burn (alerting config).
2) Metrics enrichment: pull impact signals (affected tenant count, region distribution, top N tenants by RPS, open support tickets matching pattern).
3) Heuristics engine evaluates the following scores (0–100): failure-scope, severity, customer-exposure.
4) Combined score maps to Tier: Score >= 80 -> Tier 1; 60–79 -> Tier 2; 30–59 -> Tier 3; < 30 -> Tier 4.
5) Attach classification to incident and follow the tiered remediation workflow below.

Heuristics inputs (examples)
- affected_tenant_ratio: percent of total tenant RPS impacted.
- p95_latency_delta: percent change vs baseline.
- p99_error_spike: absolute increase in error count at p99.
- active_cs_tickets: number of open tickets tagged /inference or /api.

Operational remediations by tier
Tier 1 (Immediate response)
- Page the SRE oncall and SRE lead.
- Open an incident in the incident-management system using template: tag=CUSTOMER_IMPACT_TIER_1.
- Run mitigation playbook: fallback-to-compatible-model-variant (see runbook link).
- If capacity-related: trigger immediate capacity burst policy (see CLI below).
- Notify CS and Product leadership within 15 minutes with templated Slack/Jira.

Tier 2 (High-priority)
- Notify on-call via high-severity Slack alert.
- Execute targeted throttles or tenant fair-share configuration.
- Begin canary rollback of recent model or config changes (if correlated).

Tier 3 (Operational handling)
- Create ticket and schedule on-call investigation within 30–60 minutes.
- Apply mitigations from the runbook: restart worker pool, clear KV cache, or disabled newly-deployed feature flag.

Tier 4 (Monitoring)
- Monitor metrics and let automated remediation run. If unresolved after SLA window (6 hours), escalate to Tier 3.

Sample alert rule (Prometheus-style YAML)
alert: HighAPIErrorRate
expr: |
  sum(rate(api_errors_total[5m])) / sum(rate(api_requests_total[5m])) > 0.005
for: 2m
labels:
  severity: page
  impact: "possible-customer-impact"
annotations:
  summary: "API error rate >0.5% over 5m"
  runbook: "https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/api-error-rate-mitigation"

Enrichment query examples (pseudo-SQL / analytics)
- Tenants affected in last 10m: SELECT tenant_id, count(*) FROM requests WHERE status >= 500 AND timestamp > now() - interval '10 minutes' GROUP BY tenant_id ORDER BY count DESC LIMIT 50;
- Support ticket correlation: count tickets where created_at >= now()-10m AND tags IN ('api','inference')

Capacity escalation knobs (examples)
| Trigger | Condition | Automated action | Escalation threshold |
|---------|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| headroom_exhausted | available_gpu_slots < 5% | trigger-burst-alloc --pool=shared-burst | immediate (Tier1) |
| model_warm_pool_empty | warm_pool_size < configured_min | scale-up warm pool by 2 nodes | Tier2 |
| p95_latency_spike | p95 > baseline*1.5 for 10m | enable-prefetch-kv-cache=true | Tier2 |

CLI examples
- Trigger burst capacity (operators only):
  rcmd infra capacity burst apply --pool shared-burst --reason "Tier1 SLO breach" --expires 30m
- Re-route traffic to fallback models:
  rcmd infra routing promote --from model-xyz-v2 --to model-xyz-v1 --weight 100

Communication templates
Slack template (Tier1)
- Channel: #incidents
- Message: :rotating_light: *Tier 1 SLO breach* — request_error_rate_5m at 1.2% impacting ~32% of tenants. IC: @oncall. Mitigation in progress: toggling fallback routing. Please notify CS lead @renee.zhao. Incident link: <INCIDENT_URL>

Jira template fields
- Priority: P0 for Tier1, P1 for Tier2, P2 for Tier3/4
- Components: runtime, routing, capacity, product-feature
- Labels: slo-breach, customer-impact-tier-<n>, automated-enrichment

Post-incident / Error-budget reconciliation
1) Add classification evidence (metrics, tenant list, tickets) to the incident story.
2) Compute error-budget consumption attributable to the incident window.
3) For Tier1/Tier2 incidents: schedule a joint SRE/Product/CS triage within 72 hours. The triage determines whether to take a governance action (e.g., customer credits, temporary throttle, emergency capacity commit).
4) Document remediation plan and assign owners with due dates.

Postmortem checklist (to include in incident review)
- Summary of classifier result and whether the initial tier matched customer reports.
- Timeline of detection -> mitigation -> recovery.
- Root cause and contributing factors including capacity and SLO misconfigurations.
- Action items (owner + due date) and follow-up on error-budget policy.
- Evidence attachments: metrics, logs, enrichment query results.

Automation and operational tooling notes
- The classifier runs in the telemetry enrichment pipeline (owner: telemetry-team). Inputs are Prometheus alerts, request traces, and support ticket API.
- All automated classification decisions must be auditable: store the computed scores and inputs in incident metadata.
- Use feature flags to disable automatic Tier upgrades in sensitive customer accounts.

Ownership and governance
- SLO owner: Each SLO must list a primary owner (SRE or Product) and a secondary owner. Owners are responsible for maintaining mapping in this catalog.
- Classification engine owner: telemetry-team@redwood.com.
- Incident review owner for Tier1/Tier2: SRE lead on rotation.

Runbook links (examples)
- API error mitigation: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/api-error-rate-mitigation
- Fallback routing: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/fallback-routing-and-rollbacks
- Capacity burst playbook: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/capacity-burst-and-burst-rollback

Change log
- 2026-01-15 — Initial draft published (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-02-20 — Added capacity knobs and CLI examples (review: Liam O'Connor).
- 2026-03-01 — Finalized communication templates and post-incident checklist (review: Renee Zhao).

Appendix: sample incident evidence payload (JSON snippet)
{
  \"incident_id\": \"INC-2026-0115-001\",
  \"slo\": \"api_error_rate_5m\",
  \"computed_scores\": { \"failure_scope\": 78, \"severity\": 85, \"customer_exposure\": 60 },
  \"mapped_tier\": 1,
  \"top_affected_tenants\": [\"tenant-acme\", \"tenant-vela\"],
  \"evidence_queries\": [\"tenant_impact_query_2026-01-15-10m\"]
}

If you are an on-call SRE and this workflow appears to misclassify an incident, immediately create a ticket in the SRE backlog and tag the telemetry-owner for a review.

Contact
- Document owner: Priya Natarajan (pri@redwood.com)
- Telemetry owner: telemetry-team@redwood.com
- Incident management: incidents@redwood.com

2) Compute error-budget consumption attributable to the incident window.
3) For Tier1/Tier2 incidents: schedule a joint SRE/Product/CS triage within 72 hours. The triage determines whether to take a governance action (e.g., customer credits, temporary throttle, emergency capacity commit).
4) Document remediation plan and assign owners with due dates.

Postmortem checklist (to include in incident review)
- Summary of classifier result and whether the initial tier matched customer reports.
- Timeline of detection -> mitigation -> recovery.
- Root cause and contributing factors including capacity and SLO misconfigurations.
- Action items (owner + due date) and follow-up on error-budget policy.
- Evidence attachments: metrics, logs, enrichment query results.

Automation and operational tooling notes
- The classifier runs in the telemetry enrichment pipeline (owner: telemetry-team). Inputs are Prometheus alerts, request traces, and support ticket API.
- All automated classification decisions must be auditable: store the computed scores and inputs in incident metadata.
- Use feature flags to disable automatic Tier upgrades in sensitive customer accounts.

Ownership and governance
- SLO owner: Each SLO must list a primary owner (SRE or Product) and a secondary owner. Owners are responsible for maintaining mapping in this catalog.
- Classification engine owner: telemetry-team@redwood.com.
- Incident review owner for Tier1/Tier2: SRE lead on rotation.

Runbook links (examples)
- API error mitigation: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/api-error-rate-mitigation
- Fallback routing: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/fallback-routing-and-rollbacks
- Capacity burst playbook: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/capacity-burst-and-burst-rollback

Change log
- 2026-01-15 — Initial draft published (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-02-20 — Added capacity knobs and CLI examples (review: Liam O'Connor).
- 2026-03-01 — Finalized communication templates and post-incident checklist (review: Renee Zhao).

Appendix: sample incident evidence payload (JSON snippet)
{
  \"incident_id\": \"INC-2026-0115-001\",
  \"slo\": \"api_error_rate_5m\",
  \"computed_scores\": { \"failure_scope\": 78, \"severity\": 85, \"customer_exposure\": 60 },
  \"mapped_tier\": 1,
  \"top_affected_tenants\": [\"tenant-acme\", \"tenant-vela\"],
  \"evidence_queries\": [\"tenant_impact_query_2026-01-15-10m\"]
}

If you are an on-call SRE and this workflow appears to misclassify an incident, immediately create a ticket in the SRE backlog and tag the telemetry-owner for a review.

Contact
- Document owner: Priya Natarajan (pri@redwood.com)
- Telemetry owner: telemetry-team@redwood.com
- Incident management: incidents@redwood.com

title: Service Stability: Financial Friction & Capacity Trading Manual
preview:
Overview

This manual defines the operational and financial mechanism we use to reconcile SLO-driven error budgets, cross-team capacity needs, and short-term capacity trading. The goal is to reduce friction for business-driven launches while protecting global availability and preserving fair allocation across tenants and services.

Who should read this
- SREs owning model-serving SLOs
- Product/feature owners planning launches
- Capacity planners and finance partners
- Service owners who may request temporary headroom

Key terms
- SLO: Service Level Objective (e.g. 99.5% p99 latency for text-generation).
- Error budget: allowable SLO budget (1 - SLO) consumed by poor performance.
- Capacity credit: a time-bounded token issued to a team permitting additional headroom above nominal allocation.
- Trading window: the period (default 72 hours) during which capacity credits can be used.

Principles
1) Safety first: SLO preservation is the overriding constraint; no trade may push a dependent SLO below its emergency threshold without explicit authorization.
2) Measurable economics: every capacity trade must be accompanied by an estimate of marginal cost (GPU-hour equivalent) and expected customer value.
3) Short-term flexibility: support temporary, revocable capacity expansions (credits) rather than permanent reallocation for launches.
4) Auditability: all trades are logged, reconciled weekly with finance, and visible on the SRE capacity dashboard.

Operational model summary
- Capacity credits are issued by the Capacity Steward (CS) team and are denominated in "token-headroom units" (THU). 1 THU approximates 1 vGPU-hour on our standard inference instance.
- Credits expire after the trading window and do not convert to long-term allocation without formal capacity onboarding.
- Trades may be requested via the CLI or the Capacity Portal; automated approval flows exist for low-risk requests (below threshold) and manual approvals for higher-risk requests.

SLO thresholds and gating rules (summary table)
| SLO target | Soft burn threshold | Hard gate threshold | Action when crossed | Responsible party |
|------------|---------------------|---------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| p99 latency <= 200ms | 30% error budget burn in 1 hour | 60% burn in 30 minutes | Soft gate (reduce batch size, enable fallback variant) | Oncall SRE + Service Owner |
| availability >= 99.9% | 20% error budget burn in 24h | 50% burn in 1h | Immediate rollback, capacity escrow activation | Oncall SRE + CS Team |
| embedding throughput >= 10k qps | sustained queue length > 2x baseline | queue growth > 10%/min | Throttle non-critical tenants, invoke capacity trading | Oncall SRE |

Note: The full threshold matrix is available on the SRE dashboard and is parameterized per service.

Pre-deploy economic checklist (feature owners must provide)
1) Expected incremental burst (THU) and duration.
2) Estimated marginal cost ($/THU) and business justification (expected revenue/retention uplift).
3) Rollout plan: canary size, ramp cadence, observability queries (see telemetry section).
4) Fallback plan and rollback criteria mapped to SLO thresholds.

Requesting capacity credits (example procedure)
1) Open a capacity request via: redwood-capacity request --service my-service --amount 240THU --window 72h --reason "Black Friday promotion" --owner @product-team
2) Automated checks run: cross-SLO impact simulation, thermal headroom check, and marginal cost estimate.
3) If request < 500THU and no dependent SLO simulated impact > 5%, the system issues provisional credits and notifies CS.
4) For larger requests or flagged simulations, CS schedules a 30-minute triage.

Approval decision matrix (abbreviated)
- Auto-approve: request <= 500THU AND no dependent SLO risk > 5%
- CS review: 500THU < request <= 2,000THU OR dependent SLO risk between 5–15%
- Exec review: request > 2,000THU OR dependent SLO risk > 15%

Deployment gating runbook (step-by-step)
1) Preflight (T-24h): Ensure capacity credits are active, canary plan uploaded, and telemetry queries exist. Notify oncall.
2) Canary (T-0): Start with 1% traffic. Observe p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate, queue depth, and token-level cost.
3) Canary decision (T+10m window): If soft thresholds exceeded, pause rollout and remediate (increase cache, lower batch size). If hard thresholds exceeded, rollback to previous model/version immediately.
4) Ramp phases: 1% -> 5% -> 20% -> 50% -> 100% with 15–30 minute observation windows. Each ramp requires a green light in the dashboard.
5) Post-rollout (T+24h): Reconcile THU usage and file post-deploy evidence (see Post-deploy reconciliation).

Rollback and mitigation playbook (fast actions)
- Immediate rollback: execute redwood-deploy rollback --service my-service --to-tag stable-20260210
- Activate fallback variant: set model-routing weight to 0.25 for new model: redwood-routing set-weight --service my-service --model new-model:0.25 --model stable-model:0.75
- Lease emergency headroom: CS can issue emergency credits (up to 200THU) with exec notification; these are time-limited to 4 hours.

Telemetry & dashboards (what to monitor)
- latency.p99.% (service.my-service.latency.p99)
- request.error_rate (service.my-service.errors.total / requests.total)
- queue.depth (service.my-service.request_queue.depth)
- token_cost.per_minute (finance.service.token_cost.min)
- capacity.thu_consumed (capacity.service.thu_conso)
- dependent_slo.impact_estimate (simulation.run.latest)

Alerting rules (examples)
- PAG (P1): p99 > SLO and error budget burn > 60% in 30m -> PAGER (sre-oncall + product oncall)
- PAGE (P2): sustained soft-burn (30% in 1h) -> channel #sre-deployments + page if escalation conditions met

Audit & reconciliation (weekly cadence)
1) Every Monday CS publishes capacity ledger for the week: issued credits, consumed THU, refunded/expired credits, and marginal cost tallies.
2) Finance and Product reconcile expected marginal cost vs actual; discrepancies > 20% trigger a postmortem and a new cost estimate requirement for future trades.
3) SRE publishes list of removed trades (reversed due to SLO impact) and corrective actions.

Post-deploy evidence template (attach to capacity request)
- Start/End timestamps for the rollout
- THU allocated and THU consumed (by tag)
- Canary metrics snapshot (p50, p95, p99, error rate)
- Any mitigation actions taken and timestamps
- Final decision (complete / partial rollback / rollback) and justification

Example capacity trade request (JSON payload example for API)
{
  "service": "my-service",
  "amount_thu": "240",
  "window_hours": "72",
  "justification": "Seasonal campaign - expected 4x baseline for 12 hours",
  "owner": "product-team@redwood.com"
}

(Submit via POST /api/v1/capacity/trades with API key scope=capacity:request)

Failure modes & mitigations
- Unbounded consumption: implement token bucket throttles tied to THU, auto-throttle non-critical tenants.
- Simulation underestimates dependent impact: apply conservative multipliers and require manual signoff for repeated underestimates.
- Disputed chargebacks: escalate to financial reconciliation committee; freeze future auto-approvals for offending product teams until resolved.

Ownership & escalation paths
- Capacity Steward (CS) Team: issues credits, maintains ledger, performs weekly reconciliation. Contact: cs-team@redwood.com
- SRE Oncall: operational lead during deployment windows and incident response. Contact: #sre-oncall
- Product Launch Owner: provides business justification and ROI estimates.

Appendix A: Example CLI commands
- Request credits: redwood-capacity request --service my-service --amount 240 --window 72 --reason "marketing surge"
- Check credits: redwood-capacity status --service my-service
- Revoke credits: redwood-capacity revoke --id TR-20260301-42 --force

Appendix B: Recommended dashboard queries
- Canary snapshot: rate(api.requests{service="my-service",route="/v1/generate"}) by (quantile)
- Burn rate query: sum(increase(error_budget_burn[1h])) / error_budget_total

Appendix C: Example scenarios
- Small launch (<=500THU): Use auto-approval, standard canary. Post-deploy reconciliation required within 48 hours.
- Large launch (>2000THU): CS + SRE + Finance tabletop prior to issuance. Post-launch cost variance threshold set to 10% for immediate remediation.

Document change log
- 2025-11-20: Created by Priya Ramanathan (initial draft).
- 2026-01-12: Added automated approval thresholds and CLI examples (reviewer: Ethan Park).
- 2026-03-01: Finalized gating thresholds and reconciliation cadence (reviewers: Maya Singh, Liam O'Connor).

Related artifacts
- SRE capacity dashboard (internal link)
- Capacity API spec (internal link: /confluence/engineering/api/capacity-spec)

End of document
