- Quality/eval-driven rollback (handled in a separate guardrails track)

## Outcome statement
By end of H1 2026, Redwood will:
- Automatically pause or rollback rollouts on confirmed SLO breaches (latency/error), with per-service defaults and per-rollout overrides.
- Provide auditable decision traces and metrics snapshots for every automated action.
- Provide dashboards and alerts that route to the right responders without paging storms.
- Enforce minimum monitoring and configuration prerequisites before auto-mitigation can be enabled for a service/route.

## Success metrics (initiative KPIs)
We measure success using a mix of outcome metrics and enablement/coverage metrics.

### Outcome metrics (primary)
1) Rollout-related customer-impact minutes
   - Target: 50% reduction vs 2025 H2 baseline for incidents attributed to rollouts (latency/error regressions)

2) MTTR for rollout regressions (time from first SLO breach to mitigation)
   - Target: p50 MTTR < 10 minutes, p90 MTTR < 25 minutes for monitored rollouts

3) Automated mitigation latency
   - Target: median time from verified breach to action execution < 90 seconds

4) False-positive rate (guardrail quality)
   - Target: < 1% of rollouts trigger mitigation without corroborated user impact (as judged in post-incident review)

### Coverage metrics (secondary)
5) Monitor coverage for rollout-scoped SLOs
   - Target: 95% of production rollout-enabled routes have required latency and error monitors with correct scoping labels

6) Auditability completeness
   - Target: 100% of auto-actions have an audit event with policy version, monitor identifiers, decision reason, and snapshot references

7) On-call readiness
   - Target: runbook exists and is validated in game day for each tier (Hosted, Dedicated, Private) before broad enablement

## Scope and deliverables
### Product / Platform deliverables (owned by Platform + Console)
- Rollout controller integration with SLO evaluation (burn-rate windows + min-sample + debounce)
- Action decisioning: pause vs rollback vs shift traffic back to baseline for A/B
- Policy configuration in policy engine (defaults, safe bounds, suppression windows)
- Admin-only override for suppression with justification logging
- Console surfaces: rollout timeline includes auto-actions and “why” details

### SRE / Observability deliverables (owned by SRE + Observability)
- SLO eligibility guidance (which SLOs can gate rollouts; recommended thresholds by customer tier)
- Standard metrics and labels for evaluation outcomes and decisions
- Dashboards: triggers, suppressions, stale monitor conditions, per-service rollout health
- Alert routing rules: notify vs page; prevent paging storms
- Runbooks and incident playbook updates
- Game day simulations validating pause/rollback behavior under failure modes

## Guardrails and reliability principles
Auto-mitigation must be conservative and explainable. Core principles:
- Dual-window confirmation (fast + slow burn-rate) to reduce sensitivity to brief spikes
- Minimum sample sizes to avoid low-traffic flapping and noisy percentiles
- Debounce/holdoff to prevent oscillation during known transient conditions
- Fail-safe handling of missing/stale monitors (explicit outcomes and visible alerts)
- Idempotent, rate-limited rollback execution (safe under partial failures and retries)
- Always produce an audit trail record for any decision (including suppression)

## Operational policy (H1 defaults)
These are the defaults we plan to recommend for services opting into auto-mitigation. Final values are governed by policy templates in the policy engine.

### Eligible signals
- Latency: p95 and p99 (rollout-scoped)
- Error rate: 5xx + timeouts (rollout-scoped)
- Saturation: optional, service-specific (only where signal-to-noise is adequate)

### Evaluation behavior
- Windows: 5m fast burn + 30m slow burn (default)
- Minimum sample: must meet minimum request counts per window before action is taken
- Debounce: require consecutive evaluations to confirm breach; add holdoff after action
- Suppression: allow temporary suppression during known incidents or telemetry degradation, with justification required

### Actions (default)
- Canary rollouts: pause on first confirmed breach; rollback on sustained breach (policy-defined)
- A/B tests: shift traffic back to baseline if variant is responsible and breach is variant-scoped
- Staged deployments: rollback to last known-good version if breach meets rollback criteria

## Milestones (H1 2026)
Dates are planning targets; actual ship is gated by readiness checks.

### M1 — Foundations: semantics + standards (Jan)
- SLO eligibility definitions for rollout safety are published and reviewed
- ADRs finalized for breach evaluation strategy and audit event schema
- Metric names/labels standard published (with cardinality guidance)

Exit criteria:
- All teams agree on: burn-rate windows, debounce, min-sample, staleness behavior, and audit requirements

### M2 — MVP in staging: auto-pause only (Feb)
- Controller integrates SLO evaluator hook and can pause rollout steps
- Audit events emitted and visible via Console API
- Initial dashboards/alerts available in staging
- E2E tests simulate latency/error breach and validate pause + audit trail

Exit criteria:
- Staging game day demonstrates: correct pause behavior, no flapping, and clear investigation path

### M3 — Production internal canary: pause + limited rollback (Mar)
- Enable behind feature flag for a small set of internal routes in one region
- Implement safe rollback executor for canary/A-B traffic splits (rate-limited, idempotent)
- Incident-bot notifications to Slack (non-paging initially)

Exit criteria:
- At least 2 weeks of internal canary with no critical false positives and acceptable alert noise

### M4 — Broader production enablement (Apr–May)
- Expand to additional internal routes and one external low-risk cohort
- Enforce release gates: required monitors/dashboards must exist before enabling
- Add admin override + suppression flow with audit logging

Exit criteria:
- Monitor coverage targets met for the enabled cohort
- On-call playbook validated; paging rules tuned

### M5 — General availability for supported tiers (June)
- Default templates published (per tier)
- Rollout policy editor UX stabilized; safe defaults and warnings in place
- Post-launch review: KPI tracking and follow-up plan for H2

Exit criteria:
- GA announcement internally; enablement checklist owned by release engineering and SRE is in place

## Cross-team responsibilities (RACI-style)
### Accountable (A)
- SRE: Sean Gallagher (SLO semantics, on-call readiness, paging policy)
- Platform/Control plane: Priya Natarajan (feature delivery across controller/policy)

### Responsible (R)
- Rollout controller: Jada Williams (integration points, action execution safety)
- Console/backend surfaces: Chloe Martin (API exposure, RBAC alignment)
- Policy engine schema: Benji Okafor, Jordan Lee (config model + validation + safe bounds)
- Observability: Aisha Bello (dashboards + burn-rate alerts), Allison Grant (metrics standards)
- Release coordination: Connor O’Brien (staged enablement and backout plan)

### Consulted (C)
- Security/audit: Kevin Osei (audit trail constraints and retention/privacy)
- Runtime: serving-runtime leads (latency signals; batching/kv-cache behavior)
- Customer-facing validation: Liam O’Rourke (enterprise expectations and rollout communications)

### Informed (I)
- Support + incident response rotation (auto-action interpretation and comms)
- Service owners (opt-in, monitor prerequisites, post-action follow-up)

## Dependencies and prerequisites
- SLO toolkit must support rollout-scoped burn-rate queries (route/model/region/tier labels)
- Telemetry ingestion must meet freshness SLOs; monitor staleness must be detectable
- Policy engine must enforce safe bounds and reject unsafe configurations
- Console must display audit events and provide links to dashboards for rapid triage
- Release gates must prevent enabling auto-mitigation without required monitors

## Risks and mitigations
1) False positives leading to unnecessary rollbacks
   - Mitigation: dual-window confirmation, min-sample gating, conservative defaults, staged rollout, post-action review

2) Missing/stale data causes either unsafe fail-open or unsafe fail-closed behavior
   - Mitigation: explicit “monitor missing/stale” outcomes; alerting; suppression option; release gates for monitor presence

3) Oscillation (rollback, re-rollout, rollback)
   - Mitigation: debounce + holdoff after action; require human acknowledgement to resume in some templates

4) Alert noise / paging storms
   - Mitigation: tiered alerts (notify vs page), aggregation, cooldown windows, incident-bot formatting standards

5) Rollback executor failures under partial outages
   - Mitigation: idempotent actions, step-down traffic shifts, retries with backoff, audit of execution state

## Reporting cadence
- Weekly: initiative standup (SRE + Platform + Observability + Release Eng)
- Biweekly: KPI review (coverage + false positive rate + time-to-mitigation)
- After any auto-rollback in production: lightweight review within 48 hours (was it correct, what did we learn, did we page correctly)

## Game day plan (required)
We will run at least one formal game day per environment tier before broader enablement:
- Scenario A: p99 latency spike during canary, monitor healthy → expect pause then rollback (depending on policy)
- Scenario B: error rate spike isolated to A/B variant → expect traffic shift back to baseline
- Scenario C: monitor staleness (ingestion lag) → expect no rollback, explicit “stale monitor” outcome, alerts to SRE
- Scenario D: partial rollback executor failure → expect idempotent retry and clear audit trail

Success criteria:
- Correct controller state transitions
- Clear audit events and dashboard links
- Runbook steps allow an on-call to diagnose and resolve within 15 minutes

## Enablement checklist (high level)
Before enabling auto-mitigation for a production route/model scope:
- Required SLO monitors exist and are labeled correctly (route/model/region/tier)
- Dashboards are accessible to on-call and service owners
- Alert routes and severity are confirmed (notify vs page)
- Runbook updated and acknowledged by on-call rotation
- Feature flag strategy and backout plan confirmed with release engineering

## Open questions (tracked separately)
- Which services should default to rollback vs pause-only templates?
- Should we require human approval to resume a paused rollout in certain tiers?
- What are the long-term retention and export requirements for audit events (esp. Private deployments)?

## Appendix: Definitions
- “Rollout-scoped SLO”: an SLO evaluation scoped to the specific rollout dimensions (service/route, model version, region, customer tier) rather than global service health.
- “Breach evaluation”: a ruleset that considers burn-rate over multiple windows, minimum sample sizes, and staleness detection to decide whether to act.
- “Auto-action”: controller-triggered pause/rollback/traffic-shift decision recorded in audit logs and surfaced in Console.

title: Kernel Auto-Selection v2 — SLO Impact Assessment (Runtime 1.21 Rollout)
preview:
## Summary
Kernel Auto-Selection v2 ("KASv2") changes the kernel choice logic in the Serving Runtime based on model family/architecture, sequence-length regime (prefill/decode characteristics), and GPU SKU. The change is intended to reduce p95/p99 latency and stabilize throughput across heterogeneous fleets (A100/H100/L40S), but it introduces a new failure surface: mis-selection (wrong kernel for a workload or SKU), tail-latency spikes on long-context prefill, and telemetry-driven alert noise.

This SRE assessment defines:
- Projected SLO impact (positive/negative risks) and what we will measure to validate.
- Canary criteria and stop/rollback triggers.
- Alert adjustments required for safe staging and to prevent false positives.
- Error-budget policy during rollout (Hosted first, then Dedicated pools).

**Decision:** Proceed with staged rollout behind feature gates with strict regression budgets and automated rollback recommendations. Default state remains KASv1 unless allowlisted.

## Scope
In-scope for this assessment:
- Hosted API runtime pools in managed regions (initially: us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1).
- Dedicated GPU pools (customer-isolated) where we can enable by pool/model allowlist.
- KASv2 selection telemetry, dashboards, and SLO gating checks used by Release Engineering.

Out of scope:
- Model quality/output correctness evaluation (owned by Perf & Evals). We require that correctness gates remain hard blockers.
- Hardware/driver upgrades. Driver/CUDA guardrails are treated as prerequisites (denylist/disable v2).

## Services and user journeys impacted
KASv2 primarily impacts runtime execution latency and request success paths:
- **Text generation/chat:** latency distribution (prefill vs decode dominated), time-to-first-token (TTFT), tokens/sec.
- **Embeddings/rerank:** minimal kernel selection exposure in this release (still monitored; no planned enablement in initial canary).

Key user journeys:
1) Short prompts with moderate decode (typical chat).
2) Long-context prefill (summarization, retrieval-augmented prompts).
3) Mixed workloads (heterogeneous seq lengths interleaved) where scheduling hints may influence selection.

## Current SLOs (reference)
SLOs below reflect the operational guardrails we use for Hosted inference pools. Dedicated/Private have contract-specific SLAs; this doc defines rollout safety gates independent of contract language.

### Hosted SLOs (runtime-facing)
| Metric | SLO target | Notes |
|---|---:|---|
| Request success rate | >= 99.90% (30d) | Success = non-5xx, non-timeout from runtime. |
| p95 end-to-end latency | Service-defined per route/model tier | We gate on *regressions* rather than absolute due to heterogeneity. |
| p99 end-to-end latency | Service-defined per route/model tier | Tail protection is primary risk area. |
| Runtime error rate (5xx) | <= 0.10% | Includes OOM, kernel launch failures surfaced as 5xx. |

### Rollout regression budgets (guardrails)
Because KASv2 is a performance initiative, the acceptance criteria are expressed as **delta vs control** (KASv1) within the same region/SKU/model.

**Hard gates (stop the rollout):**
- p99 latency regression > **+5%** (relative) sustained for **>= 30 minutes** in canary slice.
- p95 latency regression > **+3%** sustained for **>= 60 minutes**.
- Runtime 5xx error rate increase > **+0.05 percentage points** sustained for **>= 15 minutes**.
- Any detected correctness regression or determinism/replay mismatch (as reported by runtime CI/replay tests) associated with the candidate build.

**Soft gates (investigate, may proceed if understood and mitigated):**
- Selection distribution shift > **20% absolute** for a given (model_family, sku, seq_bucket) without an explained reason.
- TTFT regression > **+3%** where end-to-end p95 is flat (often indicates prefill kernel mismatch).
- Increased variance in throughput (tokens/sec) beyond **+10% CV** in steady-state workloads.

## Projected impact (expected vs risk)
### Expected improvements (based on pre-rollout perf characterization)
- Lower p95/p99 on SKUs where v1 used conservative kernels.
- Reduced tail spikes for mixed workloads via better seq-regime classification and scheduler hints.
- More stable throughput in heterogeneous fleets by avoiding SKU-mismatched kernels.

### Primary risks to SLOs
1) **Long-context prefill misclassification**
   - Symptom: TTFT and p99 increase disproportionately on long prompts (e.g., 8k–32k tokens).
   - Mitigation: long-context thresholds + explicit fallback hierarchy; denylist rules for affected (model_family, sku, driver).

2) **SKU normalization gaps (A100 variants, MIG, cloud naming)**
   - Symptom: kernel registry lookup misses → unexpected fallback kernel; may be safe but slower or unstable.
   - Mitigation: canonical SKU mapping in runtime + control-plane-config; metric dimensions must use canonical SKU.

3) **Kernel instability on specific driver/CUDA combos**
   - Symptom: kernel launch failures → elevated 5xx.
   - Mitigation: guardrail to disable KASv2 on known-bad driver versions; rollout requires driver allowlist coverage.

4) **Observability overhead and metric cardinality**
   - Symptom: elevated CPU overhead, increased ingestion cost, noisy alerts (esp. due to high-cardinality labels).
   - Mitigation: sampling policy; strict label set; aggregation strategy; alert query updates before canary.

## Required telemetry and dashboard readiness
KASv2 must emit sufficient context to diagnose regressions quickly without creating uncontrolled cardinality.

### Required per-request / sampled event fields
Required (always present; sampled emission allowed):
- selector_version (v1|v2)
- selected_kernel
- model_id (stable internal id)
- model_family (llama|mistral|qwen|gemma|other)
- gpu_sku_canonical (e.g., a100-80gb, h100-sxm, l40s)
- seq_bucket (short|medium|long|xl) based on prefill tokens
- prefill_tokens, decode_tokens (binned for metrics; exact values only in traces)
- fallback_reason (none|missing_kernel|denylisted|unstable_driver|safety_rule)

Optional (trace-only, not in metric labels):
- candidate_kernels list
- selector_feature_vector hash
- selector_decision_trace

### Sampling policy (SRE requirement)
- Default telemetry sampling for KASv2 decision events: **0.5%** of requests per (region, model_id) with burst cap.
- Temporarily increase sampling to **2%** for a canary model/SKU during incident investigation, time-boxed to **<= 2 hours**.
- Trace sampling for detailed decision traces: **0.05%** with explicit oncall enablement.

### Rollout dashboards
Before any production canary enabling KASv2, the following must be green and validated:
- Dashboard panels: selection distribution by (model_family, sku, seq_bucket) and selector_version.
- Latency delta panels: p50/p95/p99 for v1 vs v2 slices.
- TTFT panel split by seq_bucket.
- Error rate panel split by selector_version and sku.
- Fallback reason panel (top contributors).

Owner for dashboard readiness: **Allison Grant** (SRE/Observability) with **Omar El-Sayed** (Runtime observability) for runtime-side emission.

## Alert adjustments and new alerts
We will adjust existing alerts and add a small number of rollout-specific alerts.

### Alert changes (to reduce false positives)
1) **High cardinality guardrails**
   - Remove/avoid alerting directly on labels: model_id + selected_kernel + gpu_sku simultaneously.
   - Prefer aggregation: by selector_version and region; drill down in dashboards.

2) **Use comparative canary alerts**
   - During rollout, latency alerts should compare v2 slice to v1 slice rather than to a static threshold.

3) **Adjust evaluation windows**
   - p99 is noisy; require sustained breaches over 30 minutes for rollback triggers, unless accompanied by error rate increases.

### New rollout-specific alerts
| Alert | Trigger | Action |
|---|---|---|
| KASv2 p99 regression vs v1 | > +5% for 30m in canary slice | Page oncall + recommend rollback; follow runbook. |
| KASv2 error rate increase | +0.05pp for 15m | Page oncall; disable v2 for affected model/SKU/region. |
| KASv2 fallback spike | fallback_reason != none exceeds 5% for 15m | Investigate registry gaps or denylist overreach; may preempt regressions. |
| Selection distribution anomaly | >20% shift in selected_kernel share for stable workload | Investigate scheduler hints/feature extraction changes; consider freezing canary. |

## Canary plan and criteria
KASv2 rollout proceeds in phases with explicit entry/exit criteria. Canary always starts with a model allowlist and a limited region/SKU slice.

### Phase 0: Pre-prod and readiness
Entry criteria:
- KASv2 determinism/replay tests passing in CI.
- Benchmark suite coverage includes at minimum: Llama/Mistral/Qwen/Gemma across short/long seq buckets on A100/H100/L40S.
- Dashboard panels deployed and validated against a known dataset.

Exit criteria:
- No correctness regressions.
- No unexplained >3% p95 regression in perf canary environments.

### Phase 1: Hosted canary (single region)
Plan:
- Enable KASv2 in **us-east-1** only.
- Traffic allocation: **1%** of eligible requests (by model allowlist) with ability to hard-disable by (model_id, sku, region).
- Target SKUs: a100-80gb and h100-sxm first; l40s after 48 hours of stable results.

Entry criteria:
- Oncall staffed with named rollback authority.
- Rollback knobs verified (feature gate + allow/deny list hot reload).

Success criteria (48-hour window):
- p99 regression <= +2% (aggregate) and no sustained breaches of hard gates.
- Error rate non-inferior vs v1 (<= +0.02pp change).
- Fallback rate < 1% overall, with no single fallback_reason dominating unexpectedly.

### Phase 2: Hosted expansion (multi-region)
Plan:
- Increase to **5%** in us-east-1 and add **us-west-2** at 1%.
- After 24h stable, add **eu-west-1** at 1%.

Success criteria (72-hour window):
- No region shows p99 regression > +3% sustained.
- No increase in incident rate attributable to kernel selection.

### Phase 3: Dedicated pools (opt-in)
Plan:
- Enable per-pool and per-model allowlist for 1–2 internal Dedicated pools first.
- Expand to external customers only after Hosted GA and after SKU mapping is validated for that customer’s GPU types.

Dedicated criteria:
- Customer has agreed rollback plan and a defined escalation path.
- For constrained SKUs, keep KASv2 behind a “pinned kernel fallback available” requirement.

## Error budget policy during rollout
We treat the rollout as a **change event** but do not relax customer-facing reliability expectations. Instead, we define how to consume error budget for KASv2-related regressions and when to halt.

### Hosted error budget policy
- If any KASv2-related incident consumes > **10% of monthly error budget** for a region/service tier, pause expansion and return to KASv1 defaults while investigating.
- If KASv2 canary slice causes repeated near-misses (3+ soft gate breaches in 7 days), require a formal review with Runtime + SRE before proceeding.

### Dedicated error budget policy
- Dedicated rollouts are treated as **opt-in changes**; we will not use customer error budget as a test harness.
- Any customer escalation indicating tail-latency regression results in immediate disable/pin per the runbook, followed by a postmortem if impact lasted > 30 minutes.

### Rollback authority and comms
- Rollback authority during canary: **SRE oncall** (primary) with **Connor O’Brien** (Release) as secondary.
- Runtime engineering must provide a “known-safe” configuration (KASv1 default + pinned kernel options) that oncall can apply without code deploy.
- For customer-facing impact, follow standard incident comms; for canary-only impact, log an internal incident entry if rollback is triggered.

## Operational playbook (what oncall does)
When a regression is suspected during KASv2 canary:
1) Confirm scope: region, model_id, gpu_sku_canonical, seq_bucket.
2) Check comparative panels: v2 slice vs v1 slice.
3) If hard gate breached, execute immediate mitigation:
   - Disable KASv2 by (model_id, region) first; if localized, disable by (sku) as well.
   - Optionally pin kernel to last-known-good for affected tuple.
4) Validate recovery:
   - p99 returns to baseline within 15–30 minutes.
   - Error rate returns to baseline within 10 minutes.
5) Escalate to Runtime Kernels + Observability with selection traces and fallback_reason distributions.

See: Kernel Selection v2 Override and Rollback Runbook (linked in Related Pages).

## Known monitoring pitfalls and mitigations
- **Metric cardinality changes can break alerts.** All alert queries must be validated against the new label set before enabling KASv2 in production.
- **Workload shifts can resemble regressions.** Use matched cohorts where possible (same model_id, sku, region) and confirm traffic mix (seq_bucket distribution).
- **Small canary slices have noisy p99.** Use longer windows and confirm with TTFT + error signals.

## Open items / follow-ups
- Confirm canonical GPU SKU mapping coverage for MIG and cloud-specific A100 variants; track remaining gaps before Dedicated expansion.
- Validate long-context thresholds for model families not in the primary benchmark matrix.
- Post-GA: reduce sampling to baseline and remove rollout-only alerts, keeping only high-signal anomaly detection.

## Approvals
- SRE: Aisha Bello (owner), Sean Gallagher
- Runtime: Noah Patel, Yuki Sato
- Release Engineering: Connor O’Brien
- Observability/Standards: Allison Grant

Last reviewed for Runtime 1.21 canary readiness on 2026-02-24.

- Default telemetry sampling for KASv2 decision events: **0.5%** of requests per (region, model_id) with burst cap.
- Temporarily increase sampling to **2%** for a canary model/SKU during incident investigation, time-boxed to **<= 2 hours**.
- Trace sampling for detailed decision traces: **0.05%** with explicit oncall enablement.

### Rollout dashboards
Before any production canary enabling KASv2, the following must be green and validated:
- Dashboard panels: selection distribution by (model_family, sku, seq_bucket) and selector_version.
- Latency delta panels: p50/p95/p99 for v1 vs v2 slices.
- TTFT panel split by seq_bucket.
- Error rate panel split by selector_version and sku.
- Fallback reason panel (top contributors).

Owner for dashboard readiness: **Allison Grant** (SRE/Observability) with **Omar El-Sayed** (Runtime observability) for runtime-side emission.

## Alert adjustments and new alerts
We will adjust existing alerts and add a small number of rollout-specific alerts.

### Alert changes (to reduce false positives)
1) **High cardinality guardrails**
   - Remove/avoid alerting directly on labels: model_id + selected_kernel + gpu_sku simultaneously.
   - Prefer aggregation: by selector_version and region; drill down in dashboards.

2) **Use comparative canary alerts**
   - During rollout, latency alerts should compare v2 slice to v1 slice rather than to a static threshold.

3) **Adjust evaluation windows**
   - p99 is noisy; require sustained breaches over 30 minutes for rollback triggers, unless accompanied by error rate increases.

### New rollout-specific alerts
| Alert | Trigger | Action |
|---|---|---|
| KASv2 p99 regression vs v1 | > +5% for 30m in canary slice | Page oncall + recommend rollback; follow runbook. |
| KASv2 error rate increase | +0.05pp for 15m | Page oncall; disable v2 for affected model/SKU/region. |
| KASv2 fallback spike | fallback_reason != none exceeds 5% for 15m | Investigate registry gaps or denylist overreach; may preempt regressions. |
| Selection distribution anomaly | >20% shift in selected_kernel share for stable workload | Investigate scheduler hints/feature extraction changes; consider freezing canary. |

## Canary plan and criteria
KASv2 rollout proceeds in phases with explicit entry/exit criteria. Canary always starts with a model allowlist and a limited region/SKU slice.

### Phase 0: Pre-prod and readiness
Entry criteria:
- KASv2 determinism/replay tests passing in CI.
- Benchmark suite coverage includes at minimum: Llama/Mistral/Qwen/Gemma across short/long seq buckets on A100/H100/L40S.
- Dashboard panels deployed and validated against a known dataset.

Exit criteria:
- No correctness regressions.
- No unexplained >3% p95 regression in perf canary environments.

### Phase 1: Hosted canary (single region)
Plan:
- Enable KASv2 in **us-east-1** only.
- Traffic allocation: **1%** of eligible requests (by model allowlist) with ability to hard-disable by (model_id, sku, region).
- Target SKUs: a100-80gb and h100-sxm first; l40s after 48 hours of stable results.

Entry criteria:
- Oncall staffed with named rollback authority.
- Rollback knobs verified (feature gate + allow/deny list hot reload).

Success criteria (48-hour window):
- p99 regression <= +2% (aggregate) and no sustained breaches of hard gates.
- Error rate non-inferior vs v1 (<= +0.02pp change).
- Fallback rate < 1% overall, with no single fallback_reason dominating unexpectedly.

### Phase 2: Hosted expansion (multi-region)
Plan:
- Increase to **5%** in us-east-1 and add **us-west-2** at 1%.
- After 24h stable, add **eu-west-1** at 1%.

Success criteria (72-hour window):
- No region shows p99 regression > +3% sustained.
- No increase in incident rate attributable to kernel selection.

### Phase 3: Dedicated pools (opt-in)
Plan:
- Enable per-pool and per-model allowlist for 1–2 internal Dedicated pools first.
- Expand to external customers only after Hosted GA and after SKU mapping is validated for that customer’s GPU types.

Dedicated criteria:
- Customer has agreed rollback plan and a defined escalation path.
- For constrained SKUs, keep KASv2 behind a “pinned kernel fallback available” requirement.

## Error budget policy during rollout
We treat the rollout as a **change event** but do not relax customer-facing reliability expectations. Instead, we define how to consume error budget for KASv2-related regressions and when to halt.

### Hosted error budget policy
- If any KASv2-related incident consumes > **10% of monthly error budget** for a region/service tier, pause expansion and return to KASv1 defaults while investigating.
- If KASv2 canary slice causes repeated near-misses (3+ soft gate breaches in 7 days), require a formal review with Runtime + SRE before proceeding.

### Dedicated error budget policy
- Dedicated rollouts are treated as **opt-in changes**; we will not use customer error budget as a test harness.
- Any customer escalation indicating tail-latency regression results in immediate disable/pin per the runbook, followed by a postmortem if impact lasted > 30 minutes.

### Rollback authority and comms
- Rollback authority during canary: **SRE oncall** (primary) with **Connor O’Brien** (Release) as secondary.
- Runtime engineering must provide a “known-safe” configuration (KASv1 default + pinned kernel options) that oncall can apply without code deploy.
- For customer-facing impact, follow standard incident comms; for canary-only impact, log an internal incident entry if rollback is triggered.

## Operational playbook (what oncall does)
When a regression is suspected during KASv2 canary:
1) Confirm scope: region, model_id, gpu_sku_canonical, seq_bucket.
2) Check comparative panels: v2 slice vs v1 slice.
3) If hard gate breached, execute immediate mitigation:
   - Disable KASv2 by (model_id, region) first; if localized, disable by (sku) as well.
   - Optionally pin kernel to last-known-good for affected tuple.
4) Validate recovery:
   - p99 returns to baseline within 15–30 minutes.
   - Error rate returns to baseline within 10 minutes.
5) Escalate to Runtime Kernels + Observability with selection traces and fallback_reason distributions.

See: Kernel Selection v2 Override and Rollback Runbook (linked in Related Pages).

## Known monitoring pitfalls and mitigations
- **Metric cardinality changes can break alerts.** All alert queries must be validated against the new label set before enabling KASv2 in production.
- **Workload shifts can resemble regressions.** Use matched cohorts where possible (same model_id, sku, region) and confirm traffic mix (seq_bucket distribution).
- **Small canary slices have noisy p99.** Use longer windows and confirm with TTFT + error signals.

## Open items / follow-ups
- Confirm canonical GPU SKU mapping coverage for MIG and cloud-specific A100 variants; track remaining gaps before Dedicated expansion.
- Validate long-context thresholds for model families not in the primary benchmark matrix.
- Post-GA: reduce sampling to baseline and remove rollout-only alerts, keeping only high-signal anomaly detection.

## Approvals
- SRE: Aisha Bello (owner), Sean Gallagher
- Runtime: Noah Patel, Yuki Sato
- Release Engineering: Connor O’Brien
- Observability/Standards: Allison Grant

Last reviewed for Runtime 1.21 canary readiness on 2026-02-24.

title: Tying incident types to SLOs and error budgets
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood’s **incident taxonomy** is used to:
1) map incidents to the right **SLOs** (availability vs. latency vs. streaming) and corresponding **error budgets**, and
2) produce consistent reporting that breaks down error-budget burn by **incident_type** and **owner**.

This is part of the Reliability Program goal to reduce incident frequency and improve MTTR via clearer ownership, consistent classification, and better tooling.

---

## Scope
**Applies to:** Hosted API, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

**In-scope incident classes (taxonomy v1):**
- Availability (5xx/overload)
- Latency regressions
- Streaming disconnects
- Routing/fallback misbehavior
- Capacity/fleet shortages
- Control plane outages
- Auth/RBAC failures
- Telemetry/metrics blind spots
- Private deployment connectivity/config issues

**Out of scope on this page:** deep root-cause analysis methodology (handled in postmortems).

---

## Key concepts and definitions
### SLO vs. error budget
- **SLO (Service Level Objective):** a reliability target expressed as an objective (e.g., “99.9% request success per month”).
- **Error budget:** the allowed amount of unreliability in a window (commonly 30 days) implied by the SLO.

### Incident vs. SLO violation
- **Incident:** an event handled through the incident process (IC, comms, mitigation, timeline) and classified with a required `incident_type` + optional `subtype`.
- **SLO violation (budget burn):** measured degradation against the SLO’s metric(s).

Not every incident burns error budget (e.g., a control plane incident affecting config writes only), and not every budget burn results in a formally declared incident (e.g., short-lived blips caught and mitigated by automation). The taxonomy is still required for declared incidents; budget analysis uses both incident records and SLO metric math.

### Customer tiers
For this document we use:
- **Hosted:** shared multi-tenant API
- **Dedicated:** reserved capacity / isolated fleet
- **Private:** VPC and/or on-prem deployment package

Tier matters for:
- which SLOs are “primary” in reporting,
- how impact is attributed (shared vs customer-scoped), and
- what gets escalated and communicated.

---

## Our current “primary” SLO families (for incident mapping)
This page doesn’t define the exact numerical targets for every endpoint/model. Instead, it describes which SLO family an incident burns.

We use these families consistently in dashboards and error-budget reviews:

1) **Availability SLOs (request success):**
   - Typically measured as successful requests / total requests (or 5xx rate) for inference endpoints.
   - Most direct customer impact during outages, overload, and certain auth/routing failures.

2) **Latency SLOs (end-to-end and/or TTFT):**
   - Measured as p95/p99 latency for key routes.
   - For streaming, we track both **TTFT (time-to-first-token)** and end-to-end completion latency (where applicable).

3) **Streaming health SLOs (stream continuity):**
   - Measured as rate of unexpected stream resets / disconnects, and/or % of streams completing successfully.

4) **Control plane SLOs (config reads/writes):**
   - Applies to routing policy updates, model pinning, capacity settings, and similar operations.
   - Often impacts enterprise workflows without immediately breaking inference, but may lead to inference impact if mitigations depend on control plane operations.

5) **Telemetry coverage SLOs (observability integrity):**
   - Internal SLO family used to prevent “flying blind.”
   - These incidents may not burn customer-facing budgets but do burn an internal error budget and trigger mandatory follow-ups.

---

## Mapping: incident taxonomy → SLO family → error budget bucket
This table defines the standard mapping used by incident-bot, dashboards, and SLO review reporting.

Notes:
- The taxonomy has a **single primary incident_type** per incident. Contributing causes can be captured in postmortems (and optionally as contributing_systems), but reporting uses the primary type to keep metrics low-cardinality.
- When an incident impacts multiple SLO families, we still pick one primary `incident_type` (see “Multi-symptom incidents” below). The SLO math will reflect burn across multiple metrics, but “what to fix first” is driven by the primary classification.

| Taxonomy incident_type (v1) | Typical symptoms (examples) | Primary SLO family affected | Budget bucket for reporting | Common primary owners (for context) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Availability: 5xx/overload | 5xx spike, 429/overload errors, upstream timeouts, queue saturation | Availability SLO | Availability burn (Inference) | SRE / Fleet / Routing (varies by subtype) |
| Latency regression | p95/p99 latency increase, TTFT increase, timeout rate increase without 5xx | Latency SLO | Latency burn (Inference) | Serving Runtime / Routing / Fleet |
| Streaming disconnects | stream resets, client reconnect loops, elevated `stream_reset_rate` | Streaming health SLO | Streaming burn | Platform Edge/Gateway / Runtime / Network |
| Routing/fallback misbehavior | wrong model/region, fallback flapping, policy misapply, sticky routing failures | Availability or Latency (depending on impact) | Routing-induced burn | Smart Routing / Control Plane |
| Capacity/fleet shortage | GPU saturation, provisioning lag, insufficient warm capacity, regional scarcity | Availability or Latency (depending on impact) | Capacity-induced burn | Fleet/Infra |
| Control plane outage | config write failures, policy update failures, rollout orchestration failure | Control plane SLO (and secondary inference impact) | Control plane burn (Ops) | Platform Control Plane |
| Auth/RBAC failures | elevated 401/403, token validation errors, RBAC policy regression | Availability SLO (Auth-gated) | Auth-induced burn | Security/Platform Auth |
| Telemetry/metrics blind spot | missing metrics, tracing gaps, logging pipeline failure, cardinality blowups causing drops | Telemetry coverage SLO | Observability burn (Internal) | Observability |
| Private deployment connectivity/config | DNS/TLS errors, VPC routing issues, private gateway misconfig, upgrade-related connectivity regressions | Availability or Streaming (Private) | Private connectivity burn | Private Deployments / Network |

---

## Counting rules: how incidents contribute to error-budget analysis
### Source of truth
We use two sources and reconcile them in review:
1) **SLO math (metrics-based):** budget burn is computed from SLO definitions and the underlying metrics (authoritative for “how much budget burned”).
2) **Incident records (process-based):** incident taxonomy classification is authoritative for “why did we burn budget” and “who should prioritize work.”

### How we attribute budget burn to incidents
We attribute burn to an incident when all of the following are true:
- The incident has a defined **impact window** (start/end timestamps).
- The incident is classified with `incident_type` (and subtype where applicable).
- During the impact window, at least one relevant SLO metric crosses its objective (or contributes measurable error budget burn).

If the incident is real but the SLO metric cannot be evaluated (telemetry gaps), see “Telemetry gaps and unknown burn” below.

### Budget burn allocation when multiple incidents overlap
When incidents overlap in time:
- We do not attempt to perfectly allocate per-request burn across concurrent incidents in v1.
- Reporting uses a deterministic rule:
  1) if one incident is clearly causal (e.g., control plane outage triggers routing misbehavior), classify the causal incident as primary;
  2) otherwise, allocate burn to the incident with the highest severity and the longest overlap.

This rule is designed for stable reporting and prioritization; nuanced allocation belongs in postmortems.

### Incidents that do not burn customer-facing budgets
Some incidents may be “high-severity internal” but not burn customer-facing error budgets (examples):
- telemetry pipeline failure (burns internal observability budget)
- control plane config write failures affecting only admin operations (burns control plane budget)

These incidents still require taxonomy classification and are reviewed in reliability reporting.

---

## Multi-symptom incidents: choosing the primary incident_type
On-call frequently sees symptoms across categories (e.g., 5xx + latency + fallback).

**Rule of thumb (v1): classify by the earliest failing layer that drove customer impact.**

Decision guide:
1) If inference requests are failing (5xx/timeout at the gateway), choose **Availability: 5xx/overload** unless there is clear evidence the failures are secondary to auth.
2) If requests succeed but are slow, choose **Latency regression**.
3) If streaming sessions start but fail mid-stream, choose **Streaming disconnects**.
4) If the primary abnormality is routing decisions (wrong model/region or fallback flapping), choose **Routing/fallback misbehavior** even if it manifests as 5xx/latency.
5) If the system is healthy but there isn’t enough capacity and autoscaling/provisioning is the binding constraint, choose **Capacity/fleet shortage**.

If unsure during mitigation:
- choose the best primary type within 30 minutes (per incident policy),
- add a short note in the incident channel describing the ambiguity,
- update the type later once evidence is available.

---

## Telemetry gaps and “unknown burn” handling
Telemetry incidents are special because they can prevent SLO evaluation.

### If telemetry is broken but customer impact is suspected
- Classify the incident based on observed customer symptoms (Support signals, elevated retries, client errors, status page reports).
- Mark the incident as having **impaired measurement** in the postmortem.
- In the SLO review, we record burn as:
  - **Measured burn** (from metrics), plus
  - **Unmeasured suspected burn** (bounded estimate), when approved by SRE.

### If telemetry is broken and there is no customer impact
- Classify as **Telemetry/metrics blind spot**.
- This burns the internal observability error budget and triggers follow-ups (restore coverage + add safeguards).

---

## How taxonomy enables error-budget breakdown and prioritization
### Standard review questions we can now answer
With `incident_type` required and stable:
- What % of our error budget burn is driven by **capacity** vs **routing** vs **runtime regressions**?
- Which owners are associated with the largest share of **availability** or **latency** burn?
- Which incident types recur most frequently but burn little (high noise), vs burn a lot (high impact)?
- Are we improving in the categories we claimed were top drivers last quarter?

### Error-budget review outputs (monthly)
SRE publishes a monthly summary for each tier:
- Total burn by SLO family (availability/latency/streaming/control plane)
- Burn by `incident_type` and by owner team
- Top 3 drivers (by burn) and top 3 drivers (by incident count)
- Action plan: projects that reduce the dominant burn category

### Prioritization guidance
Use this rule set when deciding what to prioritize:
1) **Budget burn beats count:** prioritize types with highest burn even if incident count is low.
2) **Sev0/Sev1 repeats escalate:** repeated high severity in a category triggers an engineering priority even if measured burn is modest (because it indicates fragility).
3) **Dedicated and Private are customer-scoped:** prioritize fixes that remove repeated customer-scoped burn (even if global Hosted burn is low) when it affects committed enterprise accounts.
4) **Telemetry issues are multipliers:** prioritize telemetry incidents that prevent rapid mitigation or accurate SLO attribution.

---

## Worked examples
### Example A: Regional capacity shortage causing elevated 429s
- Symptoms: increased 429/overload errors in `us-east`, GPU saturation, provisioning lag.
- Classification: **Capacity/fleet shortage** (primary), subtype “regional capacity constraint.”
- SLO family: Availability SLO.
- Reporting bucket: Capacity-induced burn.
- Outcome: error budget burn is counted under availability, but *attributed* to capacity.

### Example B: Routing policy regression causes wrong-model fallback and latency spike
- Symptoms: correct route should be Model A; traffic shifts to Model B; p95 latency increases and some timeouts occur.
- Classification: **Routing/fallback misbehavior**.
- SLO family: Latency SLO (and possibly availability if timeouts cause failures).
- Reporting bucket: Routing-induced burn.

### Example C: Control plane config write outage (no immediate inference impact)
- Symptoms: customers cannot update routing policies; inference continues normally.
- Classification: **Control plane outage**.
- SLO family: Control plane SLO.
- Reporting bucket: Control plane burn (Ops).

---

## Implementation notes (tooling + guardrails)
### Required fields
Per incident classification policy, incident-bot requires:
- `incident_type` (required)
- `subtype` (required when the type defines subtypes)
- `tier` (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
- resolved owner team (auto-resolved from mapping; IC confirms)

### Cardinality constraints
To protect metrics systems:
- `incident_type` is an allowlisted enum (v1) with a small set of values.
- `subtype` is also allowlisted and reviewed before addition.
- We do **not** label high-volume request metrics with free-form incident identifiers.

### Where to find reporting
- Incident taxonomy dashboards: see “Incident taxonomy dashboards spec” (related page).
- Error budget math: computed by slo-toolkit; the taxonomy is joined in reporting pipelines via incident-bot records.

---

## Operational checklist for SLO review owners
When producing monthly/quarterly SLO + budget reviews:
1) Pull SLO burn totals by tier and SLO family.
2) Join incidents in the same period and verify classification coverage (% incidents classified, % with tier set).
3) Break down burn by `incident_type` and validate the top drivers against incident timelines.
4) Flag:
   - incidents with missing/late classification,
   - incidents with ambiguous primary type,
   - telemetry gaps preventing burn evaluation.
5) Publish a short prioritized list of remediation themes aligned to top burn categories.

---

## Open items / v1 limitations
- We do not yet have automated burn allocation for overlapping incidents beyond deterministic rules.
- Some incident types can legitimately burn multiple SLO families; we will revisit whether secondary “impact_family” should be captured as a low-cardinality field.
- Private tier SLO coverage is still being standardized across VPC vs on-prem; mapping rules apply, but SLO definitions may vary by customer contract.

For taxonomy changes, follow the review process described on the taxonomy v1 definitions page (related).

### If telemetry is broken and there is no customer impact
- Classify as **Telemetry/metrics blind spot**.
- This burns the internal observability error budget and triggers follow-ups (restore coverage + add safeguards).

---

## How taxonomy enables error-budget breakdown and prioritization
### Standard review questions we can now answer
With `incident_type` required and stable:
- What % of our error budget burn is driven by **capacity** vs **routing** vs **runtime regressions**?
- Which owners are associated with the largest share of **availability** or **latency** burn?
- Which incident types recur most frequently but burn little (high noise), vs burn a lot (high impact)?
- Are we improving in the categories we claimed were top drivers last quarter?

### Error-budget review outputs (monthly)
SRE publishes a monthly summary for each tier:
- Total burn by SLO family (availability/latency/streaming/control plane)
- Burn by `incident_type` and by owner team
- Top 3 drivers (by burn) and top 3 drivers (by incident count)
- Action plan: projects that reduce the dominant burn category

### Prioritization guidance
Use this rule set when deciding what to prioritize:
1) **Budget burn beats count:** prioritize types with highest burn even if incident count is low.
2) **Sev0/Sev1 repeats escalate:** repeated high severity in a category triggers an engineering priority even if measured burn is modest (because it indicates fragility).
3) **Dedicated and Private are customer-scoped:** prioritize fixes that remove repeated customer-scoped burn (even if global Hosted burn is low) when it affects committed enterprise accounts.
4) **Telemetry issues are multipliers:** prioritize telemetry incidents that prevent rapid mitigation or accurate SLO attribution.

---

## Worked examples
### Example A: Regional capacity shortage causing elevated 429s
- Symptoms: increased 429/overload errors in `us-east`, GPU saturation, provisioning lag.
- Classification: **Capacity/fleet shortage** (primary), subtype “regional capacity constraint.”
- SLO family: Availability SLO.
- Reporting bucket: Capacity-induced burn.
- Outcome: error budget burn is counted under availability, but *attributed* to capacity.

### Example B: Routing policy regression causes wrong-model fallback and latency spike
- Symptoms: correct route should be Model A; traffic shifts to Model B; p95 latency increases and some timeouts occur.
- Classification: **Routing/fallback misbehavior**.
- SLO family: Latency SLO (and possibly availability if timeouts cause failures).
- Reporting bucket: Routing-induced burn.

### Example C: Control plane config write outage (no immediate inference impact)
- Symptoms: customers cannot update routing policies; inference continues normally.
- Classification: **Control plane outage**.
- SLO family: Control plane SLO.
- Reporting bucket: Control plane burn (Ops).

---

## Implementation notes (tooling + guardrails)
### Required fields
Per incident classification policy, incident-bot requires:
- `incident_type` (required)
- `subtype` (required when the type defines subtypes)
- `tier` (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
- resolved owner team (auto-resolved from mapping; IC confirms)

### Cardinality constraints
To protect metrics systems:
- `incident_type` is an allowlisted enum (v1) with a small set of values.
- `subtype` is also allowlisted and reviewed before addition.
- We do **not** label high-volume request metrics with free-form incident identifiers.

### Where to find reporting
- Incident taxonomy dashboards: see “Incident taxonomy dashboards spec” (related page).
- Error budget math: computed by slo-toolkit; the taxonomy is joined in reporting pipelines via incident-bot records.

---

## Operational checklist for SLO review owners
When producing monthly/quarterly SLO + budget reviews:
1) Pull SLO burn totals by tier and SLO family.
2) Join incidents in the same period and verify classification coverage (% incidents classified, % with tier set).
3) Break down burn by `incident_type` and validate the top drivers against incident timelines.
4) Flag:
   - incidents with missing/late classification,
   - incidents with ambiguous primary type,
   - telemetry gaps preventing burn evaluation.
5) Publish a short prioritized list of remediation themes aligned to top burn categories.

---

## Open items / v1 limitations
- We do not yet have automated burn allocation for overlapping incidents beyond deterministic rules.
- Some incident types can legitimately burn multiple SLO families; we will revisit whether secondary “impact_family” should be captured as a low-cardinality field.
- Private tier SLO coverage is still being standardized across VPC vs on-prem; mapping rules apply, but SLO definitions may vary by customer contract.

For taxonomy changes, follow the review process described on the taxonomy v1 definitions page (related).

title: Error Budget Policy and Enforcement (Hosted vs Dedicated)
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s canonical **error budget policy** for tiered SLOs (Hosted and Dedicated) and how we **enforce** it via burn-rate alerting, incident thresholds, mitigation expectations, and release/deploy gates.

This policy exists to:
- Make SLO adherence measurable and comparable across teams and regions.
- Create predictable operational behavior during reliability regressions (latency spikes, 5xx bursts, stream disconnects).
- Align Product SLAs/expectations with engineering measurement and operational controls.

## Scope
**In scope (Q1 2026 program phase):**
- Tiers: **Hosted (shared)**, **Dedicated (reserved capacity)**
- Services/paths: API Gateway, Routing/Control Plane policy evaluation, model serving request path, telemetry needed to compute SLIs
- SLIs: success rate (non-5xx), latency percentiles, streaming reliability (disconnect/error events)

**Out of scope (referenced for future work):**
- Private deployments (VPC/on-prem) enforcement gates (we still recommend adopting the same SLI semantics).

## Definitions (canonical)
### Error budget
For a given SLO over a reporting window, the **error budget** is the allowed amount of “bad” events (or time) before an SLO is breached.

- For an **availability / success-rate SLO** (e.g., 99.9%):
  - Error budget fraction = (1 - SLO)
  - Example: SLO 99.9% → error budget = 0.1% of requests may be “bad” in the window.

- For a **latency SLO** (e.g., p95 < threshold):
  - Error budget is the allowed fraction of requests exceeding the threshold percentile SLO (as computed by the approved SLI formula).

- For **streaming reliability**:
  - Error budget is the allowed fraction of streams with server-side disconnect/error classifications (per the streaming SLI spec).

### Reporting windows
We use two windows for reliability management:
- **Primary window:** rolling **28 days** (standard SLO/error budget window)
- **Fast window (operational):** rolling **7 days** for early warning and quicker recovery expectations

Dashboards show both; policy enforcement keys off the 28-day budget unless explicitly stated.

### What counts against the budget
SLI semantics (inclusions/exclusions) are defined in: *SLI Specification: API Gateway and Routing*.

At a high level, budgets count:
- Server-side 5xx errors (gateway, routing, serving) for success-rate SLIs
- Latency as measured end-to-end at the defined measurement points
- Streaming disconnect/error events classified as server-initiated or server-fault

Budgets do **not** count (unless explicitly included by the SLI spec):
- Client aborts (e.g., client closes connection)
- Invalid requests (4xx validation errors)
- Rate-limited requests (429) — tracked separately as capacity signals
- Retries/fallback attempts are accounted for as defined in the SLI spec (avoid double counting)

### Burn rate
Burn rate is how fast we are consuming error budget relative to the allowed rate.

- Burn rate = (observed bad-event rate) / (allowed bad-event rate)
- Burn rate of **1.0** means we are spending budget exactly at the sustainable rate.
- Burn rate of **10** means we are spending budget 10× too fast and will breach quickly.

## Policy: states and thresholds
We operate in three states per **(tier, region, endpoint class)** SLO:

| State | Definition (28-day window unless noted) | Operational expectation |
| --- | --- | --- |
| GREEN | Budget remaining >= 50% and no active high burn alerts | Normal operations |
| YELLOW | Budget remaining 10–49% OR sustained moderate burn | Mitigate and de-risk releases |
| RED | Budget remaining < 10% OR active high burn OR SLO breach | Reliability-first; enforce gates and incident rules |

Notes:
- We track state at both **tier-level rollups** and **regional breakdowns**; gating uses the tier-level rollup with safeguards to avoid hiding regional burns.
- Dedicated accounts may have **account-level** SLO views. Enforcement is still tier-based unless an explicit account-specific contract addendum exists (handled via exception process).

## Burn-rate alerting (multi-window)
We use **multi-window burn-rate alerts** to catch both fast regressions and slow burns (ADR-0143).

### Standard burn-rate alert set
For each SLO, we configure burn-rate alerts at two severities:

**SEV2 (fast burn / imminent breach)**
- Condition: burn rate >= **14×** for **1h** AND >= **6×** for **6h**
- Interpretation: at this rate, a 28-day budget can be exhausted in ~2 days (or faster depending on thresholds).

**SEV3 (slow burn / needs attention)**
- Condition: burn rate >= **3×** for **6h** AND >= **1×** for **3d**
- Interpretation: not an immediate outage, but we will breach without mitigation.

### Paging and routing
- **SEV2** pages the owning on-call rotation (SRE primary + service owner) and posts to #eng-sre and the service channel.
- **SEV3** posts to Slack and creates an incident candidate; paging is optional based on time-of-day rules and recent history.

### Alert grouping rules
To avoid alert storms:
- Alerts group by: tier, region, endpoint class, and “dominant failure mode” (error vs latency vs stream)
- We do not page per model or per customer. Contributing dimensions are available via dashboard drill-down.

## When burn triggers an incident
We do not want every SLO alert to become a full incident, but we also need predictable escalation.

### Incident trigger rules
An incident **must** be opened when any of the following is true:
1) **SEV2 burn-rate alert** fires for a tier rollup (Hosted or Dedicated) and persists for **>15 minutes** after initial triage.
2) Any SLO enters **RED** due to budget remaining < 10% *and* the current burn rate is > 1× (i.e., still degrading).
3) Customer-impact signals indicate sustained impact (CS escalation, status page criteria met) even if burn-rate alert is suppressed (e.g., telemetry gaps).

Incident execution details live in: *Error Budget Breach Incident Process*.

### Required roles (minimum)
- Incident Commander (IC)
- Comms Lead (if customer-facing impact)
- Scribe

## Mitigation expectations (what “good” looks like)
When a tier is **YELLOW or RED**, the default posture is **mitigation-first**.

### Within 30 minutes of SEV2
The on-call team should:
1) Confirm SLI validity (data quality, tagging, sampling) and identify dominant failure mode.
2) Determine blast radius (tier, region(s), endpoint class, top contributing routes/models).
3) Apply first-line mitigations (see below) and decide whether to roll back recent changes.
4) Decide on customer comms threshold and engage CS as needed.

### First-line mitigations (ordered)
Depending on failure mode:
- **Rollback / disable recent rollout** (preferred when a clear change correlates with burn)
- **Traffic shaping / overload shedding**
  - protect Dedicated capacity first when Dedicated SLOs are at risk
  - enforce per-tenant limits and drop/queue best-effort Hosted traffic where appropriate
- **Routing policy adjustments**
  - shift traffic to healthier region(s) when policy allows
  - adjust fallback to compatible model variants (ensure SLI accounting remains correct)
- **Capacity actions** (in partnership with Infra)
  - add headroom to affected pools
  - shift capacity from non-critical workloads
- **Degrade non-critical features**
  - disable expensive inference options, lower max tokens, reduce parallel tool calls
  - note: any change must be evaluated for correctness and communicated if it changes product behavior

For step-by-step actions, use: *Error Budget Breach Mitigation Runbook* and *Capacity Mitigation Guidelines for SLO Events*.

## Release freeze and deploy gate policy
### Philosophy
If a tier is burning budget, shipping more changes increases risk. Our policy is to **slow down change** when reliability is degraded while keeping an explicit path for emergency fixes.

### Gates (current enforcement)
We support three phases:
1) **Warn-only**: CI/CD posts warnings and requires an acknowledgement for high-risk deploys.
2) **Soft gate**: blocks “non-critical” deploys unless a mitigation checkbox + on-call approval is attached.
3) **Hard gate**: blocks all non-emergency deploys when a tier is in RED or budget exhausted.

As of **2026-02-21**, default posture:
- Hosted: Soft gate in YELLOW; Hard gate in RED
- Dedicated: Hard gate in RED; Soft gate in YELLOW for control plane/routing/serving changes

(Exact implementation follows the deploy-gate design and the release gate owner’s config.)

### What is blocked
Unless approved as emergency:
- New feature rollouts affecting request path (gateway, routing, serving)
- Kernel/runtime upgrades, model version changes, quantization profile changes
- Policy changes that increase load (batching config, concurrency limits)

### What is allowed during freeze
Allowed changes include:
- Rollbacks and mitigations
- Observability fixes required to restore SLI correctness
- Emergency fixes that reduce customer impact

### Emergency deploy process
To perform an emergency deploy during a hard gate:
1) Create an emergency deploy entry (ticket/template in the release system) including:
   - impacted tier(s)/region(s)
   - hypothesis and rollback plan
   - expected risk
2) Get approval from:
   - On-call IC (if incident open) OR SRE primary
   - Owning EM (or delegate) for the service
3) Execute deploy with enhanced monitoring; annotate dashboards.
4) Document outcome in the incident timeline.

All emergency overrides are logged and reviewed weekly by SRE leadership.

## Escalation ladder
Escalation is based on tier and burn severity.

### SEV2 burn (or RED state)
- Primary: Service owner on-call + SRE primary
- Engage within 30 minutes:
  - Routing owner (Mina Haddad) if routing/fallback involved
  - Control plane owner (Ethan Park) if policy eval or config pushes involved
  - Telemetry owner (Logan Wright) if data quality/SLI validity concerns
  - Infra on-call if capacity headroom is implicated
- Escalate to SRE Director (Sean Gallagher) if:
  - mitigation is unclear after 60 minutes
  - burn persists > 2 hours
  - comms/SLA risk for Dedicated

### Dedicated-specific escalations
When Dedicated is impacted (or at risk):
- Notify Dedicated PM (Lauren Bishop) and CS escalation owner (Rachel Kim)
- Prefer capacity preservation for Dedicated pools when policy allows
- Follow tiered comms guidance; avoid mixing Hosted and Dedicated messaging unless necessary

## Exception process
Exceptions are sometimes necessary (e.g., regulatory deadlines, urgent customer commitments, or infrastructure migrations). Exceptions must be explicit and time-bound.

### What can be excepted
- Release gates (temporary override)
- SLO evaluation windows (rare; typically only when telemetry is demonstrably wrong)
- SLI exclusions (only via SLI spec change + review)

### What cannot be excepted
- Incident communication requirements during customer-impacting events
- Post-incident review and follow-up requirements for material breaches

### Request procedure
Submit an exception request with:
- Tier(s), region(s), endpoint class
- Requested exception type and duration (max 7 days without re-approval)
- Risk assessment + mitigation plan
- Rollback plan
- Owner accountable for follow-up

Approvals required:
- SRE delivery lead (Rafael Mendes) or delegate
- Relevant product owner (Nikhil Sharma for Hosted, Lauren Bishop for Dedicated) when customer expectations/SLA may be impacted
- VP Infra & Reliability (Neha Kapoor) for high-risk exceptions (Dedicated RED, or repeated exceptions)

All approved exceptions must be:
- Logged in the exception register
- Linked from the incident or change request
- Reviewed in the weekly reliability review

## Post-breach requirements
If an SLO is breached (budget exhausted) OR if SEV2 burn occurs, the owning teams must:
- Produce a post-incident write-up (or attach to existing incident) within **5 business days**
- Include:
  - what failed (technical and process)
  - customer impact
  - why mitigations did/did not work
  - follow-ups with owners and dates
- Track follow-ups until closure; repeated breaches trigger an exec review.

## Reporting and review cadence
- Weekly: Reliability review (SRE + Platform + Infra + PMs)
  - top budget spenders by tier/region
  - chronic slow burns
  - exception review
- Monthly: SLO target review with Product
  - evaluate whether targets are realistic and aligned with SLAs
  - confirm measurement integrity

## Common scenarios (guidance)
### Scenario A: Hosted latency regression after a deploy
- If SEV2 burn: rollback first unless disproven.
- Enter soft/hard gate based on state; freeze additional non-mitigation releases to request path.
- Document contributing factor and add regression tests/evals if needed.

### Scenario B: Dedicated customer sees elevated 5xx in EU only
- Treat as Dedicated impact; prioritize mitigation and capacity in affected region.
- Avoid global policy changes that harm other regions without clear benefit.
- Ensure comms are tier-specific and provide timelines/updates per comms policy.

### Scenario C: Streaming disconnect spike with unclear attribution
- Validate streaming classification (client abort vs server fault).
- If telemetry is wrong, open a telemetry incident and apply a time-bound exception only if approved.
- Do not “exclude” by default; fix classification.

## Ownership
- Policy owner: **SRE** (Rafael Mendes, Aisha Bello)
- Enforcement owners:
  - Deploy gates: Connor O'Brien, Sahana Iyer
  - Tier tagging/measurement integrity: Allison Grant, Logan Wright
  - Routing/fallback behaviors: Mina Haddad, Ravi Menon
  - Capacity actions: Infra on-call + Neha Kapoor’s org

## Change log
- 2026-02-21: Clarified gate behavior by tier and added explicit incident trigger persistence window (15 minutes).
- 2026-02-14: Updated burn-rate thresholds to standard 14x/6x and 3x/1x multi-window pairs; aligned paging rules.
- 2026-02-08: Initial publication aligned to Tiered SLO program Q1 2026.
