title: SLO Binding Contract and Proactive Capacity Heuristics for Inference Services
preview:
Overview

This page defines the standardized SLO Binding Contract between service/product teams and SRE for model-inference services, and documents a practical set of proactive capacity heuristics SRE should apply during onboarding, steady-state operations, and incident remediation. The intent is to make the negotiation explicit, reduce revision cycles, and provide reproducible capacity calculations for inference workloads (generation, chat, embeddings, reranking).

Why this exists

- Inference workloads have multi-dimensional resource patterns (tokens/sec, sequence length, KV cache longevity, batchability). Generic capacity rules lead to overprovisioning or SLO regressions.
- We need a reproducible contract so product teams understand the operational impact of SLOs (cost + availability) and SRE has a clear set of inputs for capacity planning and runbook actions.

Scope

- Applies to all customer-facing inference services deployed on Redwood Hosted and Dedicated clusters.
- Covers SLO definition, error budget policy, capacity heuristics, monitoring queries, admission checklist, and runbook steps for capacity shortfalls.

1) SLO Binding Contract (template)

Summary: the SLO Binding is a short, signed agreement captured in the service onboarding ticket that contains: service name, primary SLIs, target SLOs, error budget cadence, initial capacity commitment, escalation path, and rollback conditions.

Table: Contract fields (use this as the canonical onboarding snippet)

| Field | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| service_name | Human and infra identifier | search-api-v2 |
| owner | Product/Service owner and SRE steward | search-pm@redwood / sre-search@redwood |
| primary_SLIs | Prometheus/trace level SLIs used to measure the SLO | p95_request_latency_ms, success_rate_5m |
| target_SLOs | Clear numeric goals and window | p95(latency) < 300ms (30d), success_rate >= 99.9% (30d) |
| error_budget_policy | Burn handling and recovery cadence | 5% monthly. Auto-throttle after 2x burn in 1h. Escalate at 4x monthly burn. |
| initial_capacity | Minimum serving units committed at launch | 12 GPU-inference-pods + warm pool 50 concurrent sessions |
| capacity_margin | Safety multiplier for headroom | 1.35 (35% headroom) |
| quantization_profile | Allowed model variants and perf tradeoffs | int8_q + fallback fp16 |
| warm_pool_policy | Warm pool sizing and TTL | min_warm=6, scale_up_threshold=80% utilization, ttl=600s |
| rollout_policy | Canary and fallback instructions | 5% traffic canary for 24h, rollback if p95 > 200ms or errors +0.2% |
| monitoring_hooks | Dashboards and alert names | slo_search_p95, cap_headroom_pct |
| acceptance_criteria | Readiness checks to mark onboarding complete | canary stable 24h, cost forecast within 20% of estimate |

Contract enforcement checklist (short):

1. Owner and SRE steward listed in ticket.
2. SLIs instrumented and dashboards created.
3. Canary traffic configured with automated rollback.
4. Capacity allocated (pods/GPUs) with warm pool configured.
5. Cost forecast and approval recorded.

2) SLI and SLO definitions (example for inference)

- success_rate (S): fraction of inference requests with model-returned response and 2xx status within 10s. Window: 30d.
- latency_p95 (L95): 95th percentile tail of response time measured at front-door (ms). Window: 30d.
- token_throughput (T): tokens/sec delivered aggregated per deployment. Used for capacity planning. Window: 1d.

Example SLO set:
- SLO-1: success_rate >= 99.9% (30d)
- SLO-2: latency_p95 < 300ms (30d)
- SLO-3 (optional): median(token_latency_per_token) < 8ms/token for long-form generation customers (30d)

3) Error budget policy and automated responses

Policy summary:
- Error budget is tracked on a 30d rolling window.
- Automated cap/traffic shaping triggers at these thresholds:
  - Green: burn_rate < 0.5x expected -> no action.
  - Watch: 0.5x <= burn_rate < 1.5x -> SRE on-call alerted; immediate diagnostics required.
  - Throttle: 1.5x <= burn_rate < 3x -> begin automated priority-based throttling and queue shedding.
  - Emergency: burn_rate >= 3x -> escalate to engineering and trigger emergency capacity add or fallbacks.

Automated actions (examples):
- 1st minute: capture full traces for all 5xx and high-latency requests and snapshot headroom metrics.
- 5th minute: scale warm pool +15% if headroom_pct < 20% and GPU utilization < 85%.
- 10th minute: enable model-variant fallback (quantized path) for lower latency if request quality tolerates.
- 30th minute: enable tenant-level throttles following priority ledger and notify product owners.

4) Proactive capacity heuristics (how to calculate initial and steady-state capacity)

Heuristic goals: convert business-level traffic forecasts into GPU units and warm pool configuration, accounting for per-request token distribution and cache hit rates. We provide a conservative baseline formula and worked example.

Inputs required from product team:
- expected_peak_rps (requests/sec) and expected_avg_tokens_per_request
- expected_sequence_length_percentiles (p50/p90/p95)
- model_variant_profile: tokens/sec per GPU (observed with identical seq distribution)
- kv_cache_hit_rate (0-1)
- acceptable_headroom_pct (default 35%)

Baseline capacity formula:
1. Estimate tokens/sec = expected_peak_rps * expected_avg_tokens_per_request
2. Effective_tokens/sec = tokens/sec * (1 - kv_cache_hit_rate)
3. base_gpus = ceil( Effective_tokens/sec / model_tokens_per_gpu )
4. committed_gpus = ceil( base_gpus * (1 + acceptable_headroom_pct) )

Worked example
- expected_peak_rps = 120 rps
- expected_avg_tokens = 40 tokens/request -> tokens/sec = 4800
- kv_cache_hit_rate = 0.25 -> effective_tokens/sec = 3600
- model_tokens_per_gpu (int8 variant) = 900 tokens/sec/GPU
- base_gpus = ceil(3600 / 900) = 4
- acceptable_headroom_pct = 0.35 -> committed_gpus = ceil(4 * 1.35) = 6 GPUs
- warm_pool target concurrent sessions = max(6, ceil( expected_peak_rps * 0.2 )) = max(6, 24) => warm_pool=24 sessions

Notes and caveats:
- For chat/generation with long KV lifetime, kv_cache_hit_rate may be >0.5 which reduces steady-state capacity but increases warm-pool and memory needs.
- Model_tokens_per_gpu must come from benchmarking suite (link to /eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite). Use the variant that will be served in production.
- If tail-latency SLO is tight (p95 << model native latency at full batch), favor higher headroom or smaller batch sizes.

5) Monitoring primitives and example queries

Core metrics to instrument and dashboards to create before marking onboarding complete:
- slo_success_rate_30d (counter conversion)
- frontend_request_latency_ms (summary/histogram)
- model_gpu_utilization_pct (gauge)
- warm_pool_active_sessions (gauge)
- headroom_pct = (available_capacity / committed_capacity) *100

Prometheus example queries (adapt to local metric names)
- p95 latency: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(frontend_request_latency_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)) * 1000
- success rate (5m): (sum(rate(requests_total{code=~"2.."}[5m])) / sum(rate(requests_total[5m]))) * 100
- headroom pct: (1 - (sum(model_gpu_utilization_pct) / count(committed_gpus))) * 100

Alerting guideline (examples)
- slo_watch_alert: when p95 latency > 0.9 * SLO_threshold for 10m AND error_budget_burn_rate > 0.5 -> P1 alert to SRE on-call
- capacity_pressure_alert: headroom_pct < 20% for 5m -> automated warm-pool scale attempt, notify on-call
- burn_emergency_alert: error_budget_burn_rate > 3 for 1h -> page SRE and engineering lead

6) Runbook: capacity shortfall triage (step-by-step)

Follow these steps when a capacity_pressure_alert or burn_emergency_alert fires. Assume SRE on-call owns execution until handoff.

1. Triage (0-5m)
  a. Confirm alert details, scope (region, cluster, tenant).
  b. Collect high-level telemetry: p95 latency trend, 5xx rate, model GPU utilization, warm_pool_active_sessions, pending_queue_length.
  c. Fork investigation lanes: traffic anomaly vs model regression vs infra capacity.

2. Immediate mitigation (5-15m)
  a. If GPU utilization < 85% but warm_pool exhausted: increase warm_pool by +20% (CLI below).
     - CLI: rk infra warm-pool resize --service search-api-v2 --region us-west-2 --delta +20%
  b. If GPU utilization > 90% and committed_gpus < soft_limit: trigger horizontal scale (add N GPUs). Follow capacity approval policy in onboarding.
     - CLI: rk infra scale --service search-api-v2 --region us-west-2 --add-gpus 2 --reason "slo emergency"
  c. If errors spike without utilization increase: revert recent model rollout or disable model-variant change.

3. Stabilize (15-60m)
  a. Enable quantified fallbacks: lower-precision quantized model variant, reduced max_tokens per request cap, or tenant throttles.
  b. Monitor SLO and error budget burn rate closely every 5 minutes.
  c. If mitigation fails within 30m, escalate to on-call SRE manager + product owner.

4. Post-incident (after stabilization)
  a. File incident ticket and run standardized incident review template (link below).
  b. Compute postmortem metrics: time-to-detect, time-to-mitigate, total error budget consumed, cost of emergency scale.
  c. Update contract and capacity heuristics if root cause suggests underestimated inputs.

7) Operational tooling and automation recommendations

- Admission task template: build a GitHub checklist that enforces contract fields and links to dashboards.
- Autoscaling policy composer: use historical token profile to generate autoscale rules and warm-pool policies. Store generated policy in the onboarding ticket for review.
- Error-budget controller: low-risk automated throttles should be implemented as jobs with safe-undo and audit logs.
- Canary grade checker: a small service that asserts canary metrics vs baseline and writes status to the onboarding ticket.

8) Incident review linkage and continuous improvement

- Every capacity incident that consumes >30% of monthly error budget must run a 2-week remediation window: measures may include increasing committed capacity, changing quantization profile, or adding caching improvements.
- Incident review should explicitly decide whether the SLO Binding Contract needs update and record the delta in the onboarding ticket.

9) Ownership matrix and escalation contacts

| Role | Responsibility | Escalation contact |
|---|---|---|
| Product Owner | sets product SLO goals and acceptance criteria | product-lead@redwood |
| SRE Steward | enforces contract, runs capacity triage | sre-search@redwood |
| Infra (capacity ops) | executes scaling and warm pool ops | infra-ops@redwood |
| Observability | configures dashboards and alerts | observability@redwood |

10) Onboarding checklist (go/no-go)

- [ ] Contract fields completed and signed by Product and SRE steward
- [ ] Benchmarks present: model_tokens_per_gpu measured for intended variant
- [ ] Prometheus & tracing SLIs instrumented and dashboard created
- [ ] Canary configured with automated rollback rules
- [ ] Committed capacity provisioned and warm pool sized
- [ ] Cost forecast reviewed and approved
- [ ] Runbook assigned and linked to the service ticket

Appendices

A. Quick CLI snippets
- Resize warm pool:
  - rk infra warm-pool resize --service <service> --region <r> --size 24
- Scale GPUs immediately (requires infra ops approval):
  - rk infra scale --service <service> --region <r> --add-gpus 2 --reason "emergency-scale"

B. Reference dashboards and docs
- Onboarding template: /eng-sre/reliability-initiatives/onboarding-templates
- Benchmarking suite: /eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite
- Incident review template: /eng-sre/incident-review/postmortem-template

C. Change log
- 2024-11-12: Initial draft and pilot with search-api-v2.
- 2025-04-08: Added headroom_pct formula and warm-pool sizing guidance.
- 2026-02-15: Expanded automated actions, clarified error budget multipliers, and added CLI snippets.

If you are onboarding a new inference service or updating an existing one, copy the contract table into the onboarding ticket and populate the inputs in section 4 before requesting SRE review. For questions or to request a capacity calibration session, contact sre-search@redwood.
text:
Overview

This page defines the standardized SLO Binding Contract between service/product teams and SRE for model-inference services, and documents a practical set of proactive capacity heuristics SRE should apply during onboarding, steady-state operations, and incident remediation. The intent is to make the negotiation explicit, reduce revision cycles, and provide reproducible capacity calculations for inference workloads (generation, chat, embeddings, reranking).

Why this exists

- Inference workloads have multi-dimensional resource patterns (tokens/sec, sequence length, KV cache longevity, batchability). Generic capacity rules lead to overprovisioning or SLO regressions.
- We need a reproducible contract so product teams understand the operational impact of SLOs (cost + availability) and SRE has a clear set of inputs for capacity planning and runbook actions.

Scope

- Applies to all customer-facing inference services deployed on Redwood Hosted and Dedicated clusters.
- Covers SLO definition, error budget policy, capacity heuristics, monitoring queries, admission checklist, and runbook steps for capacity shortfalls.

1) SLO Binding Contract (template)

Summary: the SLO Binding is a short, signed agreement captured in the service onboarding ticket that contains: service name, primary SLIs, target SLOs, error budget cadence, initial capacity commitment, escalation path, and rollback conditions.

Table: Contract fields (use this as the canonical onboarding snippet)

| Field | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| service_name | Human and infra identifier | search-api-v2 |
| owner | Product/Service owner and SRE steward | search-pm@redwood / sre-search@redwood |
| primary_SLIs | Prometheus/trace level SLIs used to measure the SLO | p95_request_latency_ms, success_rate_5m |
| target_SLOs | Clear numeric goals and window | p95(latency) < 300ms (30d), success_rate >= 99.9% (30d) |
| error_budget_policy | Burn handling and recovery cadence | 5% monthly. Auto-throttle after 2x burn in 1h. Escalate at 4x monthly burn. |
| initial_capacity | Minimum serving units committed at launch | 12 GPU-inference-pods + warm pool 50 concurrent sessions |
| capacity_margin | Safety multiplier for headroom | 1.35 (35% headroom) |
| quantization_profile | Allowed model variants and perf tradeoffs | int8_q + fallback fp16 |
| warm_pool_policy | Warm pool sizing and TTL | min_warm=6, scale_up_threshold=80% utilization, ttl=600s |
| rollout_policy | Canary and fallback instructions | 5% traffic canary for 24h, rollback if p95 > 200ms or errors +0.2% |
| monitoring_hooks | Dashboards and alert names | slo_search_p95, cap_headroom_pct |
| acceptance_criteria | Readiness checks to mark onboarding complete | canary stable 24h, cost forecast within 20% of estimate |

Contract enforcement checklist (short):

1. Owner and SRE steward listed in ticket.
2. SLIs instrumented and dashboards created.
3. Canary traffic configured with automated rollback.
4. Capacity allocated (pods/GPUs) with warm pool configured.
5. Cost forecast and approval recorded.

2) SLI and SLO definitions (example for inference)

- success_rate (S): fraction of inference requests with model-returned response and 2xx status within 10s. Window: 30d.
- latency_p95 (L95): 95th percentile tail of response time measured at front-door (ms). Window: 30d.
- token_throughput (T): tokens/sec delivered aggregated per deployment. Used for capacity planning. Window: 1d.

Example SLO set:
- SLO-1: success_rate >= 99.9% (30d)
- SLO-2: latency_p95 < 300ms (30d)
- SLO-3 (optional): median(token_latency_per_token) < 8ms/token for long-form generation customers (30d)

3) Error budget policy and automated responses

Policy summary:
- Error budget is tracked on a 30d rolling window.
- Automated cap/traffic shaping triggers at these thresholds:
  - Green: burn_rate < 0.5x expected -> no action.
  - Watch: 0.5x <= burn_rate < 1.5x -> SRE on-call alerted; immediate diagnostics required.
  - Throttle: 1.5x <= burn_rate < 3x -> begin automated priority-based throttling and queue shedding.
  - Emergency: burn_rate >= 3x -> escalate to engineering and trigger emergency capacity add or fallbacks.

Automated actions (examples):
- 1st minute: capture full traces for all 5xx and high-latency requests and snapshot headroom metrics.
- 5th minute: scale warm pool +15% if headroom_pct < 20% and GPU utilization < 85%.
- 10th minute: enable model-variant fallback (quantized path) for lower latency if request quality tolerates.
- 30th minute: enable tenant-level throttles following priority ledger and notify product owners.

4) Proactive capacity heuristics (how to calculate initial and steady-state capacity)

Heuristic goals: convert business-level traffic forecasts into GPU units and warm pool configuration, accounting for per-request token distribution and cache hit rates. We provide a conservative baseline formula and worked example.

Inputs required from product team:
- expected_peak_rps (requests/sec) and expected_avg_tokens_per_request
- expected_sequence_length_percentiles (p50/p90/p95)
- model_variant_profile: tokens/sec per GPU (observed with identical seq distribution)
- kv_cache_hit_rate (0-1)
- acceptable_headroom_pct (default 35%)

Baseline capacity formula:
1. Estimate tokens/sec = expected_peak_rps * expected_avg_tokens_per_request
2. Effective_tokens/sec = tokens/sec * (1 - kv_cache_hit_rate)
3. base_gpus = ceil( Effective_tokens/sec / model_tokens_per_gpu )
4. committed_gpus = ceil( base_gpus * (1 + acceptable_headroom_pct) )

Worked example
- expected_peak_rps = 120 rps
- expected_avg_tokens = 40 tokens/request -> tokens/sec = 4800
- kv_cache_hit_rate = 0.25 -> effective_tokens/sec = 3600
- model_tokens_per_gpu (int8 variant) = 900 tokens/sec/GPU
- base_gpus = ceil(3600 / 900) = 4
- acceptable_headroom_pct = 0.35 -> committed_gpus = ceil(4 * 1.35) = 6 GPUs
- warm_pool target concurrent sessions = max(6, ceil( expected_peak_rps * 0.2 )) = max(6, 24) => warm_pool=24 sessions

Notes and caveats:
- For chat/generation with long KV lifetime, kv_cache_hit_rate may be >0.5 which reduces steady-state capacity but increases warm-pool and memory needs.
- Model_tokens_per_gpu must come from benchmarking suite (link to /eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite). Use the variant that will be served in production.
- If tail-latency SLO is tight (p95 << model native latency at full batch), favor higher headroom or smaller batch sizes.

5) Monitoring primitives and example queries

Core metrics to instrument and dashboards to create before marking onboarding complete:
- slo_success_rate_30d (counter conversion)
- frontend_request_latency_ms (summary/histogram)
- model_gpu_utilization_pct (gauge)
- warm_pool_active_sessions (gauge)
- headroom_pct = (available_capacity / committed_capacity) *100

Prometheus example queries (adapt to local metric names)
- p95 latency: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(frontend_request_latency_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)) * 1000
- success rate (5m): (sum(rate(requests_total{code=~"2.."}[5m])) / sum(rate(requests_total[5m]))) * 100
- headroom pct: (1 - (sum(model_gpu_utilization_pct) / count(committed_gpus))) * 100

Alerting guideline (examples)
- slo_watch_alert: when p95 latency > 0.9 * SLO_threshold for 10m AND error_budget_burn_rate > 0.5 -> P1 alert to SRE on-call
- capacity_pressure_alert: headroom_pct < 20% for 5m -> automated warm-pool scale attempt, notify on-call
- burn_emergency_alert: error_budget_burn_rate > 3 for 1h -> page SRE and engineering lead

6) Runbook: capacity shortfall triage (step-by-step)

Follow these steps when a capacity_pressure_alert or burn_emergency_alert fires. Assume SRE on-call owns execution until handoff.

1. Triage (0-5m)
  a. Confirm alert details, scope (region, cluster, tenant).
  b. Collect high-level telemetry: p95 latency trend, 5xx rate, model GPU utilization, warm_pool_active_sessions, pending_queue_length.
  c. Fork investigation lanes: traffic anomaly vs model regression vs infra capacity.

2. Immediate mitigation (5-15m)
  a. If GPU utilization < 85% but warm_pool exhausted: increase warm_pool by +20% (CLI below).
     - CLI: rk infra warm-pool resize --service search-api-v2 --region us-west-2 --delta +20%
  b. If GPU utilization > 90% and committed_gpus < soft_limit: trigger horizontal scale (add N GPUs). Follow capacity approval policy in onboarding.
     - CLI: rk infra scale --service search-api-v2 --region us-west-2 --add-gpus 2 --reason "slo emergency"
  c. If errors spike without utilization increase: revert recent model rollout or disable model-variant change.

3. Stabilize (15-60m)
  a. Enable quantified fallbacks: lower-precision quantized model variant, reduced max_tokens per request cap, or tenant throttles.
  b. Monitor SLO and error budget burn rate closely every 5 minutes.
  c. If mitigation fails within 30m, escalate to on-call SRE manager + product owner.

4. Post-incident (after stabilization)
  a. File incident ticket and run standardized incident review template (link below).
  b. Compute postmortem metrics: time-to-detect, time-to-mitigate, total error budget consumed, cost of emergency scale.
  c. Update contract and capacity heuristics if root cause suggests underestimated inputs.

7) Operational tooling and automation recommendations

- Admission task template: build a GitHub checklist that enforces contract fields and links to dashboards.
- Autoscaling policy composer: use historical token profile to generate autoscale rules and warm-pool policies. Store generated policy in the onboarding ticket for review.
- Error-budget controller: low-risk automated throttles should be implemented as jobs with safe-undo and audit logs.
- Canary grade checker: a small service that asserts canary metrics vs baseline and writes status to the onboarding ticket.

8) Incident review linkage and continuous improvement

- Every capacity incident that consumes >30% of monthly error budget must run a 2-week remediation window: measures may include increasing committed capacity, changing quantization profile, or adding caching improvements.
- Incident review should explicitly decide whether the SLO Binding Contract needs update and record the delta in the onboarding ticket.

9) Ownership matrix and escalation contacts

| Role | Responsibility | Escalation contact |
|---|---|---|
| Product Owner | sets product SLO goals and acceptance criteria | product-lead@redwood |
| SRE Steward | enforces contract, runs capacity triage | sre-search@redwood |
| Infra (capacity ops) | executes scaling and warm pool ops | infra-ops@redwood |
| Observability | configures dashboards and alerts | observability@redwood |

10) Onboarding checklist (go/no-go)

- [ ] Contract fields completed and signed by Product and SRE steward
- [ ] Benchmarks present: model_tokens_per_gpu measured for intended variant
- [ ] Prometheus & tracing SLIs instrumented and dashboard created
- [ ] Canary configured with automated rollback rules
- [ ] Committed capacity provisioned and warm pool sized
- [ ] Cost forecast reviewed and approved
- [ ] Runbook assigned and linked to the service ticket

Appendices

A. Quick CLI snippets
- Resize warm pool:
  - rk infra warm-pool resize --service <service> --region <r> --size 24
- Scale GPUs immediately (requires infra ops approval):
  - rk infra scale --service <service> --region <r> --add-gpus 2 --reason "emergency-scale"

B. Reference dashboards and docs
- Onboarding template: /eng-sre/reliability-initiatives/onboarding-templates
- Benchmarking suite: /eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite
- Incident review template: /eng-sre/incident-review/postmortem-template

C. Change log
- 2024-11-12: Initial draft and pilot with search-api-v2.
- 2025-04-08: Added headroom_pct formula and warm-pool sizing guidance.
- 2026-02-15: Expanded automated actions, clarified error budget multipliers, and added CLI snippets.

If you are onboarding a new inference service or updating an existing one, copy the contract table into the onboarding ticket and populate the inputs in section 4 before requesting SRE review. For questions or to request a capacity calibration session, contact sre-search@redwood.

title: Support SLA vs Engineering SLO Mapping (Paging Thresholds + Customer Impact)
preview:
## Purpose
This page is the reference for how Support SLAs (first response + update cadence) should map to Engineering SLOs (availability/latency) and how paging thresholds should relate to *customer impact*.

This is **not** a contractual SLA document. For contract language and tier-specific coverage windows, refer to: **Customer Success & Support → SLA policy by customer tier**.

**Goals**
- Prevent mismatches where Support promises a cadence that Engineering on-call cannot meet.
- Ensure paging triggers correlate to real customer impact (not only internal “red” alerts).
- Standardize language between Support (Jira priorities P0–P4) and Engineering incident severity (Sev0–Sev3).

---

## Definitions (shared language)
**Support SLA (operational):** Response/update targets used by Support/CS for incident comms and ticket handling. These are constrained by coverage windows and the current incident process.

**Engineering SLO:** Reliability objectives for production services (e.g., request success rate, latency). SLOs drive error budgets and alerting policies.

**Customer impact:** A combination of (a) number of customers affected, (b) tier (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private; Enterprise vs self-serve), and (c) degradation type (hard outage vs partial degradation vs “sharp edges”).

**Burn rate alerts:** Alerts that fire when error budget is being consumed quickly (fast burn) or steadily (slow burn). Burn rate is a more reliable paging signal than single-point thresholds.

**Time-to-triage (TTT):** Time from Support intake to a qualified owner engaged with enough context to act.

**Time-to-mitigation (TTM):** Time from detection/escalation to a customer-visible mitigation (rollback, routing change, scaling, workaround, etc.).

---

## Engineering SLOs (what we optimize for)
The exact targets may vary by region/model and are recorded in the SLO registry. The table below describes the *common SLO classes* and how they should inform Support expectations.

| SLO class | Primary metric (examples) | What customers feel | Typical mitigations | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| API availability | Request success rate (2xx/3xx), 5xx rate, auth failure rate | Outage, elevated errors, failed requests | Rollback, capacity shift, routing/fallback, dependency remediation | Availability issues generally justify rapid paging when widespread. |
| API latency | p95/p99 end-to-end latency; queue time; time-to-first-token (TTFT) for streaming | Slow responses, timeouts, UI/app slowness | Scaling, routing, batching controls, rollback, dependency tuning | Latency can be as severe as errors for synchronous apps; focus on duration + breadth. |
| Streaming health | Stream disconnect rate, truncated streams, reconnect loops | “Partial success” but bad UX; repeated retries | Gateway config adjustments, region shift, client guidance | Often appears as “works sometimes”; Support should collect request IDs + client details. |
| Control plane availability (Console/SSO/config) | Login success rate, token exchange, config API availability | Can’t log in, can’t manage keys/routes | Auth/SSO fixes, control plane rollback | Customer impact can be business-critical even if inference plane is healthy. |
| Async pipelines (e.g., audit logs) | Delivery lag (p95/p99), backlog depth, drop rate | Missing/late logs; compliance workflows blocked | Backlog drain, pipeline scaling, temporary export guidance | Typically not “Sev0” unless contractual/compliance deadlines are at risk. |

---

## Support SLAs (how we communicate)
Support SLAs are defined per tier. This mapping focuses on *how to adjust comms* based on Engineering signals and incident posture.

**Key principle:** When Engineering SLO burn indicates a fast-moving incident, Support update cadence should increase and escalation should occur even if an individual ticket is still gathering details.

The tiered SLAs (first response and update cadence) live in: **SLA policy by customer tier**. Support should follow that page for targets; this page clarifies *when to trigger paging and/or raise incident severity*.

---

## Mapping principles (Support SLA ↔ Engineering SLO)
1) **Support cadence must follow incident posture.**
   - If the issue qualifies as an incident (Sev0/Sev1), comms cadence is driven by the incident commander (IC) and comms owner, and Support should align rather than invent a parallel cadence.

2) **Page based on customer impact + burn rate, not “noise.”**
   - A single noisy host or one customer’s unusual traffic pattern should not page SRE without evidence of broader risk.
   - Conversely, a multi-customer degradation with high burn rate should page quickly even if absolute error % seems “small.”

3) **Prioritize actions that reduce TTM for the most customers.**
   - For Hosted: routing/fallback and regional traffic shifts are often the fastest mitigations.
   - For Dedicated: capacity and autoscaling controls are common mitigations.
   - For Private: diagnostics bundle + safe rollback paths reduce time-to-resolution.

4) **Support and Engineering severity must match.**
   - Support P0/P1 should not map to an Engineering Sev2 unless there is a clear reason (e.g., single-customer, self-inflicted, or no platform fault).

---

## Paging thresholds: tying SLOs to customer impact
This section defines recommended paging triggers for SRE/on-call. It is intentionally framed as **policy guidance**, not a rigid rule. Use judgment and document exceptions.

### A. Default paging triggers (broad customer impact)
Page SRE/on-call when *any* of the following is true:
- **Fast-burn SLO alert** fires for Hosted inference in a production region.
- **Multi-customer availability incident**: sustained elevated 5xx/auth failures affecting more than one customer or a material share of traffic in a region.
- **Region-level latency degradation**: sustained p95/p99 latency increase causing customer timeouts or meaningful throughput loss.
- **Streaming health regression**: elevated stream disconnect/truncation rate across multiple customers or across a region.

### B. Tier-weighted paging (Dedicated / Private)
Page the owning on-call (SRE or Platform/Private Deployments per escalation matrix) when:
- **Dedicated:** customer’s reserved capacity is not meeting contracted throughput/latency expectations due to platform-side causes (autoscaling instability, GPU pool churn, cluster issues), especially if customer cannot mitigate by retry/backoff.
- **Private:** upgrade/install failures blocking production usage or compliance obligations, or widespread control plane failures (SSO, key management, audit logs) with documented evidence.

### C. Single-customer escalation (high criticality)
A single enterprise customer can still justify paging when one or more is true:
- The issue blocks a critical workflow (e.g., production launch, compliance deadline) and there is no safe workaround.
- The ticket meets Support P0 criteria and there is evidence the platform is at fault.
- The ticket is likely an early signal of a broader incident (e.g., correlated in time with deploys, region signals, or similar symptoms in other tickets).

---

## Recommended alert posture (burn-rate oriented)
Engineering alerting should align to error budget policy. Support should understand what these alerts imply about urgency.

**Fast burn (page):** indicates we are on track to violate the SLO quickly (minutes to an hour). Support should treat this as P0/P1 depending on breadth.

**Slow burn (page during supported hours; consider after-hours based on tier/impact):** indicates sustained degradation that will consume significant budget over several hours.

**Example (conceptual):**
- Fast burn: consuming ~2% of monthly error budget in 1 hour
- Slow burn: consuming ~5% of monthly error budget in 6 hours

Support guidance:
- If Engineering declares Sev0/Sev1, **Support update cadence** should match the incident comms plan and be explicitly stated in customer emails.
- If a slow burn is detected but customers are not yet reporting, Support should prepare macros and proactively monitor incoming tickets.

---

## Mapping table: Support priority → Engineering signals → paging + comms
Use this table during triage and when deciding to page.

| Support priority | Typical customer impact | Engineering signals to look for | Paging expectation | Support comms expectation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P0 | Hard outage / widespread inability to use product; severe revenue or compliance risk | Fast-burn availability alert; broad 5xx/auth failures; region down; major routing failure | Page immediately; start Slack bridge; move to #incidents when criteria met | Acknowledge fast; frequent updates; align with IC; avoid speculation; share mitigations/workarounds when validated |
| P1 | Major degradation; partial outage; many customers affected or one enterprise blocked | Elevated latency/timeouts; streaming disconnect spike; slow-burn turning into fast-burn | Page quickly if sustained; otherwise engage on-call via escalation path | Set update cadence explicitly; include what we’re measuring and next checkpoint |
| P2 | Limited degradation; workaround exists; smaller blast radius | Localized metrics; no burn-rate alerts; isolated region/model | Do not page by default; escalate via owning team queue; page if trend worsens | Provide workaround; define “when to escalate” conditions; request required diagnostics |
| P3 | Minor issue; product question; no service risk | No abnormal metrics | No paging | Macro-driven response; doc links |
| P4 | Informational / enhancement request | N/A | No paging | Route to product/feedback |

Notes:
- A P1 that is clearly caused by customer misconfiguration should not be escalated as an Engineering incident, but Support should still help the customer quickly.
- If Support is uncertain whether impact is broad, treat as **P1 with a short investigation window** (e.g., 15–30 minutes) and page if evidence suggests multi-customer impact.

---

## Symptom-driven guidance: what to collect before paging
On-call efficiency depends on Support providing consistent context. Before paging, collect what you can without blocking escalation.

**Minimum required context (copy/paste):**
- Product/tier: Hosted / Dedicated / Private (+ region)
- API surface: chat/generate/embeddings/rerank/console/audit logs
- Model + version (and whether pinned)
- Time window (UTC) + first observed time
- Request IDs / trace IDs (at least 3 examples)
- Error codes/messages or latency percentiles as observed by customer
- Any recent customer-side changes (SDK version, proxy changes, retries)
- Any observed mitigations tried (retries/backoff, region switch, smaller batches)

If the issue is streaming-related, include:
- Transport details (HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2; proxy/CDN; keepalive settings)
- Client language/SDK version
- Whether failures correlate with long generations or specific token counts

---

## How Support SLAs should track Engineering SLO state
### 1) When Engineering declares an incident
- Support should **stop using generic SLA language** and instead use the incident cadence (e.g., “next update in 30 minutes”).
- Support should link the bridge channel + incident ID in Jira (SUP ticket) and keep comms consistent with the status page guidance.

### 2) When there is degradation but no incident declared (yet)
- Use P1/P2 and set an explicit checkpoint: “We are investigating and will update you by <time>.”
- If Engineering signals show burn-rate risk increasing, escalate to incident posture.

### 3) When an incident is resolved but customer symptoms persist
- Treat as a new investigation unless clearly tied to known residual impact.
- Collect fresh request IDs and confirm region/model.

---

## Examples (common scenarios)
### Example A: Elevated 429s / quota confusion
- If metrics show stable availability but ticket volume spikes and headers appear inconsistent, treat as **P2** (workaround + investigation).
- Page only if there is evidence of platform-side enforcement misclassification affecting multiple customers.
- Support should link the relevant known-issue article and capture header samples.

### Example B: Streaming resets in a single region
- If multiple customers report stream disconnects and streaming-health metrics spike, treat as **P1 → page quickly**.
- If only one customer reports and no metrics show impact, treat as **P2 with a short investigation window**; page if additional evidence appears.

### Example C: Audit log delivery lag
- If lag exceeds documented expectations and affects enterprise compliance workflows, treat as **P1** (even if inference plane is healthy).
- Page Platform/SRE depending on ownership; align comms to expected delivery windows and provide temporary export/retry guidance if validated.

---

## Ownership and escalation alignment
- Follow the **Escalation paths and contacts** directory for who to page by product area.
- Support should not bypass the incident process. If the situation warrants an incident, use the prescribed bridge conventions and move to #incidents per policy.

---

## Status page vs Support comms
Support comms, Slack bridges, and status page updates must be consistent. Refer to **Customer incident comms alignment** for:
- What qualifies for a status event
- Approval workflow
- How to avoid conflicting ETAs or overly specific root-cause claims

---

## Change management
If Support SLAs repeatedly fail to match what Engineering can operationally deliver:
1) File a ticket against this policy owner (SRE) and the SLA policy owner (Customer Success).
2) Include examples of mismatches (ticket IDs + timestamps + on-call notes).
3) Propose one of:
   - Adjust SLA wording/cadence for a tier
   - Adjust paging thresholds / alert routing
   - Add a known-issue workaround article + macros

**Owner:** SRE (Sean Gallagher) in partnership with Support Ops (Dev Patel).

---

## Appendix: Practical guidance for on-call friendly Support tickets
- Link: SUP ticket ↔ Slack bridge ↔ incident channel ↔ relevant dashboards.
- Use one “current state” comment that is continuously updated (timestamped) rather than multiple conflicting notes.
- When a workaround is provided, label it explicitly as **temporary** and include “when to escalate” criteria.
text:
## Purpose
This page is the reference for how Support SLAs (first response + update cadence) should map to Engineering SLOs (availability/latency) and how paging thresholds should relate to *customer impact*.

This is **not** a contractual SLA document. For contract language and tier-specific coverage windows, refer to: **Customer Success & Support → SLA policy by customer tier**.

**Goals**
- Prevent mismatches where Support promises a cadence that Engineering on-call cannot meet.
- Ensure paging triggers correlate to real customer impact (not only internal “red” alerts).
- Standardize language between Support (Jira priorities P0–P4) and Engineering incident severity (Sev0–Sev3).

---

## Definitions (shared language)
**Support SLA (operational):** Response/update targets used by Support/CS for incident comms and ticket handling. These are constrained by coverage windows and the current incident process.

**Engineering SLO:** Reliability objectives for production services (e.g., request success rate, latency). SLOs drive error budgets and alerting policies.

**Customer impact:** A combination of (a) number of customers affected, (b) tier (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private; Enterprise vs self-serve), and (c) degradation type (hard outage vs partial degradation vs “sharp edges”).

**Burn rate alerts:** Alerts that fire when error budget is being consumed quickly (fast burn) or steadily (slow burn). Burn rate is a more reliable paging signal than single-point thresholds.

**Time-to-triage (TTT):** Time from Support intake to a qualified owner engaged with enough context to act.

**Time-to-mitigation (TTM):** Time from detection/escalation to a customer-visible mitigation (rollback, routing change, scaling, workaround, etc.).

---

## Engineering SLOs (what we optimize for)
The exact targets may vary by region/model and are recorded in the SLO registry. The table below describes the *common SLO classes* and how they should inform Support expectations.

| SLO class | Primary metric (examples) | What customers feel | Typical mitigations | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| API availability | Request success rate (2xx/3xx), 5xx rate, auth failure rate | Outage, elevated errors, failed requests | Rollback, capacity shift, routing/fallback, dependency remediation | Availability issues generally justify rapid paging when widespread. |
| API latency | p95/p99 end-to-end latency; queue time; time-to-first-token (TTFT) for streaming | Slow responses, timeouts, UI/app slowness | Scaling, routing, batching controls, rollback, dependency tuning | Latency can be as severe as errors for synchronous apps; focus on duration + breadth. |
| Streaming health | Stream disconnect rate, truncated streams, reconnect loops | “Partial success” but bad UX; repeated retries | Gateway config adjustments, region shift, client guidance | Often appears as “works sometimes”; Support should collect request IDs + client details. |
| Control plane availability (Console/SSO/config) | Login success rate, token exchange, config API availability | Can’t log in, can’t manage keys/routes | Auth/SSO fixes, control plane rollback | Customer impact can be business-critical even if inference plane is healthy. |
| Async pipelines (e.g., audit logs) | Delivery lag (p95/p99), backlog depth, drop rate | Missing/late logs; compliance workflows blocked | Backlog drain, pipeline scaling, temporary export guidance | Typically not “Sev0” unless contractual/compliance deadlines are at risk. |

---

## Support SLAs (how we communicate)
Support SLAs are defined per tier. This mapping focuses on *how to adjust comms* based on Engineering signals and incident posture.

**Key principle:** When Engineering SLO burn indicates a fast-moving incident, Support update cadence should increase and escalation should occur even if an individual ticket is still gathering details.

The tiered SLAs (first response and update cadence) live in: **SLA policy by customer tier**. Support should follow that page for targets; this page clarifies *when to trigger paging and/or raise incident severity*.

---

## Mapping principles (Support SLA ↔ Engineering SLO)
1) **Support cadence must follow incident posture.**
   - If the issue qualifies as an incident (Sev0/Sev1), comms cadence is driven by the incident commander (IC) and comms owner, and Support should align rather than invent a parallel cadence.

2) **Page based on customer impact + burn rate, not “noise.”**
   - A single noisy host or one customer’s unusual traffic pattern should not page SRE without evidence of broader risk.
   - Conversely, a multi-customer degradation with high burn rate should page quickly even if absolute error % seems “small.”

3) **Prioritize actions that reduce TTM for the most customers.**
   - For Hosted: routing/fallback and regional traffic shifts are often the fastest mitigations.
   - For Dedicated: capacity and autoscaling controls are common mitigations.
   - For Private: diagnostics bundle + safe rollback paths reduce time-to-resolution.

4) **Support and Engineering severity must match.**
   - Support P0/P1 should not map to an Engineering Sev2 unless there is a clear reason (e.g., single-customer, self-inflicted, or no platform fault).

---

## Paging thresholds: tying SLOs to customer impact
This section defines recommended paging triggers for SRE/on-call. It is intentionally framed as **policy guidance**, not a rigid rule. Use judgment and document exceptions.

### A. Default paging triggers (broad customer impact)
Page SRE/on-call when *any* of the following is true:
- **Fast-burn SLO alert** fires for Hosted inference in a production region.
- **Multi-customer availability incident**: sustained elevated 5xx/auth failures affecting more than one customer or a material share of traffic in a region.
- **Region-level latency degradation**: sustained p95/p99 latency increase causing customer timeouts or meaningful throughput loss.
- **Streaming health regression**: elevated stream disconnect/truncation rate across multiple customers or across a region.

### B. Tier-weighted paging (Dedicated / Private)
Page the owning on-call (SRE or Platform/Private Deployments per escalation matrix) when:
- **Dedicated:** customer’s reserved capacity is not meeting contracted throughput/latency expectations due to platform-side causes (autoscaling instability, GPU pool churn, cluster issues), especially if customer cannot mitigate by retry/backoff.
- **Private:** upgrade/install failures blocking production usage or compliance obligations, or widespread control plane failures (SSO, key management, audit logs) with documented evidence.

### C. Single-customer escalation (high criticality)
A single enterprise customer can still justify paging when one or more is true:
- The issue blocks a critical workflow (e.g., production launch, compliance deadline) and there is no safe workaround.
- The ticket meets Support P0 criteria and there is evidence the platform is at fault.
- The ticket is likely an early signal of a broader incident (e.g., correlated in time with deploys, region signals, or similar symptoms in other tickets).

---

## Recommended alert posture (burn-rate oriented)
Engineering alerting should align to error budget policy. Support should understand what these alerts imply about urgency.

**Fast burn (page):** indicates we are on track to violate the SLO quickly (minutes to an hour). Support should treat this as P0/P1 depending on breadth.

**Slow burn (page during supported hours; consider after-hours based on tier/impact):** indicates sustained degradation that will consume significant budget over several hours.

**Example (conceptual):**
- Fast burn: consuming ~2% of monthly error budget in 1 hour
- Slow burn: consuming ~5% of monthly error budget in 6 hours

Support guidance:
- If Engineering declares Sev0/Sev1, **Support update cadence** should match the incident comms plan and be explicitly stated in customer emails.
- If a slow burn is detected but customers are not yet reporting, Support should prepare macros and proactively monitor incoming tickets.

---

## Mapping table: Support priority → Engineering signals → paging + comms
Use this table during triage and when deciding to page.

| Support priority | Typical customer impact | Engineering signals to look for | Paging expectation | Support comms expectation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P0 | Hard outage / widespread inability to use product; severe revenue or compliance risk | Fast-burn availability alert; broad 5xx/auth failures; region down; major routing failure | Page immediately; start Slack bridge; move to #incidents when criteria met | Acknowledge fast; frequent updates; align with IC; avoid speculation; share mitigations/workarounds when validated |
| P1 | Major degradation; partial outage; many customers affected or one enterprise blocked | Elevated latency/timeouts; streaming disconnect spike; slow-burn turning into fast-burn | Page quickly if sustained; otherwise engage on-call via escalation path | Set update cadence explicitly; include what we’re measuring and next checkpoint |
| P2 | Limited degradation; workaround exists; smaller blast radius | Localized metrics; no burn-rate alerts; isolated region/model | Do not page by default; escalate via owning team queue; page if trend worsens | Provide workaround; define “when to escalate” conditions; request required diagnostics |
| P3 | Minor issue; product question; no service risk | No abnormal metrics | No paging | Macro-driven response; doc links |
| P4 | Informational / enhancement request | N/A | No paging | Route to product/feedback |

Notes:
- A P1 that is clearly caused by customer misconfiguration should not be escalated as an Engineering incident, but Support should still help the customer quickly.
- If Support is uncertain whether impact is broad, treat as **P1 with a short investigation window** (e.g., 15–30 minutes) and page if evidence suggests multi-customer impact.

---

## Symptom-driven guidance: what to collect before paging
On-call efficiency depends on Support providing consistent context. Before paging, collect what you can without blocking escalation.

**Minimum required context (copy/paste):**
- Product/tier: Hosted / Dedicated / Private (+ region)
- API surface: chat/generate/embeddings/rerank/console/audit logs
- Model + version (and whether pinned)
- Time window (UTC) + first observed time
- Request IDs / trace IDs (at least 3 examples)
- Error codes/messages or latency percentiles as observed by customer
- Any recent customer-side changes (SDK version, proxy changes, retries)
- Any observed mitigations tried (retries/backoff, region switch, smaller batches)

If the issue is streaming-related, include:
- Transport details (HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2; proxy/CDN; keepalive settings)
- Client language/SDK version
- Whether failures correlate with long generations or specific token counts

---

## How Support SLAs should track Engineering SLO state
### 1) When Engineering declares an incident
- Support should **stop using generic SLA language** and instead use the incident cadence (e.g., “next update in 30 minutes”).
- Support should link the bridge channel + incident ID in Jira (SUP ticket) and keep comms consistent with the status page guidance.

### 2) When there is degradation but no incident declared (yet)
- Use P1/P2 and set an explicit checkpoint: “We are investigating and will update you by <time>.”
- If Engineering signals show burn-rate risk increasing, escalate to incident posture.

### 3) When an incident is resolved but customer symptoms persist
- Treat as a new investigation unless clearly tied to known residual impact.
- Collect fresh request IDs and confirm region/model.

---

## Examples (common scenarios)
### Example A: Elevated 429s / quota confusion
- If metrics show stable availability but ticket volume spikes and headers appear inconsistent, treat as **P2** (workaround + investigation).
- Page only if there is evidence of platform-side enforcement misclassification affecting multiple customers.
- Support should link the relevant known-issue article and capture header samples.

### Example B: Streaming resets in a single region
- If multiple customers report stream disconnects and streaming-health metrics spike, treat as **P1 → page quickly**.
- If only one customer reports and no metrics show impact, treat as **P2 with a short investigation window**; page if additional evidence appears.

### Example C: Audit log delivery lag
- If lag exceeds documented expectations and affects enterprise compliance workflows, treat as **P1** (even if inference plane is healthy).
- Page Platform/SRE depending on ownership; align comms to expected delivery windows and provide temporary export/retry guidance if validated.

---

## Ownership and escalation alignment
- Follow the **Escalation paths and contacts** directory for who to page by product area.
- Support should not bypass the incident process. If the situation warrants an incident, use the prescribed bridge conventions and move to #incidents per policy.

---

## Status page vs Support comms
Support comms, Slack bridges, and status page updates must be consistent. Refer to **Customer incident comms alignment** for:
- What qualifies for a status event
- Approval workflow
- How to avoid conflicting ETAs or overly specific root-cause claims

---

## Change management
If Support SLAs repeatedly fail to match what Engineering can operationally deliver:
1) File a ticket against this policy owner (SRE) and the SLA policy owner (Customer Success).
2) Include examples of mismatches (ticket IDs + timestamps + on-call notes).
3) Propose one of:
   - Adjust SLA wording/cadence for a tier
   - Adjust paging thresholds / alert routing
   - Add a known-issue workaround article + macros

**Owner:** SRE (Sean Gallagher) in partnership with Support Ops (Dev Patel).

---

- Link: SUP ticket ↔ Slack bridge ↔ incident channel ↔ relevant dashboards.

title: Traffic Reshaper and SLO Containment: Field Guide for LLM Inference
preview:
Overview
========

Short intro: This field guide documents standard operating patterns for containing SLO burn and reshaping traffic during multi-tenant LLM inference incidents. It is aimed at on-call SREs, platform engineers, and owners of tenant-facing routing policies. Readable end-to-end: detection -> immediate containment -> recovery -> post-incident actions.

Scope and intent
----------------
- Scope: Redwood hosted & Dedicated inference control plane and routing layer (traffic-proxy, admission-controller, and model-router).
- Intent: Give deterministic, low-risk steps to:
  - stop or slow SLO degradation in-flight,
  - preserve high-priority tenant commitments, and
  - buy time for capacity/provisioning or rollback.
- Out of scope: model code changes and downstream customer app fixes. Those are handled via product triage.

Why this is different for LLM inference
--------------------------------------
- Long-lived token streams (streaming responses, KV cache) mean a single request can tie up GPU lanes for seconds–minutes.
- Multi-dimensional SLOs: latency quantiles (p99/p995), successful-completion rate (stream did not abort), and quality regressions (evaluation suite).
- Cost/throughput tradeoffs: immediate mitigation often requires intelligent routing to quantized/cheaper model variants instead of full scale-up.

SLOs and error budget attribution (quick reference)
--------------------------------------------------
- Primary SLOs for inference platform (example):
  - Latency p95 <= 250ms (window: 30d)
  - Latency p99 <= 900ms (window: 30d)
  - Successful completion >= 99.5% (no stream aborts)
- Error budget math (flat examples):
  - Monthly error budget for p99 = 1 - (SLO target). If SLO target 99.5% => budget = 0.5% of requests.
  - Current burn rate = (observed_violation_rate / budget_rate).
  - If burn_rate > 4 => trigger containment and capacity gating.

SLO attribution rules (short)
- Attribution priority: network/service infra > inference runtime > model > tenant client.
- If evidence shows inference runtime is primary (elevated GPU queue depth, OOMs, KV cache thrash) attribute to runtime.
- If tenant spike is root cause (tenant p95 >> baseline, tenant token-per-second high), attribute to tenant and consider throttling or eviction.

Key observable signals and dashboards
------------------------------------
- Minimum telemetry to consult before action:
  - model-router queue-length (per-node and global)
  - GPU utilization by pool and by model-version
  - request duration histogram (split by model, route, tenant)
  - streaming-abort-rate by tenant and route
  - error budget burn-rate (service-level / tenant-level)
  - admissions metrics: rejected-by-quota, throttled-by-fairshare
- Priority dashboards: 
  - "Inference Health: quantiles & tails" (team SLO dashboard),
  - "Traffic Fabric: queues & routing" (oncall canvas), and
  - tenant-specific heatmaps (for top 50 tenants).

Immediate containment play (executive summary)
--------------------------------------------
When burn_rate > 4 for critical SLOs AND p99 breaches for > 10 minutes: follow the fast path below. The goal is to reduce tail pressure within 2 minutes.

1) Fast triage (2 minutes)
   - Open oncall canvas and confirm p99 abnormality, model-router queue growth, and GPU hot nodes.
   - Identify top 3 contributing routes/tenants by request volume and p99 contribution.

2) Apply soft backpressure (3–5 minutes)
   - Step A: Enable rate-limit gate for non-critical tenants by +30% (admission-controller). CLI: 
     ```bash
     # example: set rate limit for tenant group 'free' to 6 rps per shard
     rdbctl admission set-rate --group free --rps 6
     ```
   - Step B: Turn on fairshare pre-emption: reduce low-priority token weight to 10% using traffic-proxy config.

3) Route to cheaper variants (2–10 minutes)
   - If queue depth remains high: begin progressive redirection (10% -> 30% -> 100%) of low-priority routes to quantized model-variants or dedicated low-cost pools.
   - Sample command (model-router):
     ```bash
     model-router promote-variant --route /generate --from model-v1 --to model-v1-int8 --percent 10
     ```
   - Monitor model-quality regression metrics (sampling+eval harness) in parallel. Stop redirection if quality alert fires.

4) If still degrading -> protective eviction (10–20 minutes)
   - Evict or soft-suspend offending tenant(s) that consume > X% of GPU hairpins for > 15m. Eviction is last resort; use tiered warnings to customers first.
   - Use automated eviction: 
     ```bash
     trafctl tenant-evict --tenant-id t-12345 --grace 30s --reason SLO-burn
     ```

Traffic-shaping patterns (detailed)
----------------------------------
- Token-priority shaping: prioritize short responses and low-token requests; defer streaming long-running sessions.
- Admission gating: global per-shard RPS caps and per-tenant token-s/second caps.
- Sampling & quota reduction: reduce sample rate for telemetry (not for top-tier tenants) to preserve capacity for production traffic.
- Canary reduction: fallback tenants to cached responses or pre-generated suggestions when appropriate.

Capacity anchoring and headroom policy (table)
----------------------------------------------
| Policy item | Trigger threshold | Immediate action | Owner |
|-------------|-------------------|------------------|-------|
| Headroom reserve | avg utilization > 72% across inference pools for 10m | Reserve 10% capacity, halt non-critical rollout | eng-sre-capacity |
| Emergency burst fund | p99 latency > SLO for 15m AND burn_rate > 2 | Allow autoscale up to 1.5x but require post-approval | eng-sre-oncall |
| Eviction threshold | single-tenant usage > 35% of pool for 30m | Start eviction pipeline (audit + notify) | tenancy-sre |

(Converting to plain text table above is intentional for Confluence consumption).

Incident review evidence checklist (minimum required)
----------------------------------------------------
- Time window with SLO breach (start, peak, recovery).
- Top contributing tenant IDs and routes with counts and percent-of-burn.
- Node-level telemetry: GPU queue depth, OOM logs, kernel failures.
- Admission controller logs: decisions to throttle/reject and exact config changes.
- Model-router actions: variant promotions, percent shifts, and timestamps.
- Any changes to autoscaling/terraform events in the same window.
- Chat transcripts and customer escalations (if received).

Runbook commands & safe toggles (copyable snippets)
--------------------------------------------------
- Show active queue depths (aggregated by route):
  curl -s https://internal-metrics/api/v1/queues?agg=route | jq '.'

- Temporarily reduce free-tier rps to 5 (admission-controller CLI):
  rdbctl admission set-rate --group free --rps 5 --reason "SLO containment"

- Progressive redirect of route to quantized variant:
  for p in 10 30 60 100; do model-router promote-variant --route /chat --from r-v2 --to r-v2-int8 --percent $p; sleep 120; done

- Revert traffic redirection:
  model-router promote-variant --route /chat --from r-v2-int8 --to r-v2 --percent 100 --reason revert-quality-regression

Decision thresholds and governance
--------------------------------
- Any eviction or more-than-1.5x autoscale requires a conference with oncall lead and product steward within 30 minutes.
- Any permanent model-variant rollouts triggered by containment must pass a 24h quality shadowing window and an SRE signoff.

Ownership & escalation matrix
----------------------------
- First responder: oncall SRE (inference-platform rotation) — owns runbook execution.
- Secondary: eng-serving-runtime pager — owns node-level mitigations and kernel rollback.
- Tertiary: product oncall — notifies customer success and product steward for communication.
- Exec notify: if Tier-1 customer experiences > 5% error rate or if system-level SLOs impaired > 12h.

Post-incident actions and priority class types
--------------------------------------------
- Triage tags (use these in postmortem): 
  - "capacity-root" (need capacity changes), 
  - "routing-misconfig", 
  - "eviction-required", 
  - "model-quality-regression".
- Action timeline: start work within 48h for P0 items; complete P1 within 14 days.

Templates and artifacts (what to attach to postmortem)
----------------------------------------------------
- CSV: top-ten tenant contribution for breach window
- JSON: admission-controller decisions (timestamps + request ids)
- Screenshots: p95/p99 heatmap and GPU utilization timelines
- Runbook actions log: sequence of commands executed (copied to the incident channel)

Related pages and links
-----------------------
- See: /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/service-onboarding-runway-and-slo-admission-playbook-2026 for onboarding constraints.
- See: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/oom-cascade-and-memory-throttling-runbook-2026 for node-level memory failure actions.

Appendix: escalation CLI quick cheatsheet
----------------------------------------
- Query tenant request-rate: 
  curl -s "https://api.internal/tenants/t-12345/metrics?until=now-30m" | jq '.requests_per_s'
- Temporarily add headroom label to pool(s):
  poolctl set-headroom --pool inference-gpu-a --percent 10 --reason "SLO containment"

Revision history
----------------
- 2025-11-10: created by Riley Morgan (initial draft).
- 2025-12-02: added progressive redirect examples (review by Priya Desai).
- 2026-02-15: expanded evidence checklist and governance thresholds; published.

Contact / owner
---------------
- Primary owner: eng-sre (alias: #eng-sre-oncall).
- Document author: Riley Morgan (riley.morgan@redwood.example)

End of guide.
