   - rollout_id, from_version, to_version
   - gate results
   - time-to-detect, time-to-rollback, time-to-recover

Canonical runbook: **Rollback Optimization Config** (linked above).

---

## Validation & gamedays
We treat reliability of rollback as a feature requiring continuous validation.

### Pre-GA checks (must pass)
- Controlled failure injection: introduce a known-bad config for canary scope and validate auto-pause.
- Race test: concurrent apply + rollback requests converge to expected target (no mixed steady state).
- Observability: all dashboards show rollout annotations; gate evaluations visible within 60 seconds.
- Audit logging: create/apply/pause/promote/rollback events present in SIEM export with required fields.

### Gameday cadence
- Internal beta: weekly gamedays for 4 weeks.
- Post-Limited GA: monthly gameday.

Success criteria (gameday):
- Detect regression within 5 minutes (alert or operator observation)
- Rollback initiated within 10 minutes
- Recovery within 20 minutes (Hosted/Dedicated)

---

## Launch readiness checklist (SRE sign-off)
SRE sign-off requires:
- All P0 alerts defined and tested in staging
- Dashboard complete with required panels and tags
- Runbook reviewed and validated end-to-end
- Error budget policy agreed with Platform/Runtime
- Quant quality gating integrated for applicable models
- Audit event coverage ≥ 99.99% in staging soak for 7 days

---

## Ownership and escalation
- Primary owner: **Eng-SRE** (Sean Gallagher)
- Control plane owner: **Platform Control Plane** (Ethan Park)
- Console workflow owner: **Console & Tooling** (Chloe Martin)
- Runtime owner: **Serving Runtime** (Noah Patel)
- Quant gate owner: **Applied ML / Evals** (Jonas Weber)
- Audit/RBAC owner: **Security & Compliance** (Marcus Lin / Dr. Aisha Rahman)

Escalation guideline:
- If rollback does not converge within 15 minutes (Hosted/Dedicated), page Runtime on-call.
- If gate evaluation is failing or stale, page Platform on-call.
- If audit events are missing for an enterprise tenant, page Security on-call.

---

## Appendix A: Example alert thresholds (starting defaults)
These are initial defaults; tune per region/model once we have 30+ days of production data.

- `GateEvalError`: > 0 errors for 5m (P0)
- `MixedVersionOutsideRollout`: > 5% for 10m (P0)
- `RollbackEndpoint5xx`: > 1% for 10m (P0)
- `RolloutLatencyRegression`: p95 +10% or +25ms for 5m during active rollout (P0)
- `Rollout5xxRegression`: +0.10% absolute for 5m during active rollout (P0)
- `ConvergenceSlow`: stage duration p99 > 30m (P1)

---

## Appendix B: Audit event expectations (high level)
For each action, an audit event must be emitted with `actor`, `tenant_id`, `scope`, `config_version`, `rollout_id`, and `request_id`:
- `optimization_config.version_created`
- `optimization_config.validated`
- `optimization_config.rollout_started`
- `optimization_config.rollout_paused`
- `optimization_config.promoted`
- `optimization_config.rollback_requested`
- `optimization_config.rollback_completed`

See Security page for full field requirements and retention.

title: Incident Ownership Mapping + Remediation SLA Matrix (v1)
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the **primary ownership mapping** for each incident type/subtype in the v1 taxonomy, plus **remediation SLAs** (time-to-mitigate and time-to-fix expectations) by **severity** and **customer tier** (Hosted / Dedicated / Private).

The intent is to:
- Make ownership **unambiguous** during live incidents (one accountable team per incident).
- Enable consistent paging/escalation and reporting by type/owner.
- Set realistic but improvement-driving remediation expectations.

**Applies to:** customer-impacting incidents and high-severity internal incidents that materially threaten reliability.

---

## Definitions (used throughout)
- **Primary owner:** the single team accountable for mitigation and driving the incident to resolution (may delegate tasks but retains accountability).
- **Secondary owner:** team likely needed for mitigation/fix; consulted early; may lead specific workstreams.
- **Consulted:** subject-matter experts who may be needed depending on symptoms.
- **Time-to-mitigate (TTM):** time from **incident declared** (Sev assigned) to a mitigation that restores service to within agreed SLO/SLA guardrails (or to “degraded but stable” per policy).
- **Time-to-fix (TTF):** time from **incident declared** to a durable fix (merged + deployed or config updated) that prevents recurrence of the same failure mode.

**Severity scale (v1):**
- **Sev0:** complete outage / widespread critical customer impact; no viable workaround.
- **Sev1:** major degradation (sustained 5xx/latency/streaming failures) affecting many customers or a top-tier Dedicated/Private tenant.
- **Sev2:** partial degradation or limited-scope impact; mitigations exist; some customers affected.
- **Sev3:** minor impact / near-miss / internal-only reliability risk.

---

## Escalation and paging rules (global)
1) **SRE is always paged for Sev0/Sev1** (IC + comms + coordination), regardless of incident type.
2) The **Primary owner on-call** is paged immediately once incident_type is set (or once the IC is confident on a likely type).
3) If incident_type is **Unknown/Unclassified** after 10 minutes:
   - IC pages **SRE + Platform Control Plane on-call** (default triage), then updates type as soon as evidence emerges.
4) **Multi-cause incidents:** pick a **single primary incident_type** based on the *first-order driver of customer impact* (see taxonomy decision tree). Record other drivers as **contributing systems** in the incident record/postmortem.
5) **Security triggers:** if auth failures look suspicious (credential stuffing, impossible travel, token anomalies) or audit findings require, **dual-track** with Security Ops (see security alignment page).
6) **Cloud provider / force majeure events:** still classify and mitigate, but SLA breach evaluation may be paused per the carve-outs section below.

**Escalation path (when primary owner cannot mitigate quickly):**
- **T+15 min (Sev0/1):** page secondary owner(s) if not already engaged.
- **T+30 min (Sev0/1):** page engineering manager (EM) for primary owner.
- **T+60 min (Sev0) / T+90 min (Sev1):** page director for primary owner; engage executive sponsor (SRE) if cross-org blockers persist.

---

## Ownership map (v1)
Notes:
- Team names align to current rotations and Slack channels.
- “Service boundary” indicates the system most likely to contain the change/fix.
- Owner mapping is implemented in control plane config (versioned + auditable) and consumed by incident-bot.

### Top-level incident types → ownership
| Incident type (v1) | Subtype (v1) | Primary owner (team) | Secondary owner(s) | Consulted | Primary service boundary | Primary Slack | Pager rotation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Availability / 5xx | Request 5xx spike (non-overload) | Platform Control Plane | Serving Runtime, Smart Routing | SRE, Observability | api-gateway, request-router, admission-control | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Availability / 5xx | Overload / admission control | Infrastructure/Fleet | Smart Routing | SRE, Observability, Runtime | fleet-manager, rate-limiters, batcher | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Availability / 5xx | Dependency outage (DB/KV/queue) | Platform Control Plane | SRE | Observability | control-plane-db, config-store | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Latency regression | Global p95/p99 latency regression | Serving Runtime | Infrastructure/Fleet | SRE, Observability | runtime-scheduler, kernel-selector, batching | #eng-runtime-oncall | serving-runtime |
| Latency regression | Model rollout regression | Serving Runtime | SRE | Product, Observability | model-registry, rollout-controller | #eng-runtime-oncall | serving-runtime |
| Latency regression | Network path / cross-region latency | Infrastructure/Fleet | Private Deployments/Networking | SRE | edge-ingress, peering, region-network | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Streaming | Stream disconnect spike | Platform Control Plane | Infrastructure/Fleet | Runtime, Support | streaming-gateway, websockets/sse layer | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Streaming | Partial tokens / stalled stream | Serving Runtime | Platform Control Plane | Observability | runtime-streamer, token-emitter | #eng-runtime-oncall | serving-runtime |
| Routing / fallback | Incorrect fallback policy execution | Smart Routing | Platform Control Plane | SRE, Observability | policy-engine, router | #eng-routing-oncall | smart-routing |
| Routing / fallback | Fallback loop / thrash | Smart Routing | Infrastructure/Fleet | Runtime, SRE | router, health-checker | #eng-routing-oncall | smart-routing |
| Routing / fallback | Region selection bug | Smart Routing | Infrastructure/Fleet | SRE | router, region-selector | #eng-routing-oncall | smart-routing |
| Capacity / fleet | Regional GPU shortage | Infrastructure/Fleet | Smart Routing | SRE, Support/CS | fleet-manager, provisioning | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Capacity / fleet | Provisioning lag / autoscaler failure | Infrastructure/Fleet | Platform Control Plane | SRE | autoscaler, capacity-planner | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Capacity / fleet | Dedicated tenant saturation | Infrastructure/Fleet | Support/CS | SRE, Product Dedicated | dedicated-scheduler, tenant-quotas | #eng-infra-oncall | infra-fleet |
| Control plane | Config write failures | Platform Control Plane | SRE | Observability, Support | control-plane-api, config-store | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Control plane | Rollout/registry outage | Platform Control Plane | Serving Runtime | SRE | model-registry, rollout-controller | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Auth / RBAC | Token issuance/validation failures | Security Ops | Platform Control Plane | SRE, Support | auth-service, api-gateway authn | #security-ops-oncall | security-ops |
| Auth / RBAC | RBAC/permission misconfig | Platform Control Plane | Security Ops | Support/CS | rbac-service, org-policy | #eng-platform-oncall | platform-control-plane |
| Telemetry / observability | Metrics ingestion outage | Observability | SRE | Platform, Runtime | metrics-pipeline, collectors | #eng-observability-oncall | observability |
| Telemetry / observability | Logging/tracing blind spot | Observability | Platform Control Plane | SRE | log-pipeline, trace-collector | #eng-observability-oncall | observability |
| Private deployments | Connectivity (DNS/TLS/VPN/peering) | Private Deployments/Networking | Infrastructure/Fleet | SRE, Support | private-gateway, customer-vpc-connect | #eng-private-oncall | private-networking |
| Private deployments | Customer config / upgrade regression | Private Deployments/Networking | Platform Control Plane | Support, SRE | private-agent, upgrade-controller | #eng-private-oncall | private-networking |

### Ownership notes for common disputed boundaries
**Overload vs routing:**
- If the *trigger* is lack of GPU capacity / saturation / provisioning lag → **Capacity/Fleet primary**.
- If the *trigger* is incorrect policy, routing loop, or wrong fallback execution even with healthy capacity → **Smart Routing primary**.
- If both present, choose the type that best explains the *first-order* customer impact and list the other as contributing.

**Streaming disconnects:**
- If disconnects correlate with gateway errors, websocket/SSE resets, or ingress churn → **Platform Control Plane primary**.
- If streams are established but stall mid-generation with runtime errors/oom/kv-cache thrash → **Serving Runtime primary**.

**Auth anomalies:**
- If it’s clearly a production bug (token validation library change, cert rotation mistake) → still classify under **Auth/RBAC**, but **Security Ops primary** unless Security explicitly delegates to Platform.

---

## Remediation SLA matrix (v1)
These are **internal remediation SLAs** used for operational discipline and reporting. Customer-facing SLA/credit terms remain governed by contracts and published policies.

### SLA philosophy by tier
- **Hosted:** optimize for fast mitigation; durable fixes follow normal change management unless Sev0/1.
- **Dedicated:** stronger expectation for mitigation + fix because customers have reserved capacity and explicit commitments.
- **Private:** mitigation may require customer participation; our SLA focuses on *our* response/mitigation within Redwood-managed components and clear handoffs.

### Default remediation SLAs by severity and tier
TTM and TTF are measured from the time the incident is declared with a severity.

| Severity | Hosted TTM | Hosted TTF | Dedicated TTM | Dedicated TTF | Private TTM | Private TTF |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Sev0 | 30 min | 72 hours | 20 min | 48 hours | 30 min | 5 business days |
| Sev1 | 60 min | 5 business days | 45 min | 3 business days | 60 min | 10 business days |
| Sev2 | 4 hours | 10 business days | 3 hours | 7 business days | 4 hours | 15 business days |
| Sev3 | 2 business days | 30 days | 2 business days | 21 days | 3 business days | 45 days |

**Interpretation notes:**
- **TTM** = “service restored” or “degraded but stable” per incident commander + SRE sign-off.
- **TTF** requires a tracked change (PR/config) and deployment (or customer-delivered upgrade for Private, if the fix is on their side; see carve-outs).
- If a durable fix requires a longer roadmap change, the TTF can be met by delivering a **risk-reducing fix** (guardrail, rollback, safer default) plus a committed follow-up milestone.

### Type-specific adjustments (overrides)
Some incident types have different practical fix timelines. Use these overrides when applicable.

| Incident type | Subtype | Override applies to | Dedicated/Hosted/Private | TTF override | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---:|---|
| Control plane | Config write failures | Sev0–Sev2 | All | 3 business days | Config integrity bugs typically require code + migration; prioritize hotfix lane. |
| Telemetry / observability | Metrics ingestion outage | Sev0–Sev2 | All | 7 business days | Durable fix often includes capacity + pipeline changes; ensure interim mitigations (sampling, backpressure) are in place. |
| Private deployments | Connectivity (DNS/TLS/VPN/peering) | Sev0–Sev2 | Private | 10 business days | May require customer networking changes; our obligation is fast triage + clear “next action owner”. |
| Capacity / fleet | Regional GPU shortage | Sev0–Sev2 | Hosted/Dedicated | 10 business days | Durable fix may be procurement/provider constrained; require documented mitigation plan + capacity forecast update. |

**Who approves overrides:** SRE on-call for live incidents; post-incident, SRE program lead (Rafael Mendes) for reporting consistency.

---

## SLA timer start/stop + breach handling
### When the clock starts
- SLA clocks start when the incident is **declared** with severity (Sev0–Sev3) in incident-bot.
- If the incident is initially declared as Sev2 and later upgraded to Sev0/1, the SLA clock for that severity starts at the **upgrade time** (we retain the original for audit).

### When the clock stops
- **TTM stops** when mitigation is in place and validated via dashboards/metrics (or customer confirmation where metrics are unavailable).
- **TTF stops** when the fix is deployed (or config change is rolled out) and a verification check is complete.

### Pausing timers (allowed but audited)
Timers may be paused only with an explicit reason recorded in incident-bot:
- **Cloud provider outage / region-wide event** that is the dominant cause.
- **Customer-caused incident** (e.g., invalid client behavior, misconfigured private networking) once confirmed.
- **Force majeure** events.

**Pauses require:** SRE approval (Sev0/1) and a post-incident note describing evidence.

### Breach escalation
- Any projected TTM breach for **Sev0/Sev1** triggers:
  - Immediate page to primary owner EM at **T-10 min** to breach.
  - Escalation to director at breach time if not already engaged.
- Any actual breach triggers:
  - A postmortem requirement (Sev0/Sev1 always; Sev2 if breach is customer-impacting).
  - A follow-up ticket tagged **reliability-sla-breach** with owner + due date.

---

## How to use this during an incident (operator checklist)
1) Declare incident + set severity.
2) Classify **incident_type/subtype** using the decision tree.
3) Confirm **Primary owner** from mapping (incident-bot will resolve after selection).
4) Page primary owner on-call; add secondary owners if the boundary is unclear.
5) Track TTM and TTF targets in the incident channel topic (incident-bot prints due times).
6) If classification changes, update incident_type and record the reason.

---

## Examples
### Example A: Dedicated tenant sees 429s during burst
- Symptoms: 429s + queue depth + GPU utilization pinned.
- Classification: **Capacity / fleet → Dedicated tenant saturation**.
- Primary owner: **Infrastructure/Fleet**.
- Secondary: **Support/CS** (customer comms + burst expectations).
- SLA (Dedicated Sev1 typical): **TTM 45 min, TTF 3 business days**.

### Example B: Global spike in streaming disconnects after gateway deploy
- Symptoms: websocket resets, elevated gateway errors, runtime healthy.
- Classification: **Streaming → Stream disconnect spike**.
- Primary owner: **Platform Control Plane**.
- SLA (Hosted Sev1 typical): **TTM 60 min, TTF 5 business days**.

### Example C: Auth failures with suspicious pattern
- Symptoms: token validation failures, anomalous IP patterns.
- Classification: **Auth / RBAC → Token issuance/validation failures**.
- Primary owner: **Security Ops** (dual-track security incident if warranted).

---

## Changelog
- **2026-02-12:** Clarified overload vs routing primary-owner rule; added TTF override for regional GPU shortage; added timer pause requirements.
- **2026-01-24:** Added Private connectivity subtype details and clarified customer handoff expectations.
- **2026-01-10:** Initial v1 published.

- If the incident is initially declared as Sev2 and later upgraded to Sev0/1, the SLA clock for that severity starts at the **upgrade time** (we retain the original for audit).

### When the clock stops
- **TTM stops** when mitigation is in place and validated via dashboards/metrics (or customer confirmation where metrics are unavailable).
- **TTF stops** when the fix is deployed (or config change is rolled out) and a verification check is complete.

### Pausing timers (allowed but audited)
Timers may be paused only with an explicit reason recorded in incident-bot:
- **Cloud provider outage / region-wide event** that is the dominant cause.
- **Customer-caused incident** (e.g., invalid client behavior, misconfigured private networking) once confirmed.
- **Force majeure** events.

**Pauses require:** SRE approval (Sev0/1) and a post-incident note describing evidence.

### Breach escalation
- Any projected TTM breach for **Sev0/Sev1** triggers:
  - Immediate page to primary owner EM at **T-10 min** to breach.
  - Escalation to director at breach time if not already engaged.
- Any actual breach triggers:
  - A postmortem requirement (Sev0/Sev1 always; Sev2 if breach is customer-impacting).
  - A follow-up ticket tagged **reliability-sla-breach** with owner + due date.

---

## How to use this during an incident (operator checklist)
1) Declare incident + set severity.
2) Classify **incident_type/subtype** using the decision tree.
3) Confirm **Primary owner** from mapping (incident-bot will resolve after selection).
4) Page primary owner on-call; add secondary owners if the boundary is unclear.
5) Track TTM and TTF targets in the incident channel topic (incident-bot prints due times).
6) If classification changes, update incident_type and record the reason.

---

## Examples
### Example A: Dedicated tenant sees 429s during burst
- Symptoms: 429s + queue depth + GPU utilization pinned.
- Classification: **Capacity / fleet → Dedicated tenant saturation**.
- Primary owner: **Infrastructure/Fleet**.
- Secondary: **Support/CS** (customer comms + burst expectations).
- SLA (Dedicated Sev1 typical): **TTM 45 min, TTF 3 business days**.

### Example B: Global spike in streaming disconnects after gateway deploy
- Symptoms: websocket resets, elevated gateway errors, runtime healthy.
- Classification: **Streaming → Stream disconnect spike**.
- Primary owner: **Platform Control Plane**.
- SLA (Hosted Sev1 typical): **TTM 60 min, TTF 5 business days**.

### Example C: Auth failures with suspicious pattern
- Symptoms: token validation failures, anomalous IP patterns.
- Classification: **Auth / RBAC → Token issuance/validation failures**.
- Primary owner: **Security Ops** (dual-track security incident if warranted).

---

## Changelog
- **2026-02-12:** Clarified overload vs routing primary-owner rule; added TTF override for regional GPU shortage; added timer pause requirements.
- **2026-01-24:** Added Private connectivity subtype details and clarified customer handoff expectations.
- **2026-01-10:** Initial v1 published.

title: Runbook: perf-canary performance regression alert (p95 latency / tokens/sec)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to triage, validate, and mitigate alerts emitted by **perf-canary** for per-model performance regressions:
- **p95 latency** increase (end-to-end and/or runtime-only)
- **tokens/sec** decrease

The goal is to:
1) quickly verify the alert is real (not noise/cardinality/baseline drift),
2) localize the regression (model vs region vs hardware vs tier),
3) attribute likely cause (kernel vs scheduler vs model config vs infra/fleet),
4) take the lowest-risk mitigation (pause rollout, rollback, drain nodes, disable a kernel, etc.),
5) route escalation to the right team with the right artifacts.

perf-canary uses **synthetic prompt shapes** (no customer prompts) and should remain under the **GPU overhead budget**. Do not attempt to “increase canary load” during incident response without SRE/runtime approval.

---

## When this runbook applies
You are in the right place if you received one of the following:
- Slack/incident-bot message: `perf-canary WARNING` or `perf-canary PAGE`
- Pager event: `PerfCanaryRegression` / `PerfCanaryTopModelRegression`

Common alert names (Grafana/Prometheus):
- `perf_canary_p95_latency_regression_warn`
- `perf_canary_p95_latency_regression_page`
- `perf_canary_tokens_per_sec_regression_warn`
- `perf_canary_tokens_per_sec_regression_page`

Not in scope:
- Customer-reported latency issues without a canary signal (start with customer-impact triage + runtime dashboards)
- Quality regressions (see Applied ML eval harness)

---

## Inputs you should have from the alert payload
Incident-bot should include (if anything is missing, request it in-thread and note for follow-up):
- **model**: canonical model identifier (e.g., `llama-3.1-70b-instruct`) and **model_version**
- **region**: e.g., `us-east`, `eu-west`
- **tier/pool**: `hosted`, `dedicated`, `private` (where applicable)
- **hardware_sku**: e.g., `h100-sxm`, `a100-80g`
- **runtime_build_id** and **runtime_version**
- **kernel_tag / kernel_selection_id** (if present)
- **metric** + **observed** vs **baseline** and **% change**
- **sample size** (requests) and **window**
- **confidence / anti-flap state** (e.g., “3/3 evaluations fired”, “sustained 15m”)
- Links:
  - Perf Canary dashboard (model pre-filtered)
  - Runtime service dashboard (scheduler/queue)
  - Recent runtime release (if the alert correlates)
  - Runbook (this page)

If the alert payload does not include runtime/kernels tags, check runtime telemetry tags in the dashboards and capture them manually (screenshots or links). Missing tags are actionable follow-up.

---

## Severity and immediate expectations
### WARNING
- Investigate within business hours or as on-call load permits.
- Goal: confirm whether the signal is real and whether it correlates with an active rollout.
- If the regression impacts a **top model** or spans multiple regions/hardware, treat as “page-like” and escalate sooner.

### PAGE
- Treat as potential Sev2 until proven otherwise.
- Goal: validate quickly (≤10 minutes), and if real, mitigate (pause/rollback) quickly (≤30 minutes) to prevent customer impact.

---

## Step 0 — Safety checks (do first)
1) **Do not modify perf-canary QPS/config** during triage.
2) Verify no customer data is involved:
   - perf-canary logs/metrics should contain only synthetic prompt shape identifiers (bucket IDs), not raw text.
   - If you see raw prompts or identifiers, stop and page Security & Compliance (and Runtime TL).

---

## Step 1 — Confirm the alert is “valid” (anti-noise checklist)
Open the **Perf Canary dashboard** from the alert.

Confirm all of the following:
1) **Minimum samples met**
   - The alert should include `min_samples` gating (e.g., ≥200 requests in window; per-model values vary).
   - If sample count is below threshold, mark as likely false positive and monitor (do not page Runtime).

2) **Sustained signal**
   - Check if regression persisted across at least the dampening window (typically 10–15 minutes for paging).
   - If it spikes for 1–2 evaluations then recovers, treat as transient; watch for recurrence.

3) **Single-dimension artifact check** (common false positive pattern)
   - If regression appears only when filtering on an overly-specific label (unexpected `model_alias`, `pod`, `node`, `request_shape_id`), it may be a cardinality/config bug.
   - Remove filters and re-check the aggregated panels.

4) **Telemetry ingestion delay**
   - If dashboard “now” shows missing or delayed datapoints, check the telemetry pipeline health panel (ingestion lag).
   - If ingestion lag is high, the alert may be late/incorrect; coordinate with Telemetry Platform.

If the alert fails validity checks, do:
- Comment in incident thread: “Likely invalid due to <reason>; monitoring for 30m; no mitigation taken.”
- Create a follow-up issue to fix gating/cardinality/ingestion lag.

---

## Step 2 — Localize the regression (model vs region vs hardware)
Answer these questions from the dashboards:
1) **Is the regression isolated to one region?**
   - Compare `us-east` vs `eu-west` for the same model+version.

2) **Is it isolated to one hardware SKU?**
   - Compare `h100-sxm` vs `a100-80g` (if both run the model).

3) **Is it isolated to a tier/pool?**
   - Hosted vs Dedicated pools can diverge due to fleet differences.

4) **Is it only one model, or multiple models at once?**
   - Multiple models regressing simultaneously often indicates infra/fleet or scheduler-level changes.
   - A single model regressing suggests model config change or kernel selection change specific to that model.

Record in the incident thread:
- A short statement like: “Regression is us-east + h100 only, affects 3 models, runtime build 2026.02.11-rc3.”

---

## Step 3 — Attribute likely cause using metric breakdowns
Use the Perf Canary dashboard panels that break down latency:
- **End-to-end latency**
- **Runtime-only latency**
- **Queueing time**
- **Prefill latency**
- **Decode latency**
- **Batch size distribution**
- **(Optional) KV cache hit rate**

### A. Queueing time increased (scheduler / capacity / fleet)
Signals:
- p95 end-to-end ↑, runtime-only flat or mild ↑
- queueing_time_p95 ↑ materially
- batch sizes may increase (oversubscription) or decrease (underutilization)

Likely causes:
- capacity shortfall / fleet disruption in region
- scheduler heuristic change (batching/priority)
- noisy neighbor or node-level degradation

Actions:
1) Check recent changes:
   - runtime release notes / rollout status in the region
   - fleet changes (GPU maintenance, driver roll, node pool change)
2) Validate with runtime scheduler dashboard:
   - queue depth, admission rate, throttle events
3) Mitigate (least to most disruptive):
   - **Pause rollout** of runtime in the affected region (Release Engineering)
   - **Shift traffic** / adjust routing policy to healthy region/tier (SRE + Runtime)
   - **Scale dedicated pool** or increase reserved capacity temporarily (SRE/Infra)
   - **Drain suspect nodes** if correlated to a node pool (Infra)

Escalate to:
- Scheduling lead: **Sofia Ivanova**
- Infra/fleet: **Vanessa Ortiz**
- On-call SRE sponsor: **Sean Gallagher**


### B. Runtime-only latency increased (kernel / runtime / model config)
Signals:
- runtime-only p95 ↑ materially
- queueing time flat
- tokens/sec ↓ often accompanies runtime-only ↑

Next split:
1) **Prefill-only regression**
   - prefill_latency_p95 ↑; decode relatively stable
   - often tied to attention kernel changes for long prompts or prompt-length bucket mix sensitivity

2) **Decode-only regression**
   - decode_latency_p95 ↑; prefill stable
   - often tied to KV cache management, kernel selection for decode, or scheduler micro-batching

Likely causes:
- runtime release with new kernel selection/compilation
- kernel tag change for the model (new fused kernel, quant path, etc.)
- model config update (rope scaling, max seq len, quantization profile)

Actions:
1) Check kernel/runtime tags:
   - Compare `kernel_selection_id` and `runtime_build_id` vs baseline period.
   - If tags changed at the same time as regression, treat as prime suspect.
2) Compare against prior runtime build:
   - Use the dashboard “compare to previous release” view (if available) or manually set time ranges.
3) Mitigate:
   - **Pause runtime rollout** immediately if ongoing.
   - If regression is tied to a kernel flag/selection ID:
     - Request Kernel lead to flip selection back / disable the new kernel for that model.
   - If regression aligns with a runtime build:
     - **Rollback runtime** in the affected region to the previous stable build.
   - If regression aligns with a model config change:
     - Roll back model config/version pin for the canary target (and if already in prod, consider customer routing).

Escalate to:
- Runtime TL: **Noah Patel**
- Kernel lead: **Hiro Tanaka**
- Instrumentation: **Omar El-Sayed** (if metrics look inconsistent)


### C. Tokens/sec dropped but latency unchanged
Signals:
- tokens/sec ↓, latency panels not clearly regressed

Likely causes:
- measurement mismatch (aggregation bug, units)
- batch size distribution change (smaller batches, less continuous batching)
- GPU clock throttling / power management

Actions:
1) Check batch size histogram shift.
2) Check GPU/node health panels (throttle, ECC, utilization).
3) Verify tokens/sec computation version (dashboard panel notes).
4) If multiple models affected on same hardware/region, prioritize infra investigation.

Escalate to:
- Telemetry: **Logan Wright** / **Caleb Johnson** (if suspect metric bug)
- Infra/fleet: **Vanessa Ortiz**

---

## Step 4 — Compare observed values to baseline (sanity)
Baseline context:
- Baselines are defined per **model + model_version + runtime_version + hardware_sku** (and sometimes region).
- Alert thresholds are percentage deltas with minimum sample size.

Checklist:
1) Verify the baseline is the correct key:
   - model and version match
   - hardware SKU matches (no fallback to “unknown”)
   - runtime version/build is expected (baseline might be tied to last stable)

2) Look at variance:
   - If the baseline distribution is wide (high p95 variance), small regressions may be noise.

3) Confirm prompt-shape bucket mix matches:
   - Canary uses a predefined distribution; if it changed recently, baseline comparisons may be invalid.

If you suspect baseline mismatch/drift:
- Downgrade urgency unless customer impact is suspected.
- Escalate to Baselines owner: **Nadia Rahman**.

---

## Step 5 — Decide mitigation path
Use the following decision logic:

### If there is an active rollout (runtime, kernel, or model config)
1) **Pause the rollout** first.
2) If the signal is still present after pause and impacts a top model:
   - **Rollback** the changed component (runtime build, kernel flag, or model config) in the affected region.
3) Confirm recovery in perf-canary within 10–30 minutes (depending on evaluation window).

### If there is no active rollout
1) Check infra/fleet changes in the region (maintenance, driver, node pool).
2) If regression isolated to one SKU:
   - drain / quarantine suspect node pool (Infra)
3) If regression widespread across models and regions:
   - treat as telemetry or baseline issue until proven otherwise, but validate with spot manual perf test if available.

### Emergency stop (rare)
If perf-canary itself is suspected to be causing load or instability (e.g., QPS cap misconfigured):
- Follow **perf-canary safe-disable procedure** in the deploy/rollback runbook.
- Notify Runtime + SRE sponsor immediately.

---

## Step 6 — Evidence to capture (for fast RCA)
Paste into the incident thread:
- Dashboard links with filters applied (model, region, sku, tier)
- Before/after time ranges (baseline vs observed)
- runtime_build_id and kernel_selection_id
- Which component changed most (queueing vs runtime-only vs prefill vs decode)
- If rollback/pause performed: exact time and scope (region/tier)

If you can’t capture links, capture screenshots of:
- p95 latency panel
- queueing time panel
- tokens/sec panel
- kernel/runtime tags panel

---

## Escalation paths and ownership
Primary Slack channels:
- `#eng-oncall` (coordination)
- `#eng-sre` (SRE support)
- `#eng-runtime` (runtime/kernels/scheduler)
- `#eng-platform` (telemetry/observability)
- `#eng-releases` (rollout pause/rollback execution)

Who to page/escalate:
- Sustained regression on top model (paging alert):
  - SRE on-call + Runtime on-call (via incident-bot routing)
- Queueing/capacity/fleet indicators:
  - Infra/fleet escalation (Vanessa Ortiz) + Scheduling lead (Sofia Ivanova)
- Kernel tag change / runtime-only regression:
  - Kernel lead (Hiro Tanaka) + Runtime TL (Noah Patel)
- Baseline mismatch / drift / metric inconsistencies:
  - Nadia Rahman (baselines) + Logan Wright (telemetry) + Caleb Johnson (ingestion)

If unclear after 15 minutes on a PAGE:
- Escalate to **Sean Gallagher** (SRE sponsor) and **Mateo Alvarez** (runtime exec sponsor) with a short summary and what is blocking.

---

## Communication guidelines
- Keep incident thread updated every 10–15 minutes on paging incidents.
- Use a single owner (“incident driver”) and explicitly assign:
  - driver
  - rollback executor
  - dashboard/telemetry checker
  - runtime/kernels contact

Customer communication:
- Do not proactively message customers based solely on canary signals.
- If customer impact is confirmed via customer-facing metrics, follow the standard incident comms process.

---

## Resolution criteria
An incident can be resolved when:
- perf-canary regression clears (back within threshold) for at least one full evaluation window, and
- the suspected causal change is paused/rolled back OR the infra issue is mitigated/quarantined, and
- monitoring confirms no recurrence.

---

## Post-incident follow-ups (required)
Within 1 business day for PAGE incidents:
1) File a short RCA note (even if preliminary): what changed, what signal, what fix.
2) If rollback occurred, link the rollback artifact and open a ticket to re-introduce safely.
3) If false positive:
   - file an issue against baseline drift, label cardinality, or anti-flap tuning.
4) Update this runbook if any step was ambiguous or links were missing.

---

## Appendix: Common failure modes and what they look like
1) **Baseline drift / seasonality**
   - slow creeping alerts, multiple warnings, especially after fleet mix changes
2) **Cardinality explosion**
   - dashboards slow; alerts fire only for specific label values; sample counts look wrong
3) **Telemetry ingestion lag**
   - alert arrives late; dashboard panels have gaps; baseline comparisons inconsistent
4) **Kernel selection flip**
   - sudden step function in runtime-only latency; kernel_selection_id changes at the same timestamp
5) **Capacity event**
   - queueing time spikes; multiple models at once; region-specific

If you see a new pattern, add it here with screenshots/links and a short “how to detect” section.

2) If the signal is still present after pause and impacts a top model:
   - **Rollback** the changed component (runtime build, kernel flag, or model config) in the affected region.
3) Confirm recovery in perf-canary within 10–30 minutes (depending on evaluation window).

### If there is no active rollout
1) Check infra/fleet changes in the region (maintenance, driver, node pool).
2) If regression isolated to one SKU:
   - drain / quarantine suspect node pool (Infra)
3) If regression widespread across models and regions:
   - treat as telemetry or baseline issue until proven otherwise, but validate with spot manual perf test if available.

### Emergency stop (rare)
If perf-canary itself is suspected to be causing load or instability (e.g., QPS cap misconfigured):
- Follow **perf-canary safe-disable procedure** in the deploy/rollback runbook.
- Notify Runtime + SRE sponsor immediately.

---

## Step 6 — Evidence to capture (for fast RCA)
Paste into the incident thread:
- Dashboard links with filters applied (model, region, sku, tier)
- Before/after time ranges (baseline vs observed)
- runtime_build_id and kernel_selection_id
- Which component changed most (queueing vs runtime-only vs prefill vs decode)
- If rollback/pause performed: exact time and scope (region/tier)

If you can’t capture links, capture screenshots of:
- p95 latency panel
- queueing time panel
- tokens/sec panel
- kernel/runtime tags panel

---

## Escalation paths and ownership
Primary Slack channels:
- `#eng-oncall` (coordination)
- `#eng-sre` (SRE support)
- `#eng-runtime` (runtime/kernels/scheduler)
- `#eng-platform` (telemetry/observability)
- `#eng-releases` (rollout pause/rollback execution)

Who to page/escalate:
- Sustained regression on top model (paging alert):
  - SRE on-call + Runtime on-call (via incident-bot routing)
- Queueing/capacity/fleet indicators:
  - Infra/fleet escalation (Vanessa Ortiz) + Scheduling lead (Sofia Ivanova)
- Kernel tag change / runtime-only regression:
  - Kernel lead (Hiro Tanaka) + Runtime TL (Noah Patel)
- Baseline mismatch / drift / metric inconsistencies:
  - Nadia Rahman (baselines) + Logan Wright (telemetry) + Caleb Johnson (ingestion)

If unclear after 15 minutes on a PAGE:
- Escalate to **Sean Gallagher** (SRE sponsor) and **Mateo Alvarez** (runtime exec sponsor) with a short summary and what is blocking.

---

## Communication guidelines
- Keep incident thread updated every 10–15 minutes on paging incidents.
- Use a single owner (“incident driver”) and explicitly assign:
  - driver
  - rollback executor
  - dashboard/telemetry checker
  - runtime/kernels contact

Customer communication:
- Do not proactively message customers based solely on canary signals.
- If customer impact is confirmed via customer-facing metrics, follow the standard incident comms process.

---

## Resolution criteria
An incident can be resolved when:
- perf-canary regression clears (back within threshold) for at least one full evaluation window, and
- the suspected causal change is paused/rolled back OR the infra issue is mitigated/quarantined, and
- monitoring confirms no recurrence.

---

## Post-incident follow-ups (required)
Within 1 business day for PAGE incidents:
1) File a short RCA note (even if preliminary): what changed, what signal, what fix.
2) If rollback occurred, link the rollback artifact and open a ticket to re-introduce safely.
3) If false positive:
   - file an issue against baseline drift, label cardinality, or anti-flap tuning.
4) Update this runbook if any step was ambiguous or links were missing.

---

## Appendix: Common failure modes and what they look like
1) **Baseline drift / seasonality**
   - slow creeping alerts, multiple warnings, especially after fleet mix changes
2) **Cardinality explosion**
   - dashboards slow; alerts fire only for specific label values; sample counts look wrong
3) **Telemetry ingestion lag**
   - alert arrives late; dashboard panels have gaps; baseline comparisons inconsistent
4) **Kernel selection flip**
   - sudden step function in runtime-only latency; kernel_selection_id changes at the same timestamp
5) **Capacity event**
   - queueing time spikes; multiple models at once; region-specific

If you see a new pattern, add it here with screenshots/links and a short “how to detect” section.

title: Hosted API tail-latency guardrails for KV cache compaction/eviction rollout
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines SRE-facing guardrails to protect Hosted API tail latency (p95/p99) during the staged rollout of KV cache compaction + tier-aware eviction in the serving runtime.

The rollout introduces new runtime behaviors (budgeted compaction work and eviction under pressure) that can improve long-tail performance under bursty mixed-length decode workloads, but can also regress latency if:
- compaction work is performed on the critical path more than intended,
- eviction selection becomes too aggressive for certain traffic mixes,
- allocator state oscillates (fragmentation ↔ compaction ↔ eviction) and increases CPU/GPU overhead,
- cancellation/streaming edge cases increase lock contention.

This page standardizes:
- **SLO targets** and what counts as a rollback-worthy regression
- **Alert thresholds** and paging policy during the canary
- **Automated rollback criteria** (progressive delivery gates)
- **Human-in-the-loop authority** for kill-switch use

## Scope
**In scope:** Hosted API runtime pools (hosted multi-tenant), including chat/text generation routes that exercise long decode windows.

**Out of scope:** Dedicated and Private deployments (separate guardrails; see their rollout checklists).

## Definitions (what we measure)
We track end-to-end request latencies and compaction/eviction health signals. For the compaction rollout, we explicitly gate on **tail latency** and **error/oom/request-shedding** signals.

### Latency SLIs (Hosted API)
1) **TTFT (time to first token)** for streaming generation routes
- Sensitive to scheduler contention and any synchronous compaction budget.

2) **E2E request latency** for non-streaming requests and for “completion finished” in streaming
- Sensitive to decode slowdowns, cache pressure, and eviction thrash.

### Runtime health SLIs
- **Fragmentation ratio**: indicates KV arena health and compaction effectiveness.
- **Compaction work**: bytes moved and time spent moving blocks/pages.
- **Evictions**: count/rate; by tier and reason.
- **Allocator pressure**: OOM-prevent events, forced sheds, and emergency fallbacks.

Metric names and labels must follow: confluence://architecture-and-standards/performance-standards/kv-cache-fragmentation-metrics-standard

## SLO targets (Hosted API)
These are the operational targets used for canary gating. They are intentionally conservative; Product may maintain separate external messaging.

### Primary SLO targets (canary gating)
Applies per region (us-east, us-west, eu-west) and per primary model family pool.

| SLI | Target (steady state) | Canary regression budget | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| TTFT p99 (streaming) | <= 1.2s | +10% vs baseline for <= 10 min allowed | Gated on change vs baseline to avoid false positives due to diurnal load |
| E2E p99 (streaming “finished”) | <= 12.0s | +10% vs baseline for <= 10 min allowed | Use normalized cohorts; exclude client disconnects |
| E2E p99 (non-streaming) | <= 8.0s | +10% vs baseline for <= 10 min allowed | Only for routes where non-streaming is supported |
| Error rate (5xx) | <= 0.20% | >0.30% for 5 min triggers rollback | Measured at inference-gateway and runtime-worker |
| Shed rate (overload / forced drop) | <= 0.05% | >0.10% for 5 min triggers rollback | Includes admission control drops and “no capacity” |

### Secondary targets (investigation triggers)
These do not automatically roll back by themselves, but they should trigger an SRE+Runtime investigation and may be used as supporting evidence.

| Signal | Expected range during canary | Investigation threshold |
|---|---:|---:|
| KV fragmentation ratio (median) | 0.10–0.25 | >= 0.35 for 10 min (per pool) |
| Compaction time p99 (per step) | <= 2ms | > 5ms for 5 min |
| Compaction bytes moved rate | workload-dependent | sudden sustained > 4 GiB/min per worker (10 min) |
| Eviction rate (all reasons) | near-zero normally | sustained > 50 evictions/min/worker (10 min) |
| Evictions of non-preemptible sequences | 0 | any > 0 is a stop-ship bug |

## Canary strategy (Hosted API)
Rollout is progressive, by region and pool. A typical progression:
1) **Internal canary**: 1% of workers in one low-risk region/pool
2) **Hosted subset**: 5% → 10% in one region
3) **Multi-region**: 10% in each region
4) **Expand**: 25% → 50% → 100% (per pool)

Guardrails apply at every stage; rollback should be localized to the pool/region cohort when possible.

## Alerting policy during the canary
We run both standard Hosted API alerts and compaction-specific alerts. During canary, compaction alerts are routed to the **#eng-sre** oncall and the **#eng-runtime** secondary.

### Paging vs ticketing
- **Page (P1)** when user impact is likely or ongoing:
  - p99 latency regression gate violated (see Automated rollback criteria)
  - 5xx error gate violated
  - shed/forced-drop gate violated
  - allocator OOM/forced shed increases (even if 5xx not yet elevated)

- **Create ticket (P2/P3)** for compaction efficiency issues without user impact:
  - fragmentation remains high but latency is stable
  - compaction bytes moved is higher than expected, but within budget
  - eviction rate increases but no latency or error impact

### Recommended alert thresholds (initial rollout)
Note: thresholds are expressed as *per cohort/pool* wherever possible to avoid masking.

1) Tail latency regression (page)
- **TTFT p99**: > baseline * 1.10 for 10 minutes
- **E2E p99**: > baseline * 1.10 for 10 minutes

2) Errors and shedding (page)
- **HTTP 5xx** (inference-gateway): > 0.30% for 5 minutes
- **runtime_worker.request_failed_total / request_total**: > 0.30% for 5 minutes
- **shed rate**: > 0.10% for 5 minutes

3) Compaction/eviction safety (page)
- **kv_allocator_oom_prevent_total**: any non-zero sustained increase vs baseline for 2 minutes (cohort)
- **kv_evictions_total{eviction_class="non_preemptible"}**: any non-zero (immediate page; stop rollout)
- **kv_relocation_errors_total**: any non-zero (immediate page; stop rollout)

4) Compaction budget overrun (ticket unless correlated with latency)
- **kv_compaction_step_time_ms_p99**: > 5ms for 5 minutes
- **kv_compaction_bytes_moved_total rate**: sustained > 4 GiB/min/worker for 10 minutes

## Automated rollback criteria (progressive delivery gates)
Rollout automation (Paula Mendes / Rollout system) should continuously evaluate the following gates for the canary cohort.

### Hard rollback gates (automatic rollback)
If any gate is violated, rollback the feature flag to the previous stable state for the cohort and freeze expansion.

A) Tail latency regression
- Condition: (TTFT p99 > baseline * 1.10) OR (E2E p99 > baseline * 1.10)
- Window: 10 minutes rolling
- Baseline: previous 24h median for same weekday/time bucket OR the pre-canary 60-minute window (whichever is more stable for that pool)
- Action: rollback flag in cohort; notify #incidents and open an incident if user-visible

B) User-facing failures
- Condition: HTTP 5xx > 0.30% OR shed rate > 0.10%
- Window: 5 minutes rolling
- Action: rollback flag in cohort; page SRE oncall

C) Allocator pressure spike
- Condition: kv_allocator_oom_prevent_total rate > baseline + 0.05/min/worker (or any sharp step-change when baseline is ~0)
- Window: 2 minutes rolling
- Action: rollback flag in cohort; page SRE+Runtime

D) Safety invariants
- Condition: kv_evictions_total{eviction_class="non_preemptible"} > 0 OR kv_relocation_errors_total > 0
- Window: 1 minute rolling
- Action: rollback + stop-ship; page Runtime TL and SRE oncall

### Soft gates (pause expansion; human review)
These do not roll back automatically, but they should prevent further expansion until reviewed.

E) High fragmentation not improving
- Condition: kv_cache_fragmentation_ratio_p50 >= 0.35 AND no downward trend for 30 minutes
- Action: pause expansion; request Runtime to validate thresholds and compaction trigger logic

F) High eviction without user impact
- Condition: kv_evictions_total rate increases > 5x baseline for 20 minutes
- Action: pause expansion; check if eviction is masking a deeper capacity issue (admission control, traffic burst)

## Operational playbook (during a canary)
### 1) Triage order
When alerted, triage in this order:
1. **Is user impact real?** Check inference-gateway p99, TTFT p99, 5xx, shed.
2. **Is the impact localized to the canary cohort/pool?** Compare canary vs control pools.
3. **Is the runtime doing too much compaction on the hot path?** Check compaction step time p99 and bytes moved.
4. **Is eviction thrashing?** Check evictions/min, and whether evictions correlate with TTFT/E2E spikes.
5. **Is fragmentation climbing despite compaction?** Check fragmentation ratio trend and allocator free-list stats.

### 2) Immediate mitigations
- **Rollback the cohort flag** (preferred).
- If rollback is too slow due to config propagation, use the **kill-switch** (see Authority + kill-switch policy).
- If the pool is near capacity, consider temporary actions:
  - increase headroom (shift traffic, add workers)
  - tighten admission control (prefer protecting p99 over accepting excess load)

### 3) Post-rollback verification
Within 15 minutes of rollback:
- p99 metrics should converge toward baseline.
- compaction/eviction metrics should return to control-like patterns.
- confirm config propagation completion across workers.

Document any rollback in the incident channel and link:
- pool/region
- timestamps
- graphs (latency + compaction + eviction)
- flag states

## Authority and kill-switch policy
### Who can flip the kill-switch
- **Primary:** SRE oncall (authority to protect SLOs)
- **Secondary:** Runtime TL oncall (or Omar El-Sayed / Sofia Ivanova as delegates)

### When to flip the kill-switch (vs cohort rollback)
Flip the kill-switch if:
- automated rollback has not stabilized p99 within 10–15 minutes, OR
- config propagation is lagging and impact is increasing, OR
- safety invariants are violated (non-preemptible eviction, relocation errors)

Kill-switch behavior must be fail-safe:
- disables compaction work scheduling immediately
- disables new evictions except emergency OOM-prevent mode (per ADR)
- leaves existing sequences correct (no partial relocation)

See: confluence://eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-kv-cache-compaction-runbook

## Dashboard and query pointers
Primary dashboard panels required for the canary (per pool/region):
- TTFT p95/p99 (streaming)
- E2E p95/p99
- 5xx rate, shed rate
- fragmentation ratio p50/p95
- compaction bytes moved (rate), compaction step time p95/p99
- evictions (rate) by tier and reason
- allocator OOM-prevent events (rate)

Example PromQL-style queries (names may vary by cluster; confirm in metrics standard):
- TTFT p99: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (le, pool) (rate(hosted_ttft_ms_bucket{route=~"chat|generate", cohort="canary"}[5m])))
- Fragmentation ratio: avg by (pool) (kv_cache_fragmentation_ratio{cohort="canary"})
- Compaction step p99: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (le, pool) (rate(kv_compaction_step_time_ms_bucket{cohort="canary"}[5m])))
- Eviction rate: sum by (pool, tier, reason) (rate(kv_evictions_total{cohort="canary"}[5m]))

## Change management and review cadence
During rollout weeks:
- Daily 15-minute check (SRE + Runtime) focusing on p99 deltas and any eviction anomalies.
- Any threshold changes must be documented in:
  - the rollout ticket (runtime-1.22)
  - the metrics standard (if naming/definition changes)

## Known failure modes (what to watch for)
- **Compaction budget creep:** step time p99 rises; TTFT p99 rises first.
- **Eviction masking capacity issues:** eviction rate rises but fragmentation falls; watch shed rate and overall throughput.
- **Fragmentation not improving:** fragmentation ratio stays elevated; compaction bytes moved climbs; possible free-list/coalescing bug.
- **Cancellation edge cases:** relocation errors or allocator leaks; watch kv_relocation_errors_total and worker restarts.

## Contacts
- SRE owner: Sean Gallagher
- Observability/metrics: Allison Grant
- Runtime TL (KV cache): Omar El-Sayed
- Oncall readiness/runbooks: Rafael Mendes
- Rollout automation/gates: Paula Mendes / Marissa Cole

## Revision history
- 2026-01-20: Initial published version for runtime-1.22 canary gates.
- 2026-02-12: Updated hard rollback gates to include allocator OOM-prevent spike and clarified baseline selection guidance.

title: Multi-Region Availability SLO (2026) — Hosted API + Dedicated
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s **multi-region availability SLOs** for 2026, covering:
- **Hosted API** (shared, multi-tenant)
- **Dedicated** (reserved capacity, tenant-isolated)

The intent is to:
- Measure customer-perceived availability when traffic is eligible for **regional failover**.
- Provide clear criteria to distinguish **region-level impairment** (automation-relevant) from partial/localized degradation.
- Establish consistent **error budget policies** (alerting, operational response, change management).

This SLO is an internal engineering target and is not a replacement for customer contract language.

---

## In scope
### Products / request paths
**Hosted API (paid tiers):**
- /v1/chat.completions (streaming + non-streaming)
- /v1/completions
- /v1/embeddings
- /v1/rerank

**Dedicated (multi-region enabled tenants only):**
- Dedicated endpoints serving the same route families above, behind Dedicated routing policies.

### Region eligibility
This SLO applies only to traffic that is:
- Routed via Redwood smart routing with **at least one configured failover region**, and
- Not constrained by data residency / policy constraints that prevent routing to the configured secondary.

If a tenant is configured single-region only, it is excluded from the multi-region SLO population (but remains covered by single-region service SLOs).

---

## Definitions
### Availability (customer-perceived)
A request is considered **available** if the Redwood edge/gateway returns a successful response within the request’s budget:
- **Success:** HTTP 2xx or 3xx (including 304) from the customer perspective.
- **Unavailable:** HTTP 5xx, gateway timeouts (e.g., 504), and explicit overload rejections (429/503) *when they prevent the route from being served*.

Notes:
- **Authentication/authorization failures** (401/403) are excluded from availability calculations.
- **Customer-caused invalid requests** (400/404/422) are excluded.
- **Rate limiting** (429) is excluded only when it is attributable to customer quota/rate policy; 429/503 generated due to platform overload is included.

### Multi-region event
A **multi-region event** is any incident where customers eligible for failover experience reduced availability due to a regional impairment or the failover system’s inability to route around it.

### Region-level event vs partial degradation
We classify an issue as a **region-level event** when it represents broad impairment of a region’s ability to serve eligible traffic and is a candidate for automated failover.

We classify as **partial degradation** when the issue is localized (single service, single model family, subset of tenants, or capacity-only without elevated errors) and is typically mitigated by targeted remediation rather than cross-region failover.

---

## SLO targets (2026)
### Summary table
| Surface | Population | SLO (availability) | Measurement window |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Hosted API multi-region | Paid tiers, MR-eligible routes | 99.95% | Rolling 30 days |
| Dedicated multi-region | MR-enabled Dedicated tenants/routes | 99.90% | Rolling 30 days |

Rationale:
- Hosted API is expected to sustain higher aggregate availability due to broader regional redundancy and standardized routing.
- Dedicated multi-region typically has fewer configured regions and stricter per-tenant constraints; target is set accordingly.

---

## SLI specification
### Hosted API SLI
**Indicator:** Request success rate at the **API gateway edge** for MR-eligible traffic.

**Good events:**
- Count of requests with status_class in {2xx,3xx}

**Bad events:**
- Count of requests with status_class in {5xx} plus overload responses attributable to platform (429/503 tagged as overload)

**Excluded events:**
- 401/403
- 4xx invalid request classes (400/404/422)
- 429 due to tenant quota/rate policy (non-overload)

**Segmentation (reporting slices; not all are alerting dimensions):**
- route_family: chat.completions | completions | embeddings | rerank
- tier: paid (default), enterprise (subset)
- traffic_class: interactive | batch (where applicable)

**Notes on streaming:**
- A streaming request is counted as successful if it returns a 2xx and delivers the first token/byte within the configured gateway budget.
- Mid-stream disconnects are tracked separately as a quality metric; only disconnects that manifest as a 5xx to the client count as unavailable.

### Dedicated SLI
**Indicator:** Request success rate at the Dedicated gateway/edge for MR-enabled tenants.

**Good events:** 2xx/3xx

**Bad events:** 5xx + overload responses attributable to platform

**Excluded events:** same as Hosted.

**Tenant segmentation policy:**
- SLO measurement is computed at the fleet level for MR-enabled Dedicated traffic.
- Per-tenant reporting is available via internal dashboards/audit queries but is not used for global burn-rate paging due to cardinality.

---

## Measurement windows and reporting
### Primary compliance window
- **Rolling 30-day window** for SLO compliance tracking (Hosted and Dedicated).

### Secondary operational windows
Used for detection and burn-rate alerting:
- 5m, 30m, 2h, 6h windows (fast burn)
- 1d and 7d windows (slow burn)

We use multi-window burn-rate alerting to catch both sharp outages and prolonged degradations.

---

## Error budget policy
### Error budget math
Error budget = 1 - SLO target.
- Hosted API MR error budget: 0.05% per 30d
- Dedicated MR error budget: 0.10% per 30d

### Error budget consumption and required actions
| Burn (30d budget) | Interpretation | Required actions |
|---:|---|---|
| < 25% | Healthy | No special actions. Reliability work continues in normal sprint planning. |
| 25–50% | Elevated | SRE + owning teams review top contributors (routes/regions). Add/adjust alerts, dashboards, and mitigations. |
| 50–100% | At risk | Reliability escalation: prioritize mitigations in current cycle. Non-critical launches to routing/failover may require SRE review. |
| > 100% | Breached | Declare SLO breach internally; incident review required. Change freeze on failover/routing automation except for reliability fixes and approved mitigations. |

### Change management when budgets are at risk
- If budget burn is >50% for Hosted or Dedicated MR, changes that can materially affect routing/failover behavior require **SRE on-call reviewer signoff**.
- If breached (>100%), a temporary **reliability freeze** applies to:
  - routing policy engine behavior changes
  - failover controller automation changes
  - DNS/traffic steering changes
  - gateway retry/timeout behavior changes

Exceptions:
- Fix-forward changes that clearly reduce customer impact may proceed with Director SRE (Sean Gallagher) approval.

---

## What constitutes a region-level event (classification rules)
Region-level events are used for:
- automation trigger validation
- incident labeling and reporting
- executive reliability reporting

### Region-level event (candidate for automated failover)
A region is considered **impaired** when BOTH are true:
1) **Corroborated reachability or control-plane impairment**
   - Cross-region probes indicate reachability failure OR
   - Control plane cannot program routing for the region OR
   - Provider-level networking symptoms strongly indicate regional impairment

AND
2) **Customer impact signal in that region’s serving path**
   - Elevated 5xx error burn above threshold for MR-eligible routes OR
   - Sustained tail latency burn above threshold AND corroborated by additional signal (e.g., rising timeouts, saturation)

Minimum duration (to avoid flapping/noise):
- Must persist for **≥ 2 minutes** after dampening/hold-down rules (see ADR-042).

### Partial degradation (not a region-level event)
Classify as partial degradation when one or more of the following holds:
- Impact is limited to a single model/version (e.g., one embedding model) while other routes remain healthy.
- Impact is isolated to a subset of tenants (e.g., one Dedicated tenant) without evidence of broad regional impairment.
- Capacity headroom is low but error/timeout signals are not elevated (mitigate with autoscaling/queueing rather than failover).
- A single internal dependency is degraded (e.g., metrics pipeline), but serving remains healthy.

### Event severity mapping (operational)
| Classification | Typical response | Paging |
|---|---|---|
| Region-level impairment | Consider/execute regional failover per runbook; enable controller if safe | Page SRE on-call (high urgency) |
| Partial degradation | Targeted mitigation; may reduce capacity, roll back model, or adjust autoscaling | Page owning team; SRE if customer-facing and sustained |

---

## Alerting policy (burn-rate)
Burn-rate alerts are defined to detect rapid SLO consumption.

### Hosted MR availability burn alerts
- **Fast burn:** 2h window at 14x budget AND 30m at 14x budget (page)
- **Medium burn:** 6h at 6x budget AND 2h at 6x budget (page)
- **Slow burn:** 1d at 3x budget AND 6h at 3x budget (ticket + review)

### Dedicated MR availability burn alerts
- **Fast burn:** 2h at 10x AND 30m at 10x (page)
- **Slow burn:** 1d at 2x AND 6h at 2x (ticket + review)

Alert annotations must include:
- affected route_family
- top region(s) by error contribution
- current failover state (policy flip status)
- links to region health dashboard + incident-bot timeline

(See: confluence://eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/failover-signal-dashboards-spec)

---

## Planned events, tests, and exclusions
### General rule
We do **not** exclude planned events by default. If customers see errors, it counts.

### Controlled failover tests (“game days”)
For production tests:
- Tests must be designed to keep customer-visible impact within SLO expectations.
- We **tag and report** impact separately (for learning), but it still counts toward the SLO.

For staging and canary-only tests:
- Not included in production SLO measurement.

Dedicated exception (opt-in tests):
- If a Dedicated customer explicitly opts into a planned failover test window and acknowledges expected behavior, the window may be excluded from *internal Dedicated MR SLO reporting* for that customer’s slice, but the global fleet metric remains unchanged.
- All exclusions require a change record and must be documented in the game day plan.

---

## Data sources and example queries
Primary data source: gateway request metrics emitted by the edge and aggregated in the SLO toolkit.

### Hosted MR availability (conceptual query)
Availability = good / (good + bad)
- good: sum(rate(api_gateway_requests_total{population="mr_eligible", outcome="good"}[5m]))
- bad:  sum(rate(api_gateway_requests_total{population="mr_eligible", outcome="bad"}[5m]))

### Dedicated MR availability (conceptual query)
- good: sum(rate(dedicated_gateway_requests_total{population="mr_enabled", outcome="good"}[5m]))
- bad:  sum(rate(dedicated_gateway_requests_total{population="mr_enabled", outcome="bad"}[5m]))

Labeling guidance (cardinality constraints):
- Allowed: route_family, tier, region_group, failover_state
- Avoid in global SLO metrics: tenant_id, model_id, deployment_id

---

## Operational expectations and ownership
### Owners
- SLO definition and reporting: **Eng SRE** (owner: Aisha Bello)
- Burn-rate alert rules and dashboards: **Observability** (Allison Grant / Sergio Costa)
- Routing + failover behavior: **Smart Routing** (Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon)
- Region health aggregation: **Platform** (Ethan Park)

### Runbook linkage
When a Hosted or Dedicated MR burn alert fires, follow:
- Hosted: confluence://eng-sre/runbooks/hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook
- Controller operations: confluence://eng-platform/runbooks/routing-policy-failover-controller-runbook
- Dedicated drain/evacuation: confluence://eng-infra/runbooks/gpu-pool-drain-and-evacuation-procedure

---

## Review cadence
- Quarterly review of targets and measurement logic.
- Post-incident review after any region-level event that consumes >10% of monthly error budget.
- Annual update each December for the next year’s SLO plan.

---

## Change log
- 2025-12-18: Initial 2026 definition drafted (Hosted + Dedicated MR populations).
- 2026-01-06: Clarified streaming success criteria and overload inclusion.
- 2026-01-22: Added Dedicated opt-in planned test exclusion guidance; tightened classification rules.
- 2026-02-10: Updated burn-rate alert multipliers to match current paging hygiene guidance; added labeling/cardinality constraints.
