title: Hosted API Tail Latency SLO Guardrails (Mixed-Workload Scheduler Canary)
preview:
h1. Purpose
This page defines SRE guardrails for enabling the *mixed-workload scheduler prototype* in the *Hosted API* environment. The goal is to reduce user-visible tail latency under mixed short/long prompt distributions while keeping reliability and unit economics within acceptable bounds.

This document is specifically for *canary and ramp phases* (feature-flagged). For steady-state SLO definitions and reporting, see the canonical Hosted API SLO pages.

h1. Scope
*In scope*
* Hosted API traffic served by Redwood inference pools (regional) where the scheduler prototype is enabled behind a feature flag.
* Tail latency guardrails: p95/p99 drift, rollback triggers, and error-budget considerations.
* Regions/pools: initial canary region(s) and subsequent ramps.

*Out of scope*
* Dedicated and Private environments (separate guardrails; may reuse thresholds with different blast radius assumptions).
* Full QoS productization or customer-tier fairness policies.

h1. Definitions
* *p95/p99 latency:* percentile latency over a defined aggregation window.
* *TTFT:* time-to-first-token (streaming).
* *E2E latency:* request start to completion (non-streaming or full-stream completion time).
* *Canary pool:* subset of capacity (e.g., 1–5% of traffic or a small set of GPU pools) with feature enabled.
* *Baseline:* comparable period immediately prior to enabling the canary (same day-of-week/time-of-day) or a matched control pool (preferred).
* *Drift:* (canary metric - baseline metric) / baseline metric.

h1. Metrics to monitor (minimum set)
The canary is considered safe only if ALL of the following remain within thresholds.

h2. User-visible tail latency
Primary user-visible signals (evaluate by route/model where possible):
* Chat/completions *TTFT p95* and *TTFT p99*
* Chat/completions *E2E p95* and *E2E p99*
* Non-streaming generation *E2E p95/p99* (if applicable for specific endpoints)

Recommended dimensions:
* region, pool, model, route (chat/completions), request_type (streaming/non-streaming)

h2. Queueing and scheduler health (guardrail signals)
These are leading indicators and should be watched during ramp windows:
* Queue time p95 and p99 (overall and by scheduler class)
* Per-class queue depth (short vs long)
* Starvation counters / aging-trigger counters
* Dispatch share by class (fraction of iterations serviced)
* Prefill latency p95/p99 and decode iteration latency p95/p99

h2. Reliability and correctness
* HTTP 5xx rate (overall, by route)
* Timeout rate / client disconnect rate
* Retry rate (if applicable)
* GPU OOM / kernel errors / runtime crash loops

h2. Unit economics (secondary guardrail)
This is not a rollback trigger unless extreme, but should be tracked:
* Tokens/sec per GPU (or equivalent throughput)
* Cost-per-1K-tokens proxy metrics (internal)

h1. Guardrail thresholds (Hosted API canary)
Thresholds below assume the canary is *feature-flagged and reversible within minutes*.

h2. Evaluation windows
Use all three views to avoid missing brief spikes or slow burns:
1) *5-minute* window (fast detection of immediate regressions)
2) *30-minute* window (stability during sustained load)
3) *2-hour* window (captures diurnal patterns during ramp)

Baseline selection:
* Preferred: control pool in same region with feature disabled.
* Acceptable: immediate pre-change baseline (same day) if control is not available.

h2. Allowed drift thresholds (tail latency)
The canary must remain within the following *relative drift* limits compared to baseline/control.

|| Metric || 5-min allowed drift || 30-min allowed drift || 2-hr allowed drift || Notes ||
| TTFT p95 | +5% | +3% | +2% | TTFT is most user-visible for chat; treat as highest priority. |
| TTFT p99 | +10% | +7% | +5% | p99 is noisy; sustained drift is the key. |
| E2E p95 | +7% | +5% | +3% | Applies to streaming completion time and non-streaming E2E. |
| E2E p99 | +12% | +10% | +7% | Use additional sanity check by route/model to isolate hotspots. |

Absolute floor checks (to avoid “small relative drift” hiding large absolute impact):
* If TTFT p95 increases by *> +75ms* for a major route (top 3 by QPS) in a region, treat as a guardrail breach even if relative drift is within % thresholds.
* If TTFT p99 increases by *> +200ms* sustained over 30 minutes, treat as a guardrail breach.

h2. SLO error-budget considerations
We do not “spend” tail-latency error budget casually during canary. Guardrails tie directly to error-budget burn to prevent a slow rollout from consuming monthly budget.

Policy:
* If the canary causes an estimated *tail-latency SLO burn rate > 2x* the normal regional burn rate over a *30-minute* window, pause ramp.
* If the canary causes an estimated *tail-latency SLO burn rate > 5x* over any *15-minute* window, rollback immediately.
* If a region is already in an *error budget watch* state (burn > 1x over last 6 hours), do not expand canary in that region; use a healthier region or defer.

Notes:
* Tail latency SLO burn is computed using the existing SLO pipeline; if per-route/per-model SLO is unavailable, use the closest aggregated service-level SLO but require additional drill-down by route/model for confirmation.

h1. Rollback triggers
Rollback means: disable the scheduler feature flag in the impacted pool(s) and return to baseline scheduling policy. If rollback is not immediately possible due to config propagation issues, apply the “safe mode” parameter profile (see below) and page runtime owner.

h2. Immediate rollback (page SRE on-call + runtime owner)
Trigger any of the following:
* TTFT p95 drift > +10% for 2 consecutive 5-minute windows in the canary pool.
* TTFT p99 drift > +15% for 2 consecutive 5-minute windows.
* E2E p95 drift > +12% for 2 consecutive 5-minute windows.
* Any sustained *5xx* increase > +0.2% absolute over 10 minutes attributable to the canary pool.
* Any sign of scheduler starvation defect:
  * Starvation counter increasing continuously for > 10 minutes, or
  * Long-class queue depth grows monotonically for > 15 minutes while short-class remains stable, indicating long-class not making progress.
* GPU/runtime stability breach:
  * Crash loop, repeated kernel errors, OOM spikes correlated to canary enablement.

h2. Pause ramp (hold at current %) and investigate
Trigger any of the following:
* TTFT p95 drift > +5% sustained over 30 minutes.
* Tail-latency SLO burn rate > 2x sustained over 30 minutes.
* Queue time p95 drift > +20% sustained over 30 minutes.
* Throughput regression > 5% (tokens/sec per GPU) sustained over 2 hours with no corresponding tail-latency win.

h2. Proceed criteria (required to ramp up)
All must be true:
* No immediate rollback triggers observed in last 60 minutes.
* TTFT p95 drift <= +3% over last 30 minutes.
* TTFT p99 drift <= +7% over last 30 minutes.
* Starvation counter stable (no sustained increases) and long-class is making measurable progress.
* SLO burn rate not elevated (> 1.5x) over last 2 hours.

h1. Region and pool selection guardrails
To limit blast radius and reduce confounding:
* Start in *one region* with historically stable tail latency and sufficient headroom.
* Prefer pools with *mixed workload* signature (observed queue-time variability) to validate improvement, but avoid regions currently handling incident response or capacity constraints.
* Avoid ramping during known traffic peaks unless explicitly planned and staffed.

Suggested ramp sequence (Hosted API):
1) 1% traffic to canary pool (or smallest viable pool) for 2 hours
2) 5% for 4 hours
3) 10% for 24 hours (includes at least one peak period)
4) 25% for 24–48 hours
5) 50% and then 100% after approval checkpoint

Approval checkpoints:
* 10% -> 25% requires SRE + Serving Runtime sign-off.
* 25% -> 50% requires SRE sponsor sign-off.
* 50% -> 100% requires SRE sponsor + product stakeholder acknowledgement (Hosted API).

h1. Safe mode parameter profile (if rollback is delayed)
If the feature flag cannot be disabled quickly, apply a conservative profile to reduce tail risk:
* Reduce short/long skew (increase long share) to prevent starvation.
* Enable/strengthen aging (faster promotion of long requests).
* Tighten per-iteration token budget caps to limit long-prefill dominance.

Exact config keys differ by runtime version; reference the rollout runbook for the current knobs and validated safe bounds.

h1. Operational procedure during canary
h2. Pre-flight checklist (before enabling)
1) Confirm dashboards are live for the target region/pool.
2) Confirm control pool exists (feature OFF) and is comparable.
3) Confirm on-call coverage:
   * SRE on-call aware
   * Serving Runtime on-call aware
   * Release coordinator assigned for the ramp window
4) Confirm rollback mechanism:
   * Feature flag path validated (dry-run in staging if available)
   * Config propagation time understood (< 5 minutes target)

h2. During enablement (first 30 minutes)
1) Watch TTFT p95/p99 and queue time p95/p99 at 1–5 minute resolution.
2) Watch starvation counters and per-class queue depth.
3) If a breach occurs, rollback immediately and annotate the timeline.

h2. Post-enable monitoring (next 2–24 hours)
1) Check 30-min and 2-hr windows against drift thresholds.
2) Slice by top routes/models to catch localized regressions.
3) Confirm tail-latency improvement under mixed workloads does not hide regressions under homogeneous traffic.

h1. Alerting guidance (canary-specific)
During the canary window, enable temporary alerts scoped to the canary pool:
* TTFT p95 drift alert: > +5% for 15 minutes
* TTFT p99 drift alert: > +10% for 15 minutes
* Queue time p95 drift alert: > +20% for 15 minutes
* Starvation counter rate alert: increasing for 10 minutes
* 5xx absolute increase alert: > +0.2% for 10 minutes

Routing:
* Page SRE on-call for immediate rollback triggers.
* Notify #eng-runtime and #eng-sre for pause-ramp triggers.

h1. Logging and incident hygiene
* Every enable/disable action must include:
  * timestamp
  * region/pool
  * feature flag state
  * parameter profile (if changed)
  * reason (ramp step / rollback)
* For any rollback:
  * create an incident ticket if user impact exceeded SLO burn thresholds or if rollback was due to correctness/stability risk
  * include dashboard links and the exact guardrail breach(s)

h1. Ownership and escalation
* Primary owner: *SRE* (tail latency SLO compliance)
* Runtime owner: *Serving Runtime Scheduling* (feature behavior and parameter tuning)

Escalation path:
1) SRE on-call (rollback authority)
2) Serving Runtime on-call (root-cause and mitigation)
3) SRE sponsor (Sean Gallagher) for ramp decisions above 10%
4) Executive sponsor (Mateo Alvarez) if the canary blocks a committed release or impacts enterprise commitments

h1. Change log
* 2025-12-04: Initial guardrails published for Hosted API canary (v0).
* 2026-01-12: Added absolute TTFT floor checks and clarified burn-rate gating.
* 2026-02-10: Updated proceed criteria and added safe mode guidance for delayed rollback scenarios.
text:
h1. Purpose
This page defines SRE guardrails for enabling the *mixed-workload scheduler prototype* in the *Hosted API* environment. The goal is to reduce user-visible tail latency under mixed short/long prompt distributions while keeping reliability and unit economics within acceptable bounds.

This document is specifically for *canary and ramp phases* (feature-flagged). For steady-state SLO definitions and reporting, see the canonical Hosted API SLO pages.

h1. Scope
*In scope*
* Hosted API traffic served by Redwood inference pools (regional) where the scheduler prototype is enabled behind a feature flag.
* Tail latency guardrails: p95/p99 drift, rollback triggers, and error-budget considerations.
* Regions/pools: initial canary region(s) and subsequent ramps.

*Out of scope*
* Dedicated and Private environments (separate guardrails; may reuse thresholds with different blast radius assumptions).
* Full QoS productization or customer-tier fairness policies.

h1. Definitions
* *p95/p99 latency:* percentile latency over a defined aggregation window.
* *TTFT:* time-to-first-token (streaming).
* *E2E latency:* request start to completion (non-streaming or full-stream completion time).
* *Canary pool:* subset of capacity (e.g., 1–5% of traffic or a small set of GPU pools) with feature enabled.
* *Baseline:* comparable period immediately prior to enabling the canary (same day-of-week/time-of-day) or a matched control pool (preferred).
* *Drift:* (canary metric - baseline metric) / baseline metric.

h1. Metrics to monitor (minimum set)
The canary is considered safe only if ALL of the following remain within thresholds.

h2. User-visible tail latency
Primary user-visible signals (evaluate by route/model where possible):
* Chat/completions *TTFT p95* and *TTFT p99*
* Chat/completions *E2E p95* and *E2E p99*
* Non-streaming generation *E2E p95/p99* (if applicable for specific endpoints)

Recommended dimensions:
* region, pool, model, route (chat/completions), request_type (streaming/non-streaming)

h2. Queueing and scheduler health (guardrail signals)
These are leading indicators and should be watched during ramp windows:
* Queue time p95 and p99 (overall and by scheduler class)
* Per-class queue depth (short vs long)
* Starvation counters / aging-trigger counters
* Dispatch share by class (fraction of iterations serviced)
* Prefill latency p95/p99 and decode iteration latency p95/p99

h2. Reliability and correctness
* HTTP 5xx rate (overall, by route)
* Timeout rate / client disconnect rate
* Retry rate (if applicable)
* GPU OOM / kernel errors / runtime crash loops

h2. Unit economics (secondary guardrail)
This is not a rollback trigger unless extreme, but should be tracked:
* Tokens/sec per GPU (or equivalent throughput)
* Cost-per-1K-tokens proxy metrics (internal)

h1. Guardrail thresholds (Hosted API canary)
Thresholds below assume the canary is *feature-flagged and reversible within minutes*.

h2. Evaluation windows
Use all three views to avoid missing brief spikes or slow burns:
1) *5-minute* window (fast detection of immediate regressions)
2) *30-minute* window (stability during sustained load)
3) *2-hour* window (captures diurnal patterns during ramp)

Baseline selection:
* Preferred: control pool in same region with feature disabled.
* Acceptable: immediate pre-change baseline (same day) if control is not available.

h2. Allowed drift thresholds (tail latency)
The canary must remain within the following *relative drift* limits compared to baseline/control.

|| Metric || 5-min allowed drift || 30-min allowed drift || 2-hr allowed drift || Notes ||
| TTFT p95 | +5% | +3% | +2% | TTFT is most user-visible for chat; treat as highest priority. |
| TTFT p99 | +10% | +7% | +5% | p99 is noisy; sustained drift is the key. |
| E2E p95 | +7% | +5% | +3% | Applies to streaming completion time and non-streaming E2E. |
| E2E p99 | +12% | +10% | +7% | Use additional sanity check by route/model to isolate hotspots. |

Absolute floor checks (to avoid “small relative drift” hiding large absolute impact):
* If TTFT p95 increases by *> +75ms* for a major route (top 3 by QPS) in a region, treat as a guardrail breach even if relative drift is within % thresholds.
* If TTFT p99 increases by *> +200ms* sustained over 30 minutes, treat as a guardrail breach.

h2. SLO error-budget considerations
We do not “spend” tail-latency error budget casually during canary. Guardrails tie directly to error-budget burn to prevent a slow rollout from consuming monthly budget.

Policy:
* If the canary causes an estimated *tail-latency SLO burn rate > 2x* the normal regional burn rate over a *30-minute* window, pause ramp.
* If the canary causes an estimated *tail-latency SLO burn rate > 5x* over any *15-minute* window, rollback immediately.
* If a region is already in an *error budget watch* state (burn > 1x over last 6 hours), do not expand canary in that region; use a healthier region or defer.

Notes:
* Tail latency SLO burn is computed using the existing SLO pipeline; if per-route/per-model SLO is unavailable, use the closest aggregated service-level SLO but require additional drill-down by route/model for confirmation.

h1. Rollback triggers
Rollback means: disable the scheduler feature flag in the impacted pool(s) and return to baseline scheduling policy. If rollback is not immediately possible due to config propagation issues, apply the “safe mode” parameter profile (see below) and page runtime owner.

h2. Immediate rollback (page SRE on-call + runtime owner)
Trigger any of the following:
* TTFT p95 drift > +10% for 2 consecutive 5-minute windows in the canary pool.
* TTFT p99 drift > +15% for 2 consecutive 5-minute windows.
* E2E p95 drift > +12% for 2 consecutive 5-minute windows.
* Any sustained *5xx* increase > +0.2% absolute over 10 minutes attributable to the canary pool.
* Any sign of scheduler starvation defect:
  * Starvation counter increasing continuously for > 10 minutes, or
  * Long-class queue depth grows monotonically for > 15 minutes while short-class remains stable, indicating long-class not making progress.
* GPU/runtime stability breach:
  * Crash loop, repeated kernel errors, OOM spikes correlated to canary enablement.

h2. Pause ramp (hold at current %) and investigate
Trigger any of the following:
* TTFT p95 drift > +5% sustained over 30 minutes.
* Tail-latency SLO burn rate > 2x sustained over 30 minutes.
* Queue time p95 drift > +20% sustained over 30 minutes.
* Throughput regression > 5% (tokens/sec per GPU) sustained over 2 hours with no corresponding tail-latency win.

h2. Proceed criteria (required to ramp up)
All must be true:
* No immediate rollback triggers observed in last 60 minutes.
* TTFT p95 drift <= +3% over last 30 minutes.
* TTFT p99 drift <= +7% over last 30 minutes.
* Starvation counter stable (no sustained increases) and long-class is making measurable progress.
* SLO burn rate not elevated (> 1.5x) over last 2 hours.

h1. Region and pool selection guardrails
To limit blast radius and reduce confounding:
* Start in *one region* with historically stable tail latency and sufficient headroom.

* Avoid ramping during known traffic peaks unless explicitly planned and staffed.

Suggested ramp sequence (Hosted API):
1) 1% traffic to canary pool (or smallest viable pool) for 2 hours
2) 5% for 4 hours
3) 10% for 24 hours (includes at least one peak period)
4) 25% for 24–48 hours
5) 50% and then 100% after approval checkpoint

Approval checkpoints:
* 10% -> 25% requires SRE + Serving Runtime sign-off.
* 25% -> 50% requires SRE sponsor sign-off.
* 50% -> 100% requires SRE sponsor + product stakeholder acknowledgement (Hosted API).

h1. Safe mode parameter profile (if rollback is delayed)
If the feature flag cannot be disabled quickly, apply a conservative profile to reduce tail risk:
* Reduce short/long skew (increase long share) to prevent starvation.
* Enable/strengthen aging (faster promotion of long requests).
* Tighten per-iteration token budget caps to limit long-prefill dominance.

Exact config keys differ by runtime version; reference the rollout runbook for the current knobs and validated safe bounds.

h1. Operational procedure during canary
h2. Pre-flight checklist (before enabling)
1) Confirm dashboards are live for the target region/pool.
2) Confirm control pool exists (feature OFF) and is comparable.
3) Confirm on-call coverage:
   * SRE on-call aware

   * Release coordinator assigned for the ramp window
4) Confirm rollback mechanism:

   * Config propagation time understood (< 5 minutes target)

h2. During enablement (first 30 minutes)

title: Demo Environment SLOs, Error Budgets, and Escalation (Sales Demo Tenant)
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines service-level objectives (SLOs) and internal service-level commitments for Redwood’s **Sales Demo Environment** ("demo tenant"). The demo environment must be reliable enough for AE/SE live demos, while staying safe (synthetic data only) and cheap to operate.

**Important:** These targets are for *internal reliability management*. They are not customer-facing SLAs and should not be quoted as contractual commitments.

## Scope
In scope (what we measure and page on):
- **Demo Inference API** (hosted API entrypoint + routing + model pinning/fallback behavior for demo routes)
- **Streaming** behavior (time-to-first-token and disconnect rate)
- **Console Demo Views** (dashboards, rollout view, tracing/error analysis pages used in the talk track)
- **Telemetry ingestion for demo** (synthetic traffic → events → dashboards)
- **Demo reset + healthcheck** (SE-facing reset command and healthcheck report)

Out of scope (tracked, but not SLO-blocking):
- Non-demo tenants
- Non-critical Console pages not used in demo talk track
- Long-term analytics retention for the demo org beyond what’s needed to demonstrate features

## Definitions
- **Demo environment / demo tenant:** The isolated Redwood org + projects reserved for scripted demos. All traffic and datasets are synthetic.
- **Availability:** Requests succeed without server errors and without quota/capacity rejection beyond defined thresholds.
- **Dashboard freshness:** The time between a synthetic event occurring and it being visible in Console.
- **Demo window:** 08:00–18:00 local time in NA/EU regions on weekdays. We still run monitoring 24/7, but escalation urgency differs.

## Reliability goals (what "good" looks like)
- Any SE can run a live demo end-to-end with **no manual fixes** (no quota edits, no emergency reroutes, no "empty dashboard" moments).
- A demo failure should be **rare** and have an obvious fallback path (backup screenshots/recording).
- When something is broken, the system should fail **predictably** with clear healthcheck output and known remediation.

## Service map (components)
Primary components and owners:
- **Demo Inference Gateway** (routing, auth, rate limit decisions): Eng Platform + SRE
- **Demo Model Routes** (pinned versions + safe fallback policy): Routing team (Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon)
- **Console Demo Dashboards + Rollouts UI:** Console team (Dylan Brooks / Chloe Martin)
- **Telemetry Ingestion + Cost/Latency pipelines:** Telemetry (Logan Wright / Mei Lin)
- **Synthetic Traffic Generator:** Eng Platform (Mei Lin) + SRE for scheduling/alerts
- **Reset + Healthcheck tooling:** Eng Platform (Devon Price) + SE owner (Markus Klein)

## SLOs and internal SLAs
The table below lists the targets used for monitoring, error budget burn, and escalation.

### 1) Demo Inference API (non-streaming)
| Metric | SLO target | Measurement | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Availability (2xx + expected 4xx) | 99.5% monthly | Per-request, demo routes only | Excludes client-cancelled requests. Expected 4xx includes auth failures for intentionally-invalid-key test only when triggered by the script. |
| p95 latency (end-to-end) | \u2264 1.8s monthly | Gateway receive → first byte response | Normalized on demo routes with pinned models. |
| p99 latency (end-to-end) | \u2264 3.0s monthly | Same as above | Used as a guardrail; not the primary SLO. |
| 5xx rate | \u2264 0.3% monthly | Per-request | Any sustained spike pages SRE. |
| 429 rate | \u2264 1.0% monthly | Per-request | We expect some burst control during synthetic traffic; sustained 429s during demo window are a Sev2. |

### 2) Demo Streaming API
| Metric | SLO target | Measurement | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Streaming availability | 99.0% monthly | Successful stream completion | A completion counts when the server finishes the stream or a graceful client cancel occurs after receiving \u2265 1 chunk. |
| Time-to-first-token (TTFT) p95 | \u2264 1.2s monthly | Request start → first chunk | Customer-facing demos are sensitive to TTFT. |
| Stream disconnect rate | \u2264 0.5% monthly | Unexpected disconnects | Measured for demo routes only; excludes intentional network test steps. |

### 3) Console demo dashboards and views
| Metric | SLO target | Measurement | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Console demo view availability | 99.5% monthly | Successful page load (HTTP + API deps) | Scoped to demo-critical views: Usage (cost/latency), Errors, Traces, Rollouts. |
| Dashboard freshness (p95) | \u2264 10 minutes | Event time → visible in graphs | The primary goal is to avoid empty graphs during a live walkthrough. |
| "Empty dashboard" incidents | 0 during demo window (goal) | SE-reported + automated checks | Any occurrence triggers follow-up even if self-resolved. |

### 4) Demo reset + healthcheck
| Metric | Target | Measurement | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Reset end-to-end duration | \u2264 5 minutes (p95) | Reset command start → success | Reset includes: project cleanup, key rotation (if scheduled), seed config re-apply, traffic generator verification. |
| Healthcheck correctness | 100% (goal) | Automated validation | Healthcheck must flag: quota exhaustion risk, model pin invalid, telemetry ingestion lag, missing seeded entities. |
| Parallel demo readiness | \u2264 2 concurrent demos per region (baseline) | Capacity + quota checks | Scaling beyond baseline is planned work; see capacity runbook. |

## Error budget policy
We use error budgets to balance changes (new demo scenarios, Console tweaks, model updates) against stability.

### Budget periods
- **Monthly error budget** (calendar month) is the primary accounting period.
- During heavy demo periods (quarter-end), we may also monitor **weekly burn rates** and temporarily tighten gating.

### Burn-rate thresholds and actions
We track burn against the primary availability/freshness SLOs above.

1) **Info threshold: 1x burn rate (projected to exhaust budget by end of month)**
   - Action: Post status note in #demo-oncall and tag owners. Confirm dashboards, streaming, and reset health.

2) **Warn threshold: 2x burn rate (projected to exhaust within ~2 weeks)**
   - Action: Freeze non-essential demo changes (deck-dependent UI tweaks, new scenario additions). Require SRE sign-off for demo-tenant deploys.

3) **Critical threshold: 5x burn rate (projected to exhaust within ~3–5 days)**
   - Action: Demo environment enters **stability mode**:
     - No feature work; only reliability fixes.
     - Disable risky experiments (non-critical canaries for demo routes).
     - Ensure backup assets are up to date (screenshots/recording).
     - Daily standup in #demo-oncall until burn is below 2x.

### Release gating (what changes require extra care)
The following changes require either an SE dry-run or explicit go/no-go in #demo-oncall:
- Updates to **pinned model versions** or fallback routing rules
- Quota/rate-limit policy changes for demo org/projects
- Console changes affecting demo-critical pages (Usage, Errors, Traces, Rollouts)
- Telemetry pipeline migrations affecting freshness
- Reset/healthcheck tooling changes

## Incident severity and escalation
### Severity levels (demo environment)
- **Sev1 (demo-blocking, widespread):** Demo routes failing (5xx), streaming broken, auth impossible, Console demo-critical views unusable, or telemetry freshness > 30 minutes across demo projects during demo window.
- **Sev2 (degraded but workable):** Intermittent failures, elevated 429s, TTFT regression making live demo awkward, or freshness 10–30 minutes during demo window.
- **Sev3 (minor / off-hours / cosmetic):** Non-critical UI issue, small latency regressions outside demo window, or single-project quirks with clear workaround.

### Escalation path
1) **First responder:** SE running the demo (or GTM operator) runs the healthcheck and follows the reset runbook.
2) **#demo-oncall Slack channel:** Post the healthcheck output + what step failed (copy/paste). Tag **@demo-oncall**.
3) **SRE on-call (demo rotation):** If Sev1/Sev2 during demo window, SRE takes lead, coordinates Eng Platform + owning teams.
4) **Ownership escalation (if not resolved quickly):**
   - Control plane/auth: Ethan Park
   - Routing/fallback: Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon
   - Console: Chloe Martin / Dylan Brooks
   - Telemetry/freshness: Logan Wright / Mei Lin
   - Demo tooling: Devon Price
5) **Sales escalation:** If the incident impacts scheduled exec demos, notify Stephanie Nguyen (NA) / Lukas Meyer (EMEA) and Markus Klein for field comms and backup plan.

### Response time targets (internal)
| Severity | Acknowledge | Mitigation/Workaround | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Sev1 | 5 minutes | 15 minutes | Workaround can be "backup mode" (screenshots/recording) if live paths are broken. |
| Sev2 | 15 minutes | 60 minutes | Aim to stabilize within the same demo block. |
| Sev3 | 1 business day | Next sprint | Track as a ticket; no paging. |

## Monitoring and alerting (what we page on)
Primary alerts:
- Demo route 5xx rate > 2% for 5 minutes (Sev1)
- Demo route 429 rate > 5% for 10 minutes during demo window (Sev2)
- Streaming disconnect rate > 2% for 10 minutes (Sev2)
- Telemetry freshness p95 > 15 minutes for 15 minutes (Sev1)
- Synthetic traffic job missed two consecutive runs (Sev2)
- Healthcheck fails for pinned-model availability or quota headroom (Sev2)

Alert routing:
- Paging: SRE demo on-call
- Slack: #demo-oncall (required), #sales (FYI for Sev1 during demo window)

## Operating cadence
- **Daily (weekday):** Synthetic traffic health check and freshness spot-check in the morning (per region).
- **Weekly:** Review demo reliability dashboard, open tickets, and planned changes (SRE + Eng Platform + Demo owner).
- **Monthly:** Error budget review and any SLO recalibration (only if targets are consistently met and demos are stable).

## Reporting and success metrics
We track:
- Number of demos run per week (from synthetic + scheduled tags)
- Demo failure rate (SE self-report + incident count)
- Median reset time and p95 reset time
- "Empty dashboard" occurrences
- Mean time to mitigation for Sev1/Sev2

## Notes for sales/SE usage
- Do not claim demo-environment SLOs as customer SLAs.
- If the demo environment is degraded, use **backup mode** (approved screenshots/recording) and continue the narrative.
- Always keep demo traffic and datasets synthetic. If you suspect real customer data in the demo org, stop and page Security.

## Change log
- 2026-02-10: Added explicit dashboard freshness SLO and streaming disconnect target; clarified severity mapping for 429s during demo window.
- 2025-11-21: Initial publication.

- Any SE can run a live demo end-to-end with **no manual fixes** (no quota edits, no emergency reroutes, no "empty dashboard" moments).
- A demo failure should be **rare** and have an obvious fallback path (backup screenshots/recording).
- When something is broken, the system should fail **predictably** with clear healthcheck output and known remediation.

## Service map (components)
Primary components and owners:
- **Demo Inference Gateway** (routing, auth, rate limit decisions): Eng Platform + SRE
- **Demo Model Routes** (pinned versions + safe fallback policy): Routing team (Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon)
- **Console Demo Dashboards + Rollouts UI:** Console team (Dylan Brooks / Chloe Martin)
- **Telemetry Ingestion + Cost/Latency pipelines:** Telemetry (Logan Wright / Mei Lin)
- **Synthetic Traffic Generator:** Eng Platform (Mei Lin) + SRE for scheduling/alerts
- **Reset + Healthcheck tooling:** Eng Platform (Devon Price) + SE owner (Markus Klein)

## SLOs and internal SLAs
The table below lists the targets used for monitoring, error budget burn, and escalation.

### 1) Demo Inference API (non-streaming)
| Metric | SLO target | Measurement | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Availability (2xx + expected 4xx) | 99.5% monthly | Per-request, demo routes only | Excludes client-cancelled requests. Expected 4xx includes auth failures for intentionally-invalid-key test only when triggered by the script. |
| p95 latency (end-to-end) | \u2264 1.8s monthly | Gateway receive → first byte response | Normalized on demo routes with pinned models. |
| p99 latency (end-to-end) | \u2264 3.0s monthly | Same as above | Used as a guardrail; not the primary SLO. |
| 5xx rate | \u2264 0.3% monthly | Per-request | Any sustained spike pages SRE. |
| 429 rate | \u2264 1.0% monthly | Per-request | We expect some burst control during synthetic traffic; sustained 429s during demo window are a Sev2. |

### 2) Demo Streaming API
| Metric | SLO target | Measurement | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Streaming availability | 99.0% monthly | Successful stream completion | A completion counts when the server finishes the stream or a graceful client cancel occurs after receiving \u2265 1 chunk. |
| Time-to-first-token (TTFT) p95 | \u2264 1.2s monthly | Request start → first chunk | Customer-facing demos are sensitive to TTFT. |
| Stream disconnect rate | \u2264 0.5% monthly | Unexpected disconnects | Measured for demo routes only; excludes intentional network test steps. |

### 3) Console demo dashboards and views
| Metric | SLO target | Measurement | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Console demo view availability | 99.5% monthly | Successful page load (HTTP + API deps) | Scoped to demo-critical views: Usage (cost/latency), Errors, Traces, Rollouts. |
| Dashboard freshness (p95) | \u2264 10 minutes | Event time → visible in graphs | The primary goal is to avoid empty graphs during a live walkthrough. |
| "Empty dashboard" incidents | 0 during demo window (goal) | SE-reported + automated checks | Any occurrence triggers follow-up even if self-resolved. |

### 4) Demo reset + healthcheck
| Metric | Target | Measurement | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Reset end-to-end duration | \u2264 5 minutes (p95) | Reset command start → success | Reset includes: project cleanup, key rotation (if scheduled), seed config re-apply, traffic generator verification. |
| Healthcheck correctness | 100% (goal) | Automated validation | Healthcheck must flag: quota exhaustion risk, model pin invalid, telemetry ingestion lag, missing seeded entities. |
| Parallel demo readiness | \u2264 2 concurrent demos per region (baseline) | Capacity + quota checks | Scaling beyond baseline is planned work; see capacity runbook. |

## Error budget policy
We use error budgets to balance changes (new demo scenarios, Console tweaks, model updates) against stability.

### Budget periods
- **Monthly error budget** (calendar month) is the primary accounting period.
- During heavy demo periods (quarter-end), we may also monitor **weekly burn rates** and temporarily tighten gating.

### Burn-rate thresholds and actions
We track burn against the primary availability/freshness SLOs above.

1) **Info threshold: 1x burn rate (projected to exhaust budget by end of month)**
   - Action: Post status note in #demo-oncall and tag owners. Confirm dashboards, streaming, and reset health.

2) **Warn threshold: 2x burn rate (projected to exhaust within ~2 weeks)**
   - Action: Freeze non-essential demo changes (deck-dependent UI tweaks, new scenario additions). Require SRE sign-off for demo-tenant deploys.

3) **Critical threshold: 5x burn rate (projected to exhaust within ~3–5 days)**
   - Action: Demo environment enters **stability mode**:
     - No feature work; only reliability fixes.
     - Disable risky experiments (non-critical canaries for demo routes).
     - Ensure backup assets are up to date (screenshots/recording).
     - Daily standup in #demo-oncall until burn is below 2x.

### Release gating (what changes require extra care)
The following changes require either an SE dry-run or explicit go/no-go in #demo-oncall:
- Updates to **pinned model versions** or fallback routing rules
- Quota/rate-limit policy changes for demo org/projects
- Console changes affecting demo-critical pages (Usage, Errors, Traces, Rollouts)
- Telemetry pipeline migrations affecting freshness
- Reset/healthcheck tooling changes

## Incident severity and escalation
### Severity levels (demo environment)
- **Sev1 (demo-blocking, widespread):** Demo routes failing (5xx), streaming broken, auth impossible, Console demo-critical views unusable, or telemetry freshness > 30 minutes across demo projects during demo window.
- **Sev2 (degraded but workable):** Intermittent failures, elevated 429s, TTFT regression making live demo awkward, or freshness 10–30 minutes during demo window.
- **Sev3 (minor / off-hours / cosmetic):** Non-critical UI issue, small latency regressions outside demo window, or single-project quirks with clear workaround.

### Escalation path
1) **First responder:** SE running the demo (or GTM operator) runs the healthcheck and follows the reset runbook.
2) **#demo-oncall Slack channel:** Post the healthcheck output + what step failed (copy/paste). Tag **@demo-oncall**.
3) **SRE on-call (demo rotation):** If Sev1/Sev2 during demo window, SRE takes lead, coordinates Eng Platform + owning teams.
4) **Ownership escalation (if not resolved quickly):**
   - Control plane/auth: Ethan Park
   - Routing/fallback: Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon
   - Console: Chloe Martin / Dylan Brooks
   - Telemetry/freshness: Logan Wright / Mei Lin
   - Demo tooling: Devon Price
5) **Sales escalation:** If the incident impacts scheduled exec demos, notify Stephanie Nguyen (NA) / Lukas Meyer (EMEA) and Markus Klein for field comms and backup plan.

### Response time targets (internal)
| Severity | Acknowledge | Mitigation/Workaround | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Sev1 | 5 minutes | 15 minutes | Workaround can be "backup mode" (screenshots/recording) if live paths are broken. |
| Sev2 | 15 minutes | 60 minutes | Aim to stabilize within the same demo block. |
| Sev3 | 1 business day | Next sprint | Track as a ticket; no paging. |

## Monitoring and alerting (what we page on)
Primary alerts:
- Demo route 5xx rate > 2% for 5 minutes (Sev1)
- Demo route 429 rate > 5% for 10 minutes during demo window (Sev2)
- Streaming disconnect rate > 2% for 10 minutes (Sev2)
- Telemetry freshness p95 > 15 minutes for 15 minutes (Sev1)
- Synthetic traffic job missed two consecutive runs (Sev2)
- Healthcheck fails for pinned-model availability or quota headroom (Sev2)

Alert routing:
- Paging: SRE demo on-call
- Slack: #demo-oncall (required), #sales (FYI for Sev1 during demo window)

## Operating cadence
- **Daily (weekday):** Synthetic traffic health check and freshness spot-check in the morning (per region).
- **Weekly:** Review demo reliability dashboard, open tickets, and planned changes (SRE + Eng Platform + Demo owner).
- **Monthly:** Error budget review and any SLO recalibration (only if targets are consistently met and demos are stable).

## Reporting and success metrics
We track:
- Number of demos run per week (from synthetic + scheduled tags)
- Demo failure rate (SE self-report + incident count)
- Median reset time and p95 reset time
- "Empty dashboard" occurrences
- Mean time to mitigation for Sev1/Sev2

## Notes for sales/SE usage
- Do not claim demo-environment SLOs as customer SLAs.
- If the demo environment is degraded, use **backup mode** (approved screenshots/recording) and continue the narrative.
- Always keep demo traffic and datasets synthetic. If you suspect real customer data in the demo org, stop and page Security.

## Change log
- 2026-02-10: Added explicit dashboard freshness SLO and streaming disconnect target; clarified severity mapping for 429s during demo window.
- 2025-11-21: Initial publication.

title: Runbook: Hosted API Regional Failover (On-Call)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes the on-call procedure for executing (or approving) **Hosted API** regional failover during a real regional impairment or a controlled test. It covers triage, validation of trigger signals, initiating automation (including dry-run), monitoring during traffic shift, rollback, and post-event verification.

**Primary goal:** reduce customer-visible impact by failing over quickly when the region is actually impaired, while avoiding false positives and oscillation.

## Audience
- SRE On-Call (primary)
- Routing/Smart Routing On-Call (secondary)
- Network On-Call (as needed)
- Control Plane On-Call (as needed)

## Definitions
- **Primary region:** currently preferred region for a Hosted API route/model group (e.g., `us-east` for most NA traffic).
- **Failover:** shifting eligible traffic from an impaired region to one or more healthy regions via routing policy updates.
- **Regional impairment:** region-level loss of reachability and/or sustained service-level failure (e.g., elevated 5xx burn) consistent across multiple independent signals.
- **Partial degradation:** single model pool, single AZ, or a dependency issue not requiring region-wide failover.

## Preconditions / Safety
1) Confirm this is **Hosted API** traffic (not Dedicated-only). If Dedicated is impacted, coordinate separately per Dedicated runbooks.
2) Ensure you have the correct scope:
   - Which **route(s)**: `chat.completions`, `responses`, `embeddings`, etc.
   - Which **model group(s)**: `rw-llama-70b`, `rw-qwen-32b`, etc.
   - Which **tenant tier(s)** impacted: `free`, `pro`, `enterprise`.
3) Review automation mode for production:
   - **Dry-run** is always permitted.
   - **Execute** may require approval hooks depending on the route/tier configuration.
4) Data residency constraints: some enterprise tenants have region pinning. Failover must respect constraints (see “Do Not Fail Over” section and Security guidance).

## Signals and Trigger Hierarchy (Summary)
The authoritative prioritization is defined in **[ADR-042: Failover Trigger Signal Hierarchy]**.

Use this simplified hierarchy when making a decision:
1) **Reachability loss** (cross-region probes + edge reachability) 
2) **5xx burn-rate** for Hosted API (multi-window burn confirmation)
3) **Tail latency burn-rate** (P95/P99) with corroborating errors or reachability
4) **Capacity exhaustion / headroom collapse** (sustained) that cannot be mitigated by local scaling

**Corroboration rule (operational):** do not execute production failover based on a single signal unless it is clearly reachability loss across multiple probe sources.

## Required Dashboards / Links
Refer to **[Failover Signal Dashboards Spec]** for panel names and interpretations. Minimum set before executing:
- Region Health Summary (aggregated): `region-health-service` view
- Hosted API Availability: 2xx/4xx/5xx rate by region + route
- Burn-rate alerts: 5m/1h multi-window burn for multi-region availability SLO
- Tail latency by region: p95/p99 for key routes
- Capacity headroom by region: GPU queue depth, available tokens/sec, batcher saturation
- Routing policy flips timeline + audit events
- Cross-region probe results (perf-canary reachability/latency)

## Tools
### Failover Controller CLI
Use the internal failover controller CLI to inspect, simulate, and (if permitted) execute changes.

Common commands (examples):
- Inspect current state:
  - `rwi-failoverctl status --service hosted-api --region us-east`
- Review signals / evidence:
  - `rwi-failoverctl evidence --service hosted-api --region us-east --window 30m`
- Dry-run (recommended actions only):
  - `rwi-failoverctl recommend --service hosted-api --from us-east --to eu-west --dry-run`
- Execute (requires approval hook in some prod configs):
  - `rwi-failoverctl execute --service hosted-api --from us-east --to eu-west --reason "region impairment"`
- Rollback:
  - `rwi-failoverctl rollback --service hosted-api --change-id <id> --reason "region recovered"`

Notes:
- All dry-run and execute actions emit an audit event (see “Auditability” below).
- If the CLI cannot reach the controller/control plane, coordinate with Control Plane On-Call and consider manual routing policy change only as a last resort.

### Incident-bot integration
The failover controller posts summaries on trigger/recommend/execute/rollback. If missing, post manually with the template in “Comms”.

## Severity / When to Initiate Failover
Initiate failover actions when **either**:
- You see a page for regional impairment (reachability + service impact), or
- Multiple customers report elevated 5xx/timeouts correlated to a single region, or
- Burn-rate alert triggers for multi-region availability and is clearly tied to one region.

If impact is limited to a single route/model pool and can be mitigated locally (restart, scale, drain bad nodes), prefer local mitigation first.

## Step-by-step Procedure
### 1) Triage and confirm it’s regional
1. Open incident channel and assign roles:
   - Incident Commander (IC): SRE On-Call unless escalated
   - Routing liaison: Smart Routing On-Call
   - Network liaison: Network On-Call if reachability suspected
   - Observability support: optional
2. Confirm symptoms:
   - Customer impact: 5xx spikes, timeouts, elevated p99, streaming disconnects
   - Scope: specific region (`us-east`) vs global
3. Check Region Health Summary:
   - Is `us-east` marked `DEGRADED` or `UNHEALTHY`?
   - Which signals contributed (reachability, 5xx burn, latency burn, capacity)?
4. Validate reachability:
   - Cross-region probes: `eu-west -> us-east`, `us-west -> us-east`
   - Edge reachability: client ingress success rate in region
   - Network indicators: packet loss, BGP/anycast anomalies (if applicable)
5. Validate service-level impact:
   - Hosted API 5xx rate by region/route
   - Burn-rate: confirm multi-window (fast + slow) windows are both elevated to avoid false positives
   - Tail latency: verify not caused solely by client retry storms
6. Validate capacity/headroom:
   - GPU pool saturation, queue depth
   - Autoscaler events and whether capacity can recover within minutes

Decision checkpoint:
- If signals indicate **localized service issue** (single model, single AZ, single dependency), do not fail over region-wide yet. Escalate to service owner and mitigate locally.
- If signals indicate **region impairment**, proceed to dry-run.

### 2) Dry-run failover recommendation (required before execute)
1. Run dry-run recommendation:
   - `rwi-failoverctl recommend --service hosted-api --from us-east --to <candidate> --dry-run`
2. Review output for:
   - Recommended destination region(s) and weights
   - Evidence list (signals, timestamps, confidence)
   - Guardrails that will apply (rate limits, hold-down timers, per-tenant safety)
3. Validate destination readiness:
   - Capacity headroom meets “safe to failover” criteria for the affected routes/models
   - No concurrent degradation in destination
   - Tail latency acceptable under projected load
4. Confirm “Do Not Fail Over” constraints:
   - Data residency pinned tenants cannot be shifted (ensure policy respects pinning)
   - Known incompatibilities: model version parity, feature flags, or region-specific dependencies

If the dry-run suggests “hold / no action” due to insufficient corroboration or guardrails, do not force execute without Routing lead approval.

### 3) Approval and execute
Depending on route/tier, production execute may require explicit approval.

1. Announce intent in incident channel:
   - Source region, destination region(s)
   - Current impact (5xx/latency)
   - Signals justifying failover
   - Expected blast radius and rollback trigger
2. If approval hook is enabled:
   - Request approval from designated approver (usually SRE IC + Routing On-Call; enterprise scope may require SRE Manager)
3. Execute failover:
   - `rwi-failoverctl execute --service hosted-api --from us-east --to eu-west --reason "<ticket/incident + summary>"`
4. Confirm controller actions:
   - Routing policy updated
   - Circuit breakers / backpressure settings applied (if part of the workflow)
   - Audit event emitted with change-id

### 4) Monitor during traffic shift (first 15 minutes are critical)
Watch these panels continuously:
1. Customer-visible impact:
   - Overall 5xx rate (global)
   - Route-level error rate for key endpoints
   - Streaming stability metrics (disconnect/reconnect)
2. Destination region health:
   - GPU queue depth / saturation
   - P95/P99 latency
   - Retry-after responses (should increase under overload rather than hard timeouts)
3. Failover side effects:
   - Thundering herd (retry spikes)
   - Elevated tail latency due to cache misses / cold KV cache
   - Unexpected auth/control-plane errors

If destination starts to degrade:
- Engage load shedding controls (per gateway defaults) and consider partial failover (reduce weights) instead of full shift.
- Coordinate with Routing On-Call to adjust weights or add an additional destination.

### 5) Customer impact checks
1. Confirm representative customer success:
   - Sample synthetic requests from multiple geos
   - Check top enterprise tenants dashboards (if available) for elevated errors
2. If customer tickets are open (Support), provide:
   - Current status (mitigating via regional failover)
   - Expected behavior (brief transient errors, possible streaming reconnect)
   - Workaround guidance (retry with exponential backoff; honor Retry-After)

### 6) Stabilization and decision to hold vs rollback
Hold-down timers and anti-flap rules are defined in **[ADR-043]** and enforced by the controller. Operationally:

Rollback only when:
- Source region signals are healthy for the required stability window, AND
- Destination region is stable, AND
- You have a plan for a safe traffic return (gradual weights preferred)

Prefer **gradual rollback** (weighted return) to avoid oscillation.

### 7) Rollback procedure
1. Run rollback dry-run:
   - `rwi-failoverctl recommend --service hosted-api --from eu-west --to us-east --dry-run`
2. If recommended and stable, execute rollback:
   - `rwi-failoverctl rollback --service hosted-api --change-id <id> --reason "region recovered"`
3. Monitor for at least 30 minutes after rollback:
   - Error rate and latency in both regions
   - Policy flip rate (ensure no repeated flaps)

## Do Not Fail Over (Common False-Positive Scenarios)
Do not execute a region-wide failover if the issue is likely:
- A single model deployment regression in-region (roll back model/version instead)
- Control plane impairment affecting policy distribution (failover may not apply cleanly)
- A global dependency outage (auth, billing, shared datastore) where failover doesn’t help
- Client-side retry storms causing latency (tune retries/backoff; use Retry-After)
- Destination region lacks sufficient headroom or is already degraded
- Tenant data residency pinning prevents safe cross-region routing

When unsure, escalate to:
- Routing On-Call (policy semantics, partial failover)
- Network On-Call (reachability signal validation)
- Control Plane On-Call (controller health)

## Auditability / Evidence Collection
Every recommendation/execute/rollback should be traceable:
- Capture the controller change-id in the incident timeline
- Link dashboards used to make the decision (Region Health Summary, burn panels)
- Ensure audit event exists (used for enterprise audit requests)

If audit events are missing:
- Record the exact commands run and timestamps
- File a ticket with the failover controller team (include incident id)

## Communication Checklist
Follow **[Regional Failover Communication Playbook]**.

Minimum internal comms:
- Update incident channel with:
  - What is failing (region + symptoms)
  - What we did (dry-run, execute, weights)
  - What to watch (destination headroom, burn)

Status page (if customer-visible impact exceeds thresholds):
- Use **[Status Page Template: Regional Event]**
- Update cadence: every 15–30 minutes while actively mitigating
- Ensure wording distinguishes “regional impairment” from “planned test”

Incident-bot manual template (if automation posts fail):
- "Failover action: shifted Hosted API traffic from <from> to <to>. Reason: <reachability/5xx burn/latency burn/capacity>. Change-id: <id>. Monitoring: <dashboard link>. Rollback trigger: <criteria>."

## Post-event Validation (Closeout)
Within 1 hour of stabilization:
1. Confirm SLO impact snapshot:
   - Multi-region availability SLO burn during the window
   - Peak 5xx and duration
2. Confirm client experience:
   - Streaming reconnect metrics improved
   - Retry-after usage (no prolonged hard timeouts)
3. Confirm operational hygiene:
   - Controller did not flap (flip rate within limits)
   - Alerts returned to green and are not noisy
4. Create follow-ups:
   - Any signal gaps (missing probe, delayed burn alert)
   - Any dashboard gaps (missing annotations)
   - Any guardrails needed (rate limits, hold-down tuning)

## Escalation / Ownership
- SRE On-Call: owns incident coordination and decision to execute/rollback
- Routing On-Call (Smart Routing): owns policy semantics, weights, constraints
- Network On-Call: validates reachability anomalies and provider symptoms
- Observability: dashboards/alert correctness

If repeated flapping or unclear signals occur, escalate to:
- Miles Thornton (Failover architecture lead)
- Rafael Mendes (SRE delivery lead)

## Appendix A: Quick Validation Queries (Examples)
Use these as mental checklists (exact query syntax varies by dashboard):
- 5xx rate by region and route over last 10m vs 1h
- Burn-rate: fast window (5m) and slow window (1h) both elevated
- Cross-region probes: `success_rate < 95%` and/or latency step-change
- Destination headroom: sustained queue depth below alert threshold and autoscaler not maxed

## Appendix B: Controlled Test Notes
For planned game days:
- Always run in a limited blast radius configuration (approved tenants/routes)
- Announce start/end in #eng-sre and #support with explicit customer comms plan
- Use dry-run first, then execute with pre-agreed rollback triggers
- Ensure dashboards have automation annotations enabled before start

   - Capacity headroom meets “safe to failover” criteria for the affected routes/models
   - No concurrent degradation in destination
   - Tail latency acceptable under projected load
4. Confirm “Do Not Fail Over” constraints:
   - Data residency pinned tenants cannot be shifted (ensure policy respects pinning)
   - Known incompatibilities: model version parity, feature flags, or region-specific dependencies

If the dry-run suggests “hold / no action” due to insufficient corroboration or guardrails, do not force execute without Routing lead approval.

### 3) Approval and execute
Depending on route/tier, production execute may require explicit approval.

1. Announce intent in incident channel:
   - Source region, destination region(s)
   - Current impact (5xx/latency)
   - Signals justifying failover
   - Expected blast radius and rollback trigger
2. If approval hook is enabled:
   - Request approval from designated approver (usually SRE IC + Routing On-Call; enterprise scope may require SRE Manager)
3. Execute failover:
   - `rwi-failoverctl execute --service hosted-api --from us-east --to eu-west --reason "<ticket/incident + summary>"`
4. Confirm controller actions:
   - Routing policy updated
   - Circuit breakers / backpressure settings applied (if part of the workflow)
   - Audit event emitted with change-id

### 4) Monitor during traffic shift (first 15 minutes are critical)
Watch these panels continuously:
1. Customer-visible impact:
   - Overall 5xx rate (global)
   - Route-level error rate for key endpoints
   - Streaming stability metrics (disconnect/reconnect)
2. Destination region health:
   - GPU queue depth / saturation
   - P95/P99 latency
   - Retry-after responses (should increase under overload rather than hard timeouts)
3. Failover side effects:
   - Thundering herd (retry spikes)
   - Elevated tail latency due to cache misses / cold KV cache
   - Unexpected auth/control-plane errors

If destination starts to degrade:
- Engage load shedding controls (per gateway defaults) and consider partial failover (reduce weights) instead of full shift.
- Coordinate with Routing On-Call to adjust weights or add an additional destination.

### 5) Customer impact checks
1. Confirm representative customer success:
   - Sample synthetic requests from multiple geos
   - Check top enterprise tenants dashboards (if available) for elevated errors
2. If customer tickets are open (Support), provide:
   - Current status (mitigating via regional failover)
   - Expected behavior (brief transient errors, possible streaming reconnect)
   - Workaround guidance (retry with exponential backoff; honor Retry-After)

### 6) Stabilization and decision to hold vs rollback
Hold-down timers and anti-flap rules are defined in **[ADR-043]** and enforced by the controller. Operationally:

Rollback only when:
- Source region signals are healthy for the required stability window, AND
- Destination region is stable, AND
- You have a plan for a safe traffic return (gradual weights preferred)

Prefer **gradual rollback** (weighted return) to avoid oscillation.

### 7) Rollback procedure
1. Run rollback dry-run:
   - `rwi-failoverctl recommend --service hosted-api --from eu-west --to us-east --dry-run`
2. If recommended and stable, execute rollback:
   - `rwi-failoverctl rollback --service hosted-api --change-id <id> --reason "region recovered"`
3. Monitor for at least 30 minutes after rollback:
   - Error rate and latency in both regions
   - Policy flip rate (ensure no repeated flaps)

## Do Not Fail Over (Common False-Positive Scenarios)
Do not execute a region-wide failover if the issue is likely:
- A single model deployment regression in-region (roll back model/version instead)
- Control plane impairment affecting policy distribution (failover may not apply cleanly)
- A global dependency outage (auth, billing, shared datastore) where failover doesn’t help
- Client-side retry storms causing latency (tune retries/backoff; use Retry-After)
- Destination region lacks sufficient headroom or is already degraded
- Tenant data residency pinning prevents safe cross-region routing

When unsure, escalate to:
- Routing On-Call (policy semantics, partial failover)
- Network On-Call (reachability signal validation)
- Control Plane On-Call (controller health)

## Auditability / Evidence Collection
Every recommendation/execute/rollback should be traceable:
- Capture the controller change-id in the incident timeline
- Link dashboards used to make the decision (Region Health Summary, burn panels)
- Ensure audit event exists (used for enterprise audit requests)

If audit events are missing:
- Record the exact commands run and timestamps
- File a ticket with the failover controller team (include incident id)

## Communication Checklist
Follow **[Regional Failover Communication Playbook]**.

Minimum internal comms:
- Update incident channel with:
  - What is failing (region + symptoms)
  - What we did (dry-run, execute, weights)
  - What to watch (destination headroom, burn)

Status page (if customer-visible impact exceeds thresholds):
- Use **[Status Page Template: Regional Event]**
- Update cadence: every 15–30 minutes while actively mitigating
- Ensure wording distinguishes “regional impairment” from “planned test”

Incident-bot manual template (if automation posts fail):
- "Failover action: shifted Hosted API traffic from <from> to <to>. Reason: <reachability/5xx burn/latency burn/capacity>. Change-id: <id>. Monitoring: <dashboard link>. Rollback trigger: <criteria>."

## Post-event Validation (Closeout)
Within 1 hour of stabilization:
1. Confirm SLO impact snapshot:
   - Multi-region availability SLO burn during the window
   - Peak 5xx and duration
2. Confirm client experience:
   - Streaming reconnect metrics improved
   - Retry-after usage (no prolonged hard timeouts)
3. Confirm operational hygiene:
   - Controller did not flap (flip rate within limits)
   - Alerts returned to green and are not noisy
4. Create follow-ups:
   - Any signal gaps (missing probe, delayed burn alert)
   - Any dashboard gaps (missing annotations)
   - Any guardrails needed (rate limits, hold-down tuning)

## Escalation / Ownership
- SRE On-Call: owns incident coordination and decision to execute/rollback
- Routing On-Call (Smart Routing): owns policy semantics, weights, constraints
- Network On-Call: validates reachability anomalies and provider symptoms
- Observability: dashboards/alert correctness

If repeated flapping or unclear signals occur, escalate to:
- Miles Thornton (Failover architecture lead)
- Rafael Mendes (SRE delivery lead)

## Appendix A: Quick Validation Queries (Examples)
Use these as mental checklists (exact query syntax varies by dashboard):
- 5xx rate by region and route over last 10m vs 1h
- Burn-rate: fast window (5m) and slow window (1h) both elevated
- Cross-region probes: `success_rate < 95%` and/or latency step-change
- Destination headroom: sustained queue depth below alert threshold and autoscaler not maxed

## Appendix B: Controlled Test Notes
For planned game days:
- Always run in a limited blast radius configuration (approved tenants/routes)
- Announce start/end in #eng-sre and #support with explicit customer comms plan
- Use dry-run first, then execute with pre-agreed rollback triggers
- Ensure dashboards have automation annotations enabled before start

title: Progressive Capacity Rebalancer: Design and Runbook
preview:
Summary:
This page describes the design, configuration, and operational runbook for the Progressive Capacity Rebalancer (PCR) - a safety-first autoscaling orchestration component Redwood SRE uses to rebalance reserved GPU capacity across model tenants when traffic surges, error budgets deplete, or regional capacity events occur. The PCR complements existing autoscalers by applying SLO-aware, staged reallocation and graceful drain semantics to minimize customer impact during capacity shocks.

Goals:
- Preserve customer-facing SLOs (p99 latency and availability targets) for critical tenants while minimizing global request loss.
- Apply predictable, auditable capacity shifts with automatic rollback on SLO regressions.
- Provide an operator-run, testable mechanism for rapid capacity rebalancing during incidents and planned maintenance.

When to use PCR:
- Multi-tenant cluster shortage leading to tenant eviction risk.
- Cross-region outages where traffic must failover and reserved pools need rebalancing.
- Post-deployment surge that exhausts dedicated pools and threatens production SLOs.
- As a controlled remediation when automatic autoscaling cannot restore SLOs quickly.

High-level architecture (text diagram):
- Inputs: SLO telemetry (latency, error-rate, availability), capacity telemetry (GPU utilization, scheduled capacity, queued work), tenant priority / SLO weight catalogue, global capacity state.
- Controller: PCR service running in control plane (k8s Deployment), includes Decision Engine + Safety Gate + Action Executor.
- Executors: Cloud capacity APIs, Dedicated orchestration service (dedicated-gp-pool-manager), traffic-router to update canary/weighted routing.
- Observability: PCR dashboard, activity log, incident timeline stream to PagerDuty.

Core concepts and terms:
- Priority Class: tenant classification (critical / high / standard / best-effort) mapped to weight 10/7/4/1.
- SLO Anchor: the tenant metric we attempt to preserve (e.g., inference p99 latency <= 150ms, availability >= 99.5%).
- Guarded Rebalance Window: staged period (default 5m) where changes are executed and monitored before progressing.
- Debt Floor: the minimum capacity reserved for critical tenants (configurable).
- Decommit: voluntary temporary reduction of a tenant's reservation (shrink GPUs assigned) to free capacity.

Decision Engine (algorithm summary):
1. Detect event: PCR watches SLO alerts, capacity events, or manual triggers.
2. Score tenants: compute a stability score = (SLO_violation_severity * weight) + (utilization_penalty) - (recent_rebalances_penalty).
3. Select candidates: prefer decommit on best-effort / standard tenants with low recent-decommit counts and non-traffic-spiky patterns.
4. Plan staged actions: generate a plan with steps (decommit X GPUs from tenant A -> assign to pool B -> shift Y% traffic over T minutes).
5. Safety Gate checks: simulate SLO impact using recent traces and reject plans that predict >2% global customer error increase or violate Debt Floor.
6. Execute with observability: run first stage, monitor metrics for Guarded Rebalance Window, continue or rollback based on regression thresholds.

Configuration knobs and recommended defaults:
- guard_window_minutes: 5
- max_decommit_per_cycle: 2
- debt_floor_percent: 12
- slo_regression_threshold_percent: 3
- plan_stages_limit: 4
- simulation_confidence: 90
- cool_down_minutes: 20

Safety and invariant checks:
- Never decommit below Debt Floor for tenants in Priority Class critical.
- Never allocate GPUs across regions without cross-region routing confirmation.
- Always run the Safety Gate simulation; manual overrides require two approvers from SRE oncall and Platform.
- Maintain an immutable audit trail for every plan (who/what/when).

Operational runbook: Progressive Rebalance incident flow
1) Detection and triage
   - Trigger: PCR auto-detects sustained cluster utilization > 90% for 10 minutes or SLO alert fires for >2 tenant-critical services.
   - PagerDuty: Severity P1 if critical tenants impacted or if global availability drops below 99.0%. Notify oncall-sre and platform-ops channel (#sre-capacity).

2) Initial assessment (5-10 minutes)
   - Owner: oncall SRE.
   - Check dashboards: PCR dashboard, cluster GPU utilization, tenant SLO heatmap, routing logs.
   - Confirm scope: impacted tenants, regions, and whether this is traffic-driven or infrastructure degradation.

3) Run PCR simulation
   - Command example: pcrctl simulate --cluster CLUSTER --lookback 15m --confidence 95
   - Validate: Simulation returns plan with staged decommits and predicted SLO impact. If simulation_confidence < configured threshold, escalate to platform-engineering.

4) Execute staged plan (automated preferred, manual if confidence < threshold)
   - Step A (Stage 1): decommit up to max_decommit_per_cycle from selected best-effort tenants and assign to at-risk pools.
   - Monitor: During guard_window_minutes watch p99 latency, error-rate, queue length, and request succeeded rate per tenant.
   - Acceptance criteria to proceed: no tenant SLO regression above slo_regression_threshold_percent and no new pager triggers.

5) Roll forward or roll back
   - Roll forward: If acceptance criteria met, apply next stage.
   - Roll back: If any regression breach occurs, PCR triggers automatic rollback of last stage and notifies oncall.
   - Manual override: Two SRE approvers required to force proceed after a failed gate. Document reason in PCR activity log.

6) Post-event reconciliation (within 24 hours)
   - Owner: PCR owner + oncall SRE who handled the event.
   - Actions: revert temporary decommits (if not auto-reverted), update capacity reservations, file an incident review if critical tenants had SLO violations.
   - Deliverable: Short incident note with timeline, plan used, metrics snapshot, and recommendations. Attach to incident tracker.

Testing and validation:
- Unit test: Decision Engine deterministic outputs for synthetic telemetry.
- Integration test: end-to-end test in staging with traffic replay harness (see pcr-test-runbook).
- Chaos test: run a weekly chaos job that simulates a regional capacity loss and verifies PCR never violates Debt Floor for critical tenants.
- Canary: PCR rollout is canaryed to 10% of control plane instances and monitored for 7 days with reduced automation (manual approval required).

Telemetry and dashboards (required panels):
- Global capacity map: per-region GPU pools, free capacity, reserved capacity.
- Tenant SLO heatmap: color-coded p99 latency and availability by priority class.
- PCR activity log: last 24h executed plans, stages, operator, and auto-rollbacks.
- Simulation confidence trend and historical prediction error.

Operational tooling and CLI examples:
- Simulate plan: pcrctl simulate --cluster eu-west-1 --lookback 30m --confidence 95
- Dry-run apply: pcrctl apply --plan PLAN_ID --dry-run
- Execute plan (manual): pcrctl execute --plan PLAN_ID --approver "mchen" --approver "dalvarez"
- Rollback last stage: pcrctl rollback --plan PLAN_ID --stage 2 --reason "SLO regression p99 up 4%"

Incident review SOP (if SLO violation occurred):
1. Create incident artifact in /incidents and tag with pcr-plan-id.
2. Populate timeline with PCR plan stages, decision rationale, and metric snapshots.
3. Attach simulation inputs and outputs and identify prediction deltas.
4. Action items: tune scoring weights, increase Debt Floor if necessary, add tenant-level prefails for high-variance workloads.

Ownership and run cadence:
- Primary owner: Maya Chen (SRE Capacity & Orchestration) - operational contact: @mchen
- Secondary owners: Platform Orchestration (team leads: Diego Alvarez), Observability (Priya Natarajan)
- Weekly swimlane: automated PCR dry-run simulation every Monday 03:00 UTC against last 7-day peak.
- Quarterly: review Debt Floor and priority weights with product and customer-success for major enterprise tenants.

Security and compliance considerations:
- All plan mutation APIs are RBAC protected; manual overrides require two approvers.
- Audit logs are forwarded to S3 with a 7-year retention for enterprise audit requests.
- Any cross-region reallocations must respect customer data residency flags; PCR uses tenant metadata to block unsafe moves.

Appendix A - Example plan (text summary):
- plan_id: pcr-20260210-03
- created_by: pcr-automaton
- stages: stage 1: decommit tenant-standard-42 GPUs:1 guard_minutes:5; stage 2: assign pool eu-west-reserve-1 GPUs:1 guard_minutes:5

Appendix B - Quick checklist for emergency manual invocation:
- Post in #sre-capacity: brief summary + targeted plan_id.
- Ensure two approvers available in Slack for manual proceed.
- Run simulate and confirm plan confidence >= configured threshold.
- Begin Stage 1 with continuous watch and live metrics snapshotting.

Change log:
- 2025-11-04: Initial draft by Maya Chen.
- 2025-12-12: Added Safety Gate simulation thresholds and Debt Floor defaults (reviewed by Diego Alvarez).
- 2026-02-10: Expanded incident SOP and test matrix; published.

Related runbooks and pages:
- capacity-onboarding-checklist-for-new-model-tenants-2026 (capacity-planning)
- burst-absorber-architecture-and-decision-playbook-2026 (operational-tooling)

Questions or feedback: ping Maya Chen in #sre-capacity or open a PR against this page.
