title: Incident Retrospectives, Error-Budget Governance, and Capacity Ownership Playbook
preview:
Summary

This playbook defines a single, pragmatic process that ties SLOs and error budgets to capacity planning, incident review standards, and operational tooling. It is intended for SREs, serving-runtime engineers, platform owners, and oncall engineers who participate in incident response and reliability initiatives.

Scope

- Applies to all customer-facing inference services (hosted API, Dedicated clusters) and the routing/control plane that affects request admission or fallbacks.
- Excludes non-production test clusters unless an incident impacts production via shared infra.

Principles

1. Make decisions from measurable signals (SLOs, queue depth, GPU metrics, and latency histograms).
2. Prioritize fast guardrails over perfect prediction — we want safe defaults and clear escalation.
3. Keep reviews blameless, actionable, and fast: initial incident timeline within 72 hours, RCA within 7 days.

SLOs and Error Budget Policy

SLO definitions (examples):

| Service Area | Key Metric | SLO (monthly) | Alert Thresholds |
|-------------|------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Text generation API (default route) | Request success rate (4xx+5xx) | 99.95% | Page if 5-min rolling error rate > 0.3% and absolute errors > 50/min |
| Text generation API (default route) | P99 latency (server-side) | 300 ms (99.9% of requests <= 300ms) | Alert when 5-min p99 > 350ms or p95 > 250ms |
| Embeddings service | Median latency | 50 ms (99% <= 120ms) | Warn if 10-min median > 75ms |
| Routing/control plane | Request admission latency | 50 ms (99.9%) | Page if 5-min p99 > 100ms |

Error budget burn policy (operational):

- Compute burn rate = (error_budget_consumed) / (time_window_fraction). If burn rate > 2 for 1 hour -> SRE paging and immediate mitigation call.
- If burn rate > 4 sustained over 6 hours -> declare service-level reliability incident and trigger capacity mitigation plan (see Capacity section).
- If cumulative monthly budget consumed > 60% -> schedule a reliability initiative (owner: SRE product reliability lead).

Procedures when error budget is burning rapidly:

1. Triage (first 30 minutes): identify metric(s) consuming budget (errors vs latency). Post findings in the incident channel.
2. Apply short-term mitigations: traffic shaping, autoscale policies, route weight adjustments to lower-cost/compatible model variants, enable aggressive caching.
3. If mitigations fail and burn rate remains > 2 after 60 minutes, escalate to oncall manager + product platform owners.
4. Document every mitigation and its observed effect in the incident timeline.

Capacity Planning and Ownership

Cadence and deliverables:

- Weekly: Capacity healthboard review (light, 30-min) — owners present queue depth, GPU utilization, and forecast for next 7 days.
- Monthly: Capacity planning sync (1-hour) — review monthly growth, committed Dedicated capacity, large upcoming experiments, and quota requests.
- Quarterly: Capacity roadmap (2-hours) — long-term procurement, region expansion, and reserve planning for seasonal peaks.

Signals used in planning (minimum):

- 95/99/99.9 latency percentiles per route
- Error rate by code class (4xx vs 5xx)
- Active concurrent requests and average request length (tokens)
- GPU utilization (per-device), GPU memory pressure, NVMe IO stalls
- Queue depth / request backlog (per-service)
- Cache hit ratio (prefix and KV cache)

Capacity ownership matrix (high level):

- SRE: runbook maintenance, alerting, incident review facilitation, and capacity healthboard.
- Serving Runtime: model packing, kernel tuning, quantization profiles, autoscale policies.
- Platform/Cloud Ops: cluster provisioning, GPU fleet procurement, networking and capacity quotas.
- Product Teams: forecast workload changes (feature launches, marketing events) 4 weeks in advance.

Capacity triggers and automated actions (examples):

- Queue depth > 2000 requests for 10 minutes -> autoscale add 2 nodes (if spare pool available).
- GPU utilization > 85% and Swap/Memory pressure > 70% for 5 minutes -> scale up by 1 node and alert owners.
- Warmup cold-start rate > 10% of total requests in last 15 minutes -> prewarm policy enable for model variants.

Reliability Initiatives and Prioritization

Initiatives are prioritized by business impact and technical debt cost. Examples of repeatable initiative types:

- Cost-stability: Reduce per-token variance by implementing prefix caching for high-volume prompt shapes.
- Latency-tail: Improve attention kernel selection and batching heuristics to reduce p99 by 20%.
- Observability: Add route-specific histograms and request traces to reduce triage time.

Prioritization rubric (used by SRE and Product):

1. User-facing severity (page frequency, customer SLA exposure) — 40%
2. Expected reduction in error budget burn — 30%
3. Implementation complexity and risk — 20%
4. Cross-team dependencies and visibility — 10%

Incident Review Standards (postmortem policy)

Severity definitions (high level):

- Sev0: Global outage (>30% of production traffic impacted or >10% revenue impact).
- Sev1: Major degradation (important routes > 5% traffic affected for > 15 minutes).
- Sev2: Partial degradation (localized customer impact or transient errors).
- Sev3: Minor, single-customer, or non-customer-impacting operational issue.

Timeline and artifacts:

1. Incident declared -> incident channel and page created (T+0).
2. Initial incident timeline posted in Confluence within 72 hours (T+72h). Template must include timeline, impact summary, mitigations, and owners.
3. Root Cause Analysis and action items documented within 7 calendar days (T+7d). Action items must have an owner and target completion date.
4. Blameless review meeting scheduled within 14 days. Attendance: incident oncall, SRE manager, owning service PM and engineering lead.

Postmortem template (required headings):

- Title and incident ID
- Severity and scope
- Timeline (UTC)
- Customer impact (metrics)
- Root cause
- Contributing factors
- Actions (owners & due dates)
- Follow-up initiatives (if any)
- Learning / documentation updates

Operational Tooling & Dashboards

Key dashboards (names and intent):

- SRE / Healthboard: Global SLO dashboard with burn rate, per-route SLOs, and team ownership.
- Runtime Performance: Per-model p50/p95/p99 latency trends, GPU metrics, and batch sizes.
- Queue Insights: Backlog, inflight, retry counts, and cache hit ratios.
- Alert Triage: Active alerts, correlated symptoms, and past-24h alert volume.

Alerting best practices (do):

- Alert on symptoms you can act on in < 15 minutes.
- Prefer narrow, route-scoped alerts for noisy systems.
- Set paging thresholds conservatively; use warnings for near-threshold states.

Alert examples (concrete):

- Pager: "text-gen-default:p99 > 350ms (5m)" — PAGER -> oncall SRE + runtime lead.
- Warning: "embeddings-median > 75ms (10m)" — slack-only alert to owners.

Runbook snippets and CLI commands (copyable):

- Drain a serving node (k8s):
  1) kubectl cordon <node>
  2) kubectl delete pod -l app=serving-runtime --field-selector spec.nodeName=<node> --grace-period=60
  3) Monitor queue depth and in-flight requests until < 5% are draining

- Temporarily reduce model variant weight (routing API):
  curl -X POST https://controlplane.internal/api/v1/route-weight \
    -H 'Authorization: Bearer $CP_TOKEN' \
    -d '{"route":"text-gen-default","weights":{"fast-quant":"0.5","fallback":"0.5"}}'

- Force-enable prefix caching for route (CLI):
  redwood-cli routing cache set --route text-gen-default --mode aggressive --ttl 180

SRE Runbooks (scenario-driven)

Runbook: Persistent p99 tail latency increase (p99 > 350ms for 10 minutes)

1. Scope: Apply to text-generation and chat routes. Owner: oncall SRE.
2. Triage: Check SLO dashboard -> isolate whether latency is model compute-bound, queuing, or network.
3. Immediate mitigations (in order):
   a) Reduce max concurrency per worker to favor latency over throughput (serving-runtime knob).
   b) Shift 25–50% of traffic to smaller model variant via route-weight change.
   c) Enable aggressive batching cutoff (ends long context batching) if p95 latency unaffected.
4. If p99 remains > 350ms after 30 minutes, escalate to runtime lead and open an incident review.
5. Post-incident: Create action item to adjust kernel selection or change quantization profile.

Runbook: Sudden queue/backlog growth and dropped requests

1. Scope: Any route with sustained backlog growth (queue depth increase > 500 within 5 minutes).
2. Triage: Verify autoscaler health, node churn, and any recent deploys. Look for increased average tokens per request.
3. Immediate mitigations:
   a) Increase autoscale target (if spare capacity exists).
   b) Route-weight diversion to Dedicated or lower-latency region.
   c) Temporarily set request admission control to shed low-priority traffic.
4. Escalation: If backlog not reduced within 15 minutes -> page platform operations.
5. Follow-up: Plan capacity increase in next weekly capacity healthboard if trend continues.

Runbook: Model-serving KV/cache exhaustion (cache miss spike causing CPU/GPU pressure)

1. Triage: Correlate cache hit ratio drop with latency and GPU utilization.
2. Mitigations:
   a) Increase KV cache size (where safe) for affected fleet.
   b) Enable aggressive TTL extension for newly-seen prefixes.
   c) If cache resizing requires rollout, shift traffic to cached-compatible model variants.
3. Owners: runtime + infra. Required safety checks: memory pressure < 60% before increasing local cache size.
4. Post-incident: Add synthetic test that warms common prefixes used in production.

Metrics and Reporting

- Monthly reliability report (owner: SRE lead) must include SLO attainment, top 5 incident types, trend of error budget consumption, and active initiatives.
- Quarterly executive summary with capacity commitments and any procurement needs.

Appendix: Quick checklist for a declared incident

1. Create incident page and channel (T+0).
2. Capture initial metrics and short timeline (T+15m).
3. Apply first-line mitigations and note effects (T+30m).
4. Decide whether to page escalation (T+60m) based on burn rate/impact.
5. Post initial timeline within 72h and assign RCA within 7 days.

Related pages and references:
- SLO escalation guide: /confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/slo-oncall-escalation-and-capacity-triggers-2026
- Runbook examples: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/service-degradation-and-fallbacks-runbook-2026
- Observability standards: /confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards

Document maintenance

- Owner: SRE (Priya Natarajan). Review cadence: every 6 months or after any Sev0/Sev1 with > 4 hours time-to-resolution.
- To propose edits: open a PR in the confluence content repo and add reviewer Marcus Lee.

End of playbook.
text:
Summary

This playbook defines a single, pragmatic process that ties SLOs and error budgets to capacity planning, incident review standards, and operational tooling. It is intended for SREs, serving-runtime engineers, platform owners, and oncall engineers who participate in incident response and reliability initiatives.

Scope

- Applies to all customer-facing inference services (hosted API, Dedicated clusters) and the routing/control plane that affects request admission or fallbacks.
- Excludes non-production test clusters unless an incident impacts production via shared infra.

Principles

1. Make decisions from measurable signals (SLOs, queue depth, GPU metrics, and latency histograms).
2. Prioritize fast guardrails over perfect prediction — we want safe defaults and clear escalation.
3. Keep reviews blameless, actionable, and fast: initial incident timeline within 72 hours, RCA within 7 days.

SLOs and Error Budget Policy

SLO definitions (examples):

| Service Area | Key Metric | SLO (monthly) | Alert Thresholds |
|-------------|------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Text generation API (default route) | Request success rate (4xx+5xx) | 99.95% | Page if 5-min rolling error rate > 0.3% and absolute errors > 50/min |
| Text generation API (default route) | P99 latency (server-side) | 300 ms (99.9% of requests <= 300ms) | Alert when 5-min p99 > 350ms or p95 > 250ms |
| Embeddings service | Median latency | 50 ms (99% <= 120ms) | Warn if 10-min median > 75ms |
| Routing/control plane | Request admission latency | 50 ms (99.9%) | Page if 5-min p99 > 100ms |

Error budget burn policy (operational):

- Compute burn rate = (error_budget_consumed) / (time_window_fraction). If burn rate > 2 for 1 hour -> SRE paging and immediate mitigation call.
- If burn rate > 4 sustained over 6 hours -> declare service-level reliability incident and trigger capacity mitigation plan (see Capacity section).
- If cumulative monthly budget consumed > 60% -> schedule a reliability initiative (owner: SRE product reliability lead).

Procedures when error budget is burning rapidly:

1. Triage (first 30 minutes): identify metric(s) consuming budget (errors vs latency). Post findings in the incident channel.
2. Apply short-term mitigations: traffic shaping, autoscale policies, route weight adjustments to lower-cost/compatible model variants, enable aggressive caching.
3. If mitigations fail and burn rate remains > 2 after 60 minutes, escalate to oncall manager + product platform owners.
4. Document every mitigation and its observed effect in the incident timeline.

Capacity Planning and Ownership

Cadence and deliverables:

- Weekly: Capacity healthboard review (light, 30-min) — owners present queue depth, GPU utilization, and forecast for next 7 days.
- Monthly: Capacity planning sync (1-hour) — review monthly growth, committed Dedicated capacity, large upcoming experiments, and quota requests.
- Quarterly: Capacity roadmap (2-hours) — long-term procurement, region expansion, and reserve planning for seasonal peaks.

Signals used in planning (minimum):

- 95/99/99.9 latency percentiles per route
- Error rate by code class (4xx vs 5xx)
- Active concurrent requests and average request length (tokens)
- GPU utilization (per-device), GPU memory pressure, NVMe IO stalls
- Queue depth / request backlog (per-service)
- Cache hit ratio (prefix and KV cache)

Capacity ownership matrix (high level):

- SRE: runbook maintenance, alerting, incident review facilitation, and capacity healthboard.
- Serving Runtime: model packing, kernel tuning, quantization profiles, autoscale policies.
- Platform/Cloud Ops: cluster provisioning, GPU fleet procurement, networking and capacity quotas.
- Product Teams: forecast workload changes (feature launches, marketing events) 4 weeks in advance.

Capacity triggers and automated actions (examples):

- Queue depth > 2000 requests for 10 minutes -> autoscale add 2 nodes (if spare pool available).
- GPU utilization > 85% and Swap/Memory pressure > 70% for 5 minutes -> scale up by 1 node and alert owners.
- Warmup cold-start rate > 10% of total requests in last 15 minutes -> prewarm policy enable for model variants.

Reliability Initiatives and Prioritization

Initiatives are prioritized by business impact and technical debt cost. Examples of repeatable initiative types:

- Cost-stability: Reduce per-token variance by implementing prefix caching for high-volume prompt shapes.
- Latency-tail: Improve attention kernel selection and batching heuristics to reduce p99 by 20%.
- Observability: Add route-specific histograms and request traces to reduce triage time.

Prioritization rubric (used by SRE and Product):

1. User-facing severity (page frequency, customer SLA exposure) — 40%
2. Expected reduction in error budget burn — 30%
3. Implementation complexity and risk — 20%
4. Cross-team dependencies and visibility — 10%

Incident Review Standards (postmortem policy)

Severity definitions (high level):

- Sev0: Global outage (>30% of production traffic impacted or >10% revenue impact).
- Sev1: Major degradation (important routes > 5% traffic affected for > 15 minutes).
- Sev2: Partial degradation (localized customer impact or transient errors).
- Sev3: Minor, single-customer, or non-customer-impacting operational issue.

Timeline and artifacts:

1. Incident declared -> incident channel and page created (T+0).
2. Initial incident timeline posted in Confluence within 72 hours (T+72h). Template must include timeline, impact summary, mitigations, and owners.
3. Root Cause Analysis and action items documented within 7 calendar days (T+7d). Action items must have an owner and target completion date.
4. Blameless review meeting scheduled within 14 days. Attendance: incident oncall, SRE manager, owning service PM and engineering lead.

Postmortem template (required headings):

- Title and incident ID
- Severity and scope
- Timeline (UTC)
- Customer impact (metrics)
- Root cause
- Contributing factors
- Actions (owners & due dates)
- Follow-up initiatives (if any)
- Learning / documentation updates

Operational Tooling & Dashboards

Key dashboards (names and intent):

- SRE / Healthboard: Global SLO dashboard with burn rate, per-route SLOs, and team ownership.
- Runtime Performance: Per-model p50/p95/p99 latency trends, GPU metrics, and batch sizes.
- Queue Insights: Backlog, inflight, retry counts, and cache hit ratios.
- Alert Triage: Active alerts, correlated symptoms, and past-24h alert volume.

Alerting best practices (do):

- Alert on symptoms you can act on in < 15 minutes.
- Prefer narrow, route-scoped alerts for noisy systems.
- Set paging thresholds conservatively; use warnings for near-threshold states.

Alert examples (concrete):

- Pager: "text-gen-default:p99 > 350ms (5m)" — PAGER -> oncall SRE + runtime lead.
- Warning: "embeddings-median > 75ms (10m)" — slack-only alert to owners.

Runbook snippets and CLI commands (copyable):

- Drain a serving node (k8s):
  1) kubectl cordon <node>
  2) kubectl delete pod -l app=serving-runtime --field-selector spec.nodeName=<node> --grace-period=60
  3) Monitor queue depth and in-flight requests until < 5% are draining

- Temporarily reduce model variant weight (routing API):
  curl -X POST https://controlplane.internal/api/v1/route-weight \
    -H 'Authorization: Bearer $CP_TOKEN' \
    -d '{"route":"text-gen-default","weights":{"fast-quant":"0.5","fallback":"0.5"}}'

- Force-enable prefix caching for route (CLI):
  redwood-cli routing cache set --route text-gen-default --mode aggressive --ttl 180

SRE Runbooks (scenario-driven)

Runbook: Persistent p99 tail latency increase (p99 > 350ms for 10 minutes)

1. Scope: Apply to text-generation and chat routes. Owner: oncall SRE.
2. Triage: Check SLO dashboard -> isolate whether latency is model compute-bound, queuing, or network.
3. Immediate mitigations (in order):
   a) Reduce max concurrency per worker to favor latency over throughput (serving-runtime knob).
   b) Shift 25–50% of traffic to smaller model variant via route-weight change.
   c) Enable aggressive batching cutoff (ends long context batching) if p95 latency unaffected.
4. If p99 remains > 350ms after 30 minutes, escalate to runtime lead and open an incident review.
5. Post-incident: Create action item to adjust kernel selection or change quantization profile.

Runbook: Sudden queue/backlog growth and dropped requests

1. Scope: Any route with sustained backlog growth (queue depth increase > 500 within 5 minutes).
2. Triage: Verify autoscaler health, node churn, and any recent deploys. Look for increased average tokens per request.
3. Immediate mitigations:
   a) Increase autoscale target (if spare capacity exists).
   b) Route-weight diversion to Dedicated or lower-latency region.
   c) Temporarily set request admission control to shed low-priority traffic.
4. Escalation: If backlog not reduced within 15 minutes -> page platform operations.
5. Follow-up: Plan capacity increase in next weekly capacity healthboard if trend continues.

Runbook: Model-serving KV/cache exhaustion (cache miss spike causing CPU/GPU pressure)

1. Triage: Correlate cache hit ratio drop with latency and GPU utilization.
2. Mitigations:
   a) Increase KV cache size (where safe) for affected fleet.
   b) Enable aggressive TTL extension for newly-seen prefixes.
   c) If cache resizing requires rollout, shift traffic to cached-compatible model variants.
3. Owners: runtime + infra. Required safety checks: memory pressure < 60% before increasing local cache size.
4. Post-incident: Add synthetic test that warms common prefixes used in production.

Metrics and Reporting

- Monthly reliability report (owner: SRE lead) must include SLO attainment, top 5 incident types, trend of error budget consumption, and active initiatives.
- Quarterly executive summary with capacity commitments and any procurement needs.

Appendix: Quick checklist for a declared incident

1. Create incident page and channel (T+0).
2. Capture initial metrics and short timeline (T+15m).
3. Apply first-line mitigations and note effects (T+30m).
4. Decide whether to page escalation (T+60m) based on burn rate/impact.
5. Post initial timeline within 72h and assign RCA within 7 days.

Related pages and references:
- SLO escalation guide: /confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/slo-oncall-escalation-and-capacity-triggers-2026
- Runbook examples: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/service-degradation-and-fallbacks-runbook-2026
- Observability standards: /confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards

Document maintenance

- Owner: SRE (Priya Natarajan). Review cadence: every 6 months or after any Sev0/Sev1 with > 4 hours time-to-resolution.
- To propose edits: open a PR in the confluence content repo and add reviewer Marcus Lee.

End of playbook.

title: Regional Failover Automation Upgrade — Program Overview (Reliability Program 2026)
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is upgrading its regional failover automation as part of the 2026 Reliability Program (SLO maturity, incident reduction, and graceful degradation).

Today, region failover is a mix of manual steps and partially automated routing policies. Trigger signals are frequently noisy (false positives), slow to confirm true regional impairment, and hard to validate safely. During real incidents, this increases customer-visible errors, creates capacity cascades, and increases on-call cognitive load.

This initiative standardizes trigger signal hierarchy, adds deterministic policy evaluation and rollback guardrails, improves observability, and validates the end-to-end system via controlled failover tests (“game days”) for Hosted API and Dedicated.

---

## Problem statement
### Customer impact we are addressing
When a region is impaired (network reachability, elevated 5xx, tail latency blowups, or capacity exhaustion), customers experience:
- Elevated error rate (timeouts/5xx) and increased tail latency
- Streaming disconnects/retries during redirects
- Slower time-to-mitigate due to manual verification and decisioning
- Secondary impact from overload/thundering herd when traffic shifts unevenly

### Current-state gaps
1) **Noisy triggers and inconsistent prioritization**
- Multiple signal sources exist (gateway, runtime, network, fleet capacity), but the decision logic is inconsistent across routes and environments.
- Alerts are not always correlated to a regional impairment; local service issues can look like regional failures.

2) **Detection is slow and hard to validate**
- On-call often needs to validate impairment manually across dashboards.
- There is no reliable “region health” aggregation service acting as a single source of truth.

3) **Automation guardrails are incomplete**
- Partial automation can flip routing policies without consistent anti-flap protections.
- Rollback semantics are not standardized; oscillation risk increases under intermittent impairment.

4) **Limited game day coverage**
- Testing is ad hoc and not repeatable.
- There is no consistent, measurable success criteria (failover time, error budget burn, overload behavior).

---

## Goals (what success looks like)
### Primary goals
- **Reduce customer-visible impact** during regional impairments by shrinking detection + failover time and preventing cascading overload.
- **Standardize failover trigger hierarchy** and rollback behavior across Hosted API and Dedicated.
- **Prove reliability via controlled tests** with repeatable, low-blast-radius game days in staging and production.
- **Improve observability** so on-call can distinguish region-level impairment vs. service-level/local failures in minutes.

### Success metrics (initial targets)
These are program-level targets; exact thresholds are defined in the SLO doc and alert specs.
- **Time to detect (TTD):** reduce by 50% vs. baseline for regional impairment scenarios
- **Time to fail over (TTFO):** achieve consistent automated failover within a defined window for validated impairments (target range: low single-digit minutes, dependent on DNS/traffic steering constraints)
- **Availability outcome:** reduce multi-region availability error budget burn during regional incidents
- **False positives:** materially reduce paging/automation actions caused by non-regional issues (track “automation action rate” vs. confirmed incidents)
- **No oscillation:** enforce anti-flap limits (max policy flips per tenant per hour; hold-down timers) with auditability

---

## Non-goals
- Multi-cloud migration or changing cloud providers
- Rewriting the core serving runtime
- Changing customer contracts/SLA language (tracked separately)
- Fully bespoke automation for Private deployments (we will provide guidance and recommended patterns)

---

## Scope
### In-scope
- Hosted API + Dedicated: multi-region routing and policy evaluation
- Region health aggregation (reachability, latency/error burn, capacity, network reachability) with caching/TTL
- Failover automation controller with deterministic evaluation, dry-run mode, approval hooks (where required), and rollback guardrails
- Circuit breakers/load shedding behavior during failover to avoid thundering herd
- Dashboards, alerts, and on-call runbooks
- Incident-bot/status comms support (annotations, summaries, operator-friendly audit trails)
- Controlled failover tests in staging and production with explicit success criteria and rollback conditions

### Adjacent / partial
- Private deployments: documentation + recommended patterns, explicit boundaries on what Redwood supports

---

## Stakeholders and ownership
### Program leadership
- **Executive sponsor:** Neha Kapoor (VP Infrastructure & Reliability)
- **Reliability program lead:** Sean Gallagher (Director SRE)
- **Delivery lead:** Rafael Mendes (SRE Manager)
- **Architecture lead:** Miles Thornton (Staff SRE)

### Core engineering partners
- **Routing engineering lead:** Mina Haddad
- **Routing policy tech lead:** Ravi Menon
- **Control plane lead:** Ethan Park
- **Observability lead:** Allison Grant
- **Network reachability lead:** Victor Alvarez

### Cross-functional
- Product (Console messaging + customer expectations): Nikhil Sharma (Hosted), Lauren Bishop (Dedicated)
- Support/CS comms and escalations: Dev Patel (Support escalation owner)

---

## System overview (what we are building)
### High-level architecture (described)
1) **Signal pipeline** collects regional signals:
- Network reachability (synthetic probes + provider symptoms)
- Error-rate burn (5xx/timeout burn against SLO)
- Tail latency burn (p95/p99 burn vs SLO thresholds)
- Capacity headroom + backpressure signals (fleet)

2) **Region Health Service** aggregates signals into a consistent regional health view:
- Normalizes inputs, applies TTL/jitter, emits “region health state” and evidence

3) **Policy Engine** evaluates deterministic trigger hierarchy:
- Applies signal priority, corroboration rules, dampening, and hold-down timers

4) **Failover Controller** executes safe routing changes (or recommends via dry-run):
- Produces a decision and optionally executes policy flips
- Enforces per-tenant safety limits and anti-flap
- Emits structured audit events and annotations

5) **Data plane safeguards** reduce amplification during failover:
- Gateway circuit breaker tuning, retries/timeouts, standardized overload responses (Retry-After)
- Streaming reconnect behavior improvements on redirect

---

## Current-state workflow vs target workflow
### Current-state (simplified)
- On-call receives signal(s) (often noisy)
- Manual cross-check across dashboards and provider status
- Manual or semi-automated routing policy changes
- Ad hoc rollback and post-event validation

### Target-state workflow (simplified)
- Signals corroborate; region health summarizes impairment state with evidence
- Policy engine evaluates deterministic trigger hierarchy
- Controller runs in **dry-run** by default (initial rollout), posting recommended actions + confidence/evidence
- With guardrails satisfied (and optional approvals), controller executes automated failover
- Anti-flap + rollback semantics prevent oscillation
- Audit log + dashboard annotations provide a clear timeline

---

## Trigger hierarchy (program-level)
The authoritative policy is defined in ADR-042; the summary below exists to align stakeholders.

**Principle:** Prefer signals that indicate true inability to serve traffic over symptoms that could be local or transient.

Proposed priority order (summary):
1) **Reachability loss** (authoritative; cross-region probes + edge reachability)
2) **5xx/timeout burn rate** (SLO burn; corroborated)
3) **Tail latency burn** (p95/p99; corroborated)
4) **Capacity exhaustion / headroom collapse** (used for gating failover and for pre-failover load shedding)

Guardrails (summary):
- Dampening windows to avoid reacting to brief blips
- Hold-down timers to avoid oscillation
- Corroboration requirement for symptom-based triggers
- Per-tenant blast-radius constraints and max flips per hour

---

## Rollback semantics (program-level)
The authoritative policy is defined in ADR-043; summary below:
- Rollback is not simply “signals look better.”
- Rollback requires passing a stability window and respecting hold-down timers.
- Automation must record an audit event for every recommend/execute/rollback action, including evidence signals.
- Anti-flap rate limits apply to both failover and rollback.

---

## Service ownership map
The goal is to make ownership boundaries explicit so incidents do not stall on “who owns the knob.”

| Area | Component / Service | Primary owner | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Signals | perf-canary cross-region probes | Victor Alvarez (Network) | Publishes reachability + latency probes to ingestion stream |
| Signals | fleet headroom / capacity metrics | Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet/Capacity) | Defines headroom thresholds used for routing gates |
| Aggregation | Region Health Service | Ethan Park (Control Plane) | Source of truth for regional health state; TTL + jitter |
| Decisioning | Routing policy engine | Ravi Menon (Routing) / Jordan Lee (Policy engine) | Deterministic evaluation, hierarchy, corroboration, timers |
| Execution | Failover automation controller | Miles Thornton (SRE) / Benji Okafor (Guardrails) | Dry-run, approvals, audit trail, anti-flap, per-tenant limits |
| Data plane | API gateway circuit breakers | Peter Holtz (Overload) | Retries/timeouts, overload responses (Retry-After) |
| Data plane | Streaming redirect/reconnect behavior | Vikram Singh (Data plane) | Idempotency and reconnect guidance |
| Observability | Dashboards/alerts | Allison Grant / Sergio Costa | Burn-rate alerts, failover panels, annotations |
| Tooling | incident-bot summaries | Devon Price | Automated summaries + links posted to incident channels |
| Ops | Runbooks + paging hygiene | Tomáš Novák | Ensures on-call procedures match automation behavior |

---

## Milestones and timeline (Q1–Q2 2026)
This is a planning view; execution is tracked in Linear (ENG-2411 umbrella).

### Phase 1 — Baseline + requirements (Weeks 1–2)
- Capture current failover behavior and top incident failure modes
- Define multi-region SLO targets and event classification
- Finalize trigger hierarchy requirements and acceptance tests

Deliverables:
- Baseline KPI snapshot (TTD/TTFO, false positives, error budget burn)
- Draft signal inventory and data quality notes

### Phase 2 — Design (Weeks 2–4)
- ADRs for signal priority/corroboration/dampening and rollback semantics
- Safety design: per-tenant rate limits, blast-radius constraints
- Game day plan draft + runbook structure

Deliverables:
- ADR-042 and ADR-043 approved
- Initial dashboards spec and alerting plan

### Phase 3 — Build (Weeks 4–8)
- Implement region health aggregation + caching/TTL
- Integrate capacity + network reachability signals into decisioning
- Implement dry-run mode, audit events, approval hooks (optional)
- Improve gateway overload behavior and streaming redirect handling

Deliverables:
- Failover controller in dry-run in production for limited routes
- Audit log pipeline + dashboard annotations

### Phase 4 — Validate (Weeks 8–10)
- Staging rehearsals (synthetic impairment scenarios)
- Controlled production failover game day(s) with limited blast radius

Deliverables:
- Game day execution artifacts (checklist, timeline, metrics)
- Issues found + prioritized follow-ups

### Phase 5 — Rollout + hardening (Weeks 10–12+)
- Expand to more routes/models/tiers
- Tune thresholds/timers based on real behavior
- Finalize quarterly testing cadence and automated reporting plan

Deliverables:
- Updated runbooks, dashboards, and documented quarterly game day cadence

---

## Rollout strategy (safety-first)
### Guiding principles
- Prefer **observability and dry-run** before executing actions in production.
- Limit blast radius by **tenant tier**, **route/model**, and **region pair**.
- Every automated action must be explainable (evidence + audit event).

### Proposed rollout stages
1) **Stage A — Signals + dashboards only**
- Region health service produces health states and evidence
- Dashboards/alerts tuned; no automated routing changes

2) **Stage B — Dry-run recommendations**
- Controller posts recommended actions with confidence + evidence
- On-call validates output quality and correlates to incidents

3) **Stage C — Automation with approvals (prod)**
- Controller can execute but requires on-call acknowledgement for specific tiers/routes
- Strict per-tenant and global rate limits

4) **Stage D — Guarded full automation (prod)**
- Approval removed only after meeting acceptance criteria for false positives, oscillation, and game day success
- Continue quarterly controlled failovers as regression prevention

Feature flags (conceptual; exact names live in config):
- enable_region_health_service
- enable_failover_controller_dry_run
- enable_failover_controller_execute
- require_failover_approval_hooks
- enable_capacity_gating
- enable_overload_retry_after

---

## Risks and mitigations
- **False positives causing unnecessary traffic shifts**
  - Mitigation: corroboration rules, dampening, dry-run burn-in, per-tenant limits, auditability

- **Oscillation under intermittent impairment**
  - Mitigation: hold-down timers, anti-flap rate limits, explicit rollback semantics, SRE-owned guardrails

- **Capacity cascades during failover**
  - Mitigation: capacity gating integrated into routing decisions, pre-failover load shedding, gateway retry budget tuning

- **DNS/traffic steering constraints limit speed**
  - Mitigation: ensure TTL strategy and constraints are understood; test with planned changes; align expectations in success criteria

- **Observability cardinality / cost**
  - Mitigation: metric label review, aggregation safeguards, curated dimensions (tier/reason) with strict controls

---

## Communications and incident response alignment
- incident-bot must post:
  - failover recommended/executed/rolled back
  - reason category and evidence summary
  - links to dashboards and audit log query

- Status page/customer comms:
  - Planned tests require pre-brief for select enterprise accounts and clear wording on possible transient impact
  - Real incidents follow regional impairment playbook; approvals for external updates are owned by on-call + comms approver

---

## Open questions / pending decisions
(Owner in parentheses; track in ENG-2411 as sub-items)
- Which routes/models are in the first production automation cohort? (Mina Haddad / Rafael Mendes)
- Final thresholds for capacity gating and what constitutes “safe to fail over” headroom. (Vanessa Ortiz)
- Whether approvals are required for all enterprise tiers indefinitely or only during rollout. (Sean Gallagher / Neha Kapoor)
- Final definition of a “region-level event” vs partial degradation in SLO reporting. (Aisha Bello / Allison Grant)

---

## Action items (next 2 weeks)
- Finalize dashboard panels + alert annotations required for first rehearsal (Allison Grant / Sergio Costa)
- Complete dry-run acceptance criteria and on-call UX (Miles Thornton / Benji Okafor)
- Validate reachability probe coverage and data quality in us-east and eu-west (Victor Alvarez)
- Confirm first production game day scope + go/no-go checklist and rollback authority (Rafael Mendes / Sean Gallagher)

---

## References
- Multi-region SLO definition: confluence://eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/multi-region-availability-slo-2026
- Trigger hierarchy ADR: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-042-failover-trigger-signal-hierarchy
- Rollback ADR: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-043-automated-routing-policy-rollback
- Region Health Service: confluence://eng-platform/systems-and-services/region-health-service
- Failover controller runbook: confluence://eng-platform/runbooks/routing-policy-failover-controller-runbook
- Game day plan: confluence://oncall-and-incident-response/game-days/controlled-failover-test-plan-q1-2026
- Umbrella tracking: linear://engineering/ENG-2411
text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is upgrading its regional failover automation as part of the 2026 Reliability Program (SLO maturity, incident reduction, and graceful degradation).

Today, region failover is a mix of manual steps and partially automated routing policies. Trigger signals are frequently noisy (false positives), slow to confirm true regional impairment, and hard to validate safely. During real incidents, this increases customer-visible errors, creates capacity cascades, and increases on-call cognitive load.

This initiative standardizes trigger signal hierarchy, adds deterministic policy evaluation and rollback guardrails, improves observability, and validates the end-to-end system via controlled failover tests (“game days”) for Hosted API and Dedicated.

---

## Problem statement
### Customer impact we are addressing
When a region is impaired (network reachability, elevated 5xx, tail latency blowups, or capacity exhaustion), customers experience:
- Elevated error rate (timeouts/5xx) and increased tail latency
- Streaming disconnects/retries during redirects
- Slower time-to-mitigate due to manual verification and decisioning
- Secondary impact from overload/thundering herd when traffic shifts unevenly

### Current-state gaps
1) **Noisy triggers and inconsistent prioritization**
- Multiple signal sources exist (gateway, runtime, network, fleet capacity), but the decision logic is inconsistent across routes and environments.
- Alerts are not always correlated to a regional impairment; local service issues can look like regional failures.

2) **Detection is slow and hard to validate**
- On-call often needs to validate impairment manually across dashboards.
- There is no reliable “region health” aggregation service acting as a single source of truth.

3) **Automation guardrails are incomplete**
- Partial automation can flip routing policies without consistent anti-flap protections.
- Rollback semantics are not standardized; oscillation risk increases under intermittent impairment.

4) **Limited game day coverage**
- Testing is ad hoc and not repeatable.
- There is no consistent, measurable success criteria (failover time, error budget burn, overload behavior).

---

## Goals (what success looks like)
### Primary goals
- **Reduce customer-visible impact** during regional impairments by shrinking detection + failover time and preventing cascading overload.
- **Standardize failover trigger hierarchy** and rollback behavior across Hosted API and Dedicated.
- **Prove reliability via controlled tests** with repeatable, low-blast-radius game days in staging and production.
- **Improve observability** so on-call can distinguish region-level impairment vs. service-level/local failures in minutes.

### Success metrics (initial targets)
These are program-level targets; exact thresholds are defined in the SLO doc and alert specs.
- **Time to detect (TTD):** reduce by 50% vs. baseline for regional impairment scenarios
- **Time to fail over (TTFO):** achieve consistent automated failover within a defined window for validated impairments (target range: low single-digit minutes, dependent on DNS/traffic steering constraints)
- **Availability outcome:** reduce multi-region availability error budget burn during regional incidents
- **False positives:** materially reduce paging/automation actions caused by non-regional issues (track “automation action rate” vs. confirmed incidents)
- **No oscillation:** enforce anti-flap limits (max policy flips per tenant per hour; hold-down timers) with auditability

---

## Non-goals
- Multi-cloud migration or changing cloud providers
- Rewriting the core serving runtime
- Changing customer contracts/SLA language (tracked separately)
- Fully bespoke automation for Private deployments (we will provide guidance and recommended patterns)

---

## Scope
### In-scope
- Hosted API + Dedicated: multi-region routing and policy evaluation
- Region health aggregation (reachability, latency/error burn, capacity, network reachability) with caching/TTL
- Failover automation controller with deterministic evaluation, dry-run mode, approval hooks (where required), and rollback guardrails
- Circuit breakers/load shedding behavior during failover to avoid thundering herd
- Dashboards, alerts, and on-call runbooks
- Incident-bot/status comms support (annotations, summaries, operator-friendly audit trails)
- Controlled failover tests in staging and production with explicit success criteria and rollback conditions

### Adjacent / partial
- Private deployments: documentation + recommended patterns, explicit boundaries on what Redwood supports

---

## Stakeholders and ownership
### Program leadership
- **Executive sponsor:** Neha Kapoor (VP Infrastructure & Reliability)
- **Reliability program lead:** Sean Gallagher (Director SRE)
- **Delivery lead:** Rafael Mendes (SRE Manager)
- **Architecture lead:** Miles Thornton (Staff SRE)

### Core engineering partners
- **Routing engineering lead:** Mina Haddad
- **Routing policy tech lead:** Ravi Menon
- **Control plane lead:** Ethan Park
- **Observability lead:** Allison Grant
- **Network reachability lead:** Victor Alvarez

### Cross-functional
- Product (Console messaging + customer expectations): Nikhil Sharma (Hosted), Lauren Bishop (Dedicated)
- Support/CS comms and escalations: Dev Patel (Support escalation owner)

---

## System overview (what we are building)
### High-level architecture (described)
1) **Signal pipeline** collects regional signals:
- Network reachability (synthetic probes + provider symptoms)
- Error-rate burn (5xx/timeout burn against SLO)
- Tail latency burn (p95/p99 burn vs SLO thresholds)
- Capacity headroom + backpressure signals (fleet)

2) **Region Health Service** aggregates signals into a consistent regional health view:
- Normalizes inputs, applies TTL/jitter, emits “region health state” and evidence

3) **Policy Engine** evaluates deterministic trigger hierarchy:
- Applies signal priority, corroboration rules, dampening, and hold-down timers

4) **Failover Controller** executes safe routing changes (or recommends via dry-run):
- Produces a decision and optionally executes policy flips
- Enforces per-tenant safety limits and anti-flap
- Emits structured audit events and annotations

5) **Data plane safeguards** reduce amplification during failover:
- Gateway circuit breaker tuning, retries/timeouts, standardized overload responses (Retry-After)
- Streaming reconnect behavior improvements on redirect

---

## Current-state workflow vs target workflow
### Current-state (simplified)
- On-call receives signal(s) (often noisy)
- Manual cross-check across dashboards and provider status
- Manual or semi-automated routing policy changes
- Ad hoc rollback and post-event validation

### Target-state workflow (simplified)
- Signals corroborate; region health summarizes impairment state with evidence
- Policy engine evaluates deterministic trigger hierarchy
- Controller runs in **dry-run** by default (initial rollout), posting recommended actions + confidence/evidence
- With guardrails satisfied (and optional approvals), controller executes automated failover
- Anti-flap + rollback semantics prevent oscillation
- Audit log + dashboard annotations provide a clear timeline

---

## Trigger hierarchy (program-level)
The authoritative policy is defined in ADR-042; the summary below exists to align stakeholders.

**Principle:** Prefer signals that indicate true inability to serve traffic over symptoms that could be local or transient.

Proposed priority order (summary):
1) **Reachability loss** (authoritative; cross-region probes + edge reachability)
2) **5xx/timeout burn rate** (SLO burn; corroborated)
3) **Tail latency burn** (p95/p99; corroborated)
4) **Capacity exhaustion / headroom collapse** (used for gating failover and for pre-failover load shedding)

Guardrails (summary):
- Dampening windows to avoid reacting to brief blips
- Hold-down timers to avoid oscillation
- Corroboration requirement for symptom-based triggers
- Per-tenant blast-radius constraints and max flips per hour

---

## Rollback semantics (program-level)
The authoritative policy is defined in ADR-043; summary below:
- Rollback is not simply “signals look better.”
- Rollback requires passing a stability window and respecting hold-down timers.
- Automation must record an audit event for every recommend/execute/rollback action, including evidence signals.
- Anti-flap rate limits apply to both failover and rollback.

---

## Service ownership map
The goal is to make ownership boundaries explicit so incidents do not stall on “who owns the knob.”

| Area | Component / Service | Primary owner | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Signals | perf-canary cross-region probes | Victor Alvarez (Network) | Publishes reachability + latency probes to ingestion stream |
| Signals | fleet headroom / capacity metrics | Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet/Capacity) | Defines headroom thresholds used for routing gates |
| Aggregation | Region Health Service | Ethan Park (Control Plane) | Source of truth for regional health state; TTL + jitter |
| Decisioning | Routing policy engine | Ravi Menon (Routing) / Jordan Lee (Policy engine) | Deterministic evaluation, hierarchy, corroboration, timers |
| Execution | Failover automation controller | Miles Thornton (SRE) / Benji Okafor (Guardrails) | Dry-run, approvals, audit trail, anti-flap, per-tenant limits |
| Data plane | API gateway circuit breakers | Peter Holtz (Overload) | Retries/timeouts, overload responses (Retry-After) |
| Data plane | Streaming redirect/reconnect behavior | Vikram Singh (Data plane) | Idempotency and reconnect guidance |
| Observability | Dashboards/alerts | Allison Grant / Sergio Costa | Burn-rate alerts, failover panels, annotations |
| Tooling | incident-bot summaries | Devon Price | Automated summaries + links posted to incident channels |
| Ops | Runbooks + paging hygiene | Tomáš Novák | Ensures on-call procedures match automation behavior |

---

## Milestones and timeline (Q1–Q2 2026)
This is a planning view; execution is tracked in Linear (ENG-2411 umbrella).

### Phase 1 — Baseline + requirements (Weeks 1–2)
- Capture current failover behavior and top incident failure modes
- Define multi-region SLO targets and event classification
- Finalize trigger hierarchy requirements and acceptance tests

Deliverables:
- Baseline KPI snapshot (TTD/TTFO, false positives, error budget burn)
- Draft signal inventory and data quality notes

### Phase 2 — Design (Weeks 2–4)
- ADRs for signal priority/corroboration/dampening and rollback semantics
- Safety design: per-tenant rate limits, blast-radius constraints
- Game day plan draft + runbook structure

Deliverables:
- ADR-042 and ADR-043 approved
- Initial dashboards spec and alerting plan

### Phase 3 — Build (Weeks 4–8)
- Implement region health aggregation + caching/TTL
- Integrate capacity + network reachability signals into decisioning
- Implement dry-run mode, audit events, approval hooks (optional)
- Improve gateway overload behavior and streaming redirect handling

Deliverables:
- Failover controller in dry-run in production for limited routes
- Audit log pipeline + dashboard annotations

### Phase 4 — Validate (Weeks 8–10)
- Staging rehearsals (synthetic impairment scenarios)
- Controlled production failover game day(s) with limited blast radius

Deliverables:
- Game day execution artifacts (checklist, timeline, metrics)
- Issues found + prioritized follow-ups

### Phase 5 — Rollout + hardening (Weeks 10–12+)
- Expand to more routes/models/tiers
- Tune thresholds/timers based on real behavior
- Finalize quarterly testing cadence and automated reporting plan

Deliverables:
- Updated runbooks, dashboards, and documented quarterly game day cadence

---

## Rollout strategy (safety-first)
### Guiding principles
- Prefer **observability and dry-run** before executing actions in production.
- Limit blast radius by **tenant tier**, **route/model**, and **region pair**.
- Every automated action must be explainable (evidence + audit event).

### Proposed rollout stages
1) **Stage A — Signals + dashboards only**
- Region health service produces health states and evidence
- Dashboards/alerts tuned; no automated routing changes

2) **Stage B — Dry-run recommendations**
- Controller posts recommended actions with confidence + evidence
- On-call validates output quality and correlates to incidents

3) **Stage C — Automation with approvals (prod)**
- Controller can execute but requires on-call acknowledgement for specific tiers/routes
- Strict per-tenant and global rate limits

4) **Stage D — Guarded full automation (prod)**
- Approval removed only after meeting acceptance criteria for false positives, oscillation, and game day success
- Continue quarterly controlled failovers as regression prevention

Feature flags (conceptual; exact names live in config):
- enable_region_health_service
- enable_failover_controller_dry_run
- enable_failover_controller_execute
- require_failover_approval_hooks
- enable_capacity_gating
- enable_overload_retry_after

---

## Risks and mitigations
- **False positives causing unnecessary traffic shifts**
  - Mitigation: corroboration rules, dampening, dry-run burn-in, per-tenant limits, auditability

- **Oscillation under intermittent impairment**
  - Mitigation: hold-down timers, anti-flap rate limits, explicit rollback semantics, SRE-owned guardrails

- **Capacity cascades during failover**
  - Mitigation: capacity gating integrated into routing decisions, pre-failover load shedding, gateway retry budget tuning

- **DNS/traffic steering constraints limit speed**
  - Mitigation: ensure TTL strategy and constraints are understood; test with planned changes; align expectations in success criteria

- **Observability cardinality / cost**
  - Mitigation: metric label review, aggregation safeguards, curated dimensions (tier/reason) with strict controls

---

## Communications and incident response alignment
- incident-bot must post:
  - failover recommended/executed/rolled back
  - reason category and evidence summary
  - links to dashboards and audit log query

- Status page/customer comms:
  - Planned tests require pre-brief for select enterprise accounts and clear wording on possible transient impact
  - Real incidents follow regional impairment playbook; approvals for external updates are owned by on-call + comms approver

---

## Open questions / pending decisions
(Owner in parentheses; track in ENG-2411 as sub-items)
- Which routes/models are in the first production automation cohort? (Mina Haddad / Rafael Mendes)
- Final thresholds for capacity gating and what constitutes “safe to fail over” headroom. (Vanessa Ortiz)
- Whether approvals are required for all enterprise tiers indefinitely or only during rollout. (Sean Gallagher / Neha Kapoor)
- Final definition of a “region-level event” vs partial degradation in SLO reporting. (Aisha Bello / Allison Grant)

---

## Action items (next 2 weeks)
- Finalize dashboard panels + alert annotations required for first rehearsal (Allison Grant / Sergio Costa)
- Complete dry-run acceptance criteria and on-call UX (Miles Thornton / Benji Okafor)
- Validate reachability probe coverage and data quality in us-east and eu-west (Victor Alvarez)
- Confirm first production game day scope + go/no-go checklist and rollback authority (Rafael Mendes / Sean Gallagher)

---

## References
- Multi-region SLO definition: confluence://eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/multi-region-availability-slo-2026
- Trigger hierarchy ADR: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-042-failover-trigger-signal-hierarchy
- Rollback ADR: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-043-automated-routing-policy-rollback
- Region Health Service: confluence://eng-platform/systems-and-services/region-health-service
- Failover controller runbook: confluence://eng-platform/runbooks/routing-policy-failover-controller-runbook
- Game day plan: confluence://oncall-and-incident-response/game-days/controlled-failover-test-plan-q1-2026
- Umbrella tracking: linear://engineering/ENG-2411

title: SLO Impact Simulator and Capacity Sandbox Playbook
preview:
Summary
-------
This playbook documents the SLO Impact Simulator and Capacity Sandbox ("SISCS"), a reproducible environment and procedure for validating SLO changes, error-budget policies, autoscaling rules, and fallback strategies before applying them to production. SISCS is a low-risk way to estimate user-facing availability, latency, and cost impacts across traffic patterns and capacity tiers.

Goals
-----
- Provide an executable sandbox to simulate traffic, faults, and routing policies against a production-like stack.
- Quantify SLO and error budget outcomes for proposed changes (SLO tightening, burst handling, reduced reserved capacity).
- Surface regressions in observability, alerting, and oncall playbooks before rollout.
- Produce reproducible experiment artifacts and a clear decision log for stakeholders.

Scope and non-goals
-------------------
- In-scope: model-serving stacks (API gateway, routing layer, inference service runtime), autoscaling controllers, caching layers, and telemetry pipelines.
- Out-of-scope: customer data migration flows, storage tier redesigns, and pod-level kernel tuning. SISCS does not run on customer private deployments by default.

Architecture and components
---------------------------
- Sandbox cluster: isolated Kubernetes namespace in the staging cluster with production-mirroring config (node types, taints, instance sizes).
- Traffic driver: k6-based suite with Redwood traffic profiles (steady, diurnal, burst, spike).
- Failure injector: Chaos Toolkit integration for latency injection, pod termination, KV cache loss, and slow disk simulation.
- Metrics and tracing: direct stream to a short-retention observability pipeline (Prometheus, Tempo, and Synthetic Traces collector) using the production metric names.
- Decision recorder: Git-backed experiment manifest and result artifacts stored under /artifacts/siscs/<experiment-id>.

Telemetry inputs and canonical signals
------------------------------------
All simulations must ingest and assert against canonical telemetry signals. Use the metrics below as the minimum set for evaluation:

- request.latency.p50 (ms)
- request.latency.p95 (ms)
- request.latency.p99 (ms)
- request.error_rate (5m window)
- token.cost.per_1k (USD)
- kvcache.hit_ratio (10m rolling)
- cpu.utilization (node)
- gpu.utilization (node, if applicable)

Service-level objectives referenced in SISCS
------------------------------------------
Table: SLOs used in the simulator (examples)

| SLO name | Target | Measurement window | Alert threshold | Owner |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| API availability (ingest) | 99.95% | 30d | 99.90% over 24h | ing-service-team |
| Completion latency (p95) | < 350ms | 7d | p95 > 420ms for 1h | runtime-team |
| Embeddings throughput | >= 75 req/s | 1d | sustained < 65 req/s for 30m | embeddings-team |

Runbook: preparing and running an experiment
-------------------------------------------
1. Create an experiment manifest (YAML) in the repo redwood/siscs/experiments. Minimum fields:
   - id: <team>-<short-desc>-YYYYMMDD
   - owner: <team-or-individual>
   - hypothesis: concise statement of expected impact
   - baseline_snapshot: link to last production snapshot or config tag
   - sim_profile: steady | diurnal | burst | spike | custom
   - faults: list of injections (latency, pod_kill, cache_flush)
   - scaling_policy: reference to an autoscaler config or custom scaling YAML

2. Kick off sandbox: 
   - CLI: `./siscsctl apply --experiment experiments/<id>.yaml --wait`
   - The system creates namespace siscs-<id>, deploys manifests, and tears down after completion by default (unless `--retain-artifacts` is set).

3. Run traffic and failures:
   - Start traffic: `./siscsctl drive --experiment <id> --duration 60m`
   - Apply failures: `./siscsctl inject --experiment <id> --scenario faults.yaml`

4. Observe metrics and traces in the short-retention telemetry cluster. The runner will produce an artifacts bundle containing metrics CSVs, flamegraphs, sampled traces, and the raw k6 output.

5. Post-process and compare to baseline: use `siscs-report <artifact-bundle>` to generate a PDF summarizing SLO attainment, cost delta, and top regressions.

Decision criteria and acceptance thresholds
------------------------------------------
For a proposed change to be promoted to production we require:
- SLO attainment delta <= 0.05% absolute for availability and <= 10% relative for p95 latency under the target traffic profile.
- Error budget burn rate no greater than 2x baseline for 24h during burst and spike scenarios.
- Cost delta per 1M tokens must be within product-defined limits (see pricing-and-packaging).
- No new dominant traces causing large tail latency unexplained by simulated faults.

If any of the above fail, the change must either be revised or go through a canary with limited traffic and forced rollback thresholds. The experiment artifacts and decision rationale must be recorded in a Merge Request that references the experiment ID.

Common experiment templates
---------------------------
- Baseline validation: small steady traffic for 2 hours to verify parity with last production snapshot.
- Burst resilience: 5x baseline traffic for 30 minutes followed by 90% traffic drop to validate cold-cache behavior.
- Scale-down economics: reduce reserved replicas by 25% and measure latency/cost impact under diurnal load.
- Fallback validation: force a model fallback path (cheaper quantized variant) and validate end-to-end correctness and latency.

Ownership, roles, and communication
----------------------------------
- Experiment owner: team proposing the change. Responsible for manifest, hypothesis, and running the experiment.
- SRE sandbox steward: maintains siscsctl, traffic profiles, and staging mirrors. Contact: oncall-sre@s.redwood.com.
- Observability owner: guarantees metrics and tracing pipelines are mapped and available for experiment windows.
- Reviewers: include runtime, infra, and product owner for acceptance.

Escalation and rollback guidance
--------------------------------
- If the simulator reveals a severe regression (>= 1% absolute availability drop or sustained p95 > 2x baseline), mark the experiment as failed and open an incident review with the oncall SRE.
- For production rollouts, follow the standard SLO rollback policy: automated canary with 10% traffic for 1h, abort if SLO threshold crossed, and immediately revert traffic routing and autoscaler changes.

Artifacts and required deliverables
----------------------------------
Every experiment must produce the following artifacts and attach them to the MR:
- experiment_manifest.yaml (string)
- artifacts/<id>/metrics/*.csv (list)
- artifacts/<id>/traces/*.json (sampled traces)
- siscs_report_<id>.pdf (summary)
- decision_summary.md (1–2 paragraphs with acceptance decision)

Appendix: CLI examples and config snippets
-----------------------------------------
Example experiment manifest (minimal):
```yaml
id: runtime-fallback-quantize-20260201
owner: runtime-team
hypothesis: "Fallback to quantized model reduces cost by 35% with <10% p95 latency increase"
baseline_snapshot: prod-tag-20260120
sim_profile: burst
faults:
  - type: cache_flush
  - type: latency_injection
scaling_policy: autoscaler/v2/fast-burst
```

Start a test run and retain artifacts:

```bash
./siscsctl apply --experiment experiments/runtime-fallback-quantize-20260201.yaml --retain-artifacts
./siscsctl drive --experiment runtime-fallback-quantize-20260201 --duration 45m
./siscsctl inject --experiment runtime-fallback-quantize-20260201 --scenario faults.yaml
siscs-report artifacts/runtime-fallback-quantize-20260201/bundle.tar.gz --output pdf
```

FAQ and known limitations
-------------------------
- Q: How close is SISCS to production?
  A: We mirror config and instance classes but use short-retention telemetry and synthetic traffic. Certain third-party integrations (billing, 3rd-party auth) are stubbed. Expect small divergence in absolute cost and edge-case latencies.
- Q: Can we run SISCS against private deployments?
  A: Not by default. For enterprise customers with approved data handling, engage the Private Deployments team to schedule an isolated sandbox with customer-managed networking.

Change log
----------
- 2025-11-12: Initial draft and rollout (Ava Chen).
- 2026-02-09: Added decision criteria, new burst profile, and report automation (Miguel Torres).

Related pages and references
---------------------------
- Service capacity governance and error budget handbook (link internal)
- Error budget orchestration and traffic throttle playbook (link internal)
- Canary rollout and automatic rollback policy (link internal)

Contact
-------
For questions or to request an elevated sandbox quota, email siscs-steward@redwood.com or open a ticket in the SRE backlog (encoded as REDWOOD-SRE-QUEUE).

- Experiment owner: team proposing the change. Responsible for manifest, hypothesis, and running the experiment.
- SRE sandbox steward: maintains siscsctl, traffic profiles, and staging mirrors. Contact: oncall-sre@s.redwood.com.
- Observability owner: guarantees metrics and tracing pipelines are mapped and available for experiment windows.
- Reviewers: include runtime, infra, and product owner for acceptance.

Escalation and rollback guidance
--------------------------------
- If the simulator reveals a severe regression (>= 1% absolute availability drop or sustained p95 > 2x baseline), mark the experiment as failed and open an incident review with the oncall SRE.
- For production rollouts, follow the standard SLO rollback policy: automated canary with 10% traffic for 1h, abort if SLO threshold crossed, and immediately revert traffic routing and autoscaler changes.

Artifacts and required deliverables
----------------------------------
Every experiment must produce the following artifacts and attach them to the MR:
- experiment_manifest.yaml (string)
- artifacts/<id>/metrics/*.csv (list)
- artifacts/<id>/traces/*.json (sampled traces)
- siscs_report_<id>.pdf (summary)
- decision_summary.md (1–2 paragraphs with acceptance decision)

Appendix: CLI examples and config snippets
-----------------------------------------
Example experiment manifest (minimal):
```yaml
id: runtime-fallback-quantize-20260201
owner: runtime-team
hypothesis: "Fallback to quantized model reduces cost by 35% with <10% p95 latency increase"
baseline_snapshot: prod-tag-20260120
sim_profile: burst
faults:
  - type: cache_flush
  - type: latency_injection
scaling_policy: autoscaler/v2/fast-burst
```

Start a test run and retain artifacts:

```bash
./siscsctl apply --experiment experiments/runtime-fallback-quantize-20260201.yaml --retain-artifacts
./siscsctl drive --experiment runtime-fallback-quantize-20260201 --duration 45m
./siscsctl inject --experiment runtime-fallback-quantize-20260201 --scenario faults.yaml
siscs-report artifacts/runtime-fallback-quantize-20260201/bundle.tar.gz --output pdf
```

FAQ and known limitations
-------------------------
- Q: How close is SISCS to production?
  A: We mirror config and instance classes but use short-retention telemetry and synthetic traffic. Certain third-party integrations (billing, 3rd-party auth) are stubbed. Expect small divergence in absolute cost and edge-case latencies.
- Q: Can we run SISCS against private deployments?
  A: Not by default. For enterprise customers with approved data handling, engage the Private Deployments team to schedule an isolated sandbox with customer-managed networking.

Change log
----------
- 2025-11-12: Initial draft and rollout (Ava Chen).
- 2026-02-09: Added decision criteria, new burst profile, and report automation (Miguel Torres).

Related pages and references
---------------------------
- Service capacity governance and error budget handbook (link internal)
- Error budget orchestration and traffic throttle playbook (link internal)
- Canary rollout and automatic rollback policy (link internal)

Contact
-------
For questions or to request an elevated sandbox quota, email siscs-steward@redwood.com or open a ticket in the SRE backlog (encoded as REDWOOD-SRE-QUEUE).

title: canary-rehearsal-and-escalation-exercise-playbook-2026
preview:
Overview

This playbook codifies a recurring, cross-team canary rehearsal and escalation tabletop exercise designed to validate: SLO behavior during partial rollouts, automated and manual rollback paths, paging and escalation flows, and end-to-end telemetry (synthetic and production). The goal is to surface gaps in runbooks, alerting, and team coordination before a real incident. Exercises should be scheduled quarterly for each high-impact service and after major changes to routing, autoscaling, or rollout tooling.

Scope

- Services: model-serving frontends, request-router (smart-router), autoscaler, cache layer (prefix/KV), billing-rate-limiters.
- Failure modes simulated: degraded tail latency, model-variant regression, cache-priming failure, burst-induced overload, and partial config rollouts.
- Not in scope: full data-plane outages requiring cloud provider escalation; these are covered by separate disaster recovery drills.

Objectives

1. Verify canary traffic routing and metrics capture for the nominated SLOs within 5 minutes of cutover.
2. Confirm automatic fallback triggers (model-variant fallback or routing to older commit) work as designed.
3. Validate human escalation and paging flows to oncall, product owner, and infra leads.
4. Exercise capacity hedging: ability to increase dedicated capacity or route to burst pool within 15 minutes.
5. Capture improvement actions and update runbooks within 48 business hours.

Prerequisites

- Exercise owner assigned and communicated to participating teams 7 days prior.
- Canary configuration saved as a named spec (e.g., canary-specs/rehearsal/<service>/<date>).
- Synthetic traffic harness configured and smoke tests passing.
- Telemetry dashboard snapshot and alert policies baselined.
- Test tenant(s) or token-scoped traffic that will not be billed/charged.

Roles & Responsibilities

- Exercise Lead: schedules, coordinates, runs the tabletop, collects artifacts (1 person).
- Service Owner: responsible for executing canary cut, monitoring, and rollback (team lead for the service).
- Oncall Engineer: primary responder for the canary window.
- Platform/Autoscaler Engineer: executes capacity actions (scale-up, burst pool attach).
- Product Safety / Trust: reviews customer-impact considerations where applicable.
- Scribe: records timeline, screenshots, and action items.

Exercise Types (pick one per rehearsal)

A. Performance Degradation (tail latency regression)
- Inject: increase request size or force quantized-path fallback to simulate slow tokens.
- Validate: SLA and P99 latency rise observed on the canary slice; alerts fire.

B. Behavioral Regression (quality drift)
- Inject: swap in a model variant with a synthetic prompt set that yields measurable metric delta (e.g., top-1 rerank score).
- Validate: quality monitors flag regression; routing fallback or rollback is exercised.

C. Capacity Surge (burst)
- Inject: ramp synthetic requests to 5x baseline for 8 minutes targeted at canary slice.
- Validate: autoscaler behavior, graceful degradation policies, and fair-share throttles.

D. Telemetry Loss (observability failure)
- Inject: disable a telemetry collector for the canary slice.
- Validate: alerting on missing telemetry, alternative evidence sources, and documentation of the telemetry gap.

Step-by-step Playbook

1) Pre-day (7–1 days before)
   - Publish exercise plan, list of participants, and expected blast radius.
   - Register canary spec as: `canary-specs/rehearsal/<service>/<YYYYMMDD>-<owner>`
   - Validate synthetic harness: `scripts/harness/run_smoke.sh --spec canary-specs/...`
   - Snapshot dashboards: save as `dashshots/<service>/<YYYYMMDD>`

2) Day-of (T=0)
   - 00:00 — Kickoff call (15 minutes) for objectives, abort criteria, and roles.
   - 00:15 — Apply canary routing: `rtrctl cutover --spec canary-specs/... --weight 10%`
   - 00:20 — Start synthetic traffic for chosen exercise type.
   - 00:25 — Scribe captures initial telemetry and dashboard links.

3) Observation window (T+5 to T+30 minutes)
   - Monitor SLOs: latency, error-rate, and customer-impact indicators.
   - If automated fallback conditions met, verify actions and record timestamps.
   - If rollout not safe, follow rollback procedure (see step 5).

4) Escalation path (triggered by defined thresholds)
   - Threshold examples: P99 latency > 2500ms on canary or error-rate increase > 3x baseline for 5 minutes.
   - Primary oncall page —> Platform lead if unresolved within 10 minutes —> Eng director if unresolved within 30 minutes.
   - Use Pager shorthand: `pager duty: page-oncall --service <svc>`

5) Rollback and Containment
   - Automated rollback: confirm that the router executes the fallback within configured SLA. Record `fallback_timestamp`.
   - Manual rollback command example: `rtrctl rollback --spec canary-specs/... --to-tag v<previous>`
   - Validate system recovery: confirm metrics return to baseline for 3 consecutive minutes.

6) Wrap-up (T+60 to T+120)
   - Capture timeline, console logs, and dashboard snapshots.
   - Collect participant notes and raw screenshots into the exercise folder: `gs://redwood-exercises/<service>/<date>/`
   - Raise action tickets for observed gaps with severity, owner, and due date (max 48 business hours to triage).

Telemetry & Validation Checklist (table)

- SLOs to monitor:
  - Availability SLO: 99.9% (expected impact < 0.01% of total traffic during canary).
  - Latency SLO: P99 < 1200ms for production; canary threshold set to 2500ms for early detection.
  - Error-rate SLO: 0.1% baseline; alert at 0.3% sustained for 5 minutes.

- Dashboards and Alerts to snapshot:
  - Canary slice latency/chart (P50/P90/P99)
  - Model-quality regression chart (eval score deltas)
  - Autoscaler event stream and node attach logs
  - Request-router routing table and fallback events

Escalation Matrix (succinct)

1. Oncall engineer (first 10 minutes): ACK page, attempt quick mitigation (traffic drain, increase concurrency limit).
2. Platform lead (10-30 minutes): perform capacity attach or policy update (burst pool attach).
3. Service owner and product (30-60 minutes): authorize broader rollback or customer comms.
4. Exec oncall / Director (>60 minutes): coordinate incident declaration and support SRE in postmortem.

Runbook Checklist (pre-populated for each rehearsal)

- [ ] Canary spec committed to repo with descriptive name.
- [ ] Synthetic harness validated within test tenant.
- [ ] Dashboard snapshots created and linked.
- [ ] Alerting policies and escalation contacts verified.
- [ ] Abort criteria communicated and accepted by attendees.

Post-exercise Actions & Governance

- Immediate (within 48 business hours): triage all action items; update runbook with at least one code/config fix or documentation change.
- Medium (2 weeks): implement monitoring improvements or escalation automations agreed in the exercise.
- Quarterly: incorporate lessons into SRE onboarding training and run the exercise with a fresh set of engineers.

Artifacts to capture

- Timeline CSV with event timestamps.
- Dashboard snapshots and raw metrics export (Prometheus export or telemetry dump).
- Synthetic harness logs and request traces (trace IDs).
- Action ticket list with owners and SLAs.

Templates

- Post-exercise summary (example):

  Title: Canary Rehearsal — <service> — <YYYY-MM-DD>
  Lead: <name>
  Type: <Performance|Behavioral|Capacity|Telemetry>
  Summary: Short 3-4 sentence narrative of what happened.
  Timeline: Bullet of timestamps and events.
  Impact: Which SLOs were affected and how.
  Actions: ActionID, brief description, owner, due date.

Appendix: Common CLI snippets / examples

- Apply canary (example):
```
rtrctl cutover --spec canary-specs/rehearsal/modelsrv/20260201-mchen --weight 10 --dry-run=false
```

- Rollback (example):
```
rtrctl rollback --service modelsrv --from canary-20260201 --to v20260129 --comment "rehearsal rollback"
```

- Attach burst pool (example):
```
platformctl attach-burst --service modelsrv --pool burst-pool-1 --reason "rehearsal: capacity surge test"
```

- Export dashboards (example): `dashctl export --id <dash-id> --range "-1h" --out gs://...`

Appendix: Abort Criteria (examples)

- Any production customer impact detected outside the test tokens or test tenants.
- Data exfiltration or unexpected access to PII lists is observed.
- Observable billing meter increase affecting customer accounts.

Ownership & Review cadence

- Owner: Eng-SRE Reliability Initiatives (rotation: Maya Chen -> Arjun Patel -> Lucia Gomez).
- Review: Annual review plus a mandatory review after any relevant incident or large rollout.

Related pages and references

- Canary rollout automation checklist — internal link (see related_pages above).
- SLO alerting policy reference: `/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/slo-alerting-policy-2025`

Contact

- For questions or to request an exercise: `eng-sre-reliability@redwood.ai`

End of playbook
